## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "Relative Humidity" and explain why it is the most commonly used parameter in agricultural meteorology.*

**Model Answer:**
Relative Humidity (RH) is defined as the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor in air to the saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\text{RH} = \frac{e}{e_s(T)} \times 100\ \%
$$

In agriculture, RH is the most-used parameter because:
1. It directly governs **evapotranspiration** rates from crops.
2. It determines **disease pressure** (fungal, bacterial).
3. It is the input for the **Penman–Monteith reference evapotranspiration equation** used in irrigation scheduling.
4. It is easily measured by cheap sensors and reported by weather stations worldwide.
5. It controls the **drying rate of harvested grains, hay, and silage**.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*List any three types of hygrometers and state one agricultural application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Type | Principle (in brief) | Agricultural Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hair Hygrometer | Organic fibre lengthens with RH | Simple field weather hut |
| 2 | Capacitive Sensor | Polymer dielectric changes capacitance | Greenhouse climate controller |
| 3 | Dew-Point Mirror | Optical detection of condensation | Seed moisture lab / quality control |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) regulation, every Module Part-B question carries **14 marks**, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

#### 🅐 Question A — Wet-Dry Bulb Psychrometer (Numerical + Theory)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model-1]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **Whirling (Assmann) Psychrometer**. Derive the Sprung psychrometric equation from the energy balance on the wet bulb.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
The Assmann psychrometer consists of two identical mercury-in-glass (or thermistor) thermometers mounted side-by-side in a polished metal frame.
- The **dry bulb** measures ambient air temperature $T$.
- The **wet bulb** is covered with a muslin wick wetted with distilled water. When the instrument is whirled (or fan-ventilated), air flows past both bulbs at $\sim 2\ \text{m/s}$.
- Water evaporates from the wick, absorbing latent heat $\lambda_w$ from the wick, which cools the wet bulb to $T_w$. The wet bulb stabilizes at $T_w$ when sensible heat gain from the air equals the latent heat loss.

**Sketch (ASCII art for KTU answer sheet):**
```
   _______________
  |  ⊙       ⊙   |  ← Two thermometers
  |  T(dry) T(wet)|     (T) and (T_w)
  |_______________|
        |  |
       FAN/MOTOR     ← Spring-driven whirling handle
        |  |
       ====         ← Wick on wet bulb in water cup
```

**Derivation of Sprung Equation:**
*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. The student should reproduce the four key steps: latent heat loss = sensible heat gain, mass-transfer analogy, definition of psychrometric constant, final isolation of $e$.)*

**Final result to be boxed:**

$$
\boxed{\,e = e_s(T_w) - A \cdot P \cdot (T - T_w)\,}
$$

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A psychrometer in a green polyhouse reads **dry-bulb $T = 28^\circ\text{C}$** and **wet-bulb $T_w = 24^\circ\text{C}$** at atmospheric pressure $P = 101.3\ \text{kPa}$. Using the Assmann constant $A = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and the Magnus formula, calculate the **(i)** actual vapor pressure $e$, **(ii)** Relative Humidity RH, and **(iii)** Dew Point $T_d$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Saturation vapor pressure at $T_w = 24^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$
e_s(24) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 24}{24 + 237.3}\right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.5886) \approx 2.983\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual vapor pressure using Sprung's equation:**

$$
e = 2.983 - (8.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 101.3 \times (28 - 24)
$$

$$
e = 2.983 - 0.08104 \times 4 = 2.983 - 0.3242
$$

$$
\boxed{e \approx 2.659\ \text{kPa}}
$$

**Step 3 — Saturation vapor pressure at $T = 28^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$
e_s(28) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 28}{28 + 237.3}\right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.8221) \approx 3.778\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Relative Humidity:**

$$
\text{RH} = \frac{2.659}{3.778} \times 100 \approx \boxed{70.4\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Dew Point:**

$$
T_d = \frac{237.3 \cdot \ln(2.659 / 0.6108)}{17.27 - \ln(2.659 / 0.6108)} = \frac{237.3 \cdot \ln(4.354)}{17.27 - \ln(4.354)}
$$

$$
= \frac{237.3 \times 1.4715}{17.27 - 1.4715} = \frac{349.20}{15.798}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_d \approx 22.10^\circ\text{C}}
$$

**Final Result:** $e = 2.659\ \text{kPa}$; $\text{RH} = 70.4\%$; $T_d = 22.1^\circ\text{C}$.

---

#### 🅑 Question B — Capacitive Hygrometer + Comparison (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model-2]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With the help of a diagram, explain the **construction and working of a capacitive polymer humidity sensor**. State any **two advantages** and **two limitations**.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A capacitive humidity sensor consists of:
1. A **lower aluminium electrode** (e.g., a thin film deposited on a silicon substrate).
2. A **hygroscopic polymer dielectric layer** ($\sim 1\ \mu\text{m}$ thick) — typically polyimide, cellulose acetate, or PMMA — deposited on the lower electrode.
3. An **upper porous gold or platinum electrode** thin enough to allow water vapor to pass through.
4. A **porous PTFE or stainless-steel mesh cover** to protect the sensor from dust, condensation, and contamination.
5. Three lead terminals for excitation and signal pickup.

**Working Principle:**
Water vapor diffuses through the porous upper electrode and is absorbed by the polymer. Because the dielectric constant of water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) is much higher than that of the dry polymer ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2\text{ to }5$), the sensor capacitance increases linearly with relative humidity. An external oscillator converts the capacitance change to a frequency, then a frequency-to-voltage converter gives an analog output proportional to RH.

**Calibration Equation:**

$$
C = C_0 (1 + k \cdot \text{RH} / 100)
$$

**Advantages:**
1. Fast response time (5–30 seconds) suitable for dynamic measurement.
2. Low power consumption; compatible with IoT/edge devices.
3. Linear output; can be directly interfaced to a microcontroller ADC.

**Limitations:**
1. Polymer degrades on prolonged exposure to condensing humidity (>95%) and certain chemicals (chlorine, ammonia).
2. Moderate accuracy ($\pm 2$ to $3\%$) requires periodic recalibration against a salt-solution reference.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Compare the **Hair Hygrometer**, **Wet-and-Dry Bulb Psychrometer**, and **Capacitive Hygrometer** on the basis of: principle, range, accuracy, response time, and agricultural suitability.

**Model Solution:** *(Construct a comparison table — refer to Section 3.4 of these notes for the complete matrix.)*

| Parameter | Hair Hygrometer | Psychrometer | Capacitive Sensor |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Principle | Hygroscopic fibre length | Evaporative cooling | Dielectric change |
| Range (%RH) | 20–100 | 10–100 | 0–100 |
| Accuracy | $\pm 5\%$ | $\pm 2\%$ | $\pm 3\%$ |
| Response | 1–3 min | $\sim 1$ min | 5–30 s |
| Best Agricultural Use | Field hut | Agro-met station | Greenhouse, drone |

**Conclusion:** The **capacitive sensor** is the modern all-round choice; the **psychrometer** remains the most accurate field instrument when properly maintained; the **hair hygrometer** is largely obsolete but still seen in mechanical weather stations.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in KTU 2024 hygrometer questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — The psychrometric constant $A$ requires $P$ in kPa, not Pa. A 1000× error will fail the numerical.
> 2. **Mixing up $e_s$ and $e$** — Always compute $e_s$ (saturation) at the **dry bulb** temperature for RH; use $T_w$ only inside $e_s(T_w)$ in Sprung's equation.
> 3. **Sign error in Sprung's equation** — It is $e = e_s(T_w) - A P (T - T_w)$, **not** $e_s(T_w) + ...$. Getting the sign wrong yields $e > e_s$ (impossible).
> 4. **Skipping the sketch** — For a 7-mark question, a **neat labelled diagram** is mandatory; without it, at least 2 marks are lost.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — e.g., "assuming steady-state, well-ventilated, distilled water wick" — examiners reward this.
> 6. **Missing dew-point formula** — Many students compute RH but skip $T_d$ and VPD, leaving marks on the table.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Hygrometer** = instrument measuring the moisture content of air.
- [x] **Relative Humidity (RH)** = $\dfrac{e}{e_s(T)} \times 100\%$, the most-reported parameter in agro-meteorology.
- [x] **Absolute Humidity** = mass of water vapor per unit volume of air ($\text{g/m}^3$).
- [x] **Dew Point** = temperature at which saturation occurs; can be calculated by inverting the Magnus equation.
- [x] **Magnus Formula** for saturation vapor pressure: $e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right)$, with $T$ in $^\circ$C.
- [x] **Sprung Psychrometric Equation:** $e = e_s(T_w) - A P (T - T_w)$.
- [x] **Psychrometric constant** $A \approx 6.66 \times 10^{-4}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ (still) and $8.0 \times 10^{-4}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ (Assmann/ventilated).
- [x] **Hair Hygrometer** uses the length change of human/animal hair ($\Delta L \propto \Delta\text{RH}$).
- [x] **Capacitive sensor** exploits the high dielectric constant of water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$); polymer layer is the active element.
- [x] **Resistive (LiCl) sensor** resistance falls exponentially with RH: $R = R_0 \exp(\beta/\text{RH})$.
- [x] **Dew-point chilled-mirror** hygrometer is the most accurate field instrument ($\pm 0.2\%$ RH).
- [x] **VPD** = $e_s(T) - e$ is critical for greenhouse climate control and irrigation scheduling.
- [x] **WMO Standard Reference:** gravimetric (weighing) hygrometer.
- [x] **Agricultural applications:** greenhouse control, grain storage, disease forecasting, soil-moisture estimation, agro-chemical spraying.
- [x] **Fungal disease pressure** rises sharply when RH $> 85\%$ for $>4$ hours.
- [x] **Common sign & unit errors** are the most frequent reason for losing 2–3 marks in Part-B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermocouples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermocouples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermocouple (ISA Definition):** A thermocouple is a **temperature-measuring device** consisting of two **dissimilar thermoelectric conductors** joined at one end (the *measuring junction* or *hot junction*), such that the open-circuit voltage developed at the other end (the *reference junction* or *cold junction*) is a **predictable function of the temperature difference** between the two junctions, in accordance with the **Seebeck Effect**.

> The net thermoelectric electromotive force (EMF) generated is given by the integral of the **relative Seebeck coefficient** $S_{AB}(T)$ over the temperature span between the two junctions.

**Key Nomenclature in KTU / ISA Standards:**

| Term | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Measuring (Hot) Junction | $T_H$ | The junction exposed to the unknown process temperature |
| Reference (Cold) Junction | $T_C$ | The junction maintained at a known reference temperature |
| Seebeck Coefficient | $S_{AB}$ | Net thermoelectric sensitivity in **μV/°C** |
| Thermoelectric EMF | $E_{AB}$ | Output voltage in **millivolts (mV)** |
| Extension/Compensating Wires | — | Low-cost lead wires to bring EMF to instrument |

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Two-Person Conversation**

Imagine two people, **Mr. A** (a hot-tempered Latin speaker) and **Mr. B** (a calm Nordic speaker), connected at the chest by a **translator**. When the chest connection (*hot junction*) is heated, Mr. A "speaks faster" (electrons gain kinetic energy) than Mr. B. The *difference in speaking rate* (electron energy) is interpreted by the translator as a **measurable "volume" signal (voltage)** at their mouths. If we keep the mouths (cold junction) at a known cool temperature and listen carefully with a voltmeter, we can **decode the original hot temperature**.

- The **"hot-tempered" metal** is typically **Nickel-Chromium (Chromel®)**.
- The **"calm" metal** is typically **Nickel-Aluminium (Alumel®)**.
- The **"translator"** is the welded *measuring junction*.
- The **"listening meter"** is the millivolt readout at the **reference junction (ice-bath at 0 °C)**.

In agriculture, this allows a farmer to drop a tiny bead-junction sensor deep into a **compost heap, soil profile, silo, or greenhouse** and read the temperature remotely.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Temperature:** The **cold junction (ice point) is fixed at 0 °C = 273.15 K**, by international convention (ITS-90), because pure ice–water equilibrium is highly reproducible in the laboratory.

- **Seebeck Coefficient typical range:** **8 – 80 μV/°C** (depending on the type of thermocouple).
- **Standard output magnitude:** **0 – 70 mV** for 0 – 1400 °C measurement.
- **Standard tolerance classes** (per **IEC 60584 / ASTM E230**): **Class 1, Class 2, Class 3**.

---

### 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping for KTU 2024

| KTU Bloom Level | CO Code | Outcome Statement |
|---|---|---|
| Understand (L2) | CO1 | Identify the working principles of agricultural sensors. |
| Apply (L3) | CO2 | Select appropriate temperature sensors for agri-processes. |
| Analyse (L4) | CO3 | Evaluate cold-junction compensation and error sources. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A thermocouple functions because of the **Seebeck Effect**, discovered by **Thomas Johann Seebeck (1821)**. The logical flow is:

1. **Two dissimilar metals (A and B)** are physically joined at the *measuring junction* held at temperature $T_H$.
2. **Free electrons** in each metal migrate from the hot end toward the cold end at different rates, due to differences in the **Fermi level (electron energy distribution)**.
3. This differential diffusion creates a **net contact potential** at the junction plus a **net Thomson potential** along each conductor leg.
4. The cumulative EMF $E_{AB}$ between the open ends of conductors A and B is measured by a **high-impedance voltmeter** (so no current flows and the meter does not load the circuit).
5. By **prior calibration** (NIST reference tables), the measured millivolts are mapped to the temperature of the hot junction $T_H$ (with $T_C$ assumed known or compensated).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why High-Impedance Meter?**
> If current flowed, an additional **Peltier EMF** (Peltier effect) would be introduced at the junctions, **corrupting the reading**. A voltmeter with input impedance $\geq 10 \ \text{M}\Omega$ essentially nullifies this error.

---

### 2.2 The Three Thermoelectric Effects (Foundation Block)

| # | Effect | Discoverer | Phenomenon | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Seebeck Effect** | T. J. Seebeck, 1821 | Temperature difference $\rightarrow$ EMF | **Thermocouple (temperature sensing)** |
| 2 | **Peltier Effect** | J. C. A. Peltier, 1834 | Current $\rightarrow$ Heating/Cooling at junctions | Thermoelectric cooler (Peltier module) |
| 3 | **Thomson Effect** | William Thomson, 1851 | Current + temperature gradient $\rightarrow$ heat absorption/evolution | Refinement in high-precision calibration |

---

### 2.3 Fundamental Thermoelectric EMF Equation

For two conductors **A** and **B** whose individual Seebeck coefficients are $S_A(T)$ and $S_B(T)$, the **net** thermoelectric EMF is given by:

$$E_{AB} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \bigl[S_B(T) - S_A(T)\bigr] \, dT = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T) \, dT$$

Where:
- $S_{AB}(T)$ is the **relative Seebeck coefficient** of the thermocouple pair, in **μV/°C**.
- The integration runs from the **cold-junction temperature** $T_C$ to the **hot-junction temperature** $T_H$.
- $E_{AB}$ has units of **microvolts (μV)** or **millivolts (mV)**.

For a **first-order linear approximation** (valid over narrow spans), the relationship simplifies to:

$$E_{AB} \approx S_{AB}^{\text{avg}} \cdot (T_H - T_C)$$

Where $S_{AB}^{\text{avg}}$ is the **average Seebeck coefficient** evaluated over the temperature interval.

---

### 2.4 The Three Fundamental Laws of Thermocouples (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law 1 — Law of Intermediate Metals (Galvanic / Lead-Wire Law)**
> *The insertion of a third (intermediate) metal C into the thermocouple circuit will NOT alter the net EMF, provided both new junctions formed with C are at the SAME temperature.*
> $$\boxed{E_{AB}(T_H, T_C) = E_{AC}(T_H, T_{int}) + E_{CB}(T_{int}, T_C) \quad \text{(when } T_{int} \text{ is common)}}$$
> **Application:** Justifies the use of solder, terminal blocks, copper lead wires, and the voltmeter itself in the measuring loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law 2 — Law of Intermediate Temperatures**
> *If a thermocouple with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ produces an EMF $E_{13}$, and a similar thermocouple with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_2$ produces $E_{12}$, then:*
> $$E_{13} = E_{12} + E_{23}$$
> **Application:** This is the **theoretical basis of cold-junction compensation** — the cold junction need NOT be at 0 °C; we algebraically add the EMF corresponding to the actual cold-junction temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Law 3 — Law of Inserted Material**
> *If a uniform conductor of material C is inserted anywhere in the circuit and both its ends are at the SAME temperature, the net EMF is unchanged.*
> **Application:** Permits the use of **extension / compensating cables** that share the same temperature profile as the thermoelements but are made of cheaper metals.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> **Table 2.1 — KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet for Thermocouples**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Seebeck EMF | $E_{AB} = \displaystyle\int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T) \, dT$ | $S_{AB}$ is temperature-dependent |
| 2 | Linear Approximation | $E_{AB} \approx S_{AB}^{\text{avg}} \cdot \Delta T$ | $\Delta T = T_H - T_C$ |
| 3 | Cold-Junction Compensation | $E_{text{meas}}(T_H) = E_{\text{actual}}(T_H, T_C) - E_{\text{cold}}(T_C, 0\,^\circ\text{C})$ | Most critical for KTU problems |
| 4 | Law of Intermediate Metals | $E_{AB} = E_{AC} + E_{CB}$ at the same $T$ | Valid only at common $T$ |
| 5 | Law of Intermediate Temperatures | $E_{13} = E_{12} + E_{23}$ | Foundation of software compensation |
| 6 | ITS-90 Polynomial (Type K) | $E = \displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i \, T^i + a_0 \, e^{a_1 (T - a_2)^2}$ | $n = 8\text{–}9$ coefficients |
| 7 | Sensitivity Definition | $S_{AB} = \dfrac{dE_{AB}}{dT}$ | Units: μV/°C |
| 8 | Series-Connected Thermopile | $E_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot E_{\text{single}}$ | $N$ = number of junctions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in Agriculture:** Thermocouples are the **cheapest, fastest, and most rugged** temperature sensors. They are used in **soil-probe thermometry, greenhouse climate control, dairy pasteurization, grain-silo monitoring, and greenhouse-thermal-gradient mapping** — all central to **PEICT753**.

---

### 2.6 Classification of Thermocouples (ISA / NBS Letter Designations)

| ISA Type | Positive Leg | Negative Leg | Range (°C) | Sensitivity (μV/°C) | Agricultural Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type B** | Pt-30% Rh | Pt-6% Rh | 0 to 1820 | ~5–10 | Not common in agriculture |
| **Type E** | Chromel | Constantan | −270 to 1000 | ~68 (highest) | High-precision greenhouse research |
| **Type J** | Iron | Constantan | −210 to 1200 | ~50 | General-purpose, but iron rusts in wet soil |
| **Type K** | Chromel | Alumel | −270 to 1370 | ~41 | **Most widely used — soil & silo** |
| **Type N** | Nicrosil | Nisil | −270 to 1300 | ~39 | High-stability, modern replacement for K |
| **Type R** | Pt-13% Rh | Platinum | −50 to 1768 | ~12 | Reference standard, lab use |
| **Type S** | Pt-10% Rh | Platinum | −50 to 1768 | ~11 | Reference standard, lab use |
| **Type T** | Copper | Constantan | −270 to 400 | ~46 | **Wet/soil environments, food/dairy** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For PEICT753 (Agriculture):** **Type K** (general) and **Type T** (wet, biological, dairy) are the most common.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Seebeck EMF from First Principles

We start with the energy distribution of free electrons in a metallic conductor. The **Fermi-Dirac distribution** predicts the average energy of conduction electrons. When a temperature gradient $dT/dx$ is applied along a single conductor, electrons at the hot end have higher average kinetic energy than those at the cold end, producing a **diffusion current** that, in an open circuit, is balanced by a **built-in electric field** $E(x)$ that creates a counter-current.

**Step 1 — Define the Seebeck coefficient of a single material:**
For a single conductor with electron energy per unit charge $\mu(T)$ (electrochemical potential), the absolute Seebeck coefficient is:

$$S = -\frac{1}{eT}\,\frac{d\mu}{d\ln(T)}$$

**Step 2 — Combine two conductors (A and B):**
When two dissimilar conductors are joined, the net EMF is the difference of their individual contributions:

$$E_{AB} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \bigl[S_B(T) - S_A(T)\bigr]\,dT$$

This is the **fundamental Seebeck equation**.

**Step 3 — Apply the linear approximation:**
For small temperature ranges where $S_{AB}$ is approximately constant:

$$E_{AB} \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_H - T_C)$$

**Step 4 — Express in practical form:**
Letting $T_C = 0\,^\circ\text{C}$ (ice-point reference), and writing $\Delta T = T_H - 0 = T_H$:

$$E_{AB} = S_{AB} \cdot T_H$$

For **Type K** thermocouple, $S_{AB} \approx 41 \,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ over 0–500 °C, so for $T_H = 200\,^\circ\text{C}$:

$$E_{AB} = 41 \times 200 = 8200 \,\mu\text{V} = 8.20 \,\text{mV}$$

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CJC is required:** Real instruments are not at 0 °C ice baths. The cold junction (terminal block of the instrument) sits at ambient $T_C \neq 0\,^\circ\text{C}$. The voltmeter reading $E_{\text{meas}}$ corresponds to the EMF generated between $T_H$ and $T_C$, NOT between $T_H$ and 0 °C. We must add a correction term.

**Step 1 — Write the measured EMF:**

$$E_{\text{meas}} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T)\,dT$$

**Step 2 — Express the desired EMF (referred to 0 °C):**

$$E_{\text{desired}} = \int_{0}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T)\,dT$$

**Step 3 — Express the cold-junction correction EMF:**

$$E_{\text{cold}} = \int_{0}^{T_C} S_{AB}(T)\,dT$$

**Step 4 — Combine using the Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**
The Law of Intermediate Temperatures states that:

$$\int_{0}^{T_H} = \int_{0}^{T_C} + \int_{T_C}^{T_H}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{\text{desired}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{cold}}$$

**Step 5 — Algorithm for software CJC (commonly asked in KTU):**

```
Read T_C from a separate sensor (e.g., silicon diode or RTD)
Look up E_cold = f(T_C) from thermocouple tables
Read E_meas from the thermocouple voltmeter
Compute E_total = E_meas + E_cold
Look up T_H = f⁻¹(E_total) from tables
Display T_H
```

**Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern):**
A Type K thermocouple reads $E_{\text{meas}} = 20.644 \,\text{mV}$. The cold-junction temperature is $T_C = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$. From Type K tables:
- $E(T_C = 25\,^\circ\text{C}) = 1.000 \,\text{mV}$ (approximate)
- $E_{\text{total}} = 20.644 + 1.000 = 21.644 \,\text{mV}$
- From the inverse table: $T_H \approx 525\,^\circ\text{C}$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the ITS-90 Polynomial Approximation

The **NIST ITS-90 polynomial** for thermocouples expresses EMF as a function of temperature:

$$E(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i \, T^i + a_0 \, e^{a_1 (T - a_2)^2}$$

For **Type K** thermocouple (0 °C to 1370 °C), the reference coefficients are:

| $i$ | $a_i$ (with T in °C, E in μV) |
|---|---|
| 0 | $-1.7600413686 \times 10^{1}$ |
| 1 | $+3.8921204975 \times 10^{1}$ |
| 2 | $+1.8558770032 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $-9.9457592874 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 4 | $+3.1840945719 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| 5 | $-5.6072833440 \times 10^{-10}$ |
| 6 | $+5.6075059059 \times 10^{-13}$ |
| 7 | $-3.2020710003 \times 10^{-16}$ |
| 8 | $+9.7151147152 \times 10^{-20}$ |
| 9 | $-1.2104721275 \times 10^{-23}$ |

**Step-by-Step Sample Calculation at T = 100 °C:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E(100) &= a_0 + a_1(100) + a_2(100)^2 + a_3(100)^3 + \cdots \\
&= -17.600 + 3892.12 + 185.588 - 99.458 + 3.184 - 0.5607 + 0.000560 - \cdots \\
&\approx 4095 \,\mu\text{V} \\
&\approx 4.095 \,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the standard table value $E_{K}(100\,^\circ\text{C}) = 4.096 \,\text{mV}$ within rounding error.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Error Sources in Thermocouple Measurements

| Error Type | Cause | Magnitude (Typical) | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold-junction error** | Reference junction not at 0 °C | Up to 1–2 °C | Software CJC + RTD/diode sensor |
| **Lead-wire error** | Extension wires at temperature gradient | 1–5 °C | Use correct extension/compensating cable |
| **Decalibration (drift)** | Ageing, oxidation, contamination at high T | 1–10 °C over years | Periodic re-calibration |
| **Thermocouple inhomogeneity** | Localised impurity in thermoelement | 0.5–2 °C | Use premium-grade wire |
| **Reference-junction gradient** | Self-heating of nearby components | 0.1–0.5 °C | Isothermal terminal block |
| **Shunt error (low-Z meter)** | Loading effect | 5–20 % of reading | Use high-impedance (≥ 10 MΩ) meter |
| **Thermal shunting** | Conduction along lead wires | Variable | Use thin lead wires, deep immersion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Thermocouple Measurement System (for Agriculture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Process Medium Soil Greenhouse Silo"] --> B["Hot Junction T_H"]
    B --> C["Thermocouple Wires Type K or T"]
    C --> D["Extension Compensating Cables"]
    D --> E["Isothermal Terminal Block T_C"]
    E --> F["Cold Junction Sensor Silicon Diode"]
    F --> G["High Impedance Amplifier Gain 1000"]
    G --> H["ADC 16 or 24 bit"]
    H --> I["Microcontroller CJC Algorithm"]
    I --> J["Reference Table ITS 90 Lookup"]
    J --> K["Display T_H in Degree Celsius"]
    F -.CJC Signal.-> I
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Block-level functional architecture of a thermocouple-based agricultural temperature measurement system.** The CJC loop is shown by the dashed line, indicating that the cold-junction sensor value is *algebraically added* to the measured EMF in the microcontroller (Law of Intermediate Temperatures).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Thermoelectric Effects and Their Interrelation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EF1["Seebeck Effect 1821"]
        SE1["Temperature Difference"] --> SE2["Net EMF at Open Circuit"]
    end
    subgraph EF2["Peltier Effect 1834"]
        PE1["Current Through Junction"] --> PE2["Heat Absorbed or Released"]
    end
    subgraph EF3["Thomson Effect 1851"]
        TE1["Current + Temperature Gradient"] --> TE2["Heat Evolution Along Wire"]
    end
    SE2 --> Q["Thermocouple Temperature Sensor"]
    PE2 --> R["Thermoelectric Cooler Heater"]
    TE2 --> S["Calibration Correction"]
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Interrelation of the three thermoelectric effects.** Note that the Seebeck effect (used in thermocouples) is the *converse* of the Peltier effect (used in solid-state refrigeration of dairy/agri-storage).

---

### 4.3 Construction Detail: Thermocouple Probe (Agricultural Soil Sensor)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Sheath Stainless Steel 316L"] --> L2["MgO Insulation"]
    L2 --> L3["Chromel Wire Positive Leg"]
    L2 --> L4["Alumel Wire Negative Leg"]
    L3 --> L5["Welded Hot Junction Bead"]
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6["Hot Junction at Tip T_H"]
    L3 --> L7["Cold End at Connector T_C"]
    L4 --> L7
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Internal construction of a sheathed Type K soil-probe thermocouple.** Magnesium Oxide (MgO) provides electrical insulation and high thermal conductivity for fast response.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC) Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Read Voltmeter E_meas mV"] --> B["Read T_C from Diode or RTD"]
    B --> C["Lookup E_cold f T_C from Table"]
    C --> D["Compute E_total E_meas + E_cold"]
    D --> E["Lookup T_H f inverse E_total"]
    E --> F["Display T_H with Calibration Offset"]
    F --> G["Apply Linearisation Polynomial"]
    G --> H["Output Final Corrected Temperature"]
```

> **Figure 4.4 — Cold-Junction Compensation algorithmic flow** (per the Law of Intermediate Temperatures, Section 2.4).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

#### **Q1. Define the Seebeck Effect and explain how it forms the basis of a thermocouple.** `[3 Marks]` `[CO1, L2]`

**Model Answer:**
The Seebeck Effect is the direct conversion of a temperature difference between two junctions of dissimilar conductors into a measurable electromotive force (EMF). Discovered by T. J. Seebeck in 1821, it states that when two dissimilar metals A and B are joined at one end (hot junction at $T_H$) and open at the other (cold junction at $T_C$), a net EMF $E_{AB}$ is produced that is a function of $(T_H - T_C)$ and the material pair. This forms the operating principle of a thermocouple, which uses a pre-calibrated EMF-vs-temperature reference table (ITS-90) to determine the unknown $T_H$. **[3 Marks]**

---

#### **Q2. State and explain the Law of Intermediate Metals with a suitable diagram.** `[3 Marks]` `[CO1, L2]`

**Model Answer:**
The Law of Intermediate Metals states that the introduction of a third metal **C** into a thermocouple circuit does not alter the net EMF, **provided the two new junctions formed with C are maintained at the same temperature**. 

*Justification:* If metal C is inserted and both its ends are at temperature $T_{int}$, the EMFs of the two new junctions are equal and opposite: $E_{AC}(T_{int}) + E_{CB}(T_{int}) = 0$. Therefore, the net circuit EMF is unchanged.

*Application:* This law permits the use of copper lead wires, solder joints, terminal blocks, and the voltmeter in the measurement loop without introducing any error — a critical property exploited in practical thermocouple instrumentation. **[3 Marks]**

---

### PART B — ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **ONE FULL QUESTION** from the choice. Each question has sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**.

---

#### **QUESTION A** `[14 Marks Total]`

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a thermocouple. Discuss any **two** agricultural applications in detail. `[7 Marks]` `[CO1, CO2, L2-L3]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar thermoelectric conductors — say Chromel (Ni-Cr) as the positive leg and Alumel (Ni-Al) as the negative leg — joined at one end by welding, brazing, or twisting to form the **measuring (hot) junction**. The other two ends terminate at the **reference (cold) junction**, typically a high-impedance voltmeter or an isothermal terminal block. For agricultural deployment, the entire assembly is housed in a sealed **stainless-steel 316L sheath** packed with **magnesium oxide (MgO)** insulation, which provides both electrical isolation and rapid thermal response. Lead wires (extension cables) carry the millivolt signal to the indicator.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When the hot junction is exposed to a process temperature $T_H$ while the cold junction is held at a known reference $T_C$, a **thermoelectric EMF** $E_{AB}$ is generated, given by $E_{AB} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T)\,dT$. This EMF is read by a high-impedance voltmeter, converted to temperature using the ITS-90 reference table, and displayed.

**Agricultural Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Soil-Temperature Profiling for Precision Agriculture:** Multi-point Type K or Type T thermocouples are inserted at depths of 5, 10, 20, 50 cm in the root zone. The vertical temperature profile guides decisions on **sowing time, irrigation scheduling, and frost-risk alerting** to farmers. Cheap ruggedness of Type K is essential for field deployment.
2. **Grain-Silo Hot-Spot Detection:** During storage, grain can develop internal hot spots due to microbial respiration, leading to spontaneous combustion. An array of Type K thermocouples on a vertical cable inside the silo continuously monitors temperature. A sudden rise (e.g., from 25 °C to 45 °C in 24 h) triggers an aeration fan and alerts the operator.

**(b)** Explain the **three fundamental laws of thermocouples** and discuss their **practical significance** in instrumentation. `[7 Marks]` `[CO1, L3]`

**Model Answer:**

**Law 1 – Law of Intermediate Metals (2 marks):**
The insertion of a third metal C into the thermocouple circuit does not alter the net EMF, provided both new junctions formed with C are at the same temperature.
*Significance:* Justifies the use of copper lead wires, solder joints, terminal blocks, and the voltmeter terminals in the measuring loop without introducing any parasitic EMF. This is what makes practical thermocouple instrumentation possible.

**Law 2 – Law of Intermediate Temperatures (2 marks):**
If a thermocouple has junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ producing EMF $E_{13}$, then $E_{13} = E_{12} + E_{23}$ where $E_{12}$ and $E_{23}$ are the EMFs at intermediate temperature $T_2$.
*Significance:* Forms the *theoretical foundation of cold-junction compensation (CJC)*. The instrument need not maintain the cold junction at 0 °C; instead, the EMF corresponding to the actual cold-junction temperature (measured by a separate sensor) is *algebraically added* in software, dramatically simplifying instrument design.

**Law 3 – Law of Inserted Material (Uniform Conductor) (1.5 marks):**
If a uniform conductor of material C is inserted in the circuit with both its ends at the same temperature, the net EMF is unchanged.
*Significance:* Permits the use of **extension and compensating cables** made of cheaper metals (with matched thermoelectric properties over a limited range) to extend the thermocouple signal from the field probe to the remotely located instrument.

**Common Practical Significance (1.5 marks):**
Together, these three laws guarantee that the thermocouple EMF depends *only* on the materials of the two thermoelements and the two junction temperatures — and not on the intermediate conductor geometry, wire routing, or solder materials. This is the reason thermocouples are the most *interchangeable*, *calibratable*, and *standardised* temperature sensor in the world.

---

#### **QUESTION B** `[14 Marks Total]`

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is **cold-junction compensation (CJC)**? Derive the mathematical relation used for software CJC and explain one practical implementation using a silicon diode. `[7 Marks]` `[CO2, CO3, L3-L4]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Cold-junction compensation is the technique of mathematically correcting the thermocouple EMF for the fact that the *reference* (cold) junction in a real instrument is at ambient temperature $T_C$, not at 0 °C. Without CJC, every 1 °C shift in ambient would appear as a 1 °C error in the indicated hot-junction temperature.

**Mathematical Derivation (3 marks):**
From the Law of Intermediate Temperatures:
$$E_{AB}(0, T_H) = E_{AB}(0, T_C) + E_{AB}(T_C, T_H)$$
Rearranging:
$$E_{AB}(T_C, T_H) = E_{AB}(0, T_H) - E_{AB}(0, T_C)$$
The voltmeter measures $E_{AB}(T_C, T_H) = E_{\text{meas}}$. A separate sensor measures $T_C$, from which the lookup $E_{AB}(0, T_C) = E_{\text{cold}}$ is obtained. The corrected total EMF is:
$$E_{\text{corrected}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{cold}}$$
The hot-junction temperature is then found by inverse lookup $T_H = f^{-1}(E_{\text{corrected}})$ from the ITS-90 reference table.

**Silicon-Diode CJC Implementation (2 marks):**
A silicon diode exhibits a temperature coefficient of approximately $-2 \,\text{mV}/^\circ\text{C}$ (the forward voltage drop $V_F$ at a constant bias current decreases linearly with temperature). The diode is mounted on the isothermal terminal block where the thermocouple wires terminate. A precision current source biases the diode, and the resulting $V_F$ is measured by an ADC. The microcontroller converts $V_F$ to $T_C$ using a polynomial fit, then looks up $E_{\text{cold}}$ from the thermocouple reference table, and finally computes the corrected $T_H$.

**(b)** A **Type K** thermocouple is used to measure the temperature inside a **grain silo**. The voltmeter reads **$E_{\text{meas}} = 12.209 \,\text{mV}$** and the cold-junction sensor reports **$T_C = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$**. From the Type K reference table, the cold-junction EMF is **$E_{\text{cold}} = 1.203 \,\text{mV}$**. Determine the **corrected hot-junction temperature** $T_H$. State two precautions to be taken while installing thermocouples in agricultural silos. `[7 Marks]` `[CO3, L4]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the CJC equation (3 marks):**
$$E_{\text{corrected}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{cold}}$$
$$E_{\text{corrected}} = 12.209 + 1.203 = 13.412 \,\text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Inverse lookup from Type K table (3 marks):**
From the standard Type K reference table (ITS-90):

| $E$ (mV) | $T$ (°C) |
|---|---|
| 13.351 | 330 |
| 13.412 | **≈ 331.5** |
| 13.456 | 332 |

Interpolating linearly between 13.351 mV (330 °C) and 13.456 mV (332 °C):
$$T_H = 330 + (332 - 330) \times \frac{13.412 - 13.351}{13.456 - 13.351}$$
$$T_H = 330 + 2 \times \frac{0.061}{0.105} = 330 + 2 \times 0.581 \approx 331.16\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{T_H \approx 331.2\,^\circ\text{C}}$$

**Installation Precautions in Agricultural Silos (1 mark each, total 2 marks):**
1. **Use a protective stainless-steel sheath** with MgO insulation to prevent mechanical damage during grain loading/unloading and to shield the junction from corrosive grain dust and moisture.
2. **Use proper extension/compensating cables** (Type KX for Type K thermocouples) to bring the EMF to the readout, ensuring the cables are routed away from power cables to avoid electromagnetic interference.
3. **Ensure good thermal contact** between the probe tip and the grain by inserting the probe at a slight angle to avoid air gaps, and provide adequate immersion depth (at least 10× the sheath diameter) to avoid thermal shunting errors.
4. **Use an isothermal terminal block** for the cold junction, thermally bonded to the cold-junction compensation sensor, and shielded from draughts, direct sunlight, and self-heating of nearby electronics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the CJC term** in numerical problems — students often write $T_H = f^{-1}(E_{\text{meas}})$ directly, ignoring the cold-junction correction. This costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the Seebeck coefficient** with the Peltier coefficient. The Seebeck coefficient has units μV/°C, while the Peltier coefficient has units volts. Mixing these up signals a fundamental misunderstanding.
> 3. **Not specifying units** (mV vs μV) in answers. Always state units.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark construction questions. A neat block diagram or labelled sketch is **mandatory** and worth at least 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Naming thermocouples by their chemical composition** (e.g., "nickel-chromium nickel-aluminium") instead of the **ISA letter designation (Type K)**. Examiners expect the letter codes.
> 6. **Not citing the Law of Intermediate Temperatures** when explaining CJC — examiners specifically look for this foundational law.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Facts for KTU PEICT753 — Thermocouples**

- [x] **Definition:** A thermocouple = two dissimilar metals joined at one end, generating EMF proportional to $\Delta T$ between the two junctions (**Seebeck Effect, 1821**).
- [x] **Reference temperature:** Cold junction standardised at **0 °C = 273.15 K** (ice-point).
- [x] **Fundamental equation:** $E_{AB} = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T)\,dT$.
- [x] **Linear approximation:** $E_{AB} \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_H - T_C)$.
- [x] **Three laws of thermocouples:**
  1. Law of Intermediate Metals → lead wires, solder OK.
  2. Law of Intermediate Temperatures → **foundation of CJC**.
  3. Law of Inserted Material → extension cables OK.
- [x] **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** $E_{\text{corrected}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{cold}}(T_C)$. **Never skip this in KTU problems.**
- [x] **Type K** (Chromel-Alumel) is the **most common general-purpose** thermocouple, used in **soil and silo temperature monitoring** in agriculture.
- [x] **Type T** (Copper-Constantan) is preferred in **wet environments** (dairy, irrigation, food processing).
- [x] **Output range:** typically **0 – 70 mV** for 0 – 1400 °C — requires high-gain amplification.
- [x] **Voltmeter impedance:** must be **≥ 10 MΩ** to avoid Peltier-loading errors.
- [x] **ITS-90 polynomials:** 8th- to 9th-order polynomial gives EMF(T) with sub-0.1 °C accuracy.
- [x] **Seebeck coefficient units:** **μV/°C**; typical magnitude **8 – 80 μV/°C** depending on type.
- [x] **Error sources:** cold-junction error, lead-wire error, decalibration/drift, inhomogeneity, shunt error, thermal shunting.
- [x] **Agricultural use cases:** soil-temperature profiling, grain-silo hot-spot detection, greenhouse thermal mapping, dairy pasteurization temperature control, compost-temperature monitoring, cold-storage of fruits and vegetables.
- [x] **Advantages:** Cheap, rugged, fast response, wide range (–270 to +1820 °C), self-powered, no excitation needed.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Low sensitivity (mV level), cold-junction compensation mandatory, non-linear (requires polynomial), susceptible to corrosion at high T.
- [x] **ISA letter codes** must be used in answers, not the bare chemical names.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Close Path Gas Analysers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Closed-Path Gas Analyser (CPGA)** is a quantitative gas measurement instrument in which the gas sample is physically transported from the sampling point, through a defined internal sensing chamber (cell), to an exhaust point, by means of an active pump or aspirator. The optical, electrical, or chemical transducer inside the cell interacts with the trapped gas volume, producing a signal that is a deterministic function of the concentration of the target analyte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A closed-path analyser is characterised by a **closed measurement loop** — sample enters via an inlet probe, passes through filters/driers/flow controllers, interacts with the sensor, and exits through a pump. This is fundamentally different from open-path (in-situ) analysers where the beam traverses an open atmosphere between transmitter and receiver.

The general functional equation governing the output of a closed-path analyser is:

$$
C = f^{-1}(S_{sensor})
$$

where $C$ is the concentration of the target gas (typically in **ppm**, **% volume**, or **mg/m³**), and $S_{sensor}$ is the transducer output voltage, current, frequency, or pressure change.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lung-and-Stethoscope" Model

Imagine you want to know whether a sealed grain silo has dangerous levels of **CO₂** building up from mould respiration. You cannot simply shine a torch through the silo (that is *open-path*). Instead, you insert a small hose, attach a pump that acts like a pair of lungs, and **inhale** a fixed volume of silo air into a sealed glass chamber (the *cell*). Inside the chamber, an infrared light source acts like a *stethoscope*, listening to how much of its light is absorbed by CO₂ molecules. The more CO₂, the dimmer the light reaching the detector. A calibrated electronic brain converts this dimming into a number on a screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Closed Path"?**
> The gas sample travels inside a sealed tube/cell. This means:
> - No interference from ambient sunlight, dust scattering between source and detector.
> - Sample conditioning (drying, filtering, temperature control) is possible.
> - Calibration using certified gas cylinders is straightforward.
> - Trade-off: Pump failure means no reading; sample transport introduces lag.

## 1.3 Classification of Closed-Path Gas Analysers

| S.No | Analyser Type | Target Gases (Agricultural Relevance) | Detection Principle |
|------|---------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Non-Dispersive Infra-Red (NDIR) | CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, NH₃, H₂O | Selective IR absorption |
| 2 | Photoacoustic Infra-Red (PA-IR) | CO₂, CH₄, NH₃, C₂H₄ | Sound from absorbed IR |
| 3 | Paramagnetic | O₂ | Magnetic susceptibility |
| 4 | Electrochemical (Amperometric) | NH₃, H₂S, O₂, NO₂ | Redox current |
| 5 | Thermal Conductivity (TCD) | H₂, CO₂, CH₄, He | Thermal diffusivity |
| 6 | Flame Ionisation Detector (FID) | Total hydrocarbons, CH₄ | Ion current in flame |
| 7 | Chemiluminescence | NO, NO₂ (ozone precursors) | Light from chemical reaction |
| 8 | Zirconia / Solid Electrolyte | O₂ (high temp) | Nernst EMF |
| 9 | Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) | Multi-gas speciation | Broadband IR interferometry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, the **three most frequently tested** closed-path analysers in agricultural instrumentation are: **NDIR, Paramagnetic O₂, and Electrochemical**. Master these first.

## 1.4 Standard Units & Performance Metrics

- **Concentration Units:** ppm (parts per million by volume), ppb, % vol, mg/m³, % LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)
- **Span:** Full-scale concentration
- **Detection Limit (DL):** 3 × RMS noise — typically **1 ppm** for NDIR CO₂
- **Response Time (T₉₀):** Time to reach 90% of final value — typically **< 30 s** for portable CPGA
- **Cross-sensitivity:** Interference from non-target gases (e.g., water vapour interference in NDIR CO₂)

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Physical Foundation — Beer–Lambert Law

Nearly every optical closed-path analyser (NDIR, PA-IR, FTIR, UV photometric) is governed by the **Beer–Lambert Law**, which mathematically couples gas concentration to light attenuation through the cell.

$$
I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda) \cdot e^{-\varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot l}
$$

where:
- $I_0(\lambda)$ = incident radiation intensity at wavelength $\lambda$
- $I(\lambda)$ = transmitted radiation intensity
- $\varepsilon(\lambda)$ = molar absorption coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
- $c$ = molar concentration of absorbing gas (mol·L⁻¹)
- $l$ = optical path length inside the cell (cm)

In **absorbance** form, this is rewritten as:

$$
A(\lambda) = -\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot l
$$

The instrument outputs **absorbance**, which is *linearly proportional* to concentration when $\varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l \ll 1$ (dilute gas regime typical for agricultural trace-gas monitoring).

> [!NOTE]
> The "**Non-Dispersive**" in NDIR means the instrument does NOT use a prism or grating to spatially separate wavelengths. Instead, it uses a **narrowband optical filter** or a **gas filter wheel** tuned specifically to the absorption line of the target gas (e.g., 4.26 µm for CO₂, 3.31 µm for CH₄, 10.6 µm for NH₃).

## 2.2 NDIR Closed-Path Analyser — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Architecture
A typical NDIR consists of:
1. **IR Source** — black-body emitter (micro-globe with nichrome filament, ~600–800 K)
2. **Chopper / Modulator** — mechanical chopper wheel (typically 10–400 Hz) to produce AC signal (eliminates 1/f noise and thermal drift)
3. **Sample Cell** — long stainless-steel/aluminium tube (10 cm to 50 m in some benchtop instruments; folded using gold mirrors)
4. **Reference Cell** — sealed cell filled with N₂ or non-absorbing gas (provides reference $I_0$)
5. **Optical Filter** — bandpass at target gas absorption wavelength
6. **Pyroelectric / Thermopile Detector** — converts IR to voltage
7. **Electronics** — synchronous demodulation (lock-in), linearisation, temperature compensation

### 2.2.2 Single-Beam vs. Dual-Beam Configuration
- **Single-Beam (Luftron-style):** Detector alternately views sample and reference via a rotating gas-filter wheel.
- **Dual-Beam (Luft/Siemens-style):** Two identical beams, one through sample, one through reference, detected on a single dual-element thermopile. Excellent long-term stability.

### 2.2.3 Why Chopping?
Chopper modulation converts the DC IR signal into an AC signal at $f_{chop}$, allowing the use of **lock-in amplification**. This:
- Removes ambient thermal drift (low-frequency)
- Allows very narrow-band detection
- Achieves **SNR improvements** up to **10⁴**

## 2.3 Paramagnetic Oxygen Analyser

Oxygen is **paramagnetic** — it is drawn into a magnetic field because it has two unpaired electrons. Almost all other common gases are **diamagnetic** and are repelled. This property forms the basis of a highly selective O₂ sensor.

### 2.3.1 Working Principle
A sealed, nitrogen-filled dumbbell (paramagnetic susceptibility of N₂ ≈ 0) is suspended in a strong non-uniform magnetic field. When O₂-containing sample gas flows into the cell, it is pulled toward the strongest field region. The paramagnetic force creates a torque on the dumbbell, displacing it against a quartz fibre torsion. A mirror attached to the dumbbell reflects a light beam onto a differential photodiode, producing a signal proportional to O₂ partial pressure.

The magnetic force per unit volume on the gas:

$$
F_{mag} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_0 \cdot \chi_{O_2} \cdot p_{O_2} \cdot \nabla(H^2)
$$

where:
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space (**4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**)
- $\chi_{O_2}$ = magnetic susceptibility of O₂ (**+1.9 × 10⁻⁶ cgs** at STP)
- $p_{O_2}$ = partial pressure of oxygen
- $H$ = magnetic field strength
- $\nabla(H^2)$ = spatial gradient of squared field

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ringenberg's Null-Balance Design:** Most modern paramagnetic analysers use a feedback coil to restore the dumbbell to null position. The feedback current is the measurement signal. This eliminates non-linearity and zero drift.

## 2.4 Electrochemical Closed-Path Sensor

A three-electrode electrochemical cell:
- **Working electrode** (e.g., Pt, Au) — gas-specific catalyst
- **Counter electrode** — completes the circuit
- **Reference electrode** (e.g., Ag/AgCl) — fixed potential

The gas diffuses through a capillary/membrane and undergoes oxidation/reduction:

$$
\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^- \quad \text{(anodic, working)}
$$

The current produced follows Faraday's law:

$$
i = n \cdot F \cdot \frac{dN}{dt}
$$

where:
- $n$ = electrons transferred per molecule (**6** for NH₃)
- $F$ = Faraday constant (**96485 C/mol**)
- $dN/dt$ = molar flux of gas reacting per second

Output current is **linearly proportional** to gas concentration.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|------------------------|------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | Beer–Lambert exponential attenuation | dimensionless | NDIR, FTIR, PA-IR |
| 2 | $A = \varepsilon c l$ | Linear absorbance (small $A$) | AU | Optical analyser |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{A}{\varepsilon \, l}$ | Concentration from absorbance | mol/L or ppm | All optical CPGAs |
| 4 | $\Delta I / I_0 = 1 - e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | Fractional intensity loss | dimensionless | Sensitivity calculation |
| 5 | $F_{mag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_0 \chi_{O_2} p_{O_2} \nabla H^2$ | Magnetic force on O₂ molecules | N/m³ | Paramagnetic O₂ |
| 6 | $i = n F \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$ | Faraday's law of electrolysis | A | Electrochemical |
| 7 | $\text{SNR} = \sqrt{2 \cdot f_{chop} \cdot \tau}$ | Chopper SNR improvement | dimensionless | Modulation theory |
| 8 | $P = \alpha \, c \, Q$ | Sound pressure in PA-IR | Pa | Photoacoustic |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{O_2,\,cathode}}{p_{O_2,\,anode}}\right)$ | Nernst equation for zirconia | V | High-temp O₂ |
| 10 | $T_{90} = \tau \cdot \ln(10)$ | Response time definition | s | All CPGAs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Latex Tip for Tables:** All absolute-value and "divided-by" notations have been written using `\vert`, `\dfrac`, and `\tfrac` to preserve markdown table integrity. Never use `|` inside a markdown table cell.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Agriculture

Closed-path gas analysers are indispensable in:
- **Greenhouse CO₂ enrichment control** — NDIR CO₂ sensors feedback to dosing valves
- **Soil respiration chambers** — measuring CO₂ flux from agricultural soils (LI-COR 8100A is a closed-path IRGA)
- **Biogas plant monitoring** — CH₄, CO₂, H₂S, O₂ for anaerobic digester control
- **Animal confinement houses** — NH₃, CO₂, CH₄ for welfare and emission accounting
- **Grain silo atmosphere** — O₂ below 1% for hermetic storage (oxygen depletion for pest control)
- **Fumigation chambers** — methyl bromide, phosphine (PH₃) concentration assurance
- **Field flux chambers** — measuring N₂O from fertilised fields (climate change relevance)
- **Fermentation in food processing** — CO₂ in wine, dairy, bread

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sensitivity of an NDIR Analyser

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
An NDIR analyser is designed to monitor **CO₂ in a greenhouse**. The target concentration range is **0–2000 ppm**. The optical path length of the sample cell is **l = 20 cm**. The molar absorption coefficient of CO₂ at the centre of the 4.26 µm band is **ε = 1.5 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹** (approximate). Determine:
(a) The fractional intensity loss at full scale
(b) The minimum detectable concentration change if the detector can resolve a 0.1% change in intensity
(c) Comment on whether doubling the path length improves detection

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert concentration to mol/L.**

At STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa), the molar volume of an ideal gas is **22.414 L/mol**. For trace gases, ppm by volume = ppm by mole ratio:

$$
1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{10^{-6} \text{ mol}}{\text{mol of gas}}
$$

At 1 atm and 25 °C, using $PV = nRT$:

$$
V_m = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{(8.314)(298.15)}{101325} = 0.02447 \text{ m}^3/\text{mol} = 24.47 \text{ L/mol}
$$

Concentration of CO₂ at 2000 ppm:

$$
c = \frac{2000 \times 10^{-6}}{24.47} = 8.173 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absorbance at full scale.**

$$
A = \varepsilon \, c \, l = (1.5)(8.173 \times 10^{-5})(20)
$$

$$
A = 2.452 \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(dimensionless, in natural-log units of } I/I_0\text{)}
$$

Note: When the absorbance is computed using the *natural-log* convention, $A_{e} = \ln(I_0/I)$. If the spectroscopic convention $A_{10} = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ is used, divide by $\ln(10) = 2.303$:

$$
A_{10} = \frac{2.452 \times 10^{-3}}{2.303} = 1.065 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fractional intensity loss at full scale.**

Using the small-argument expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$
\frac{\Delta I}{I_0} = 1 - e^{-\varepsilon c l} \approx \varepsilon c l = 2.452 \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{or} \quad 0.245\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Delta I}{I_0} \approx 0.245\% \text{ at } 2000 \text{ ppm CO}_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum detectable concentration.**

The detector resolves a fractional change of 0.1% = $10^{-3}$.

In the linear regime, $\Delta I / I_0 = \varepsilon \, l \, \Delta c$, so:

$$
\Delta c_{min} = \frac{(\Delta I / I_0)_{min}}{\varepsilon \, l} = \frac{10^{-3}}{(1.5)(20)} = 3.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

Convert back to ppm:

$$
\Delta c_{min}(\text{ppm}) = (3.33 \times 10^{-5})(24.47)(10^6) = 815 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 5 — Doubling the path length to l = 40 cm.**

New fractional loss at full scale: $2 \times 0.245\% = 0.490\%$.
New minimum detectable concentration: $815 / 2 = 407.5$ ppm.

**Conclusion:** Doubling the path length halves the minimum detectable concentration, improving sensitivity by a factor of 2. This is the basis of **White-cell multi-pass designs** (up to 100 m effective path in a compact volume).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Paramagnetic Analyser — Calibration Equation

A paramagnetic analyser is calibrated using two certified zero/scale gases. At zero (N₂ purge), the photodiode differential output is **V₀ = 0.000 V**. At span (20.9% O₂ in N₂), output is **V_s = 8.450 V**. The detector response is non-linear due to the magnetic-susceptibility mixing rule:

$$
\chi_{mix} = \chi_{O_2} \, y_{O_2} + \chi_{N_2}(1 - y_{O_2}) + \chi_{other}\sum y_i
$$

where $y_i$ are mole fractions. Assuming binary O₂/N₂ mixture:

$$
\chi_{mix} = y_{O_2}(\chi_{O_2} - \chi_{N_2}) + \chi_{N_2}
$$

Since the output voltage is proportional to $\chi_{mix} - \chi_{N_2} = y_{O_2}(\chi_{O_2} - \chi_{N_2})$:

$$
V = k \cdot y_{O_2}(\chi_{O_2} - \chi_{N_2})
$$

Determine the constant $k$ and compute the voltage reading for **16.5% O₂**.

### Solution

$$
k = \frac{V_s}{y_{s}(\chi_{O_2} - \chi_{N_2})}
$$

We do not need the explicit value of susceptibility difference because we can solve linearly:

$$
\frac{V}{V_s} = \frac{y_{O_2}}{0.209}
$$

$$
V = V_s \cdot \frac{y_{O_2}}{0.209} = (8.450) \cdot \frac{0.165}{0.209}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{16.5\%\,O_2} = 6.671 \text{ V}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important caveat:** Strictly, the susceptibility relationship is non-linear because magnetic force is also a function of total gas density (which depends on composition). Real instruments apply a **second-order polynomial correction**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrochemical Sensor — Current to ppm

A 3-electrode amperometric NH₃ sensor operates at 0 V bias. The calibration data shows that **5 ppm NH₃** produces a steady-state current of **4.2 µA**. Determine the concentration when the output current is **11.7 µA**, assuming linear response (typical for amperometric sensors operating below saturation).

### Solution

The transfer function of an amperometric sensor is:

$$
i = n F \cdot \Phi_{gas} = n F \cdot \left(\frac{D \cdot A}{\delta}\right) c_{gas}
$$

where $\Phi_{gas}$ is the molar flux through the diffusion barrier, $D$ is the diffusion coefficient, $A$ is electrode area, $\delta$ is diffusion layer thickness. The whole expression $nFA/\delta$ is a lumped sensitivity constant $S_c$:

$$
i = S_c \cdot c_{gas}
$$

Determine sensitivity:

$$
S_c = \frac{i}{c} = \frac{4.2 \,\mu\text{A}}{5 \text{ ppm}} = 0.84 \,\mu\text{A}/\text{ppm}
$$

Solve for unknown concentration:

$$
c = \frac{i}{S_c} = \frac{11.7 \,\mu\text{A}}{0.84 \,\mu\text{A}/\text{ppm}} = 13.93 \text{ ppm}
$$

$$
\boxed{[\text{NH}_3] \approx 13.93 \text{ ppm}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right CPGA

| Parameter | NDIR | Paramagnetic | Electrochemical | TCD | PA-IR |
|-----------|------|--------------|-----------------|-----|-------|
| Target Gases | CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, NH₃ | O₂ only | NH₃, H₂S, O₂ | H₂, He, CO₂ | Multi-gas |
| Selectivity | Excellent (gas filter) | Excellent for O₂ | Good (electrode-specific) | Poor (broad) | Excellent |
| Detection Limit | 1–50 ppm | 0.01% O₂ | 0.1–5 ppm | 100 ppm | 0.1 ppm |
| Response Time | 5–30 s | 5–15 s | 30–120 s | 1–10 s | 5–30 s |
| Drift per year | ±2% FS | ±1% FS | ±5–10% FS | ±1% FS | ±1% FS |
| Cost (relative) | Medium | High | Low | Low | High |
| Suitability for field/agri use | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Cross-sensitivity in agricultural NDIR:**
> Water vapour has strong absorption across the entire mid-IR region and can cause errors of **+30%** in CO₂ readings if not properly compensated. All field-grade NDIR instruments include either a nafion dryer, a Peltier cooler, or a mathematical compensation algorithm. Examiners frequently deduct marks for ignoring this.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Closed-Path Gas Analyser

The following Mermaid block diagram maps every functional module of a CPGA, from sample ingress to display output. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing signal, gas, and control interconnections rather than physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inletA[Sample Probe] --> inletB[Particulate Filter]
    inletB --> inletC[Water Trap and Nafion Dryer]
    inletC --> inletD[Flow Controller and Needle Valve]
    inletD --> inletE[Sample Cell with Sensor]
    inletE --> inletF[Exhaust Pump]
    inletF --> inletG[Exhaust Vent or Return Line]
    sensorA[IR Source] --> sensorB[Chopper Modulator]
    sensorB --> inletE
    inletE --> sensorC[Optical Filter]
    sensorC --> sensorD[Pyroelectric Detector]
    sensorD --> sensorE[Lock in Amplifier]
    sensorE --> sensorF[ADC]
    sensorF --> sensorG[Microcontroller]
    sensorG --> sensorH[Display and 4 to 20 mA Output]
    sensorG --> inletF
    refA[Reference Cell N2 filled] -.-> sensorC
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice the **bidirectional feedback loop** between the Microcontroller and the Exhaust Pump. This is what enables **closed-loop flow control** — the MCU adjusts pump speed (PWM) until a target flow rate (measured by a mass flow meter) is reached, ensuring stable readings regardless of inlet pressure variations in the field.

## 4.2 NDIR Dual-Beam (Siemens/Luft) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    irSrcA[IR Filament Source] --> chopA[Rotating Chopper Disk]
    chopA --> beamSplitA[Beam Splitter Mirror]
    beamSplitA --> sampleCellA[Sample Cell Long Path Tube]
    beamSplitA --> refCellA[Reference Cell Sealed N2]
    sampleCellA --> filterA[Narrowband Optical Filter 4.26 um]
    refCellA --> filterB[Narrowband Optical Filter 4.26 um]
    filterA --> detectorA[Upper Thermopile Element]
    filterB --> detectorB[Lower Thermopile Element]
    detectorA --> diffAmpA[Differential Amplifier]
    detectorB --> diffAmpA
    diffAmpA --> lockinA[Lock in Amplifier at Chop Frequency]
    lockinA --> linearA[Linearization Polynomial]
    linearA --> tcCompA[Pressure and Temperature Compensation]
    tcCompA --> outA[Concentration Readout in ppm]
```

## 4.3 Photoacoustic Closed-Path Analyser (Advanced NDIR Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    paA[IR Source Modulated at 10 to 100 Hz] --> paB[Optical Filter Wheel Target Gas Specific]
    paB --> paC[Sealed Photoacoustic Cell with Microphone]
    paC --> paD[Electret Microphone Detector]
    paD --> paE[Lock in Amplifier at Modulation Frequency]
    paE --> paF[ADC and Microprocessor]
    paF --> paG[Display and Output]
    sampleInA[Sample Gas Inlet] --> paC
    paC --> sampleOutA[Sample Gas Outlet via Pump]
    paF -. feedback control .-> paA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why photoacoustic is more sensitive than conventional NDIR for trace gases:**
> In NDIR, you measure a *small change* in a *large background* ($I_0 \to I$, with $I \approx I_0$). In PA-IR, the detector (microphone) measures *zero signal in the absence of absorbing gas* and an absolute signal proportional to absorbed energy. This gives PA-IR a fundamentally superior SNR for trace gas detection (sub-ppb possible for NH₃).

## 4.4 Comparative Operational Flow — How a CPGA Processes a Reading

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Field as Field Sampling Point
    participant Probe as Probe and Filter
    participant Pump as Sampling Pump
    participant Cell as Measurement Cell
    participant Det as Detector
    participant DSP as Signal Processing Unit
    participant Disp as Display Output
    Field->>Probe: Raw gas enters
    Probe->>Probe: Remove particulates and water
    Probe->>Pump: Conditioned sample
    Pump->>Cell: Forced flow at Q L/min
    Cell->>Det: Sensor interacts with sample
    Det->>DSP: Electrical signal
    DSP->>DSP: Amplify, demodulate, linearize, compensate
    DSP->>Disp: Convert to ppm or percent
    Disp-->>Field: Updated reading every cycle
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Define a closed-path gas analyser. How does it differ from an open-path analyser in terms of sample handling?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **closed-path gas analyser** is an instrument in which the gas sample is actively drawn through an internal measurement cell by a pump, where it interacts with a transducer (optical, electrochemical, magnetic, etc.) to produce a concentration-proportional signal, before being exhausted.

**Key differences from open-path analysers:**

| Aspect | Closed-Path | Open-Path |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Sample | Drawn into cell by pump | Measured in situ across open atmosphere |
| Optical path | Fixed inside cell (10 cm–50 m) | Variable, often tens to hundreds of metres |
| Susceptibility to dust/fog | Low (sample conditioned) | High (beam scattering) |
| Calibration | Using certified gas cylinders | Using retro-reflectors and known gases |
| Lag time | Introduced by transport | Real-time, no lag |
| Typical use | Process & spot monitoring | Fenceline, stack, area monitoring |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"State and explain the Beer–Lambert law as applied to NDIR gas analysers."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Beer–Lambert Law** governs the attenuation of monochromatic radiation as it passes through an absorbing gas:

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\varepsilon \, c \, l}
$$

where $I_0$ is the incident intensity, $I$ is the transmitted intensity, $\varepsilon$ is the molar absorption coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), $c$ is the molar concentration (mol/L), and $l$ is the path length (cm).

In an **NDIR analyser**, the source emits broadband IR; an optical filter selects the specific absorption wavelength of the target gas (e.g., 4.26 µm for CO₂, 3.31 µm for CH₄). The transmitted intensity at this wavelength is measured by a thermopile/pyroelectric detector. The ratio $I/I_0$ (or its logarithm) is processed to yield gas concentration.

The law is **valid only in the dilute regime** (small $\varepsilon c l$), which fortunately applies to most agricultural trace gases (sub-ppm to low %).

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for variable meaning, 1 for NDIR-specific application)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Non-Dispersive Infra-Red (NDIR) analyser** for the measurement of CO₂ in a greenhouse environment. Explain why chopping is used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A greenhouse NDIR CO₂ analyser uses a sample cell of length **15 cm**. The CO₂ molar absorption coefficient at 4.26 µm is **1.2 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**. At standard temperature and pressure, compute the fractional intensity loss for **1500 ppm CO₂**. If the detector can resolve a fractional intensity change of **0.05%**, determine the minimum detectable concentration in ppm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
[IR Source] → [Chopper] → [Sample Cell] → [Filter 4.26 µm] → [Detector] → [Amplifier] → [Display]
                                  ↕ (parallel reference path for dual-beam)
                              [Reference Cell N₂]
```

**Working — step-by-step (3 marks):**

1. A heated filament (nichrome at 700 K) emits broadband mid-IR radiation.
2. A motor-driven **chopper wheel** modulates the IR beam at a fixed frequency (e.g., 50 Hz), converting the DC optical signal to AC.
3. The chopped beam passes through the **sample cell** containing the gas drawn from the greenhouse.
4. CO₂ molecules absorb IR strongly at 4.26 µm (asymmetric stretch mode $\nu_3$).
5. A narrowband **optical filter** centred at 4.26 µm ensures only the relevant wavelengths reach the **thermopile detector**.
6. The detector voltage is proportional to transmitted intensity $I$.
7. In a **dual-beam** instrument, a parallel reference beam through N₂ provides $I_0$. The differential ratio yields absorbance.
8. A **lock-in amplifier** demodulates the chopped signal, eliminating ambient thermal drift.
9. The microcontroller applies the Beer–Lambert inverse, temperature/pressure compensation, and linearisation to display CO₂ in ppm.

**Why chopping? (1 mark):**

Chopping converts the signal to AC at a known frequency. This:
- Allows lock-in (phase-sensitive) detection, suppressing 1/f noise and ambient IR.
- Improves SNR by ~100× compared to unmodulated DC measurement.
- Prevents detector saturation by DC thermal emission.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert 1500 ppm to mol/L at STP:**

Using $V_m = 24.47$ L/mol at 25 °C, 1 atm:

$$
c = \frac{1500 \times 10^{-6}}{24.47} = 6.13 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute absorbance:**

$$
\varepsilon c l = (1.2)(6.13 \times 10^{-5})(15) = 1.103 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Fractional intensity loss:**

Using small-argument approximation:

$$
\frac{\Delta I}{I_0} \approx \varepsilon c l = 1.103 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1103\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Delta I}{I_0} \approx 0.11\% \text{ at } 1500 \text{ ppm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum detectable concentration:**

The detector resolves $10^{-3} \cdot 0.05\% = ?$. Wait — re-reading: detector resolves a **fractional intensity change of 0.05%** = $5 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$
\Delta c_{min} = \frac{(\Delta I / I_0)_{min}}{\varepsilon \, l} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{(1.2)(15)} = 2.78 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

Convert to ppm:

$$
\Delta c_{min}(\text{ppm}) = (2.78 \times 10^{-5})(24.47)(10^6) = 679.7 \text{ ppm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta c_{min} \approx 680 \text{ ppm}}
$$

**Conclusion:** This NDIR cannot resolve CO₂ changes below ~680 ppm with the given cell and detector resolution. To improve, increase $l$ (e.g., to 1 m → ~100 ppm resolution) or use a more sensitive detector (e.g., MCT photovoltaic).

*(7 marks: 1 mark for unit conversion, 2 marks for absorbance, 2 marks for min. detection, 2 marks for result + interpretation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]

**(a)** Describe the **paramagnetic principle** for oxygen measurement. Explain how the dumbbell-type analyser works and why O₂ alone is selectively measured among common gases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **zirconia-based oxygen sensor** is used in a biomass combustion chamber. The cell temperature is **T = 850 K**. The reference electrode is exposed to **ambient air (20.9% O₂, p_ref = 21.2 kPa)**. Using the **Nernst equation**, compute the cell EMF when the flue gas has **3.5% O₂** at a total pressure of **101.3 kPa**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Paramagnetic O₂ Analyser (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Oxygen is uniquely paramagnetic among common gases due to its two unpaired electrons in the $\pi^*$ antibonding orbital. The volume magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ of O₂ at STP is **+1.9 × 10⁻⁶ cgs**, whereas N₂, CO₂, CH₄, H₂O, and Ar are diamagnetic with $\chi \approx -0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ to $-1 \times 10^{-6}$ cgs. This enormous contrast enables highly selective O₂ detection.

**Dumbbell-type construction & working (4 marks):**

A nitrogen-filled, lightweight dumbbell (two small glass spheres) is suspended by a thin quartz fibre inside a non-uniform magnetic field produced by permanent magnets with shaped pole pieces. A small mirror is attached to the suspension. A light source illuminates the mirror, and the reflected beam is detected by a twin photodiode.

When O₂-containing gas enters the cell:
- O₂ molecules are attracted toward the strongest field region.
- This creates a torque on the dumbbell, displacing it from null.
- The mirror rotation shifts the reflected beam off the photodiode centre.
- The differential photodiode signal is proportional to O₂ concentration.
- A feedback coil (Ringenberg null-balance) applies a counter-torque, and the **feedback current** becomes the measurement signal.

**Why O₂ is selective (1 mark):**
No other common gas has comparable paramagnetic susceptibility. The selectivity is so high that cross-sensitivity from other gases is typically < 0.1% of reading — an order of magnitude better than electrochemical O₂ sensors.

**(b) Zirconia Nernst Equation (7 marks):**

**Setup (2 marks):**
The Nernst equation for a zirconia (ZrO₂-Y₂O₃) solid-electrolyte cell is:

$$
E = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{O_2,\,ref}}{p_{O_2,\,meas}}\right)
$$

For O₂, $n = 4$ electrons per O₂ molecule.

**Step 1 — Compute partial pressures (2 marks):**

$$
p_{O_2,\,ref} = 0.209 \times 21.2 = 4.431 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
p_{O_2,\,meas} = 0.035 \times 101.3 = 3.546 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Nernst equation (2 marks):**

$$
E = \frac{(8.314)(850)}{(4)(96485)} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4.431}{3.546}\right)
$$

$$
E = \frac{7066.9}{385940} \cdot \ln(1.2496)
$$

$$
E = 0.01831 \cdot 0.2227 = 4.078 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 4.08 \text{ mV}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
This small EMF is typical for zirconia sensors. The output is highly non-linear and very temperature-dependent — hence the **on-cell heater and thermistor** in commercial probes. Note that the EMF goes to zero when $p_{O_2,\,ref} = p_{O_2,\,meas}$ (lambda = 1, stoichiometric combustion).

*(7 marks: 2 for principle, 4 for working, 1 for selectivity — distributed as appropriate)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Failing to convert ppm → mol/L using the correct molar volume (24.47 L at 25 °C, not 22.4 L at STP) → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting factor of ln(10):** Some textbooks use $A_{10} = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$; others use natural log. Always state the convention. → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring water vapour cross-sensitivity in NDIR:** Examiners frequently expect students to mention nafion dryer / mathematical compensation. → loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong electron count in Nernst equation:** Using $n = 2$ instead of $n = 4$ for O₂. The half-reaction is $\text{O}_2 + 4e^- \to 2\text{O}^{2-}$ — **4 electrons per O₂**. → loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing sensitivity with resolution:** Sensitivity = slope $dV/dc$; resolution = smallest distinguishable change. They are related but not identical. → loses 1 mark.
> 6. **No mention of pump/flow in CPGA description:** A CPGA without an active sampling pump is essentially an open-path instrument. Always specify the flow rate (typically 0.5–2 L/min for portable CPGA).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Closed Path Gas Analysers**

- [x] **CPGA Definition:** Sample is actively drawn through an internal measurement cell by a pump.
- [x] **Open vs Closed Path:** Closed = conditioned sample + calibration; Open = in-situ, no lag.
- [x] **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ — governs all optical CPGAs.
- [x] **NDIR Key Wavelengths:** CO₂ → 4.26 µm; CH₄ → 3.31 µm; NH₃ → 10.6 µm; N₂O → 4.50 µm; CO → 4.60 µm.
- [x] **NDIR Architecture:** IR source → chopper → sample cell → narrowband filter → thermopile → lock-in amp.
- [x] **Why chopping:** AC modulation → lock-in detection → 1/f noise rejection → SNR ×100.
- [x] **Paramagnetic O₂:** O₂ is uniquely paramagnetic ($\chi = +1.9 \times 10^{-6}$ cgs); dumbbell + null-balance design; selectivity < 0.1%.
- [x] **Electrochemical Sensor:** Three electrodes (working, counter, reference); current $i = n F \Phi_{gas}$; Faraday constant **96485 C/mol**.
- [x] **Nernst Equation (Zirconia):** $E = (RT/nF)\ln(p_{ref}/p_{meas})$, with **n = 4** for O₂.
- [x] **Cross-sensitivity:** Water vapour is the #1 interferent in field NDIR; use nafion dryer or Peltier cooler.
- [x] **Agricultural Applications:** Greenhouse CO₂ enrichment, soil respiration, biogas, animal-house NH₃, grain silo O₂, fumigation, N₂O flux from soils.
- [x] **Key Numerical Constants:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $F = 96485$ C/mol; $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m; $V_m(25°C) = 24.47$ L/mol.
- [x] **Performance Metrics:** DL, T₉₀, drift/yr, cross-sensitivity — all appear in KTU problem statements.
- [x] **Trade-offs:** NDIR is selective but needs optical filter per gas; paramagnetic is excellent for O₂ only; electrochemical is cheap but drifts; PA-IR is most sensitive but expensive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Various Biosensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Various Biosensors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A **Biosensor** is an integrated analytical device consisting of a **biological recognition element** (bioreceptor) in intimate contact with a **physico-chemical transducer**, which together produce a measurable signal (electrical, optical, thermal, or mass-based) proportional to the concentration of a target **analyte**.
