topics.title: Flow Diagram of Sugar Plant, Sensors and Instrumentation Set Up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Diagram of Sugar Plant, Sensors and Instrumentation Set Up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sugar Plant Process Flow Diagram (PFD):** A comprehensive schematic representation of the unit operations, material streams (solid, liquid, vapour), and energy flows that govern the conversion of **sugarcane** (*Saccharum officinarum*) into refined crystalline sugar, integrating the mass-energy balance across milling, clarification, evaporation, crystallization, and centrifugal separation stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sugar Plant Instrumentation Set-Up:** A distributed measurement and control architecture employing primary sensors, secondary transmitters, final control elements (valves, VFDs), and a DCS/PLC network to monitor and regulate critical process variables — **flow, level, temperature, pressure, pH, conductivity, density, Brix, turbidity, and vibration** — across each unit operation of the sugar refinery.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Recipe" Intuition

Imagine you are making **rock candy at home** in a kitchen:

| Kitchen Analogy | Sugar Plant Equivalent |
|-----------------|------------------------|
| Crushing sugarcane with a rolling pin | **Milling / Diffusion** stage |
| Straining muddy juice through cloth | **Clarification** with lime & CO₂ |
| Boiling water in a saucepan to thicken juice | **Multi-Effect Evaporation** |
| Adding a seed crystal to thick syrup | **Vacuum Pan Crystallization** |
| Spinning wet clothes in a washing machine | **Centrifugal Separation** |
| Air-drying wet clothes on a line | **Rotary / Fluidized Bed Drying** |

In both cases, the *goal* is the same — **separate pure sugar crystals from water and impurities**. The sugar plant simply performs this at industrial scale, with hundreds of sensors ensuring every gram of juice is measured, every degree of temperature is controlled, and every crystal is formed uniformly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Metric (KTU High-Yield):** A modern Indian sugar plant processes **~2500–5000 Tonnes of Cane per Day (TCD)** with a typical recovery of **~10.5 %** sugar by cane weight, requiring instrumentation accuracy of **±0.05 °Brix** in the final pan stage.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical & Process Constants

- **Sucrose Molecular Weight:** **342.30 g/mol**
- **Density of Bagasse (loose):** **80–120 kg/m³**
- **Mixed Juice Brix (incoming):** **~14–18 °Bx**
- **Syrup Brix (post-evaporation):** **~55–65 °Bx**
- **Massecuite Brix (in pan):** **~88–92 °Bx**
- **Standard Clarification pH:** **6.8–7.2**
- **Pan Boiling Vacuum:** **−0.85 bar (gauge) ≈ 85 kPa absolute**
- **Steam Pressure (1st effect):** **~2.5 bar (abs)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sequential Unit Operations — Structured Logic

The sugar manufacturing process is broken into the following tightly-coupled stages. Each stage has its own **instrumentation cluster**.

#### **Stage 1 — Cane Reception & Preparation**
- **Operation:** Cane is unloaded, weighed (weighbridge with **load cells**), and chopped in **cane knives / shredders** (driven by high-torque motors monitored by **current transformers** and **VFDs**).
- **Why Instrumentation?** To ensure uniform feed size, which directly affects juice extraction efficiency.

#### **Stage 2 — Juice Extraction (Milling / Diffusion)**
- **Operation:** A tandem of **3–5 roller mills** squeezes juice from shredded cane, OR a **diffuser** leaches sucrose with hot water (**70–75 °C**).
- **Critical Variables:**
  - **Mill Roll Pressure:** 200–350 bar (hydraulic load cells)
  - **Imbibition Water Flow:** 18–25 % on cane weight
  - **Mixed Juice Temperature:** 55–65 °C
  - **Mill Speed:** 5–8 rpm (tachogenerator / encoder)

#### **Stage 3 — Juice Heating & Clarification (Juice Purification)**
- **Operation:** Mixed juice is heated to **~100–105 °C** in tubular heaters using **vapour from the 1st evaporator effect**, then dosed with **milk of lime [Ca(OH)₂]** and **sulphur dioxide (SO₂)** to precipitate impurities as **calcium phosphate / sulphite mud**.
- **Critical Variables:**
  - **pH** (controlled to **6.8–7.2** via SO₂ injection)
  - **Temperature** (RTD Pt-100 in heater tubes)
  - **Level in clarifier** (differential pressure / ultrasonic)
  - **Turbidity** of clarified juice (nephelometric sensor)

#### **Stage 4 — Evaporation (Multi-Effect Evaporators — MEE)**
- **Operation:** Clarified juice (~14 °Bx) is concentrated to **syrup (~60 °Bx)** using a **Quadruple / Quintuple Effect Evaporator**. Vapour from the 1st effect boils the 2nd, and so on, achieving **thermal economy of ~2.5–3.5 kg water/kg steam**.
- **Critical Variables:**
  - **Vacuum** in last effect (~−0.85 bar gauge) — measured by **vacuum transmitter / capacitance manometer**
  - **Vapour body temperature** (RTD in each effect)
  - **Syrup Brix** (density meter / refractometer — **most critical**)
  - **Level** in each calandria (DP transmitter)

#### **Stage 5 — Pan Boiling (Crystallization)**
- **Operation:** Syrup + seed crystals are boiled under **vacuum** in **batch pans (A, B, C)** to grow crystals of progressively smaller size.
  - **A-pan (Massecuite):** Largest crystals for finished sugar
  - **B-pan & C-pan:** Recover residual sucrose from molasses
- **Critical Variables:**
  - **Vacuum** (capacitance manometer, **±2 mmHg**)
  - **Temperature** (T = **75–85 °C** at full vacuum)
  - **Conductivity / Brix** (in-line conductivity probe for supersaturation control)
  - **Steam flow** to calandria (orifice / vortex meter)
  - **Pan Level** (radar / DP)

#### **Stage 6 — Centrifugation**
- **Operation:** Massecuite is spun in **batch / continuous basket centrifuges** at **1000–1500 rpm** to separate crystals from mother liquor (molasses).
- **Critical Variables:**
  - **Vibration** (accelerometer / proximity probe) — **safety-critical**
  - **Speed** (tachometer, encoder)
  - **Wash water flow** (mag flow meter)
  - **Cane sugar loss in molasses (target < 1.2 %)** — derived variable from conductivity

#### **Stage 7 — Drying, Cooling & Bagging**
- **Operation:** Wet sugar (moisture ~1 %) is dried in **rotary / fluidized bed dryers** with hot air at **~110 °C**, then cooled to **~35 °C** in a vibratory cooler, and bagged (50 kg / 1 tonne).
- **Critical Variables:**
  - **Inlet air temperature** (thermocouple K-type)
  - **Outlet sugar moisture** (NIR / microwave sensor)
  - **Bulk density** of sugar (load cell on conveyor)

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-tested** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module-2 questions. Memorize the boundary conditions, units, and physical meaning of each.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------------------|--------------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Brix (°Bx)** | $Brix = \dfrac{\text{Mass of dissolved solids}}{\text{Total mass of solution}} \times 100$ | °Bx | Measure of sugar concentration in juice/syrup |
| 2 | **Purity of Juice** | $Purity = \dfrac{\text{Pol \%}}{\text{Brix \%}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates sucrose-to-total-solids ratio |
| 3 | **Recovery % on Cane** | $Recovery = \dfrac{\text{Sugar produced (kg)}}{\text{Cane crushed (kg)}} \times 100$ | % | Plant efficiency KPI |
| 4 | **Boiling Point Elevation (Dühring's Rule)** | $\Delta T_b = k_s \cdot Brix$ where $k_s \approx 0.054 \, ^\circ\text{C}/^\circ\text{Bx}$ | °C | Pan boiling control |
| 5 | **Mass Flow (Coriolis)** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(v_1 - v_2)\,A$ | kg/s | Primary flow measurement |
| 6 | **Density–Brix Correlation (Plato)** | $\rho \, [\text{kg/L}] \approx 1 + 0.00386 \cdot Brix$ | kg/L | In-line density to Brix |
| 7 | **Conductivity vs Brix (Empirical)** | $\kappa = \kappa_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot Brix}$ with $\alpha \approx 0.025$ | µS/cm | Pan supersaturation control |
| 8 | **Weighbridge Load** | $W = \dfrac{V_{out}}{S_{cell}}$ where $S_{cell}$ = sensitivity (mV/V) | tonnes / kg | Cane intake accounting |
| 9 | **Heat Transfer in Evaporator (Q = UA·ΔT)** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | W | Evaporator capacity |
| 10 | **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)** | $SSC = \dfrac{\text{Steam (kg)}}{\text{Water evaporated (kg)}}$ | kg/kg | Thermal efficiency KPI |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table above, the absolute-value bars have been replaced with `\vert` or parentheses to **protect markdown table integrity** — a common student error in exam script preparation.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Audits:** The Brix × Flow transmitter pair enables **real-time mass-balance reconciliation** across the plant boundary — used for ISO 50001 energy certification.
- **DCS / PLC Integration:** All sensor 4–20 mA / HART signals are routed to a **Distributed Control System (e.g., Siemens PCS 7, ABB 800xA, Honeywell Experion)** for closed-loop control.
- **Sugar Industry 4.0:** Modern plants deploy **soft-sensors (virtual Brix)** using ANN models with inputs from conductivity, temperature, and vacuum — achieving **±0.05 °Bx accuracy** without physical refractometers.
- **By-product Valorization:** Instrumentation on bagasse conveyors, boilers, and cogeneration turbines supports **cogeneration of 25–35 MW** power from bagasse.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Brix from Density Using the Plato Correlation

**Problem:** A clarified juice sample has a measured density of **1.055 kg/L** at 20 °C. Find its Brix using the Plato approximation.

**Step 1 — Start with the Plato empirical relation:**

$$
\rho = 1 + 0.00386 \cdot Brix
$$

where density $\rho$ is in **kg/L** and Brix is in **°Bx** (a dimensionless %).

**Step 2 — Substitute the given density $\rho = 1.055 \, \text{kg/L}$:**

$$
1.055 = 1 + 0.00386 \cdot Brix
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the Brix term by subtracting 1 from both sides:**

$$
1.055 - 1 = 0.00386 \cdot Brix
$$

$$
0.055 = 0.00386 \cdot Brix
$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by 0.00386 to obtain Brix:**

$$
Brix = \frac{0.055}{0.00386}
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the numerical division:**

$$
Brix = 14.248 \, ^\circ\text{Bx}
$$

**Step 6 — Round to engineering significance (typical plant resolution = 0.1 °Bx):**

$$
\boxed{Brix \approx 14.2 \, ^\circ\text{Bx}}
$$

This is a realistic value for **clarified mixed juice** entering the evaporator station.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boiling Point Elevation (Dühring's Rule) for Pan Control

**Problem:** The Brix of massecuite in an A-pan has reached **80 °Bx**, and the absolute pressure inside the pan is held at **15 kPa (abs)**. Find the **boiling point of water at this pressure** and the **actual boiling point of the massecuite**.

**Step 1 — Use the steam-table value for saturation temperature at 15 kPa (abs):**

From standard steam tables:

$$
T_{sat}(15 \, \text{kPa}) = 54.0 \, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dühring's rule for boiling point elevation:**

$$
\Delta T_b = k_s \cdot Brix
$$

with $k_s = 0.054 \, ^\circ\text{C} / ^\circ\text{Bx}$ (sucrose-water system at moderate vacuum).

**Step 3 — Substitute $Brix = 80 \, ^\circ\text{Bx}$:**

$$
\Delta T_b = 0.054 \times 80 = 4.32 \, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the elevation to the pure-water saturation temperature:**

$$
T_{boil}^{mc} = T_{sat} + \Delta T_b
$$

$$
T_{boil}^{mc} = 54.0 + 4.32 = 58.32 \, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer with engineering rounding:**

$$
\boxed{T_{boil}^{mc} \approx 58.3 \, ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The pan operator must hold massecuite at this temperature for proper crystal growth. A deviation of **±0.5 °C** indicates false-grain or false-grain risk, triggering a steam-flow cut-off via DCS.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass Balance Across the Evaporator Station

**Problem:** A quintuple-effect evaporator receives clarified juice at **14 °Bx** and delivers syrup at **60 °Bx**. If feed flow = **100,000 kg/h**, find the **syrup mass flow** and the **water evaporated**.

**Step 1 — Apply the solids (sugar) mass balance** (solids are conserved through the evaporator):

$$
\dot{m}_{feed} \cdot Brix_{feed} = \dot{m}_{syrup} \cdot Brix_{syrup}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
100{,}000 \times 14 = \dot{m}_{syrup} \times 60
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for syrup flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{syrup} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 14}{60} = 23{,}333.33 \, \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply overall mass balance to find water evaporated:**

$$
\dot{m}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{feed} - \dot{m}_{syrup}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{evap} = 100{,}000 - 23{,}333.33 = 76{,}666.67 \, \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answers:**

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{syrup} \approx 23{,}333 \, \text{kg/h}, \quad \dot{m}_{evap} \approx 76{,}667 \, \text{kg/h}}
$$

**Engineering note:** This 76.7 t/h evaporation load determines the **boiler steam duty** at a Specific Steam Consumption of **~0.32 kg steam/kg water** → steam demand ≈ **24.5 t/h**, sized for the cogeneration plant.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection vs Process Variable

| Process Variable | Primary Sensor | Range (Plant) | Output | Installation Point | Loop Accuracy |
|------------------|----------------|---------------|--------|--------------------|---------------|
| **Cane weight** | Compression load cell (weighbridge) | 0–60 tonnes | 4–20 mA / HART | Pit-mounted under truck ramp | ±0.1 % FS |
| **Mill roll pressure** | Hydraulic pressure transmitter | 0–400 bar | 4–20 mA | Hydraulic ram of each mill | ±0.5 % FS |
| **Mixed juice flow** | Electromagnetic flow meter (magmeter) | 0–500 m³/h | 4–20 mA | After mill discharge | ±0.5 % |
| **Juice pH** | Glass + reference pH probe | 0–14 pH | 4–20 mA | After sulphur tower | ±0.05 pH |
| **Juice temperature** | RTD Pt-100 (Class A) | 0–200 °C | Pt100 / 4–20 mA | Heater outlet | ±0.3 °C |
| **Syrup Brix** | In-line refractometer / density meter | 0–90 °Bx | 4–20 mA + HART | Last effect discharge | ±0.1 °Bx |
| **Pan vacuum** | Capacitance manometer | 0 to −1 bar | 4–20 mA | Pan vapour space | ±0.2 % FS |
| **Massecuite Brix** | In-line conductivity probe (correlated) | 70–95 °Bx | 4–20 mA | Inside pan | ±0.5 °Bx |
| **Centrifuge vibration** | Piezoelectric accelerometer | 0–50 g RMS | 4–20 mA / IEPE | Bearing housing | ±5 % |
| **Bagasse moisture** | Capacitive / NIR sensor | 0–60 % w.b. | 4–20 mA | Conveyor to boiler | ±0.5 % |
| **Steam flow (calandria)** | Vortex shedding flow meter | 0–50 t/h | 4–20 mA | Steam main to pan | ±1 % |
| **Sugar final moisture** | NIR moisture sensor | 0–2 % w.b. | 4–20 mA | Dryer discharge chute | ±0.05 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sugar Plant — Complete Block-Level Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below represents the **functional architecture** of a complete sugar plant, with **process blocks, sensor clusters, and control signal flow** to the central DCS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cane Reception\nWeighbridge Load Cell] --> B[Cane Preparation\nKnife + Shredder\nMotor Current Sensor]
    B --> C[Milling Tandem 1-4\nMill Roll Pressure Tx\nMag Flow Juice]
    C --> D[Diffuser / 5th Mill\nLevel + Temp Sensors]
    D --> E[Juice Heaters\nRTD Pt-100 + Pressure Tx]
    E --> F[Clarifier Station\npH Probe + Turbidity + Level]
    F --> G[Multi-Effect Evaporator\nVacuum Tx + Density Brix + Level]
    G --> H[Vacuum Pan A B C\nVacuum Tx + Conductivity Brix\n+ Steam Flow + Level]
    H --> I[Centrifuges\nVibration + Speed + Wash Flow]
    I --> J[Dryer and Cooler\nK Type TC + NIR Moisture]
    J --> K[Bagging and Dispatch\nLoad Cell + Metal Detector]

    A -. 4-20 mA HART .- L[Central DCS]
    B -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    C -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    D -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    E -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    F -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    G -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    H -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    I -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    J -. 4-20 mA HART .- L
    K -. 4-20 mA HART .- L

    L --> M[Control Valves + VFDs\nFinal Control Elements]
    L --> N[Operator HMI Stations\nAlarm + Trend Logs]

    C --> O[Bagasse Conveyor\nMoisture NIR + Load Cell]
    O --> P[Boiler + Cogeneration\nDrum Level + Steam Pressure]
    P -- Superheated Steam --> H
    F --> Q[Filter Cake / Mud\nCake Density + Conveyor Speed]
    H --> R[Final Molasses Tank\nLevel + Temperature + Density]
```

---

### 4.2 Stage-Wise Instrumentation Loop Diagram (Pan Boiling Focus)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP1[Vacuum Tx in Pan] --> DCS[DCS Controller]
    SP2[RTD Pt100 in Massecuite] --> DCS
    SP3[Conductivity Probe] --> DCS
    SP4[Pan Level Radar] --> DCS
    SP5[Steam Vortex Flowmeter] --> DCS

    DCS --> CV1[Vacuum Control Valve\nVapour Bleed]
    DCS --> CV2[Steam Control Valve\nTo Calandria]
    DCS --> CV3[Inlet Syrup Valve\nFrom Feed Tank]
    DCS --> CV4[Water Injection Valve\nFor Dilution]

    DCS --> HMI[Operator HMI\nBrix + Vacuum + T Trend]
    DCS --> ALM[Alarm Management\nHigh T / Loss of Vacuum]
```

---

### 4.3 Signal Flow — Sensor-to-DCS Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Field
    S1[Load Cell]:::sensor
    S2[RTD]:::sensor
    S3[pH Probe]:::sensor

    end

    JB1[Junction Box]:::jb

    end

    subgraph Control Room

    HMI[Operator HMI]:::dcs
    end

    S1 --> JB1

    S5 --> JB1

    classDef sensor fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *What is Brix? How is it measured in a sugar plant?*

**Model Answer:**
Brix (°Bx) is a hydrometer-scale measure of the **mass percent of dissolved solids (primarily sucrose) in a sugar solution**, where **1 °Bx ≈ 1 % dissolved solids by mass**. In a sugar plant, Brix is measured by three main methods:
1. **Bench Brix hydrometer / refractometer** (lab, manual, ±0.1 °Bx).
2. **In-line refractometer** (process, optical critical-angle, ±0.05 °Bx) installed in evaporator and pan discharge lines.
3. **Density / Coriolis meter** (process, ±0.1 °Bx) using the Plato correlation $\rho = 1 + 0.00386 \cdot Brix$.

In-line Brix is critical for syrup (target 60 °Bx) and massecuite (80–92 °Bx) control. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for methods.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *List any six sensors used in the pan boiling section of a sugar plant and state the variable measured by each.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Sensor | Variable Measured |
|---|--------|--------------------|
| 1 | Capacitance manometer | **Vacuum** (vapour space pressure) |
| 2 | RTD Pt-100 | **Massecuite temperature** (75–85 °C) |
| 3 | In-line conductivity probe | **Brix / supersaturation** (correlated) |
| 4 | Radar / DP level transmitter | **Pan level** (massecuite height) |
| 5 | Vortex flow meter | **Steam flow** to calandria |
| 6 | Pneumatic / motorised valve positioner | **Steam / bleed valve position** (control) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 — ESE Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **flow diagram of a sugar plant** with all major unit operations from cane reception to sugar bagging. List the **primary sensor and the process variable** measured at each stage.

**Model Answer:**

A complete sugar plant flow diagram was rendered in **Section 4.1** (Mermaid block diagram). The major unit operations and their sensors are tabulated below:

| Stage | Unit Operation | Primary Sensor(s) | Process Variable(s) |
|-------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Cane reception | **Load cell** (weighbridge) | Cane weight (tonnes) |
| 2 | Cane preparation | **Current transformer / encoder** | Motor current, RPM |
| 3 | Milling | **Pressure Tx, mag flow, RTD** | Mill pressure, juice flow, temp |
| 4 | Juice heating | **RTD Pt-100, pressure Tx** | Temperature, steam pressure |
| 5 | Clarification | **pH probe, turbidity, level** | pH 6.8–7.2, NTU, mud level |
| 6 | Evaporation | **Vacuum Tx, density, level** | Vacuum, Brix, calandria level |
| 7 | Pan boiling | **Vacuum, conductivity, RTD, level** | Vacuum, Brix, temp, pan level |
| 8 | Centrifugation | **Accelerometer, tachometer, mag flow** | Vibration, RPM, wash water |
| 9 | Drying | **K-type TC, NIR moisture** | Air temp, sugar moisture |
| 10 | Bagging | **Load cell, metal detector** | Bag weight, ferrous contamination |

*(4 marks for diagram, 3 marks for the table.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle and instrumentation loop of a vacuum pan** used for sugar crystallization. Discuss the role of **conductivity-based Brix measurement** in supersaturation control.

**Model Answer:**

A vacuum pan is a large vertical cylindrical vessel equipped with:
- A **calandria** (steam-heated tubular bundle) at the bottom
- A **vapour space** at the top connected to a **condenser + vacuum pump**
- Mechanical **agitator** with a hollow shaft (for seeding and feed addition)
- A **discharge valve** at the bottom for massecuite drop to the centrifuge

**Instrumentation Loop:**
- **Vacuum transmitter** (capacitance manometer) → 4–20 mA to DCS → DCS modulates **vapour bleed valve** to hold vacuum at **−0.85 bar (g)**.
- **RTD Pt-100** in massecuite → DCS → cuts **steam control valve** when temperature rises sharply (indicating grain formation or "strike").
- **Conductivity probe** (toroidal or contacting type) immersed in massecuite → 4–20 mA → DCS uses the **conductivity–Brix correlation** $\kappa = \kappa_0 e^{-\alpha \cdot Brix}$ to estimate Brix in real time.
- **Radar level transmitter** monitors pan level; when full, DCS initiates the **strike** (grain formation) or **drop** (discharge) sequence.

**Role of Conductivity in Supersaturation Control:**
As syrup concentrates by boiling, water evaporates and Brix rises. Conductivity **decreases exponentially** because the ion mobility drops in viscous, high-sugar media. By maintaining the conductivity in a narrow band (corresponding to the **metastable supersaturation zone**), the operator ensures that:
- Crystals **grow** rather than nucleate new ones (avoiding false grain).
- The pan does **not cross into the labile zone** where uncontrolled nucleation ruins crystal size distribution.

This loop is the **heart of the A/B/C pan control strategy** in any DCS-driven sugar plant. *(4 marks for sketch + loop, 3 marks for supersaturation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **sensor and instrumentation set-up for a multi-effect evaporator station** in a sugar plant. Explain how **Brix is measured in-line** and why it is critical for evaporator control.

**Model Answer:**

The multi-effect evaporator (MEE) station — typically **Quadruple or Quintuple Effect** — concentrates clarified juice from ~14 °Bx to syrup at ~60 °Bx. Each effect consists of:
- A **calandria** (heat exchanger with steam inside tubes)
- A **vapour body** (large cylindrical shell for vapour–liquid separation)
- A **vapour pipe** to the next effect
- A **condenser** (last effect only) and **vacuum pump**

**Sensor & Instrumentation Set-Up:**

| Sensor | Location | Function |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Capacitance vacuum Tx** | Top vapour space of each effect | Hold desired vacuum profile (e.g., 1st effect = 2.5 bar abs, 5th = 15 kPa abs) |
| **RTD Pt-100** | Calandria outlet / vapour space | Measure saturation temperature; compute ΔT for Boiling Point Elevation |
| **DP level Tx** | Calandria shell | Maintain juice level above top tube sheet |
| **Density / Coriolis meter** | Syrup discharge line | Continuous Brix measurement of outlet syrup |
| **Vortex flow meter** | Steam inlet (1st effect) | Steam economy KPI |
| **Mag flow meter** | Feed juice line | Mass balance and capacity check |
| **Conductivity probe** | Last effect recirculation | Backup Brix estimate & scaling detection |

**In-line Brix Measurement (Most Critical):**
Two primary methods are used:
1. **In-line refractometer** (e.g., Anton Paar, Atago) — uses critical-angle optical refraction; output 4–20 mA; range 0–90 °Bx; accuracy ±0.05 °Bx; non-invasive via sapphire window.
2. **Coriolis mass flow + density meter** (e.g., Endress+Hauser Promass) — measures true mass flow and density; applies Plato correlation; accuracy ±0.1 °Bx.

**Why Brix is Critical:**
- It **directly sets the steam economy** — under-concentration (low Brix) means more steam used downstream in pans; over-concentration (high Brix) causes **caramelization** and **colour rise** in finished sugar.
- It governs **pan station capacity** — if syrup Brix drops, the pan must work harder, increasing strike cycle time.
- It is the **plant's principal quality KPI** — any deviation triggers an alarm on the HMI.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **Mermaid block diagram**, explain how a **centrifuge in a sugar plant** is instrumented for safe operation. Why is **vibration monitoring** essential?

**Model Answer:**

A sugar centrifuge is a high-speed rotating basket (1000–1500 rpm for batch, up to 2500 rpm for continuous) used to separate massecuite into **crystal A-sugar** and **molasses**. Instrumentation is mission-critical for both **process control** and **machine safety**.

**Instrumentation Set-Up:**

| Sensor | Variable | Purpose |
|--------|----------|---------|
| **Piezoelectric accelerometer** | Vibration (g RMS) | Detect imbalance, bearing fault, loss of sugar charge |
| **Proximity probe / eddy current** | Shaft displacement | Rotor orbit, eccentricity |
| **Tachometer / encoder** | Speed (RPM) | Cycle control, overspeed trip |
| **Mag flow meter** | Wash water flow | Wash profile control |
| **Conductivity probe** | Molasses conductivity | Brix of mother liquor → sugar loss KPI |
| **Load cell** (some designs) | Basket weight | Mass-based cycle termination |
| **RTD** | Bearing temperature | Thermal protection of bearings |
| **Pressure Tx** | Hydraulic brake pressure | Braking force verification |

**Vibration Monitoring — Why Essential:**

1. **Imbalance Detection:** A centrifuging sugar charge can shift if the massecuite feed is non-uniform → vibration rises sharply at running speed → potential catastrophic failure of the basket.
2. **Bearing Health:** Centrifuge bearings operate at high radial load; **early vibration signature analysis** (1×, 2×, 3× running frequency) can predict failure weeks in advance.
3. **Loss-of-Charge Detection:** If the sugar cake breaks off during spin, an instant vibration transient can damage the basket — vibration trip at **> 5 g RMS** triggers emergency stop.
4. **Safety Integrity Level (SIL-1):** Vibration trip is part of the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** — independent of the DCS — and trips the drive motor + applies the brake within 200 ms.
5. **Compliance:** Sugar centrifuges are covered under **ISO 10816-3** for vibration severity on rigid foundations.

**Suggested block diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACC[Vibration Accelerometer] --> AMPL[Charge Amplifier / IEPE] --> SIG[Signal Conditioner]
    SIG --> DCS[DCS Monitoring and Trends]
    SIG --> SIS[SIS Trip Logic SIL1]
    SIS --> BRK[Hydraulic Brake + Motor Stop]
    TACH[Tachometer] --> DCS
    WASH[Mag Flow Wash Water] --> DCS
    TEMP[Bearing RTD] --> DCS
    DCS --> HMI[Operator HMI and Alarms]
```

*(4 marks for diagram, 3 marks for vibration rationale.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Sugar Plant Questions (KTU Board Examiner's Notes):**
> 1. **Confusing Brix with Pol %:** Brix = total dissolved solids, Pol % = sucrose only (measured by polarimeter). Purity = (Pol % / Brix %) × 100. Examiners **strictly deduct** 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Wrong vacuum unit:** Always specify **gauge (barg) or absolute (bara)**; do not write "vacuum = 0.85 bar" without sign convention.
> 3. **Bypassing material balance:** Any numerical question on syrup flow / water evaporated **must begin with a clear mass balance equation**; jumping to a numeric answer costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting transmitter output:** Sensor-only answers (no 4–20 mA / HART mention) are penalized — **the loop is the answer**, not the sensor alone.
> 5. **No safety loop for centrifuge:** Vibration trip is part of the **SIS** (Safety Instrumented System), independent of DCS — many students wrongly tie it to the DCS only.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Sugar plant has 10 unit operations** — Reception → Preparation → Milling → Heating → Clarification → Evaporation → Pan Boiling → Centrifugation → Drying → Bagging.
- [ ] **Brix (°Bx)** = % dissolved solids; Pol = % sucrose; **Purity = (Pol / Brix) × 100**.
- [ ] **Clarification pH target = 6.8–7.2**; pH probe is a **glass + reference electrode**.
- [ ] **Evaporator** uses **4–5 effects**; vacuum increases from 1st to last; Brix rises from 14 → 60 °Bx.
- [ ] **In-line Brix measurement** uses **refractometer** or **density/Coriolis** (Plato correlation $\rho = 1 + 0.00386 \cdot Brix$).
- [ ] **Pan boiling** is at **−0.85 bar (g)**; temperature **75–85 °C**; massecuite Brix **80–92 °Bx**.
- [ ] **Conductivity–Brix** is exponentially inverse: $\kappa = \kappa_0 e^{-\alpha \cdot Brix}$.
- [ ] **Dühring's rule:** $\Delta T_b = 0.054 \cdot Brix$.
- [ ] **Centrifuge vibration** is monitored by **piezoelectric accelerometer**; trip > **5 g RMS**; part of **SIL-1 SIS**.
- [ ] **Sugar final moisture** is measured by **NIR** (target **< 0.05 %** for ICUMSA-45 grade).
- [ ] **Bagasse moisture** is measured by **NIR / capacitive** sensor (target **48–52 %** w.b. for boiler feed).
- [ ] **All sensors output 4–20 mA / HART** to a central **DCS** (Siemens PCS 7 / ABB 800xA / Honeywell Experion).
- [ ] **By-products:** Bagasse → boiler → cogeneration (25–35 MW); Filter cake → fertilizer; Molasses → ethanol / rum.
- [ ] **Recovery % on cane = 10–11 %** (typical Indian plant KPI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** For a 14-mark question, **always draw the diagram (Mermaid or sketch) FIRST, then write the answer**. Diagrams carry **30–40 % of the marks** in sugar-plant questions, and examiners reward them heavily.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Diagram of Fermenter, Sensors and Instrumentation Set Up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Diagram of Fermenter, Sensors and Instrumentation Set Up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Fermenter (Bioreactor)

A **fermenter** (or **bioreactor**) is a closed, aseptic, vessel-based engineered system designed to provide a precisely controlled environment for the growth of microorganisms, plant cells, animal cells, or enzymes to carry out a biochemical reaction (fermentation) for the production of biomass, metabolites, pharmaceuticals, biofuels, or other value-added bio-products.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A fermenter is a device or system that supports a biologically active environment. In the context of agricultural instrumentation, it is used for biofertilizer production (e.g., *Rhizobium*, *Azotobacter*), biopesticide production, biogas generation, and silage processing. The fermenter must maintain optimal **Temperature (°C)**, **pH (0–14)**, **Dissolved Oxygen (DO, % saturation)**, **Agitation (RPM)**, and **Foam level** to ensure maximum microbial activity.

### 1.2 Key Components of a Fermenter — The Intuitive Analogy

**Real-World Analogy — "The Fermenter is Like a High-Tech Pressure Cooker for Microbes":**

Imagine you are cooking a very delicate dish that requires:
1. A **sealed pot** (the vessel) that does not let any external contamination in.
2. A **gas stove** that keeps the temperature constant (the **heat exchanger/jacket**).
3. A **whisk/stirrer** that mixes the ingredients gently (the **impeller/agitator**).
4. A **small pipe** at the bottom that bubbles air (the **sparger**).
5. A **thermostat and timer** that automatically controls heat and mixing (the **sensors and controllers**).

A fermenter does exactly this — but instead of cooking food, it grows millions of microorganisms to produce useful bio-products for agriculture.

| Fermenter Component | Domestic Pressure Cooker Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Vessel | Sealed pot | Houses the culture medium |
| Impeller | Whisk | Mixes nutrients and microbes |
| Sparger | Air pump pipe | Supplies oxygen (for aerobic fermentation) |
| Jacket | Outer heating layer | Controls temperature |
| Sensors (pH, DO, T) | Thermometer / Timer | Monitors critical parameters |
| Control Valves | Gas regulator knob | Automates parameter adjustment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Fermenter Operating Metrics (Bold Constants for KTU Exams):**
> - **Operating Volume:** 60–80 %** of total vessel capacity.
> - **Aspect Ratio (Height : Diameter):** Typically **2 : 1 to 3 : 1** for stirred-tank reactors.
> - **Working Temperature (Mesophilic):** **30 °C ± 0.1 °C**.
> - **Working pH:** **6.8 ± 0.05** (varies with organism).
> - **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Set-point:** **20–40 % saturation** for aerobic cultures.
> - **Sterilization (SIP) Temperature:** **121 °C at 15 psig for 20–30 min**.

### 1.3 Classification of Fermenters (As Required by KTU Module 2)

1. **Stirred-Tank Fermenter (STF / CSTR)** — Most common in agriculture and industry.
2. **Airlift Fermenter** — Used for low-shear applications (e.g., animal cell culture, biopesticides).
3. **Bubble Column Fermenter** — Simple, low-cost, used for biogas production.
4. **Packed-Bed Fermenter** — Used for immobilized enzyme/cell bioreactors (e.g., ethanol from sugarcane bagasse).
5. **Photobioreactor** — For microalgae cultivation (biofertilizer, biostimulants).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** For agricultural instrumentation, the **Stirred-Tank Fermenter (STF)** is the most exam-relevant type. Memorize its flow diagram, sensor placement, and control loops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of a Fermenter — Step-by-Step Logic

A typical fermenter operates in a sequential batch or continuous mode. The flow of materials, energy, and information is as follows:

**Step 1 — Media Preparation & Sterilization:**
The nutrient broth (carbon source, nitrogen source, minerals, vitamins) is prepared in a separate vessel and sterilized in-situ at **121 °C / 15 psig** for **20 min** to eliminate contaminating microbes.

**Step 2 — Inoculation:**
A pure microbial culture (e.g., *Azotobacter* for nitrogen-fixing biofertilizer) is aseptically introduced into the sterilized medium through the inoculation port.

**Step 3 — Aeration & Agitation (The "How" of Microbial Growth):**
- Filtered sterile air is sparged through a **ring sparger** at the bottom of the vessel.
- The **Rushton turbine impeller** (typically 2–4 blades) shears the air bubbles into fine bubbles, increasing the surface area for oxygen transfer.
- **Baffles** (4 vertical flat plates) prevent vortex formation and improve mixing efficiency.

**Step 4 — Parameter Monitoring (Sensors do the "Seeing" Job):**
Sensors continuously measure:
- **Temperature** (RTD / Pt-100 in the thermowell)
- **pH** (Glass-membrane combined electrode)
- **Dissolved Oxygen** (Galvanic or Polarographic DO probe)
- **Foam Level** (Conductivity-based foam probe)
- **Agitation Speed** (Tachogenerator on motor shaft)
- **Liquid Level** (Differential pressure level transmitter)
- **Pressure** (Piezo-resistive pressure transmitter)

**Step 5 — Feedback Control (The "Brain" of the Fermenter):**
- pH deviation → ON/OFF control of acid/base pump.
- DO drop → Cascade control of agitation RPM + Air flow.
- Temperature deviation → PID control of cooling water valve + steam valve.
- Foam rise → Anti-foam pump activation.

**Step 6 — Harvesting:**
After 24–96 hours (depending on organism), the broth is drained through the harvest valve for downstream processing (centrifugation, filtration, formulation).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Sterilization | Prevents contamination by foreign microbes that outcompete the desired culture. |
| Aeration | Supplies O₂ for aerobic metabolism → ATP generation → cell growth. |
| Agitation | (a) Disperses air bubbles, (b) Homogenizes nutrients, (c) Enhances heat transfer. |
| Sensor Monitoring | Without real-time data, the culture would crash due to undetected parameter drift. |
| Feedback Control | Maintains the **steady-state** required for reproducible, high-yield fermentation. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are the MOST LIKELY to appear in KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations (ESE). Memorize the units, NOT just the symbols.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Meaning & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) | $\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^{*} - C_L)$ | $k_L a$ = volumetric mass transfer coefficient (**s⁻¹**), $C^{*}$ = saturated DO concentration (**mg/L**), $C_L$ = actual DO concentration (**mg/L**) |
| 2 | Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR) | $\text{OUR} = q_{O_2} \cdot X$ | $q_{O_2}$ = specific oxygen uptake rate (**mg O₂ / g cells·h**), $X$ = cell concentration (**g/L**) |
| 3 | Steady-State DO Balance | $\text{OTR} = \text{OUR}$ | At dynamic equilibrium, both rates are equal — **critical for control loop design**. |
| 4 | Impeller Reynolds Number | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D_i^{2}}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = density (**kg/m³**), $N$ = impeller speed (**rev/s**), $D_i$ = impeller diameter (**m**), $\mu$ = viscosity (**Pa·s**) |
| 5 | Power Number (Newton) | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D_i^{5}$ | $N_p$ = dimensionless power number (5.5 for Rushton turbine in turbulent regime), $N$ = speed (**rev/s**), $D_i$ = impeller diameter (**m**) |
| 6 | Power Requirement (Ungassed) | $P_g = P \cdot f$ | $P$ = ungassed power (**W**), $f$ = gassing correction factor (≈ **0.4–0.8** for air). |
| 7 | Mixing Time Correlation | $N \cdot t_m = \text{const}$ | $t_m$ = mixing time (**s**); constant ≈ **4–6** for baffled stirred tanks. |
| 8 | Heat Transfer Rate | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**), $A$ = jacket area (**m²**), $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (**K**) |
| 9 | Volumetric Flow Rate (Aeration) | $Q_{air} = \text{VVM} \cdot V_L$ | VVM = volume of air / volume of medium / minute, $V_L$ = working volume (**L**) |
| 10 | pH Control (Mass Balance) | $\dot{n}_{\text{acid/base}} = \dfrac{\Delta C \cdot V_L}{M \cdot \Delta t}$ | For PID tuning of pH controller |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Agricultural Engineering:** Biofertilizer production (*Rhizobium*, *Azotobacter*, PSB), biopesticides (*Trichoderma*, *Bacillus thuringiensis*), and silage inoculants.
- **Bioprocess Engineering:** Industrial enzymes, antibiotics, vitamins.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Composting accelerators, biogas (methane) generation.
- **Food Engineering:** Yogurt, cheese, bread, beer, wine, vinegar production.
- **Pharmaceutical Engineering:** Insulin, monoclonal antibodies, vaccines.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Cross-Link:** The fermenter control system is essentially a **Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO) process** with strong interactions — e.g., increasing agitation increases DO but also increases heat generation. This is why **decoupling control** and **cascade control** strategies are heavily used.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Mass Balance for Dissolved Oxygen (DO)

**Statement:** At steady state, the rate of oxygen transfer from the gas bubble to the liquid must equal the rate of oxygen consumption by the microbial cells.

**Assumptions:**
1. Well-mixed liquid phase (CSTR model).
2. Constant gas composition in bubbles.
3. No oxygen accumulation in the liquid at steady state.

**Step 1 — Write the General Mass Balance Equation:**

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Assumption of Steady State (Accumulation = 0):**

$$0 = \text{Input}_{O_2} - \text{Output}_{O_2} + 0 - \text{Consumption}_{O_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Physical Terms:**

The rate of oxygen transfer from gas to liquid is given by the **Two-Film Theory** (Lewis-Whitman, 1924):

$$\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^{*} - C_L) \quad \text{(units: mg/L·s)}$$

The rate of oxygen consumption by cells is given by the **Monod-Kinetics** approximation (zero-order for high DO):

$$\text{OUR} = q_{O_2} \cdot X \quad \text{(units: mg/L·s)}$$

**Step 4 — Equate OTR and OUR at Steady State:**

$$k_L a \cdot (C^{*} - C_L) = q_{O_2} \cdot X$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Critical DO Concentration $C_L$:**

$$C_L = C^{*} - \dfrac{q_{O_2} \cdot X}{k_L a}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- If $C_L$ falls below the **critical DO concentration** ($C_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.5 \cdot C^{*}$ for most bacteria), oxygen becomes **limiting** → cells switch to anaerobic metabolism → ethanol/lactate byproducts form.
- This is the fundamental equation used to **size the sparger** and **set the controller set-point** for the DO loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** A typical agricultural fermenter has $C^{*} \approx 7.5$ **mg/L** at **30 °C** and 1 atm. If $q_{O_2} = 5$ **mg/g·h**, $X = 2$ **g/L**, and $k_L a = 100$ **h⁻¹**, then $C_L = 7.5 - (5 \times 2)/100 = 7.4$ **mg/L** (well above $C_{\text{crit}}$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Requirement for Impeller Agitation

**Step 1 — Start with the Power Number Definition:**

The power number is defined as the ratio of the drag force to the inertial force on the impeller blade:

$$N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D_i^{5}}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for Power $P$:**

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D_i^{5}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Turbulent Flow Regime Condition:**

For a **Rushton 6-blade turbine** in a baffled tank under turbulent flow ($N_{Re} > 10^{4}$):

$$N_p \approx 5.5 \text{ (constant)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values (Example):**

Let $\rho = 1000$ **kg/m³** (water-like medium), $N = 2$ **rev/s** (120 RPM), $D_i = 0.3$ **m**:

$$P = 5.5 \times 1000 \times (2)^{3} \times (0.3)^{5}$$

$$P = 5.5 \times 1000 \times 8 \times 0.00243$$

$$P \approx 107 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Gassing Correction (since air is sparged):**

$$P_g = P \cdot f = 107 \times 0.6 = 64.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Power per Unit Volume (P/V — critical for scale-up):**

$$\dfrac{P_g}{V_L} = \dfrac{64.2}{0.05} \approx 1284 \text{ W/m³}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scale-Up Rule (KTU 2024):** Maintain constant **$P/V$ ratio** when scaling fermenter volume from lab (10 L) to plant (10,000 L). Typical $P/V$ = **1000–3000 W/m³** for industrial aerobic fermentation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mixing Time Estimation

**Step 1 — Define Mixing Time:**

Mixing time $t_m$ is the time required to achieve a homogeneity of **95–99 %** after a tracer pulse injection.

**Step 2 — Use the Dimensionless Correlation for Baffled Stirred Tanks:**

$$N \cdot t_m = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_t}{D_i}\right)^{2.3} \cdot N_{Re}^{0.1}$$

where $D_t$ = tank diameter, $K$ = geometry constant.

**Step 3 — Simplify for Turbulent Regime:**

In the turbulent regime, the exponent on $N_{Re}$ becomes negligible, yielding:

$$t_m \approx \dfrac{4}{N} \text{ to } \dfrac{6}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute for $N = 2$ rev/s (120 RPM):**

$$t_m \approx \dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**
- Mixing time must be **< 1/10 of the doubling time** of the microorganism to prevent substrate gradients.
- For *E. coli* (doubling time ≈ 20 min), $t_m < 2$ min is acceptable.
- For slower-growing *Azotobacter* (doubling time ≈ 60–90 min), $t_m < 9$ min is acceptable.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Performance Comparison for Fermenter Applications

| Sensor | Principle | Range | Response Time | Sterilizability | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pt-100 RTD** | Resistance vs. Temperature | 0–150 °C | 5–10 s | In-situ (thermowell) | High |
| **pH Probe (Glass)** | Nernst Equation (mV vs. H⁺) | 0–14 pH | 10–30 s | Autoclavable | Very High |
| **DO Probe (Polarographic)** | Clark Electrode (current vs. O₂) | 0–100 % sat. | 30–60 s | Autoclavable | Very High |
| **DO Probe (Galvanic)** | Self-powered fuel cell | 0–100 % sat. | 60–180 s | Autoclavable | High |
| **Foam Probe (Conductivity)** | Two electrodes detect liquid contact | ON/OFF | < 1 s | In-situ | Medium |
| **Pressure Transmitter** | Piezo-resistive bridge | 0–2 bar | < 1 s | In-situ | Medium |
| **Level Transmitter (DP)** | Differential Pressure Cell | 0–100 % | < 1 s | In-situ | Low |
| **Tachometer** | Magnetic pickup / Optical | 0–5000 RPM | < 0.1 s | External | Medium |

---

### 3.5 PID Controller Tuning for the Temperature Control Loop (Analytical)

The fermenter temperature is controlled by manipulating a **cooling water valve** (and a steam valve for heating) based on the **temperature error** $e(t) = T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{actual}}$.

**PID Output Equation:**

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Cohen-Coon Tuning Approximation (for first-order + dead time process):**

$$K_p = \dfrac{\tau}{K \cdot L} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{L}{3\tau}\right)$$

$$T_i = L \cdot \dfrac{30 + 3(L/\tau)}{9 + 20(L/\tau)}$$

$$T_d = L \cdot \dfrac{4}{11 + 2(L/\tau)}$$

where $K$ = process gain (**°C / % valve opening**), $\tau$ = time constant (**s**), $L$ = dead time (**s**).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern:** A 14-mark question will often give you $K$, $\tau$, $L$ values and ask to calculate $K_p$, $T_i$, $T_d$ for a fermenter temperature loop. Practice the Cohen-Coon table values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Fermenter Instrumentation Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **complete flow of materials, signals, and control actions** in a typical stirred-tank fermenter used in agricultural bioprocess applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inlet["INLET & FEED STREAMS"]
        AirComp["Air Compressor"]
        AirFilter["0.2 micron Air Filter"]
        NutrientTank["Nutrient Feed Tank"]
        AcidTank["Acid Reservoir"]
        BaseTank["Base Reservoir"]
        AntiFoam["Anti-foam Reservoir"]
        InocPort["Inoculation Port"]
    end

    subgraph Vessel["STERILE VESSEL (CSTR)"]
        Jacket["Cooling/Steam Jacket"]
        Baffles["4 Vertical Baffles"]
        Sparger["Ring Sparger"]
        Impeller["Rushton Turbine Impeller"]
        FoamProbe["Foam Sensor"]
        TempProbe["Pt-100 RTD"]
        pHProbe["pH Electrode"]
        DOProbe["Dissolved O2 Probe"]
        PressTx["Pressure TX"]
        LevelTx["DP Level TX"]
    end

    subgraph Outlet["OUTLET STREAMS"]
        Exhaust["Exhaust Gas Filter"]
        Harvest["Harvest Valve to Centrifuge"]
        SamplePort["Sampling Port"]
    end

    subgraph Control["INSTRUMENTATION & CONTROL (DCS / PLC)"]
        TempCtrl["Temperature PID Controller"]
        pHCtrl["pH PID Controller"]
        DOCtrl["DO Cascade Controller"]
        FoamCtrl["Foam ON/OFF Controller"]
        AgitCtrl["Agitation VFD"]
    end

    AirComp --> AirFilter --> Sparger
    NutrientTank --> Vessel
    AcidTank --> pHCtrl
    BaseTank --> pHCtrl
    AntiFoam --> FoamCtrl
    InocPort --> Vessel
    Sparger --> Vessel
    Jacket --> Vessel
    Impeller --> Vessel
    Baffles --> Vessel

    Vessel --> Exhaust
    Vessel --> Harvest
    Vessel --> SamplePort

    TempProbe --> TempCtrl --> Jacket
    pHProbe --> pHCtrl
    DOProbe --> DOCtrl
    FoamProbe --> FoamCtrl
    PressTx --> Vessel
    LevelTx --> Vessel
    DOCtrl --> AgitCtrl
    DOCtrl --> Sparger
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Diagram:**
> - **Rectangular boxes** = physical equipment or sensors.
> - **Arrows** = direction of material flow (substances) or signal flow (sensor/controller).
> - **subgraph blocks** = logical grouping of related components (Inlet, Vessel, Outlet, Control).
> - The **dashed/separate Control subgraph** represents the **DCS/PLC** that does NOT physically contact the broth but receives sensor signals and sends control commands back to valves and motors.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Control Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **classical closed-loop feedback architecture** of a fermenter temperature control system. It maps the signal flow from sensor → controller → final control element → process.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SetPoint["Set-Point 30 degC"]
    Sum["Summing Junction"]
    TempSensor["Pt-100 RTD Sensor"]
    Transmitter["Temperature Transmitter 4-20 mA"]
    PID["PID Controller Kp Ki Kd"]
    Valve["Pneumatic Control Valve Air-to-Close"]
    Jacket["Cooling/Steam Jacket"]
    Process["Fermenter Broth"]
    Disturb["Disturbance e.g. Metabolic Heat"]

    SetPoint --> Sum
    TempSensor --> Transmitter --> Sum
    Sum -->|"Error Signal e(t)"| PID
    PID -->|"Output 3-15 psi"| Valve
    Valve -->|"Cooling Water Flow"| Jacket
    Jacket -->|"Heat Transfer"| Process
    Process -->|"Temperature Feedback"| TempSensor
    Disturb -.->|"Heat Load"| Process
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observations from the Block Diagram:**
> 1. The **Set-Point** and **Feedback** are subtracted at the summing junction — this is the **error signal**.
> 2. The **error signal** is amplified, integrated, and differentiated by the **PID controller**.
> 3. The controller output drives a **pneumatically-actuated valve** (3–15 psi standard signal).
> 4. **Disturbances** (e.g., heat released by exothermic microbial metabolism) enter the process loop and must be rejected by the controller.
> 5. The **Jacket** acts as the final heat exchanger that physically removes or adds heat to the broth.

---

### 4.3 P&ID Symbol Legend (For Examination Sketches)

| Symbol | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **F** in circle | Flow Transmitter | Measures gas/liquid flow rate |
| **T** in circle | Temperature Transmitter | Measures temperature |
| **P** in circle | Pressure Transmitter | Measures pressure |
| **L** in circle | Level Transmitter | Measures liquid level |
| **A** in circle | Analyzer (pH, DO) | Measures chemical composition |
| **PCV** | Pressure Control Valve | Automatic pressure regulation |
| **TCV** | Temperature Control Valve | Automatic temperature regulation |
| **XV** | On/Off Solenoid Valve | Discrete control (e.g., anti-foam) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**List the major online sensors used in a fermenter and state the principle of measurement of any two.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The major **online sensors** used in a fermenter are:

1. **Temperature sensor** — Pt-100 RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector).
2. **pH sensor** — Glass membrane combined electrode (Nernst equation: $E = E_0 - \dfrac{2.303 RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$).
3. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) sensor** — Clark-type polarographic electrode (current proportional to O₂ partial pressure).
4. **Foam sensor** — Conductivity probe (ON/OFF detection).
5. **Agitation speed sensor** — Tachogenerator (voltage proportional to RPM).
6. **Pressure transmitter** — Piezo-resistive strain gauge.
7. **Level transmitter** — Differential pressure (DP) cell.

**Principle of any two (2 marks):**
- **pH probe:** Generates a potential difference across a glass membrane selective to H⁺ ions, following the Nernst equation (59.16 mV per pH unit at 25 °C).
- **DO probe:** A polarized **cathode** (Pt) reduces O₂, generating a current proportional to the dissolved O₂ concentration in the medium.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **offline** sensors (e.g., HPLC, spectrophotometer) with **online/inline** sensors. The KTU question specifically asks for **online sensors** — i.e., those giving continuous real-time data without manual sampling.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Define the term "volumetric mass transfer coefficient ($k_L a$)" and explain its significance in fermenter design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **volumetric mass transfer coefficient ($k_L a$)** is defined as the product of the **liquid-side mass transfer coefficient ($k_L$, in m/s)** and the **specific interfacial area ($a$, in m²/m³)** of gas-liquid contact. Its units are **s⁻¹** (reciprocal time).

$$k_L a = \dfrac{\text{OTR}}{C^{*} - C_L}$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
- It is the **single most important parameter** that characterizes the oxygen transfer capability of a fermenter.
- It depends on **agitation speed, impeller type, air flow rate (VVM), and medium viscosity**.
- Typical industrial values: $k_L a = 100$–$500$ **h⁻¹**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2]**

**(a)** Draw the **flow diagram of a typical stirred-tank fermenter** and label all major components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the **closed-loop control system** used for **pH control** in a fermenter. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer:

### Part (a) — Flow Diagram (7 Marks)

**Major Components of a Stirred-Tank Fermenter (2 marks for diagram + 5 marks for explanation):**

The fermenter consists of a **vertical cylindrical vessel** (typically stainless steel SS-316L for corrosion resistance) with the following parts:

1. **Vessel body:** Closed tank with **dish-shaped top and bottom** for easy drainage and pressure containment. Aspect ratio **2:1 to 3:1**.

2. **Agitator/Impeller assembly:** A **motor-driven shaft** with **2–3 Rushton turbine impellers** mounted on it. The shaft enters from the top through a **mechanical seal** to maintain sterility.

3. **Baffles:** **4 flat vertical plates** mounted on the inner wall to prevent vortex formation and improve radial mixing.

4. **Sparger:** A **ring-shaped perforated pipe** at the bottom of the vessel that delivers **sterile air** (filtered through a 0.2 μm membrane) into the broth.

5. **Jacket / Heat exchanger:** A **double-walled outer shell** through which cooling water (or steam) circulates to maintain temperature.

6. **Inlet ports:** For **air, nutrients, acid, base, anti-foam, and inoculum**.

7. **Outlet ports:** For **exhaust gas, harvest, and sample withdrawal**.

8. **Sensor ports:** For **pH, DO, temperature, foam, pressure, and level** sensors.

9. **Control valves:** Pneumatically actuated valves (3–15 psi) on each utility line.

10. **Vent filter (Exhaust):** A 0.2 μm hydrophobic filter to prevent contamination release.

### Part (b) — pH Closed-Loop Control (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The pH control system follows the **classical feedback loop**:

$$\text{pH Probe} \rightarrow \text{pH Transmitter} \rightarrow \text{PID Controller} \rightarrow \text{Acid/Base Pump or Valve} \rightarrow \text{Fermenter Broth}$$

**Detailed Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Measurement:** The **pH probe** (combined glass electrode) generates a **millivolt (mV) signal** according to the Nernst equation. This is converted to a standard **4–20 mA** signal by the transmitter.

2. **Comparison:** The transmitter output is compared with the **set-point** (e.g., pH 6.8) at the summing junction. The difference is the **error signal** $e(t) = \text{pH}_{\text{set}} - \text{pH}_{\text{actual}}$.

3. **PID Action:** The controller computes:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

4. **Actuation:** The controller output (4–20 mA or 3–15 psi) drives a **peristaltic pump** (or solenoid valve) that injects either **sterile acid (0.5 M H₂SO₄)** or **sterile base (0.5 M NaOH)** into the fermenter.

5. **Correction:** As acid/base is added, the pH of the broth shifts toward the set-point, and the error reduces to zero (steady state).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — KTU Examiner Note:**
> Students frequently **omit the dead band / hysteresis** in ON/OFF pH control. Real fermenters use **PID control with anti-windup** because pure ON/OFF control causes excessive acid/base dosing and pH oscillation. Mentioning **anti-windup** and **reset windup protection** fetches full 7 marks.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a Dissolved Oxygen (DO) probe** used in a fermenter. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fermenter has $C^{*} = 7.5$ **mg/L**, $q_{O_2} = 4$ **mg O₂/g·h**, $X = 3$ **g/L**, and $k_L a = 120$ **h⁻¹**. Calculate the **steady-state dissolved oxygen concentration** $C_L$. Is the culture oxygen-limited? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer:

### Part (a) — Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Probe (7 Marks)

**Principle (3 marks):**
The **Clark-type polarographic DO probe** operates on the principle of **amperometric measurement** of oxygen reduction. Oxygen diffuses through a **gas-permeable membrane** (typically PTFE or polypropylene) and is electrochemically reduced at a **cathode (Pt)** polarized at approximately **–0.7 V** with respect to a **reference anode (Ag/AgCl)**.

**Cathode reaction:**
$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$$

**Anode reaction:**
$$4Ag + 4Cl^{-} \rightarrow 4AgCl + 4e^{-}$$

The **current** produced is **directly proportional** to the dissolved oxygen partial pressure in the broth.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- A **gold or platinum cathode** at the tip.
- A **silver anode** surrounding the cathode.
- A **gas-permeable membrane** (PTFE) stretched over the electrode assembly.
- An **electrolyte solution** (KCl) between the electrodes and the membrane.
- The whole assembly is housed in a **stainless steel body** that can be autoclaved.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The probe is **polarized** by applying a fixed voltage.
- Oxygen from the broth diffuses through the membrane at a rate determined by Fick's law.
- The resulting **current (typically nA to μA range)** is measured by a **millivoltmeter** and converted to % saturation or mg/L.

### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $C^{*} = 7.5$ **mg/L**
- $q_{O_2} = 4$ **mg O₂ / g cells·h**
- $X = 3$ **g/L**
- $k_L a = 120$ **h⁻¹**

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$C_L = C^{*} - \dfrac{q_{O_2} \cdot X}{k_L a}$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**

$$C_L = 7.5 - \dfrac{4 \times 3}{120}$$

**Computation (2 marks):**

$$C_L = 7.5 - \dfrac{12}{120} = 7.5 - 0.1 = 7.4 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
The dissolved oxygen is **7.4 mg/L**, which is **98.7 %** of saturation. Since this is well above the **critical DO concentration** ($C_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.5 \cdot C^{*} = 3.75$ **mg/L** for most bacteria), the culture is **NOT oxygen-limited**. The fermenter is operating with excess DO capacity, meaning the agitation/aeration could be **slightly reduced** to save energy (P/V optimization).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is **mixing up the units** of $k_L a$. If the problem gives $k_L a$ in **s⁻¹** but $q_{O_2}$ in **h⁻¹**, you MUST convert all to the same time unit (either both in s⁻¹ or both in h⁻¹) before substituting. Failing to do so results in a **2-mark deduction** even if the final answer is "close."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 (Fermenter Flow Diagram, Sensors & Instrumentation):**

- [x] A **fermenter** is a **closed, sterile, instrumented vessel** for biological reactions.
- [x] Standard **aspect ratio** = **2:1 to 3:1**; working volume = **60–80 %** of total.
- [x] Sterilization is done in-situ at **121 °C / 15 psig for 20 min**.
- [x] **Rushton turbine** impeller + **4 vertical baffles** = most common agitator setup.
- [x] **Ring sparger** at the bottom delivers **0.2 μm-filtered air**.
- [x] **Double jacket** (or internal coil) provides **temperature control**.
- [x] **Sensors in a fermenter** = Temperature (Pt-100), pH (glass electrode), DO (Clark probe), Foam (conductivity), Pressure (piezo), Level (DP), Agitation (tacho).
- [x] **Oxygen Transfer Rate** $\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^{*} - C_L)$.
- [x] At **steady state**, OTR = OUR = $q_{O_2} \cdot X$.
- [x] **Power input** $P = N_p \rho N^{3} D_i^{5}$; in turbulent regime $N_p \approx 5.5$.
- [x] **Gassed power** $P_g = P \cdot f$ (f ≈ 0.4–0.8).
- [x] **Scale-up** is based on **constant $P/V$ ratio** (typically 1000–3000 W/m³).
- [x] **Mixing time** $t_m \approx 4$–$6$ / $N$ (in seconds).
- [x] **VVM** = Volume of air per Volume of medium per Minute (typical: **0.5–1.5 VVM**).
- [x] **pH control** is achieved by **PID-controlled acid/base dosing** with **anti-windup**.
- [x] **DO control** is achieved by **cascade control** of **agitation RPM** and **air flow rate**.
- [x] **Temperature control** is achieved by **PID-controlled cooling water valve** (and steam for heating).
- [x] **Foam control** is achieved by **ON/OFF anti-foam dosing** triggered by a conductivity probe.
- [x] **Critical DO concentration** $C_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.5 \cdot C^{*}$. Below this, cells become **oxygen-limited**.
- [x] **P&ID symbols**: F = flow, T = temperature, P = pressure, L = level, A = analyzer, PCV/TCV = control valves.
- [x] **Agricultural applications** of fermenters = biofertilizers (*Rhizobium*, *Azotobacter*), biopesticides (*Trichoderma*, *Bt*), biogas, silage inoculants, and compost accelerators.
- [x] **Cohen-Coon** or **Ziegler-Nichols** tuning methods are used for **PID controller** design in fermenter loops.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Topic:** Flow Diagram of Dairy Industry and Instrumentation Set Up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Dairy Industry Process Flow Diagram** is a comprehensive schematic representation that documents the sequential unit operations involved in transforming raw milk received from dairy farms into finished, packaged, market-ready dairy products (such as pasteurized milk, cream, butter, cheese, yogurt, and milk powder). It integrates the **Physical**, **Chemical**, **Biological**, and **Thermal** processing steps along with the corresponding **Instrumentation Set-Up** required for the measurement, monitoring, automation, and control of critical process parameters in compliance with standards set by the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** and the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**.

The **Instrumentation Set-Up** refers to the systematic deployment of sensors, transmitters, controllers, recorders, valves, and Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) / Distributed Control Systems (DCS) at strategic locations along the process line to ensure **Quality Assurance (QA)**, **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)**, and **Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point (HACCP)** compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to draw and label the complete dairy process flow diagram and identify the location, type, and function of at least **8 different instruments** used across the plant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **railway station with multiple platforms** where a train (raw milk) enters and undergoes a series of "checkpoints" (processing units) before reaching the final destination (the consumer).

- Each **platform** is a unit operation (cooling, separation, pasteurization).
- Each **ticket-checker and security scanner** is a sensor/instrument (temperature, flow, pH).
- The **central control room** coordinating everything is the **DCS/PLC**.

Just as a station master ensures every train follows the right track at the right time, the **instrumentation system** ensures that milk is heated to the **exact correct temperature (72 °C for 15 s in HTST pasteurization)**, flows at the **right rate (e.g., 10,000 L/h)**, and maintains the **right pressure (avoiding pump cavitation)** — all automatically, without human error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Dairy Metric:** The universal reference temperature for milk volume measurement in India is **4.4 °C** (the temperature at which milk has its maximum density of approximately **1.032 kg/L**), and the reference fat content standard is **3.5% Fat and 8.5% SNF (Solid-Not-Fat)** for standardized cow milk.

### 1.3 Why Instrumentation is Non-Negotiable in Dairies

1. **Safety** – Pathogens like *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and *Brucella* must be destroyed.
2. **Shelf Life** – Pasteurized milk has a shelf life of only 2–4 days at 4 °C.
3. **Legal Compliance** – FSSAI mandates specific microbiological and compositional standards.
4. **Economic Efficiency** – Precise standardization prevents over-payment for fat content.
5. **Automation & CIP** – Clean-in-Place systems cannot function without sensor feedback.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sequential Unit Operations of a Dairy Plant

The dairy plant operates on a **continuous flow basis** (large plants) or a **batch basis** (small/cheese plants). Below is the structured logical breakdown:

**Step 1 — Milk Reception (Platform of Entry)**
- Raw milk is received in insulated tankers or cans.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Lactometer** → measures specific gravity to detect water adulteration.
  - **Gerber Centrifuge / Butyrometer** → measures fat percentage.
  - **Weighbridge / Load Cell** → weighs incoming milk (1 liter of cow milk ≈ **1.03 kg**).
  - **TDS / Conductivity Meter** → detects urea, salt, or water adulteration.
  - **Clot-on-Boiling (COB) Test** → alcohol test for acidity (must be ≤ **0.14% lactic acid**).

**Step 2 — Pre-cooling / Chilling**
- Milk is cooled from ~35 °C to **4 °C** using a **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)**.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **RTD (Pt-100)** sensors at inlet and outlet.
  - **Flow switch** to detect no-flow condition.
  - **Pressure transmitter** to monitor chilled water/ refrigerant side.

**Step 3 — Storage in Silos**
- Raw milk stored in **SS-304 silos** at 4 °C for < 24 h.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Capacitive / Hydrostatic Level Transmitter** for silo level.
  - **RTD** for bulk temperature.
  - **Agitator speed sensor** for gentle mixing (slow-speed, < 50 RPM).

**Step 4 — Clarification and Separation**
- **Clarifier** removes dust, dirt, somatic cells.
- **Cream Separator** (centrifuge at 6,000–8,000 RPM) splits milk into **cream (40% fat)** and **skim milk (0.05% fat)**.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Vibration sensor** on centrifuge bearings.
  - **Optical / Turbidity sensor** at clarifier outlet.
  - **Density meter** for cream standardization.

**Step 5 — Standardization**
- Cream and skim milk are blended to achieve a target fat percentage (commonly **3.5%** for toned/double-toned milk).
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Infrared (IR) Milk Analyzer** — measures fat, protein, lactose, SNF, and added water.
  - **Coriolis Mass Flow Meter** — for accurate ratio control.
  - **Magnetic flow meter** (electromagnetic flowmeter) on both lines.

**Step 6 — Homogenization**
- Milk is forced at high pressure (typically **150–250 bar**) through a narrow orifice to break fat globules to < 2 μm.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Pressure transmitter** (high-pressure side).
  - **Differential pressure transmitter** (to detect clogged homogenizer valve).
  - **Temperature sensor** (homogenization raises temperature by 2–3 °C).

**Step 7 — Pasteurization (HTST — High Temperature Short Time)**
- **Standard HTST Conditions:** **72 °C for 15 seconds** (or 63 °C for 30 min — LTLT).
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **RTD (Class A Pt-100)** at heater outlet.
  - **PID Temperature Controller** with steam control valve.
  - **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** — diverts under-pasteurized milk back to raw milk tank.
  - **Chart Recorder / Digital Data Logger** for legal proof of pasteurization.

**Step 8 — Cooling After Pasteurization**
- Milk is cooled to **4 °C** using PHE against chilled water.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Temperature transmitter** at PHE outlet.
  - **Conductivity sensor** for CIP return line.

**Step 9 — Packaging**
- Done in pouches, bottles, or tetra-pak.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Positive Displacement Flow Meter** (for accurate volume per pouch).
  - **Check-weigher** (load cell) to ensure 500 mL or 1 L fill.
  - **Vision / Inductive Seal Inspection System**.
  - **Batch coding printer** (date, MRP, FSSAI logo).

**Step 10 — Cold Storage and Distribution**
- Finished product held at 4 °C in cold rooms; transported in insulated reefers.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Data loggers** (temperature + humidity).
  - **IoT-based GPS temperature trackers** in delivery vehicles.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the high-yield formulas and standard numerical values you must memorize for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Units | Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|-------|--------------|
| 1 | Density of milk (at 4.4 °C) | $\rho_{milk} = 1.032$ | kg/L | Used in mass-to-volume conversion |
| 2 | Specific gravity correction (Lactometer) | $Corrected\ SG = Reading + 0.001 \times (T - 60)/4$ | dimensionless | Temperature compensation (60 °F reference) |
| 3 | Corrected lactometer reading (CLR) | $CLR = LR \times (1 - 0.001 \times (T - 60))$ | °L | Detects added water |
| 4 | HTST Pasteurization standard | $T = 72\ °C,\ t = 15\ s$ | °C, s | Legal FSSAI standard |
| 5 | LTLT Pasteurization | $T = 63\ °C,\ t = 30\ min$ | °C, min | Batch process |
| 6 | UHT (Ultra High Temp) | $T = 135\text{–}150\ °C,\ t = 2\text{–}5\ s$ | °C, s | Sterilized milk |
| 7 | Homogenization pressure | $P = 150\text{–}250$ | bar | Fat globule size reduction to < 2 μm |
| 8 | Pasteurization efficiency (P-value) | $P = 10^{(T - 72)/z} \times (t/15)$ | dimensionless | z = 6.5 °C for *Coxiella burnetii* |
| 9 | Fat % by Gerber | $Fat\ \% = (Reading \times 11.33) / mass\ of\ milk$ | % | Standard method |
| 10 | Flow rate (electromagnetic meter) | $Q = (D^2 \pi / 4) \times v$ | m³/s | D = pipe diameter, v = flow velocity |
| 11 | Centrifuge force (separator) | $F = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | N | $\omega$ in rad/s, r in m |
| 12 | Standardized milk fat | $F_{std} = (F_{cream} \times V_{cream} + F_{skim} \times V_{skim}) / V_{total}$ | % | Pearson's Square application |
| 13 | Cooling load (sensible heat) | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ/h | $c_p$ of milk ≈ 3.93 kJ/kg·K |
| 14 | Storage temperature | $T_{storage} = 4 \pm 1$ | °C | Mandatory cold chain |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries:** The flow diagram and P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram) form the basis of plant design, hazard analysis (HAZOP), and regulatory audits.
- **Automation Engineers:** PLC/DCS ladder logic for **Flow Diversion Valves (FDV)** in pasteurizers is a textbook example of closed-loop safety control.
- **Quality Engineers:** IR milk analyzers and lactometers enable **real-time payment to farmers** based on fat and SNF content (the foundation of cooperative dairy economics, e.g., Amul, Mother Dairy).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrected Lactometer Reading (CLR) — Adulteration Detection

The lactometer reading is taken at a temperature $T$ (in °F) but is calibrated for **60 °F**. We need to find the true reading at the standard reference.

**Step 1:** The lactometer scale is calibrated such that **density decreases by 0.001 per 1 °F rise in temperature** above 60 °F.

**Step 2:** If the actual reading is $L$ at temperature $T$, then the **Corrected Lactometer Reading (CLR)** is obtained by adding the temperature correction.

$$
\text{Temp. Correction} = 0.001 \times (T - 60)
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the corrected reading is:

$$
CLR = L + 0.001 \times (T - 60)
$$

> **Conversion to °C:** If temperature is measured in °C, use the conversion $°F = (°C \times 9/5) + 32$.

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:** Suppose a lactometer reads **28.0 °L** at **80 °F**. Find the CLR.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLR &= 28.0 + 0.001 \times (80 - 60) \\
&= 28.0 + 0.001 \times 20 \\
&= 28.0 + 0.020 \\
&= 28.02\ °L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- Pure cow milk has $CLR \approx 28$ to $30\ °L$.
- If $CLR < 26\ °L$, water adulteration is suspected.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pearson's Square for Milk Standardization

We want to produce **1000 L of standardized milk at 3.5% fat**, using **cream with 40% fat** and **skim milk with 0.05% fat**.

**Step 1:** Draw a square. Place the **desired fat % (3.5)** in the center. Place the two source fat % at the left corners (top: cream 40, bottom: skim 0.05).

**Step 2:** Subtract diagonally (always take positive value):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Top right (parts of skim)} &= 40 - 3.5 = 36.5 \\
\text{Bottom right (parts of cream)} &= 3.5 - 0.05 = 3.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total parts:

$$
\text{Total parts} = 36.5 + 3.45 = 39.95
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate volume of each component for 1000 L:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cream} &= 1000 \times (3.45 / 39.95) = 86.36\ L \\
V_{skim} &= 1000 \times (36.5 / 39.95) = 913.64\ L \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total fat} &= (86.36 \times 0.40) + (913.64 \times 0.05) \\
&= 34.544 + 45.682 \\
&= 80.226\ kg \\
\text{Total milk} &= 1000\ kg \\
\text{Fat \%} &= (80.226 / 1000) \times 100 = 8.02\ \%\ \text{(verify using mass balance if $\rho$ differs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** For more accurate results, replace volumes with **mass** by multiplying each by its density ($\rho_{cream} \approx 0.994$ kg/L, $\rho_{skim} \approx 1.035$ kg/L).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: P-Value of Pasteurization (Process Equivalent Time)

The P-value indicates the **lethality** of a pasteurization process. Standard conditions are 72 °C for 15 s, with a z-value of 6.5 °C for the target pathogen *Coxiella burnetii*.

**Step 1 — Lethality rate at any temperature T:**

$$
L = 10^{(T - 72)/z}
$$

**Step 2 — P-value (lethality equivalent to 72 °C for 15 s):**

$$
P = L \times (t / 15)
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:** Milk is held at **75 °C for 20 seconds**. Find the P-value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 10^{(75 - 72)/6.5} = 10^{3/6.5} = 10^{0.4615} \\
L &\approx 2.895 \\
P &= 2.895 \times (20 / 15) = 2.895 \times 1.333 \\
P &\approx 3.86
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** A P-value of **3.86** means this process is **3.86 times more lethal** than the minimum legal standard, ensuring a strong safety margin.

> [!NOTE]
> If $P < 1$, the milk is **under-pasteurized** and the FDV will divert it back to the raw milk silo.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Stage vs. Instrumentation

| Stage | Process Variable | Instrument / Sensor | Output Signal | Control Action |
|-------|------------------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Reception | Mass | Load cell | 4–20 mA | Tank allocation |
| Reception | Fat % | Gerber / IR analyzer | mV / digital | Payment to farmer |
| Reception | Adulteration | Lactometer | Visual (°L) | Accept / reject |
| Pre-cooling | Temperature | RTD Pt-100 | 4–20 mA | Chilled water valve |
| Silos | Level | Hydrostatic / Capacitive | 4–20 mA | Pump start/stop |
| Separator | Vibration | Piezo accelerometer | mV/g | Trip on high vibration |
| Standardization | Fat % | IR milk analyzer | Digital | Ratio control valve |
| Standardization | Flow | Coriolis mass flowmeter | 4–20 mA, HART | PID ratio control |
| Homogenizer | Pressure | Strain-gauge transmitter | 4–20 mA | Pressure relief valve |
| Pasteurizer | Temperature | Class A Pt-100 | 4–20 mA | Steam control valve (PID) |
| Pasteurizer | Flow | Magnetic flow meter | 4–20 mA | Flow Diversion Valve (FDV) |
| CIP | Conductivity | Conductivity sensor | 4–20 mA | Detergent return valve |
| Packaging | Volume | PD flow meter | Pulses | Pouch cutoff |
| Packaging | Weight | Check-weigher (load cell) | Digital | Reject pneumatic pusher |
| Cold storage | Temperature + Humidity | RTD + capacitive RH | Digital / 4–20 mA | Compressor + alarm |
| Distribution | Temperature | IoT data logger | GSM/GPS | Cold-chain compliance |

---

### 3.5 Critical Control Points (CCPs) under HACCP

1. **CCP-1:** Pasteurization — must achieve $P \geq 1$ at 72 °C.
2. **CCP-2:** Metal Detection (post-packaging).
3. **CCP-3:** Cold Storage temperature ≤ 4 °C.
4. **CCP-4:** CIP validation via conductivity / ATP swab tests.
