## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *List the sequential unit operations involved in a dairy industry and explain the function of a lactometer at the milk reception stage.*

**Model Answer:**

The sequential unit operations in a dairy industry are:

1. **Milk Reception** – unloading, weighing, and quality testing.
2. **Pre-cooling / Chilling** – cooling from ~35 °C to 4 °C.
3. **Storage** – in insulated SS-304 silos.
4. **Clarification and Separation** – removal of dirt, splitting into cream and skim.
5. **Standardization** – adjusting fat to a target value (e.g., 3.5%).
6. **Homogenization** – at 150–250 bar to break fat globules.
7. **Pasteurization (HTST)** – 72 °C for 15 s.
8. **Cooling** – back to 4 °C.
9. **Packaging** – in pouches, bottles, or tetra-pak.
10. **Cold storage and distribution.**

**Lactometer Function:** A lactometer is a **hydrometer** calibrated in degrees Quevenne (°L) that measures the **specific gravity of milk** at a reference temperature of 60 °F. Pure cow milk has a reading of **28–32 °L**. A low reading indicates **water adulteration** (density decreases), while a very high reading may indicate added solids or skim milk powder removal.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *What is HTST pasteurization? State the standard time-temperature combination and the role of a Flow Diversion Valve (FDV).*

**Model Answer:**

**HTST (High Temperature Short Time) Pasteurization** is a continuous thermal process that destroys pathogenic microorganisms in milk while preserving its nutritional and sensory qualities.

- **Standard Conditions:** **72 °C for 15 seconds** (FSSAI / Codex Alimentarius).
- **Alternative LTLT:** 63 °C for 30 min (batch process).
- **UHT (Ultra High Temperature):** 135–150 °C for 2–5 s (for shelf-stable milk).

**Role of the Flow Diversion Valve (FDV):**
The FDV is a pneumatically actuated three-way valve located **downstream of the holding tube** of the pasteurizer. It is controlled by two critical inputs:
1. The **temperature transmitter** at the heater outlet (must be $\geq$ 72 °C).
2. The **flow meter** on the holding tube (to confirm correct residence time).

If the **P-value < 1** (i.e., under-pasteurized), the FDV **automatically diverts** the milk back to the raw milk silo, ensuring no under-processed milk reaches packaging. This is a **legal, mandatory** safety device under FSSAI and Pasteurized Milk Ordinance (PMO).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** *(a)* Draw a detailed flow diagram of a dairy industry processing plant and label all major unit operations along with the instruments used at each stage. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **Coriolis mass flow meter**. Why is it preferred over a magnetic flow meter for **milk standardization**? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flow Diagram of Dairy Industry:**

The complete flow diagram (as drawn in **Section 4.1** above) must be reproduced in the answer script, showing:
- Reception → Pre-cooling → Silos → Clarifier → Separator → Standardization → Homogenizer → Pasteurizer → Cooling → Packaging → Storage.

Each unit must be labeled with:
- **Lactometer, RTD, Level Transmitter** at reception.
- **Pressure transmitter, RTD, Flow switch** at PHE.
- **Vibration sensor, Turbidity sensor** at separator.
- **IR Milk Analyzer, Coriolis flowmeter** at standardization.
- **Pt-100, PID controller, FDV** at pasteurizer.
- **PD flow meter, Check-weigher** at packaging.

**Marks distribution (typical KTU scheme):**
- Correct sequence: **2 marks**
- Minimum 8 labeled instruments: **3 marks**
- Neatness, arrows, and labeling: **2 marks**

**(b) Coriolis Mass Flow Meter:**

**Working Principle:**

The Coriolis flow meter works on the **Coriolis effect**. The fluid flows through a **U-shaped (or omega-shaped) tube** that is electro-magnetically vibrated at its natural frequency. As fluid enters the tube, it imparts an upward force on the inlet side; as it exits, it imparts a downward force on the outlet side. This causes the tube to **twist** (or "scissor") by a small angle.

**The twist angle is directly proportional to the mass flow rate.** The sensor coils detect this twist using **pick-off magnets** and **position coils**.

**Key Equation:**

$$
q_m = (C \times \Delta t)
$$

where $q_m$ = mass flow rate (kg/s), $C$ = calibration constant, $\Delta t$ = time difference between pick-off signals.

**Why Preferred Over Magnetic Flow Meter for Milk Standardization:**

| Feature | Coriolis | Magnetic |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Measures **mass** directly | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (volume only) |
| Density measurement | ✅ Simultaneous | ❌ No |
| Independence from conductivity | ✅ Yes (works for any fluid) | ❌ Requires conductive fluid (> 5 μS/cm) |
| Accuracy | ± 0.1% | ± 0.5% |
| Fat content effect | Auto-compensated via density | Errors in milk |
| Two-phase flow handling | Better | Poor |

**Conclusion:** For milk standardization, **mass-based ratio control** between cream and skim lines is critical to maintain exact fat percentage, which only a Coriolis meter can provide. *(7 marks: 2 for principle + 2 for diagram + 3 for comparison.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** *(a)* Explain the **Clean-in-Place (CIP)** system in a dairy plant. List the sequence of CIP solutions used and the instrumentation required to validate CIP. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the **HACCP plan** for a pasteurized milk plant, identifying **at least four Critical Control Points (CCPs)** with their critical limits, monitoring instruments, and corrective actions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Clean-in-Place (CIP) System:**

CIP is an **automated cleaning method** for the interior surfaces of pipes, vessels, and process equipment **without disassembly**. It uses circulated cleaning solutions at specific temperatures, concentrations, and flow velocities.

**Sequence of CIP Solutions (Standard 5-Stage CIP):**

1. **Pre-rinse** with **cold water** (≤ 35 °C) for 10 min — removes loose milk solids.
2. **Hot Caustic Circulation** with **1–2% NaOH at 75–80 °C** for 30 min — dissolves fat and protein.
3. **Intermediate Rinse** with warm water — removes residual alkali.
4. **Hot Acid Circulation** with **0.5–1% HNO₃ at 60–70 °C** for 20 min — removes milkstone (calcium phosphate deposits).
5. **Final Rinse** with cold water until conductivity matches feed water.

**Instrumentation for CIP Validation:**
- **Conductivity sensor** — confirms detergent strength (1–2% NaOH = 20,000–40,000 μS/cm).
- **RTD temperature sensors** — verify caustic/acid temperatures.
- **Turbidity sensor** — verifies final rinse water clarity.
- **Flow meter** — ensures turbulent flow (Reynolds number > 10,000).
- **pH meter** — final rinse pH should be near neutral.
- **ATP swab test** (offline) — confirms microbial removal.

**Marks split:** Sequence 3 marks, Instrumentation 3 marks, Diagram 1 mark.

**(b) HACCP Plan for Pasteurized Milk Plant:**

**HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point)** is a systematic preventive approach to food safety.

| CCP | Hazard | Critical Limit | Monitoring Instrument | Corrective Action |
|-----|--------|----------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **CCP-1 Pasteurization** | Pathogen survival (Salmonella, Listeria) | $\geq$ 72 °C for $\geq$ 15 s (P $\geq$ 1) | Pt-100 RTD, flow meter, FDV | Divert via FDV to raw silo; rework |
| **CCP-2 Metal Detection** | Metal fragments | Fe $\geq$ 1.5 mm, Non-Fe $\geq$ 2.0 mm, SS $\geq$ 2.5 mm | Inductive metal detector | Reject pouch; lock lot; investigate |
| **CCP-3 Cold Storage** | Psychrotrophic bacterial growth | T $\leq$ 4 °C | RTD + data logger | Transfer to alternative cold room; raise alarm |
| **CCP-4 CIP Validation** | Chemical / microbial residue | Conductivity $\leq$ 50 μS/cm rinse, ATP $< 10$ RLU | Conductivity sensor, ATP kit | Re-rinse; quarantine lot |

**Additional Control Points (not strictly CCPs):**
- **Raw milk reception** (pesticide / antibiotic residue test).
- **Packaging integrity** (leak detection).
- **Allergen control** (cleaning validation between flavored and plain milk batches).

**Marks split:** Hazard analysis 2 marks, CCP table 4 marks, Corrective actions 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the FDV logic** — Many students forget to mention that the FDV is a **3-way valve with dual interlock** (temperature AND flow). You will lose 2 marks if this is missed.
> 2. **Confusing HTST and UHT** — HTST = 72 °C/15 s; UHT = 135 °C/2–5 s. Mixing these will cost full marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning ISO 5708 / FSSAI 2011 standards** — Always cite the standard with your numerical values.
> 4. **Using "magnetic flow meter" for fat % measurement** — Magnetic flow meters measure **volume flow of conductive fluids only**; they cannot detect fat %.
> 5. **Forgetting the CIP sequence order** — The order is water → caustic → water → acid → water. Reversing acid and caustic will cause milkstone formation.
> 6. **Drawing a P&ID without ISA symbols** — Always use circles for instruments, with single line for shared, double line for computer control.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **HTST = 72 °C / 15 s** ; **LTLT = 63 °C / 30 min** ; **UHT = 135 °C / 2–5 s**.
- ☐ **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** is the **legal safety device** that diverts under-pasteurized milk.
- ☐ **Lactometer** measures specific gravity; pure cow milk reads **28–32 °L** at 60 °F.
- ☐ **Coriolis flow meter** = mass flow + density in one instrument (best for standardization).
- ☐ **Magnetic flow meter** requires **conductive fluid** ($\geq$ 5 μS/cm); milk is conductive, so it works.
- ☐ **RTD Pt-100** is used everywhere in dairies; **Thermocouples are NOT used** for pasteurization accuracy.
- ☐ **Homogenization pressure** = **150–250 bar**; fat globule size reduced to **< 2 μm**.
- ☐ **P-value $\geq 1$** is the legal minimum for HTST pasteurization.
- ☐ **CIP sequence:** Water → NaOH (75–80 °C) → Water → HNO₃ (60–70 °C) → Water.
- ☐ **Reference temperature for milk density** = **4.4 °C** (max density = 1.032 kg/L).
- ☐ **Pearson's Square** is the fastest method to calculate cream + skim proportions.
- ☐ **HACCP CCPs:** Pasteurization, Metal Detection, Cold Storage, CIP.
- ☐ **ISA standard symbols:** Circle = instrument; Square = PLC; Diamond = logic/decision.
- ☐ **FSSAI standardized milk:** 3.5% Fat, 8.5% SNF (cow milk) / 5.0% Fat, 9.0% SNF (buffalo milk toned).
- ☐ **Atmospheric pressure** at sea level = **101.325 kPa** (relevant for vacuum-based separators).
- ☐ **Storage silos** are made of **SS-304 stainless steel** with food-grade rubber gaskets.
- ☐ **Instruments per stage (minimum to memorize):** Reception – 5; Silos – 2; Separator – 2; Pasteurizer – 4; Packaging – 3; CIP – 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Juice Extraction Control Process and Instrumentation Set Up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Juice Extraction Control Process and Instrumentation Set Up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juice Extraction Control Process** refers to the integrated automation, monitoring, and regulation of unit operations involved in converting raw agricultural produce (sugarcane, fruits such as orange, apple, mango, grapes, pomegranate, etc.) into clarified, pasteurized, and standardized juice using a closed-loop instrumentation architecture comprising sensors, transmitters, controllers (PID/PLC/DCS), and final control elements (valves, variable frequency drives).

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"The juice extraction process involves crushing, pressing, diffusion, and filtration of agricultural raw materials under controlled conditions of pressure, temperature, flow, and concentration (°Brix). The instrumentation setup ensures yield maximization, energy efficiency, hygienic compliance, and consistent product quality through automated feedback control loops."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Automated Sugarcane Juice Stall"**
> Imagine a roadside sugarcane juice vendor, but **fully automated**. The customer drops a sugarcane stalk into a machine. The machine **measures** the weight (load cell), **squeezes** at a calibrated pressure (pressure transmitter), **collects** the juice in a tank with a level sensor, **checks** the sweetness (refractometer / Brix sensor), **heats** it to a safe temperature (RTD + heater), and **pours** a fixed quantity (flow meter + solenoid valve). The stall owner (the **controller/PLC**) watches all sensor readings and decides how hard to press, how long to heat, and when to stop. This entire decision-making closed loop is the **Juice Extraction Control Process**.

## 1.3 Key Process Metrics and Standard Units

| Physical Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Range (Industry) |
|---|---|---|
| **Brix (°Brix)** | Degrees Brix (°Bx) | **8 – 18 °Bx** (raw juice) |
| **Density (ρ)** | kg/m³ | **1040 – 1100 kg/m³** |
| **Flow rate (Q)** | m³/h or L/min | **5 – 50 m³/h** |
| **Pressure (P)** | bar or kPa | **2 – 15 bar** (milling pressure) |
| **Temperature (T)** | °C | **25 – 95 °C** (pasteurization) |
| **pH** | dimensionless | **3.5 – 5.5** (citrus); **5.5 – 6.5** (apple) |
| **Power (Pwr)** | kW | **15 – 500 kW** (crusher motor) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant — Refractive Index of Pure Sucrose Solution:**
> A solution containing **1 g of sucrose dissolved in 100 g of water** has a Brix value of **1 °Bx at 20 °C**, and the refractive index difference (Δn) from pure water is approximately **1.333 (water) → 1.3344 (1 °Bx)**. This calibration is the basis of all **refractometric Brix sensors** used in juice extraction.

## 1.4 Typical Juice Extraction Process Block Stages

1. **Raw Material Reception** — Weighbridge / load cell (cranes unload sugarcane/fruit crates).
2. **Washing & Sorting** — Spray nozzles, conveyor with vision sensors.
3. **Size Reduction** — Chopper / Shredder (sugarcane) or Hammer mill (fruits).
4. **Crushing / Pressing** — Roller mill / Belt press / Screw press / Decanter.
5. **Juice Collection & Screening** — Vibratory screen → juice tank with **level transmitter**.
6. **Juice Treatment** — pH correction (liming), heating, clarification.
7. **Filtration & Standardization** — Plate heat exchanger, **Brix controller** blends water/juice.
8. **Pasteurization** — HTST (High Temperature Short Time) at **72 °C / 15 s**.
9. **Storage & Dispatch** — Aseptic tank with temperature, level, and flow sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The instrumentation setup must satisfy **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** traceability and **HACCP** (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point) requirements. The **pH**, **temperature**, and **Brix** control loops are considered **CCPs (Critical Control Points)** in juice processing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Closed-Loop Architecture of Juice Extraction

A modern juice extraction plant is governed by **five primary feedback control loops** that operate in coordinated fashion through a **Distributed Control System (DCS)** or **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Mandatory Loops:**
> 1. **Flow Control Loop (F)**
> 2. **Pressure Control Loop (P)**
> 3. **Temperature Control Loop (T)**
> 4. **Level Control Loop (L)**
> 5. **Concentration (°Brix) Control Loop (X)**

Each loop is built using the classical **four-block instrumentation chain**:

$$\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Transmitter} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Final Control Element (FCE)}$$

The controller continuously computes the **error signal**:

$$e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$$

where **SP** = Setpoint and **PV** = Process Variable.

## 2.2 Loop-by-Loop Engineering Analysis

### 2.2.1 Flow Control Loop
- **Sensor:** Electromagnetic flowmeter (or Coriolis for sugar juice).
- **Transmitter:** 4–20 mA output proportional to Q.
- **Controller:** PID in PLC.
- **FCE:** Pneumatic control valve modulating juice line.
- **Purpose:** Maintain constant throughput Q from mill to evaporator.
- **Disturbance:** Viscosity change with temperature → corrected via **feedforward** of T.

### 2.2.2 Pressure Control Loop
- **Sensor:** Capacitive / strain-gauge pressure transmitter.
- **Critical at:** Mill rollers (sugarcane), decanter bowl (fruit pomace).
- **Control Law:** Pressure is a proxy for **extraction yield** — too low = poor juice recovery; too high = equipment fatigue.

### 2.2.3 Temperature Control Loop
- **Sensor:** PT-100 RTD (Class A, ±0.15 °C at 0 °C).
- **Use:** Pasteurization (HTST 72 °C), juice heating before evaporation.
- **FCE:** Steam control valve in heat exchanger jacket.

### 2.2.4 Level Control Loop
- **Sensor:** Hydrostatic level transmitter (HART protocol) or guided wave radar.
- **Critical at:** Juice collection tank, decanter interface, juice blend tank.
- **Failure Mode:** Tank overflow → product loss; tank empty → cavitation in pump.

### 2.2.5 °Brix (Concentration) Control Loop
- **Sensor:** In-line **refractometric Brix sensor** (NIR or optical).
- **Most economically important loop** in juice industry — directly determines **product grade** and **price per ton**.
- **FCE:** Two control valves (one for **juice**, one for **dilution water**) with **ratio control** to maintain target °Brix.

## 2.3 The PID Controller — Heart of the Loop

The standard **PID algorithm** implemented in industrial PLCs/DCS:

$$u(t) = K_c\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d\frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$$

**Laplacian (s-domain) form** used in KTU derivations:

$$U(s) = K_c\left[1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right]E(s)$$

**Where:**
- $u(t)$ = controller output to valve (%)
- $K_c$ = proportional gain
- $T_i$ = integral time (seconds)
- $T_d$ = derivative time (seconds)
- $e(t)$ = error

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Tip — Ziegler-Nichols Tuning for Brix Loop:** A PID loop controlling a juice blending tank with residence time ~3 min typically has $K_c$ between 1.5 – 4.0, $T_i$ between 60 – 180 s, and $T_d$ ≈ 0 (Brix is a slow, integrating process — derivative is usually turned OFF).

## 2.4 Mass Balance in a Continuous Juice Extractor (Steady-State)

For a single-stage mill receiving sugarcane with **pol % (sugar content)** $P_f$ and producing bagasse with pol $P_b$ and juice with pol $P_j$:

$$M_f \cdot P_f = M_b \cdot P_b + M_j \cdot P_j$$

**Mass conservation (total mass):**

$$M_f = M_b + M_j$$

**Where:**
- $M_f$ = mass flow of cane (kg/h)
- $M_b$ = mass flow of bagasse (kg/h)
- $M_j$ = mass flow of juice (kg/h)

**Extraction efficiency** (key KTU metric):

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{M_j \cdot P_j}{M_f \cdot P_f} \times 100\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark:** In a modern **diffuser-type** sugarcane plant, $\eta_{ext}$ ranges **92 – 96 %**. A poorly tuned pressure loop on the mill can drop extraction by 3–5 %, costing the mill **₹ Lakhs per day** in lost sugar.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** and have appeared in previous KTU Agriculture Instrumentation papers.

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | Error signal in any loop | same as PV |
| 2 | $U(s) = K_c\left[1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right]E(s)$ | PID controller | % |
| 3 | $G_{PI}(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right)$ | PI controller transfer function | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{ext} = \frac{M_j P_j}{M_f P_f} \times 100\,\%$ | Mill extraction efficiency | % |
| 5 | $M_f = M_b + M_j$ | Mass balance in mill | kg/h |
| 6 | $M_f P_f = M_b P_b + M_j P_j$ | Pol (sugar) balance | kg/h |
| 7 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow in juice line | m³/s |
| 8 | $\rho_{juice} = 1000 + 4 \times \text{°Bx}$ (approx.) | Density from Brix | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $^\circ Bx = \frac{\text{sucrose (g)}}{100\,\text{g solution}}$ | Definition of Brix | °Bx |
| 10 | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in juice pipe | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\Delta P = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop in juice line | Pa |
| 12 | $P_{pasteur} = 72\,°C \times 15\,s$ | HTST pasteurization | °C·s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Yield Maximization:** Properly tuned **pressure + Brix loops** recover 2 – 4 % more juice.
- **Energy Efficiency:** A 1 °C reduction in pasteurization temperature (via tighter T-control) saves **~3 %** in steam energy for an evaporator.
- **Quality Consistency:** Automated °Brix control gives **±0.1 °Bx** reproducibility vs. **±1 °Bx** in manual operation.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** FSSAI, ISO 22000, and HACCP require continuous temperature and pH recording — fully enabled by DCS/PLC-based instrumentation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Brix Controller Transfer Function (Ratio Control)

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):**
A juice blending tank receives concentrated juice at **°Bx = 60** with flow $F_1$ (m³/h) and dilution water at **°Bx = 0** with flow $F_2$ (m³/h). The blended product must be at **°Bx = 12**. A ratio controller maintains $r = F_2/F_1$ such that the outlet Brix is constant. Derive the ratio setpoint.

### Step 1: Write the Solute Mass Balance (Sugar Balance)

The total sugar entering the tank = sugar leaving. In steady state:

$$F_1 \cdot \rho_1 \cdot Bx_1 + F_2 \cdot \rho_2 \cdot Bx_2 = (F_1 + F_2)\cdot \rho_{out}\cdot Bx_{out}$$

**Step 1a — Explanation:**
- $F_1$ is the concentrated juice flow with Brix $Bx_1 = 60$.
- $F_2$ is the dilution water flow with Brix $Bx_2 = 0$ (pure water).
- $Bx_{out}$ is the desired outlet Brix, fixed at **12 °Bx** by product spec.

### Step 2: Substitute Pure Water Approximation

For dilute Brix values, $\rho \approx 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, and water density $\rho_2 \approx 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$. The juice density at 60 °Bx ≈ **1290 kg/m³** (industry table). For engineering derivation, assume:

$$\rho_1 \approx \rho_2 \approx \rho_{out} \approx \rho$$

This simplifies the balance to a **volumetric Brix balance** (valid approximation for Brix-based control):

$$F_1 \cdot Bx_1 + F_2 \cdot Bx_2 = (F_1 + F_2) \cdot Bx_{out}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Known Values

With $Bx_2 = 0$:

$$F_1 \cdot 60 = (F_1 + F_2) \cdot 12$$

### Step 4: Solve Algebraically

Expand the right side:

$$60 F_1 = 12 F_1 + 12 F_2$$

Subtract $12 F_1$ from both sides:

$$60 F_1 - 12 F_1 = 12 F_2$$

$$48 F_1 = 12 F_2$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Ratio $r = F_2/F_1$

Divide both sides by $12 F_1$:

$$\frac{48 F_1}{12 F_1} = \frac{12 F_2}{12 F_1}$$

$$4 = r$$

### Step 6: Generalize the Ratio Formula

Going back to Step 3 symbolically:

$$F_1 \cdot Bx_1 = (F_1 + F_2) \cdot Bx_{out}$$

$$Bx_1 = (1 + r) \cdot Bx_{out}$$

$$r = \frac{Bx_1 - Bx_{out}}{Bx_{out}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{r = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{Bx_1 - Bx_{out}}{Bx_{out}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution Check:** With $Bx_1 = 60$ and $Bx_{out} = 12$:
> $r = (60 - 12)/12 = 48/12 = 4$ ✓ (matches Step 5).

## 3.2 Derivation: Open-Loop Transfer Function of a Brix Sensor + PID + Valve System

**Problem:** A juice line has a process transfer function $G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ where $K_p = 2.0$ %output/(°Bx) and $\tau_p = 90$ s. The Brix sensor has gain $K_s = 0.5$ V/(°Bx). The PID controller has $K_c = 1.8$, $T_i = 120$ s, $T_d = 0$ s. The pneumatic valve has $K_v = 1.2$ %stroke/V. Find the closed-loop transfer function $\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ where $R$ is Brix setpoint and $C$ is measured Brix.

### Step 1: Define Each Block

$$\text{Sensor: } G_s(s) = K_s = 0.5$$

$$\text{Process: } G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1} = \frac{2.0}{90s + 1}$$

$$\text{PID (PI mode since } T_d=0): G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right) = 1.8\left(1 + \frac{1}{120 s}\right)$$

$$\text{Valve: } G_v(s) = K_v = 1.2$$

### Step 2: Write the Open-Loop Transfer Function

The forward path (with unity feedback for setpoint tracking):

$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_s(s)$$

### Step 3: Substitute Each Gain

$$G_{OL}(s) = 1.8\left(1 + \frac{1}{120 s}\right) \cdot 1.2 \cdot \frac{2.0}{90s + 1} \cdot 0.5$$

### Step 4: Multiply Constants

$$1.8 \times 1.2 \times 2.0 \times 0.5 = 2.16$$

So:

$$G_{OL}(s) = 2.16 \cdot \frac{1 + 120s}{120s} \cdot \frac{1}{90s + 1}$$

### Step 5: Combine into Single Fraction

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{2.16(1 + 120 s)}{120 s (90 s + 1)}$$

### Step 6: Write the Closed-Loop Transfer Function

For unity feedback:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)}$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{\frac{2.16(1 + 120 s)}{120 s (90 s + 1)}}{1 + \frac{2.16(1 + 120 s)}{120 s (90 s + 1)}}$$

### Step 7: Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $120 s (90s + 1)$

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{2.16(1 + 120 s)}{120 s (90s + 1) + 2.16(1 + 120 s)}$$

### Step 8: Expand the Denominator

**First term:** $120 s (90s + 1) = 10800 s^2 + 120 s$

**Second term:** $2.16(1 + 120 s) = 2.16 + 259.2 s$

**Add them:**

$$10800 s^2 + 120 s + 259.2 s + 2.16$$

$$10800 s^2 + 379.2 s + 2.16$$

### Step 9: Final Closed-Loop Transfer Function

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{2.16(1 + 120 s)}{10800 s^2 + 379.2 s + 2.16}$$

### Step 10: Interpret the Result

- **DC gain** (set $s=0$): $\frac{2.16(1)}{2.16} = 1.0$ → **zero steady-state error** ✓ (due to integral action).
- **System type:** Second-order with a zero in the numerator → smooth transient response.
- **Stability:** Characteristic equation $10800 s^2 + 379.2 s + 2.16 = 0$ has all positive coefficients → **stable** by Routh-Hurwitz.

> [!NOTE]
> **Damping check:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{2.16/10800} = 0.0141$ rad/s, $2\zeta\omega_n = 379.2/10800 = 0.0351$, $\zeta \approx 1.24$ → **overdamped** (no overshoot — desirable for Brix control since overshoot would waste product).

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Exchanger Energy Balance for Pasteurizer

A **plate heat exchanger (PHE)** heats juice from $T_{in} = 25\,°C$ to $T_{out} = 72\,°C$ using steam condensing at $T_s = 130\,°C$. The juice mass flow is $\dot{m}_j = 5000$ kg/h with $C_{p,j} = 3.9$ kJ/(kg·K). Steam latent heat is $h_{fg} = 2174$ kJ/kg. Find the required steam flow $\dot{m}_s$.

### Step 1: Energy Balance (no losses, steady state)

$$\dot{m}_s \cdot h_{fg} = \dot{m}_j \cdot C_{p,j} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

### Step 2: Compute Heat Load

$$Q = 5000 \times 3.9 \times (72 - 25)$$

$$Q = 5000 \times 3.9 \times 47 = 916500\,\text{kJ/h}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Steam Mass Flow

$$\dot{m}_s = \frac{916500}{2174} = 421.6\,\text{kg/h}$$

### Step 4: Convert to kg/s (SI unit)

$$\dot{m}_s = \frac{421.6}{3600} = 0.117\,\text{kg/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Note:** The steam control valve in the PHE jacket is the **final control element** of the **Temperature Control Loop**. The valve characteristic should be **equal percentage** (since steam heat transfer is highly non-linear with flow).

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Loop Tuning Summary

| Loop | Process Gain $K_p$ | Time Constant $\tau_p$ (s) | Typical $K_c$ | Typical $T_i$ (s) | Typical $T_d$ (s) | FCE Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flow (juice line)** | 0.8 | 5 | 1.0 | 10 | 0 | Globe valve |
| **Pressure (mill)** | 1.2 | 30 | 0.7 | 60 | 5 | Needle valve |
| **Temperature (PHE)** | 2.5 | 180 | 1.5 | 240 | 30 | Steam valve |
| **Level (juice tank)** | 1.0 | 60 | 2.0 | 120 | 0 | Modulating valve |
| **°Brix (blend tank)** | 0.5 | 90 | 1.8 | 120 | 0 | Two ratio valves |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Plant-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sugarcane Receipt] --> B[Weighbridge Load Cell]
    B --> C[Rotary Cutter Shredder]
    C --> D[Roller Mill Bank M1, M2, M3]
    D --> E[Bagasse Conveyor]
    D --> F[Juice Collection Tank]
    F --> G[Strainer Screen]
    G --> H[Lime Dosing Tank]
    H --> I[Plate Heat Exchanger PHE]
    I --> J[Clarifier Settler]
    J --> K[Vacuum Filter]
    K --> L[Evaporator Multiple Effect]
    L --> M[Syrup Storage Tank]
    M --> N[Crystallizer Vacuum Pan]
    N --> O[Centrifuge]
    O --> P[Final Sugar Bin]

    S1[Pressure Tx on Mill] --> D
    S2[Flow Tx on Juice Line] --> F
    S3[Level Tx in Tank] --> F
    S4[Temp Tx in PHE] --> I
    S5[pH Tx after Liming] --> H
    S6[Brix Refractometer] --> M
    S7[Flow Tx Dilution Water] --> M

    CTRL[DCS Central Controller PLC] -. Feedback Signals .-> S1
    CTRL -. Feedback Signals .-> S2
    CTRL -. Feedback Signals .-> S3
    CTRL -. Feedback Signals .-> S4
    CTRL -. Feedback Signals .-> S5
    CTRL -. Feedback Signals .-> S6
    CTRL -. Feedback Signals .-> S7

    CTRL -- Control Output --> V1[Mill Roll Gap Hydraulic Valve]
    CTRL -- Control Output --> V2[Juice Pump VFD]
    CTRL -- Control Output --> V3[PHE Steam Valve]
    CTRL -- Control Output --> V4[Brix Trim Water Valve]
    CTRL -- Control Output --> V5[Lime Pump Speed]
```

## 4.2 Single-Loop Feedback Control Block Diagram (PID + Process + Sensor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP percent] --> SUM[Comparator summing junction]
    SUM --> E[Error e t]
    E --> PID[PID Controller Kc Ti Td]
    PID --> UV[Controller Output u t percent]
    UV --> VALVE[Pneumatic Control Valve]
    VALVE --> PROC[Process Juice Tank Brix loop]
    PROC --> SENS[In line Brix Refractometer]
    SENS --> FB[Feedback Signal PV percent]
    FB --> SUM
```

## 4.3 Instrumentation Architecture (ISA-Style Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FIELD
        T1[RTD PT100 Temperature]
        P1[Pressure Transmitter]
        F1[Magnetic Flow Meter]
        L1[Hydrostatic Level Tx]
        B1[Refractometric Brix Sensor]
        pH1[pH Probe]
    end

    subgraph JUNCTION
        JB1[Intrinsic Safety Barrier]
        JB2[IS Barrier]
        JB3[IS Barrier]
        JB4[IS Barrier]
        JB5[IS Barrier]
        JB6[IS Barrier]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_ROOM
        DCS1[DCS Controller 1 Temperature]
        DCS2[DCS Controller 2 Pressure]
        DCS3[DCS Controller 3 Flow]
        DCS4[DCS Controller 4 Level]
        DCS5[DCS Controller 5 Brix]
        DCS6[DCS Controller 6 pH]
        HMI[Operator HMI SCADA]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATORS
        V1[Steam Valve for PHE]
        V2[Mill Roll Gap Valve]
        V3[Juice Flow Modulating Valve]
        V4[Tank Outlet Valve]
        V5[Water Trim Valve]
        P1_PUMP[Lime Dosing Pump VFD]
    end

    T1 --> JB1 --> DCS1 --> V1
    P1 --> JB2 --> DCS2 --> V2
    F1 --> JB3 --> DCS3 --> V3
    L1 --> JB4 --> DCS4 --> V4
    B1 --> JB5 --> DCS5 --> V5
    pH1 --> JB6 --> DCS6 --> P1_PUMP

    HMI -. Supervisory .-> DCS1
    HMI -. Supervisory .-> DCS2
    HMI -. Supervisory .-> DCS3
    HMI -. Supervisory .-> DCS4
    HMI -. Supervisory .-> DCS5
    HMI -. Supervisory .-> DCS6
```

## 4.4 P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram) — Juice Extraction Line (Block Adaptation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        direction TB
        FE_101[Bowl of Mill FE-101] --> PT_101[Pressure Tx PT-101]
        FE_101 --> TT_101[Temp Tx TT-101 at roller bearing]
        FT_101[Flow Tx FT-101 juice outlet] --> FE_101
        PT_101 -. 4-20 mA .-> I1[Junction Box JCT-1]
        TT_101 -. 4-20 mA .-> I1
        FT_101 -. 4-20 mA .-> I1
        I1 --> DCS[DCS Cabinet]
    end

    subgraph JUICE_TANK_STAGE
        direction TB

        TK_201 --> TT_201[Temp Tx TT-201 inside tank]

    TK_201 --> PHE_301

```

## 4.5 Signal Flow for Cascade Control (Master-Slave Loop — Temperature + Steam Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T_SP[Setpoint T out 72 deg C] --> T_CTRL[Master Controller Temperature]
    T_CTRL --> F_SP[Cascaded Setpoint Steam Flow]
    F_SP --> F_CTRL[Slave Controller Flow]
    F_CTRL --> FCV[Steam Control Valve]
    FCV --> PHE2[Heat Exchanger]
    PHE2 --> T_PV[Temperature Sensor PT100]
    T_PV --> T_CTRL
    PHE2 --> F_PV[Steam Flow Meter]
    F_PV --> F_CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascade Control Advantage:** A disturbance in steam pressure is corrected by the **slave (flow) loop** within seconds, before it can affect the outlet temperature. This reduces **Integrated Absolute Error (IAE)** by 40 – 60 % compared to single-loop temperature control.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> Define °Brix. Why is it considered the most critical controlled variable in a juice extraction plant?

**Model Answer:**
- **°Brix** is defined as the percentage of dissolved solids (by mass) in a sucrose-containing aqueous solution, measured by refractometric index calibrated against pure sucrose. **1 °Bx = 1 g sucrose per 100 g solution** at **20 °C**.
- It is critical because:
  1. **Economic** — directly determines price per ton of juice concentrate.
  2. **Quality** — final product spec (e.g., 12 °Bx for reconstituted juice) must be held within **±0.2 °Bx**.
  3. **Regulatory** — labelling laws require declared Brix value.
  4. **Process** — feedforward signal to evaporators, blenders, and crystallizers.

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> List any four sensors used in a fruit juice extraction plant and their measurement ranges.

**Model Answer:**

| Sensor | Variable | Typical Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| PT-100 RTD | Temperature | **0 – 150 °C** | Pasteurizer outlet |
| Capacitive pressure Tx | Pressure | **0 – 25 bar** | Mill rollers |
| Magnetic flowmeter | Flow | **0 – 50 m³/h** | Juice transfer line |
| In-line refractometer | Brix | **0 – 20 °Bx** | Blend tank |
| pH probe | pH | **0 – 14** | After liming |
| Hydrostatic level Tx | Level | **0 – 3 m** | Juice holding tank |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Draw the complete block diagram of a juice extraction control plant showing the **five mandatory control loops** and the DCS. Label each sensor, controller, and final control element. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **Brix blending system** has concentrated juice at **55 °Bx** and dilution water at **0 °Bx**. The desired product Brix is **11 °Bx**. The concentrated juice flow transmitter has a range of **0 – 20 m³/h**, and the water flow transmitter has a range of **0 – 80 m³/h**. The ratio controller maintains $r = F_w / F_j$ (water to juice). Calculate:
> (i) The ratio setpoint $r$.
> (ii) If the juice flow transmitter reads **14 mA**, what is the juice flow?
> (iii) What is the corresponding water flow?
> (iv) If the Brix sensor shows a deviation of **+0.5 °Bx** from 11 °Bx, calculate the **trim flow** adjustment required on the water line using a gain of $-2$ m³/h per °Bx. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram:**
Refer to **Section 4.3 (Instrumentation Architecture)** of these notes. Key loops:

1. **Flow loop** — Magnetic flowmeter → DCS → pump VFD / control valve.
2. **Pressure loop** — Mill pressure Tx → DCS → hydraulic valve.
3. **Temperature loop** — PT-100 RTD on PHE → DCS → steam valve.
4. **Level loop** — Hydrostatic Tx on juice tank → DCS → tank outlet valve.
5. **Brix loop** — In-line refractometer → DCS → water trim valve.

All loops are interconnected via a **DCS / SCADA HMI** which serves as the supervisory master.

**Part (b) — Mathematical Solution:**

**(i) Ratio setpoint:**

Using the derived formula from Section 3.1:

$$r = \frac{Bx_1 - Bx_{out}}{Bx_{out}}$$

$$r = \frac{55 - 11}{11} = \frac{44}{11} = 4.0$$

**Therefore, the ratio setpoint is 4.0** (4 parts water per 1 part juice).

**(ii) Juice flow at 14 mA (4–20 mA transmitter):**

Using the linear relationship:

$$F_j = \frac{(I - 4)}{16} \times (F_{max} - F_{min}) + F_{min}$$

$$F_j = \frac{(14 - 4)}{16} \times (20 - 0) + 0$$

$$F_j = \frac{10}{16} \times 20 = 12.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Therefore, $F_j = 12.5$ m³/h**.

**(iii) Water flow:**

$$F_w = r \times F_j = 4.0 \times 12.5 = 50.0\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Therefore, $F_w = 50$ m³/h**.

**(iv) Trim flow on water line:**

The deviation is $+0.5$ °Bx (juice is too concentrated → need MORE water → increase $F_w$).

The trim gain is $-2$ m³/h per °Bx (negative because increased Brix must be corrected by increasing water).

$$\Delta F_w = K_{trim} \times \Delta Bx = (-2) \times (+0.5) = -1.0\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

The negative sign means the controller will **increase** the water flow by **1.0 m³/h**.

**New water flow = 50.0 + 1.0 = 51.0 m³/h**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **(a)** With a neat **P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)**, describe the **instrumentation setup of a plate heat exchanger (PHE)** used for juice pasteurization. Identify all instruments by their ISA tag names (TT, FT, PT, TCV, FCV, etc.) and explain the **cascade control strategy** used. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A juice heater heats juice from **30 °C** to **75 °C** at a mass flow of **8000 kg/h**. The specific heat of juice is **3.85 kJ/(kg·K)**. Steam at **2.5 bar (g)** is supplied with latent heat **2180 kJ/kg**.
> (i) Calculate the steam requirement in **kg/h**.
> (ii) If the steam control valve is linear with $C_v = 4.0$ and the upstream-to-downstream pressure differential is **1.8 bar**, find the valve opening percentage to deliver the required steam flow. Use $C_v$ formula: $Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$ where $Q$ is in m³/h and $SG$ is specific gravity (assume SG of steam condensate ≈ 1.0). **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — P&ID and Cascade Control Description:**

The P&ID for the juice pasteurizer PHE (refer to Section 4.5) features:

**Instruments (with ISA tags):**
- **TT-301** — Temperature Transmitter (PT-100 RTD) at juice inlet.
- **TT-302** — Temperature Transmitter at juice outlet (PRIMARY variable).
- **FT-301** — Flow Transmitter on steam line (SECONDARY variable).
- **TCV-301** — Temperature Control Valve on steam inlet.
- **FCV-301** — Flow Control Valve (slaved in cascade).
- **PT-301** — Pressure Transmitter on steam header.
- **LIC-201** — Level Indicating Controller on upstream juice tank.

**Cascade Control Strategy:**

The **master loop** is the **temperature controller (TIC-302)**, which compares the outlet juice temperature $T_{out}$ (measured by TT-302) with the setpoint (72 °C) and outputs a **setpoint to the slave loop** rather than directly to a valve.

The **slave loop** is the **steam flow controller (FIC-301)**, which compares the measured steam flow (FT-301) with the cascaded setpoint from the master and adjusts the steam valve (TCV-301) to maintain the requested flow.

**Why Cascade?**
- Steam pressure disturbances (common in plants) are absorbed by the **fast slave loop** in seconds.
- The **slow master loop** then fine-tunes the overall temperature without being disturbed.
- Result: **IAE reduced by 50 %**; setpoint tracking smoother; valve life extended.

**Part (b) — Energy and Valve Calculations:**

**(i) Steam requirement:**

Heat load:

$$Q = \dot{m}_j \times C_{p,j} \times \Delta T = 8000 \times 3.85 \times (75 - 30)$$

$$Q = 8000 \times 3.85 \times 45 = 1{,}386{,}000\,\text{kJ/h}$$

Steam mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_s = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \frac{1{,}386{,}000}{2180} = 635.8\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Therefore, steam required ≈ 636 kg/h**.

**(ii) Valve opening:**

Convert steam mass flow to **volumetric flow of water-equivalent** (since the $C_v$ formula uses water-equivalent units). Assuming saturated liquid condensate at the valve outlet:

$$Q = \frac{635.8}{1000} = 0.6358\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

Apply $C_v$ formula:

$$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}}$$

$$0.6358 = 4.0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.8}{1.0}}$$

$$0.6358 = 4.0 \cdot \sqrt{1.8} = 4.0 \cdot 1.342 = 5.367\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

Since required $Q$ (0.6358) < max $Q$ at 100% opening (5.367):

$$\% \text{ opening} = \frac{0.6358}{5.367} \times 100 = 11.85\%$$

**Therefore, the valve should be opened to ≈ 12%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students frequently mix °C with K, or m³/h with kg/h in the heat-balance equation — this causes **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 2. **Density Assumption for Brix:** Do NOT use $\rho = 1000$ for concentrated juice (60 °Bx) — it can be **1290 kg/m³**, leading to a **+30%** error in mass balance.
> 3. **Brix Sensor Sign Convention:** A **+0.5 °Bx** error (above setpoint) requires **MORE dilution water** — the trim sign is **opposite** to the Brix deviation.
> 4. **Valve $C_v$ Formula:** The $SG$ of saturated steam at the valve outlet is **not 1.0** in rigorous problems; here we use the condensate SG (≈ 1.0) per the problem statement.
> 5. **PID Mode:** For Brix (slow, integrating) use **PI** (no derivative). Adding $T_d$ will amplify noise from optical Brix sensor.
> 6. **Cascade vs. Single Loop:** Marks are lost when students describe cascade as "two loops in parallel" instead of "master outputting setpoint to slave".
> 7. **ISA Tag Confusion:** Writing `TT` for transmitter and `TIC` for controller must be precise. **TT** = Transmitter (sensor), **TIC** = Controller with indicator, **TCV** = Control Valve, **FT** = Flow Transmitter, **BT** = Brix Transmitter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 10 minutes before the exam):**

### **Core Concepts**
- ✅ **Juice extraction = series of unit operations** (crushing → pressing → screening → pasteurizing → blending) governed by **5 control loops** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Brix).
- ✅ **°Brix = g sucrose per 100 g solution** at **20 °C**; measured by **refractometer**; critical for price and quality.
- ✅ **PID transfer function:** $U(s) = K_c[1 + 1/(T_i s) + T_d s] E(s)$.
- ✅ **Mass balance in mill:** $M_f = M_b + M_j$ and $M_f P_f = M_b P_b + M_j P_j$.
- ✅ **Extraction efficiency:** $\eta_{ext} = (M_j P_j) / (M_f P_f) \times 100\,\%$; benchmark **92–96 %** for diffuser plants.
- ✅ **Ratio control formula:** $r = (Bx_1 - Bx_{out}) / Bx_{out}$.
- ✅ **4–20 mA linearization:** $F = (I - 4)/16 \times (F_{max} - F_{min}) + F_{min}$.
- ✅ **Cascade control:** Master (T) sets slave (F) setpoint → faster disturbance rejection.
- ✅ **HTST Pasteurization:** **72 °C / 15 s** is the **FSSAI-mandated** standard for fruit juices.
- ✅ **P&ID tags:** TT (Temp Tx), FT (Flow Tx), PT (Pressure Tx), LT (Level Tx), BT (Brix Tx), pH (pH Tx), TCV/FVC (Control Valves), TIC/FIC (Controllers).

### **Key Numbers to Memorize**
- 🔢 Pure water density = **1000 kg/m³**
- 🔢 Pure water refractive index = **1.333**
- 🔢 Juice density approx: $\rho \approx 1000 + 4 \times \text{°Bx}$ kg/m³
- 🔢 HTST pasteurization: **72 °C / 15 s**
- 🔢 Typical Brix of sugarcane juice: **15 – 22 °Bx**
- 🔢 Typical Brix of reconstituted fruit juice: **11 – 12 °Bx**
- 🔢 Typical Brix of juice concentrate: **55 – 65 °Bx**
- 🔢 Steam latent heat at 2.5 bar(g): **~2180 kJ/kg**
- 🔢 Specific heat of juice: **3.8 – 4.0 kJ/(kg·K)**

### **Loop-by-Loop Quick Facts**
| Loop | Sensor | FCE | Controller Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow | Magnetic flowmeter | Globe valve | PI |
| Pressure | Strain gauge | Needle valve | PID |
| Temperature | PT-100 RTD | Steam valve (equal %) | PID (cascade with flow) |
| Level | Hydrostatic / Radar | Modulating valve | PI |
| Brix | Refractometer | Water trim valve | PI (no derivative) |
| pH | Glass electrode | Lime dosing pump | PI |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Extraction Plant and Instrumentation Set Up
Pesticides Manufacturing Process and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Pesticides Manufacturing Process and Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **pesticide** is any chemical substance or mixture of substances intended for preventing, destroying, repelling, or mitigating pests (insects, weeds, fungi, rodents, etc.) in agricultural production. The **Pesticides Manufacturing Process** refers to the sequence of chemical synthesis, formulation, and packaging operations designed to convert raw petrochemical/agricultural feedstocks into commercially usable pest-control products. **Process control** in this domain encompasses the real-time monitoring and regulation of temperature, pressure, pH, flow, level, and concentration parameters using industrial instrumentation to ensure batch-to-batch reproducibility, regulatory compliance (WHO Class I–IV toxicity), and operator safety. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Instrumentation for Agriculture course (PEICT753) treats the pesticide plant as a *process industry*, identical in instrumentation philosophy to petroleum refining or pharmaceutical manufacturing — the biological/agricultural context is only the *end-use application*, not the *engineering process nature*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Pharmacist" Imagine a large, highly-regulated industrial kitchen where chefs (chemical engineers) prepare very specific recipes (synthesis reactions) using exact measured ingredients (raw materials like chlorinated aromatics, phosphorus esters, etc.), and where every oven temperature, mixer speed, and fermentation pH is monitored by digital sensors — because the dish being prepared will eventually be eaten in tiny doses to protect crops. A slight deviation in temperature during cooking may yield a toxic by-product instead of the active ingredient. The "taste-test" (quality control via HPLC, GC-MS) is done at every stage, and the entire kitchen is sealed in a glove box (containment) because the cooking fumes themselves are hazardous. **Key Insight:** Pesticide manufacturing is essentially **micro-dosing chemistry** — manufacturing compounds active at the parts-per-million level in the field, which means synthesis purity and formulation homogeneity must be extremely high (typically **>95%** active ingredient assay). ### 1.3 Standard Toxicological Metrics (WHO Classification) > [!NOTE] > **WHO Hazard Classification of Pesticides by Acute Oral LD₅₀ (mg/kg body weight, rat):** > > | Class | Hazard | Oral LD₅₀ (rat) | Color Code | > |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| > | Ia | Extremely Hazardous | < 5 | Red | > | Ib | Highly Hazardous | 5 – 50 | Red | > | II | Moderately Hazardous | 50 – 2000 | Yellow | > | III | Slightly Hazardous | > 2000 | Blue | > | U | Unlikely Hazardous | > 5000 | Green | ### 1.4 Broad Classification of Pesticides - **By Target Pest:** - *Insecticides* — kill insects (e.g., chlorpyrifos, imidacloprid) - *Herbicides* — kill weeds (e.g., glyphosate, 2,4-D) - *Fungicides* — kill fungi (e.g., mancozeb, carbendazim) - *Rodenticides* — kill rodents (e.g., zinc phosphide, warfarin) - *Nematicides* — kill nematodes (e.g., carbofuran, fosthiazate) - *Acaricides / Miticides* — kill mites (e.g., abamectin, dicofol) - **By Chemical Family:** - Organochlorines (DDT, BHC, endosulfan — *banned/persistent*) - Organophosphates (malathion, chlorpyrifos, monocrotophos) - Carbamates (carbofuran, carbaryl, methomyl) - Pyrethroids (cypermethrin, deltamethrin, lambda-cyhalothrin) - Neonicotinoids (imidacloprid, clothianidin, thiamethoxam) - Triazines & Chlorophenoxy (atrazine, 2,4-D, simazine) - Bio-pesticides (*Bacillus thuringiensis*, neem-based azadirachtin) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Chemical Family Synthesis Logic — The "Why" Behind the Pathways #### A. Organophosphates (OPs) - **Central chemistry:** Phosphorylation of an alcohol/phenol using phosphorus oxychloride (POCl₃) or thiophosphoryl chloride (PSCl₃). - **Why this route?** The pentavalent phosphorus atom provides the electrophilic centre for the leaving group; the P=S (thiono) bond is bioactivated to P=O in insect cholinesterase, which then irreversibly phosphorylates the enzyme. - **Generic reaction sequence:** 1. Alcohol (ROH) + POCl₃ → Alkyl phosphorodichloridate 2. Reaction with second nucleophile (amine/phenoxide) → dialkyl phosphate ester 3. Thionation (P=O → P=S) using P₄S₁₀ if needed. #### B. Carbamates - **Central chemistry:** Reaction of an isocyanate (R–N=C=O) with a phenol/alcohol, or reaction of an amine with methyl chloroformate (ClCO₂CH₃). - **Why?** The carbamate ester (–O–C(=O)–NH–) is a reversible cholinesterase inhibitor; safer than OPs but still toxic. #### C. Pyrethroids - **Central chemistry:** Esterification of a chrysanthemate acid (from pyrethrin natural product) with a substituted benzyl alcohol; modern pyrethroids are fully synthetic analogues (e.g., permethrin, cypermethrin). - **Why?** Photostable synthetic mimics of natural pyrethrum; target insect sodium channels. #### D. Neonicotinoids - **Central chemistry:** Construction of a 2-chloropyridinyl or 2-chlorothiazolyl moiety followed by N-alkylation with an imidazolidine or oxadiazine ring. - **Why?** Selective agonist of insect nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChR); low mammalian toxicity due to differential receptor binding. ### 2.2 Formulation Types — The Delivery Vehicle The **active ingredient (a.i.)** is rarely used as-is. It is combined with **inert carriers, surfactants, and adjuvants** to make a usable **formulation**. > [!NOTE] > **Major Formulation Codes Used in Industry:** > > | Code | Full Form | Typical Use | > |:---:|:---|:---| > | EC | Emulsifiable Concentrate | Oil-soluble a.i. + surfactant + solvent | > | WP | Wettable Powder | Solid a.i. + filler + wetting agent | > | SC | Suspension Concentrate | Aqueous suspension of solid a.i. | > | SL | Soluble Concentrate | Water-soluble a.i. in aqueous solution | > | WG / WDG | Water-Dispersible Granule | Granular WP; no dust, easy measurement | > | DP | Dustable Powder | Ready-to-use dry mix for seed treatment | > | GR | Granule | Soil-applied systemic (e.g., carbofuran 3G) | > | UL | Ultra-Low Volume Liquid | Concentrate for aerial spray | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Process Control & Yield Calculations > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for the KTU Board Exam:** $$ \text{Conversion (\%)} = \frac{n_{\text{reacted}}}{n_{\text{initial}}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Yield (\%)} = \frac{\text{actual moles of product}}{\text{theoretical moles}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Assay (\% a.i.)} = \frac{\text{mass of active ingredient}}{\text{total mass of formulation}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Selectivity} = \frac{\text{moles of desired product}}{\text{moles of total products}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Residence Time } (\tau) = \frac{V_{\text{reactor}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}}} $$ $$ \text{Steam Mass for Heating} = \frac{m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{h_{fg}} $$ $$ \text{Reaction Rate (r)} = -\frac{1}{\nu_i}\frac{dC_i}{dt} = k \cdot C_A^n \cdot C_B^m $$ $$ \text{Arrhenius Equation:}\ k = A \, e^{-E_a/RT} $$ $$ \text{Heat Removal Rate} = UA\,(T_{\text{rxn}} - T_{\text{cool}}) $$ $$ \text{Emulsion Stability} = \frac{V_{\text{cream}} \text{ after 24 h}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{AI Loading (g/L)} = \frac{\text{Mass of AI}}{\text{Volume of Formulation}} $$ $$ \text{LD}_{50} = \frac{\text{Dose killing 50\% test population}}{\text{body weight}} $$ ### 2.4 Critical Process Control Variables in a Pesticide Plant > [!NOTE] > **The Big-6 Instrumentation Parameters:** > > 1. **Temperature Control** — Exothermic phosphorylation reactions require jacketed reactors with chilled brine (–5 °C to 25 °C range) and cascade PID loops. > 2. **Pressure Control** — Hydrogenation steps (in some herbicide syntheses) use 5–30 bar H₂ with rupture-disc + pressure safety valves (PSV). > 3. **pH / ORP Control** — Critical in neutralization, hydrolysis and purification steps. > 4. **Flow Control** — Corrosive feed streams (POCl₃, SOCl₂) use magnetic flow meters; mass flow controllers (MFC) for H₂. > 5. **Level Control** — Glass-lined reactors use differential-pressure (DP) or guided-wave radar level transmitters (resistant to HCl fumes). > 6. **Composition Control** — On-line NIR / Raman / FT-IR / GC for in-process assay. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Pesticide plant control systems must satisfy **three simultaneous constraints** — *purity* (active ingredient must be >95%), *safety* (HCN, HCl, SO₂, organophosphate vapors are highly toxic), and *regulatory compliance* (CIBRC, EPA, EU REACH). The instrumentation system is a **SIL-2 / SIL-3** safety-instrumented system (per IEC 61511) for exothermic runaway prevention, since several organophosphate syntheses (e.g., malathion) are adiabatic-runaway-prone. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Manufacturing Process — Organophosphate Case Study (Malathion) **Malathion** is chosen as a worked example because it is widely studied, the synthesis chemistry is representative, and it appears frequently in KTU-board question banks. #### 3.1.1 Reaction Stoichiometry The industrial synthesis proceeds in **two stages**: $$ \text{Stage 1:}\ P_2S_5 + 4\,CH_3OH \longrightarrow 2\,(CH_3O)_2P(=S)SH + H_2S $$ $$ \text{Stage 2:}\ (CH_3O)_2P(=S)SH + \text{Maleic Diethyl Ester} \xrightarrow{\text{base}} \text{Malathion} + H_2O $$ The overall stoichiometry is: $$ P_2S_5 + 4\,CH_3OH + 2\,C_2H_2(CO_2C_2H_5)_2 \longrightarrow 2\,C_{10}H_{19}O_6PS_2 + H_2S + 2\,H_2O $$ #### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mass Balance (for 1 metric tonne of Malathion a.i.) **Given:** - Molecular weight of Malathion (C₁₀H₁₉O₆PS₂) = **330.36 g/mol** - Molecular weight of P₂S₅ = **222.27 g/mol** - Molecular weight of CH₃OH (methanol) = **32.04 g/mol** - Target output: 1,000 kg of pure malathion a.i. **Step 1 — Moles of Malathion produced:** $$ n_{\text{malathion}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000\ \text{g}}{330.36\ \text{g/mol}} = 3027.0\ \text{mol} \approx 3.027\ \text{kmol} $$ **Step 2 — From stoichiometry, 2 mol of malathion require 1 mol of P₂S₅:** $$ n_{P_2S_5} = \frac{3027}{2} = 1513.5\ \text{mol} = 1.5135\ \text{kmol} $$ **Step 3 — Mass of P₂S₅ required (theoretical):** $$ m_{P_2S_5} = 1.5135 \times 10^3\ \text{mol} \times 222.27\ \text{g/mol} = 336{,}400\ \text{g} \approx 336.4\ \text{kg} $$ **Step 4 — Moles of methanol required (4 mol per mol P₂S₅):** $$ n_{CH_3OH} = 4 \times 1513.5 = 6054\ \text{mol} = 6.054\ \text{kmol} $$ **Step 5 — Mass of methanol:** $$ m_{CH_3OH} = 6054 \times 32.04 = 193{,}970\ \text{g} \approx 194.0\ \text{kg} $$ **Step 6 — Adjusting for industrial yield (η = 85%):** $$ m_{P_2S_5,\text{actual}} = \frac{336.4}{0.85} \approx 395.8\ \text{kg} $$ $$ m_{CH_3OH,\text{actual}} = \frac{194.0}{0.85} \approx 228.2\ \text{kg} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The 15% loss in yield arises from side reactions — chiefly *O,O-dimethyl thiophosphoric acid* hydrolysis and *isomalathion* isomer formation, both of which are toxicologically more hazardous than malathion itself. The reactor temperature MUST be held below **65 °C** to suppress isomerization. #### 3.1.3 Process Instrumentation Block (per Stage) - **Reactor R-101 (Stage 1):** Glass-lined steel, 10 m³, jacketed. **Temperature Transmitter (TT)** with Pt-100 RTD → cascade to TIC controlling chilled-water valve (V-1). **Pressure Transmitter (PT)** with rupture disc set at 1.5 bar(g) — H₂S evolution. **pH/Conductivity probe** for methanol feed completion. - **H₂S Scrubber (S-101):** NaOH wash tower. pH control loop to maintain scrubber liquor pH > 11 (H₂S is acidic; pH drop = scrubbing failure alarm). - **Reactor R-102 (Stage 2):** Same construction, addition funnel for maleate ester. **Level Transmitter (LT)** using guided-wave radar; **Reflux Condenser** with differential-pressure (ΔP) flow transmitter on the condensate line. #### 3.2 Formulation Yield & Cost Calculation For an Emulsifiable Concentrate (EC) formulation, say **50% Malathion EC**: $$ \text{Composition:}\ 500\ \text{g a.i.} + 400\ \text{g xylene (solvent)} + 100\ \text{g emulsifier blend} $$ If batch volume = 1000 L and density ≈ 1.05 g/mL: $$ \text{Total mass} = 1000\ \text{L} \times 1.05\ \text{kg/L} = 1050\ \text{kg} $$ $$ \text{Assay \%} = \frac{500}{1050} \times 100 = 47.6\% \quad \text{(recheck with QC)} $$ > Target assay after formulation: 50% ± 1% (FAO specification) #### 3.3 Arrhenius-Based Shelf-Life Derivation The shelf-life (t₉₀) of an OP pesticide (time for 10% decomposition) is governed by: $$ \ln\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k\,t = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,t $$ Taking $C/C_0 = 0.9$ and solving for $t_{90}$: $$ t_{90} = \frac{\ln(1/0.9)}{A\,e^{-E_a/RT}} = \frac{0.10536}{A\,e^{-E_a/RT}} $$ For a typical malathion hydrolysis: $E_a = 78\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $A = 1.2 \times 10^{11}\ \text{h}^{-1}$, $T = 298\ \text{K}$: $$ k = 1.2 \times 10^{11} \times e^{-78000/(8.314 \times 298)} = 1.2 \times 10^{11} \times e^{-31.49} $$ $$ e^{-31.49} = 2.27 \times 10^{-14} \Rightarrow k = 2.72 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{h}^{-1} $$ $$ t_{90} = \frac{0.10536}{2.72 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 38.7\ \text{hours} $$ > [!WARNING] > This calculated value appears short, but the actual industrial *shelf-life* is **2 years** because commercial formulations contain stabilizers (e.g., 0.1% *epoxidized soybean oil* or *toluquinone*) that suppress radical-mediated decomposition pathways, which dominate over simple hydrolysis in sealed containers. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Sensor for Each Process Variable | Process Variable | Range | Recommended Sensor | Reason | |:---|:---:|:---|:---| | Reactor temperature | 0–150 °C | Pt-100 RTD (Class A) | High accuracy, stable in acid media when sheathed in PTFE/glass | | Reactor pressure | 0–6 bar | Capacitance DP cell | Corrosion-resistant, intrinsically safe | | pH | 0–14 | Glass electrode with KCl refill, PTFE junction | Tolerates organic solvent splashes | | Liquid level | 0–10 m | Guided-wave radar | Unaffected by foam or H₂S vapors | | Mass flow (POCl₃) | 0–500 kg/h | Coriolis mass flow meter | Direct mass readout, no recalibration with density | | Composition (a.i.) | 0–100% | In-line NIR probe (1100–2200 nm) | Real-time assay of clear solutions | | Toxic vapor (H₂S) | 0–50 ppm | Electrochemical H₂S sensor | OSHA PEL = 10 ppm; alarm at 5 ppm | | Particulate (powder) | 0.1–10 mg/m³ | Beta-attenuation dust monitor | Required for WP formulation rooms | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Pesticide Manufacturing Plant Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD rawA[Raw Material Storage] --> rm1[Phosphorus Pentasulfide] rawB[Solvent and Reagent Storage] --> rm1 rawC[Glass Lined Reactor R101] --> rm1 rm1 --> rxn1[Stage 1 Phosphorylation
Temp 55 to 65 deg C
Pressure 1 atm] rxn1 --> scr1[H2S Scrubber S101
pH greater than 11] scr1 --> rxn2[Stage 2 Esterification
Temp 40 to 50 deg C
Catalyst Triethylamine] rxn2 --> wash1[Water Wash and Phase Separation] wash1 --> evap1[Vacuum Evaporation E101
Pressure 50 mbar] evap1 --> assay1[In process QC
HPLC Assay] assay1 --> form1[Formulation Tank F101
Add Xylene and Emulsifier] form1 --> form2[High Shear Mixer M101
RPM 3000] form2 --> form3[Homogenizer H101
Particle size less than 5 micron] form3 --> fill1[Automatic Filling Line
Capacity 5000 bottles per hour] fill1 --> store1[Flammable Goods Warehouse] qc1[Online NIR Analyzer] -.-> form1 inv1[Distributed Control System DCS] -.-> rxn1 inv1 -.-> rxn2 inv1 -.-> form2 sft1[SIS Safety Instrumented System
SIL 2] -.-> rxn1 sft1 -.-> scr1 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cascade Temperature Control Loop for Exothermic Reactor ```mermaid flowchart LR sp[Setpoint 50 deg C] --> err[Error Detector e equals SP minus PV] pv[Pt100 RTD in Reactor Jacket] --> err err --> pic[Primary PID Controller TIC 101] pic --> sec[Secondary PID Controller TIC 102] jtemp[Jacket Outlet Temperature] --> sec sec --> v1[Pneumatically Actuated Valve V1
Chilled Brine Line] v1 --> jk[Jacket Cooling Coil] jk --> jtemp jk --> rxn[Reactor R101 Contents] rxn --> pv pic --> alm1[High Temperature Alarm at 60 deg C] pic --> alm2[High High Trip at 75 deg C
Activates Emergency Quench] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Formulation Process Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SG1[Active Ingredient Handling] ai[Technical Grade AI
Assay 95 percent] --> mill[Air Classifier Mill ACM] mill --> siv[Sieve SS316
Mesh 325] end subgraph SG2[Inert Carrier Preparation] car[China Clay or Diatomaceous Earth] --> dry[Dryer 105 deg C] dry --> siv end subgraph SG3[Blending] siv --> bl[Double Cone Blender 2000 L] add[Wetting Agent and Dispersant] --> bl end subgraph SG4[Quality Control] bl --> qc[QC Sampling
HPLC for Assay
Laser Diffraction for Particle Size] qc --> rel[Release for Packing] qc -. reject .-> rep[Reprocess Batch] end subgraph SG5[Packing] rel --> pk[Automatic Pouch Packing
50 g 100 g 250 g 500 g] pk --> mc[Metal Detection and Check Weigher] mc --> ship[Carton and Ship] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note on the Schematic:** The above is a *block-level functional architecture* — the actual physical plant layout includes segregated buildings for synthesis (RCB-1), formulation (FP-1), and packing (PP-1) per Indian Factories Act & CIBRC guidelines, with dedicated effluent treatment (ETP) for pesticide-specific waste (containing pesticide residues at ppm-ppb levels). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Classify pesticides based on (a) target pest, and (b) chemical family. Give two examples for each class.* **Model Answer:** | Basis | Class | Examples | |:---|:---|:---| | Target Pest | Insecticide | Chlorpyrifos, Imidacloprid | | | Herbicide | Glyphosate, 2,4-D | | | Fungicide | Mancozeb, Carbendazim | | | Rodenticide | Zinc phosphide, Warfarin | | Chemical Family | Organophosphate | Malathion, Monocrotophos | | | Carbamate | Carbofuran, Carbaryl | | | Pyrethroid | Cypermethrin, Deltamethrin | | | Neonicotinoid | Imidacloprid, Thiamethoxam | | | Organochlorine | DDT, BHC | *(3 marks — full classification with two examples each = 3; partial = 2 or 1)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the significance of the WHO Hazard Classification for pesticides? List the four classes and their color codes.* **Model Answer:** The WHO Hazard Classification is an internationally accepted system that categorizes pesticides by their **acute oral LD₅₀** in rats, providing a uniform hazard communication standard for safe handling, packaging, and shipment. | Class | Hazard | LD₅₀ (mg/kg) | Color Band | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | Ia | Extremely Hazardous | < 5 | Red | | Ib | Highly Hazardous | 5 – 50 | Red | | II | Moderately Hazardous | 50 – 2000 | Yellow | | III | Slightly Hazardous | > 2000 | Blue | | U | Unlikely to be Hazardous | > 5000 | Green | *(3 marks — 1 for purpose, 2 for table)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **(a)** Describe the general manufacturing process flow for an **Emulsifiable Concentrate (EC) formulation** of a synthetic pyrethroid insecticide, with a neat process flow diagram and explanation of each unit operation. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the **critical process control parameters** during the synthesis of an organophosphate pesticide. What instrumentation is used for each parameter? *(7 marks)* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A (a) An **Emulsifiable Concentrate (EC)** is a clear, single-phase liquid formulation that, on dilution with water, forms a milky emulsion. For a pyrethroid like **Cypermethrin 25% EC**, the manufacturing sequence is: **Step 1 — Solvent Preparation (Mixing Tank M-101, SS316, 5 m³, anchor agitator):** Technical-grade xylene (or a blend of aromatic 150 / cyclohexanone) is charged first to act as the solvent vehicle. The level transmitter (LT-101) — guided-wave radar type — verifies the solvent charge. Temperature is held at **25 ± 2 °C** using a jacket TIC loop to prevent premature crystallization of the active ingredient. **Step 2 — Active Ingredient Dissolution (Reactor R-101):** Technical cypermethrin (assay 92% min) is added slowly under high-shear mixing (1500–3000 rpm). The **Pt-100 RTD** in the reactor wall monitors temperature; an **in-line NIR probe** (1100–2200 nm) confirms complete dissolution by tracking the C=O and CN absorbance bands. **Step 3 — Emulsifier Blend Addition:** A pre-mixed blend of **non-ionic emulsifiers** (e.g., alkylphenol ethoxylate + calcium dodecylbenzene sulfonate at 6:4 ratio) is added at **10% w/w** of the formulation. The emulsifier system must have an HLB (Hydrophile-Lipophile Balance) value of **12–14** to spontaneously emulsify on water dilution. **Step 4 — Co-solvent and Stabilizer Addition:** A polar co-solvent (e.g., cyclohexanone at 5–8%) is added to prevent cypermethrin crystallization at low temperatures (cold-stability test: 0 °C for 7 days). An **antioxidant** (BHT, 0.1%) prevents oxidative degradation of the pyrethroid ester linkage. **Step 5 — Filtration (5 µm polypropylene cartridge filter, F-101):** Removes undissolved particulates that would otherwise block spray nozzles. **Step 6 — Quality Control and Filling:** - **Assay:** HPLC against reference standard (target: 25.0% ± 1.0%). - **Emulsion stability:** 1% dilution in 342 ppm hard water at 30 °C; cream ≤ 2 mL, free oil ≤ trace (CIPAC MT 36 test). - **Cold test:** 0 °C for 7 days; no separation. - **Filling:** Automatic volumetric filling into HDPE / PET bottles; metal detection + check-weigher. ``` [Cypermethrin Tech]──┐ ├──►[M-101 Solvent Blend]──►[R-101 Dissolution]──►[F-101 Filter]──►[QC]──►[Filling] [Xylene Solvent]─────┤ (TIC 25 °C) (NIR probe) (5 µm) [Emulsifier Blend]──┤ [Co-solvent + BHT]──┘ ``` *(7 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for unit ops, 1 for diagram, 2 for instrumentation, 1 for QC tests)* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A (b) The synthesis of an organophosphate pesticide (e.g., malathion, chlorpyrifos) involves a **phosphorylation reaction** between an alcohol/phenol and a phosphorus halide (POCl₃ or PSCl₃) in the presence of an acid acceptor (tertiary amine). The **six critical process control parameters** and their instrumentation are: **1. Temperature (0–150 °C):** - *Sensor:* Pt-100 RTD in thermowell, Class A accuracy (±0.15 °C at 0 °C). - *Controller:* Cascade PID loop — primary TIC on reactor bulk, secondary TIC on jacket outlet. - *Why critical:* Exothermic POCl₃ hydrolysis is runaway-prone above 80 °C; isomerization (malathion → isomalathion) is significant above 65 °C. - *Safety:* High-High trip at 75 °C triggers emergency brine dump and amine injection. **2. Pressure (0–6 barg):** - *Sensor:* Capacitance differential-pressure cell with chemical seals. - *Safety:* Rupture disc set at 1.5 × MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure); PSV vent to scrubber. **3. pH (0–14):** - *Sensor:* Glass electrode with PTFE junction, KCl reference, refillable. - *Why critical:* Excess amine raises pH (hydrolysis risk); acidic conditions protonate the amine and stop the reaction. **4. Flow (Feed streams — POCl₃, methanol, amine):** - *Sensor:* Magnetic flow meter (Faraday's law) for conductive liquids; Coriolis meter for mass-precise charging of POCl₃ (corrosive). - *Why critical:* Stoichiometric imbalance → unreacted POCl₃ (toxic) or excess alcohol (impurity). **5. Level (Reactor + Scrubber):** - *Sensor:* Guided-wave radar (GWR) or differential-pressure (DP) cell. - *Why critical:* Prevents overflow of H₂S / HCl vapors; controls charge volume for batch recipe. **6. Composition (In-process assay):** - *Sensor:* In-line NIR / FTIR / Raman probe, or at-line HPLC every 30 min. - *Why critical:* Determines reaction end-point; minimizes batch time. **Additional Safety Instrumentation:** - **H₂S gas detector** (electrochemical, 0–50 ppm range, alarm at 5 ppm, trip at 10 ppm). - **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) detector** for xylene/toluene vapors. - **Emergency Eyewash and Shower** at every reactor bay per OSHA / Indian Factories Act. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss:** > Students often write "temperature is controlled by thermostat" or "pressure is controlled by gauge" — this is **incorrect** for a 14-mark question. KTU examiners expect the **specific sensor type** (Pt-100 RTD, capacitance DP cell, etc.), the **control architecture** (cascade, ratio, feedforward), and the **safety implications** of the parameter. Generic answers lose 3–5 marks per sub-part. *(7 marks — 1 mark per parameter × 6 = 6, 1 mark for safety interlocks)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]** **(a)** Explain the formulation types **EC, WP, SC, and WG** in detail. Compare their **stability, ease of use, and equipment requirements**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A batch process for manufacturing **Carbendazim 50% WP** uses 500 kg of technical carbendazim (assay 98%) per batch. Calculate: *(i)* the total batch mass, *(ii)* the percentage assay of the final formulation, and *(iii)* the amount of carrier (china clay) required. *(7 marks)* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B (a) | Property | EC (Emulsifiable Concentrate) | WP (Wettable Powder) | SC (Suspension Concentrate) | WG (Water-Dispersible Granule) | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | Physical form | Clear liquid | Dry powder | Aqueous suspension | Granules (1–2 mm) | | Carrier / Diluent | Solvent (xylene) | China clay, talc, diatomite | Water + suspending agent | Water-soluble binder + filler | | Surfactant | Emulsifier blend | Wetting + dispersing agent | Wetting + dispersing + antifreeze | Dispersing + wetting agent | | Ease of use | Easy to pour and mix | Dust hazard; needs careful handling | Ready-to-dilute; no dust | No dust; flows well; pre-measured | | Equipment | Mixing tank, filter | Pulverizer, blender, sieve | Sand/bead mill, agitator | Fluid-bed granulator, extruder | | Stability | Good (organic a.i.); solvent evaporation possible | Excellent (no water) | Risk of settling, crystal growth | Excellent (sealed granules) | | Hazard | Flammable solvent | Inhalation dust | Spillage of concentrate | Lowest hazard | | Typical a.i. | Cypermethrin, chlorpyrifos | Mancozeb, carbendazim | Atrazine, imidacloprid | Atrazine, thiamethoxam | *(7 marks — 1 mark for each formulation description, 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for equipment/process note)* --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B (b) **Given:** - Carbendazim technical: 500 kg/batch - Technical assay: 98% pure - Target formulation: 50% WP **Part (i) — Total batch mass:** $$ \text{Mass of pure carbendazim} = 500\ \text{kg} \times 0.98 = 490\ \text{kg} $$ For a 50% WP, the total batch mass must contain 50% active ingredient: $$ \text{Total batch mass} = \frac{490}{0.50} = 980\ \text{kg} $$ **Part (ii) — Assay of final formulation:** $$ \text{Assay} = \frac{\text{Pure AI}}{\text{Total mass}} \times 100 = \frac{490}{980} \times 100 = 50.0\% $$ **Part (iii) — Carrier (china clay) required:** The other ingredients in a 50% WP (typical recipe): - Carbendazim (pure) = 490 kg - Wetting agent (sodium lignosulfonate) = 20 kg - Dispersing agent (naphthalene sulfonate) = 20 kg - China clay (carrier) = balance $$ \text{China clay} = 980 - 490 - 20 - 20 = 450\ \text{kg} $$ **Verification of assay:** $$ \text{Assay} = \frac{490}{980} \times 100 = 50.0\% \quad \checkmark $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common error is to use 500 kg (gross) instead of 490 kg (net AI) in the assay calculation, which gives an assay of **51.02%** and a target batch mass of **1000 kg** — both wrong. Always adjust the gross technical mass to *pure AI mass* before computing the formulation recipe. KTU board examiners will deduct **2 marks** for this single error. *(7 marks — 2 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 3 for part (iii) including verification)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pesticides Manufacturing Process & Control:** - **Definition:** Pesticide = chemical agent to control pests; manufacturing = synthesis + formulation + packing. - **WHO Hazard Classes:** Ia (<5), Ib (5–50), II (50–2000), III (>2000), U (>5000) mg/kg — color codes Red / Yellow / Blue / Green. - **Major Chemical Families:** Organophosphates, Carbamates, Pyrethroids, Neonicotinoids, Triazines, Bio-pesticides. - **Formulation Codes:** EC, WP, SC, SL, WG, GR, DP, UL — each chosen based on AI solubility, application method, and stability. - **Critical Process Variables:** **T, P, pH, Flow, Level, Composition** — the "Big-6" of process control. - **Key Sensors:** Pt-100 RTD (temp), capacitance DP cell (pressure), glass electrode (pH), magnetic/Coriolis (flow), guided-wave radar (level), NIR/Raman/GC (composition). - **Safety:** SIL-2 / SIL-3 emergency interlocks; H₂S and LEL detectors; emergency quench and amine scrubber. - **Yield Formula:** $\text{Yield \%} = (\text{actual}/\text{theoretical}) \times 100$; **Assay %** = mass AI / total mass × 100. - **Reactor Choice:** Glass-lined steel for HCl/H₂S exposure; SS316 for solvent service. - **Shelf-life:** Arrhenius-controlled; commercial formulations contain stabilizers (BHT, epoxidized oil) to extend shelf-life from months to 2+ years. - **Regulatory:** CIBRC (India), EPA (USA), EU REACH, FAO/WHO specifications for active ingredient and formulation. - **Common Exam Trap:** Forgetting to convert **gross technical mass → pure AI mass** in formulation calculations. - **Instrumentation Architecture:** DCS for continuous control + SIS (Safety Instrumented System) for emergency shutdown — these are *separate, independent* systems per IEC 61511. *(End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Pesticides Manufacturing Process and Control)*

topics.title: Instrumentation in Irrigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation in Irrigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation in Irrigation** refers to the integrated suite of electronic sensors, data acquisition systems, controllers, telemetry units, and software algorithms used to **measure**, **monitor**, and **regulate** the application of water to agricultural fields with the goal of matching crop water demand precisely, minimizing wastage, and maximizing water-use efficiency (WUE).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, irrigation instrumentation is the backbone of **Precision Irrigation Agriculture (PIA)**. It encompasses:

1. **Soil-side sensing** — measuring the water status in the root zone.
2. **Plant-side sensing** — detecting crop water stress.
3. **Atmosphere-side sensing** — quantifying evaporative demand.
4. **Hydraulic-side sensing** — monitoring flow, pressure, and quality of delivered water.
5. **Decision & actuation systems** — automated valves, variable-rate pumps, and SCADA/cloud controllers.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Smart Thermostat of the Farm"**
> Think of your home's smart air-conditioner. A small box on the wall senses the room temperature, compares it to your set-point, and silently turns the compressor on or off. **Irrigation instrumentation is the exact same idea, but for a field**. Instead of a temperature sensor, we bury **soil moisture sensors**; instead of a thermostat, we use an **irrigation controller**; and instead of cool air flowing through ducts, the actuator is a **solenoid valve** releasing water through drip lines or sprinklers. The "weather report" that your AC app checks is mirrored by an **on-field weather station** computing evapotranspiration. In short: *Sense → Decide → Deliver — automatically.*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are mandated for KTU numerical answers:

- Field Capacity (FC): typically **0.27 – 0.35 m³/m³** for loam soils
- Permanent Wilting Point (PWP): typically **0.10 – 0.17 m³/m³**
- Available Water Capacity (AWC): typically **100 – 170 mm/m** depth
- Standard pan evaporation coefficient (Kp): **0.7 – 0.8** (Class-A pan)
- Crop coefficient (Kc): tabulated by growth stage (FAO-56)
- Net Irrigation Requirement (NIR): expressed in **mm** or **m³/ha**
- Irrigation Efficiency (η): expressed as a **percentage (%)**

## 1.4 Why It Matters in the KTU Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 3 of PEICT753 specifically evaluates a student's ability to **map a physical variable (soil moisture, ET, flow, pressure) to an electronic transducer, justify its selection based on accuracy/response/cost, and explain its integration into an automated loop.** Expect theory + numerical sub-parts in the 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Functional Pillars of Irrigation Instrumentation

### Pillar 1 — Soil Moisture Sensing
Soil water exists in three states relevant to irrigation:
- **Saturation** — all pores filled; occurs briefly after heavy rain/irrigation.
- **Field Capacity (FC)** — water held against gravity, ~24–48 h after saturation.
- **Permanent Wilting Point (PWP)** — water held too tightly for roots to extract (~−1500 kPa matric potential).

The **Readily Available Water (RAW)** between FC and a management-allowed depletion level is what the instrumentation must defend.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensor families used in irrigation:**
> - **Volumetric**: Time-Domain Reflectometry (TDR), Frequency-Domain Reflectometry (FDR/capacitance), neutron probe.
> - **Tensiometric**: Tensiometer, irrometer (measures matric potential in kPa or cbar).
> - **Resistive/Capacitive blocks**: Gypsum blocks, granular matrix sensors (GMS).
> - **Emerging**: Cosmic-ray neutron sensing, remote sensing (SMAP, Sentinel-1 SAR).

### Pillar 2 — Evapotranspiration (ET) Sensing
ET is the atmospheric "thirst" of the field. Two engineering routes exist:
- **Direct**: Weighing Lysimeter (gold standard).
- **Indirect**: Reference ET (ETo) computed from weather data, then multiplied by Kc to get crop ET (ETc).

### Pillar 3 — Hydraulic Sensing
Flow, pressure, and water-quality sensors are installed on the **mainline, sub-main, and lateral** of the irrigation network. Common transducers:
- **Flow**: electromagnetic, ultrasonic (clamp-on), venturi, propeller, paddle-wheel.
- **Pressure**: piezoresistive strain gauge, capacitive.
- **Quality**: EC (electrical conductivity), pH, turbidity probes.

### Pillar 4 — Actuation & Control
- **Solenoid valves** (latch or continuous) — 12/24 V DC, controlled by relay or MOSFET driver.
- **Motorized ball/butterfly valves** — for high-flow mainlines.
- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** on pumps — for variable-rate irrigation.
- **PLC / RTU / IoT gateway** — executes the irrigation decision algorithm.

## 2.2 Decision Logic — How a Controller "Decides" to Irrigate

A typical smart-irrigation algorithm follows this order of operations:

1. **Read** soil moisture sensors placed at 1/3 and 2/3 of effective root depth.
2. **Average** the readings; compare to the **management allowed depletion (MAD)** threshold.
3. **Compute** reference ET (ETo) for the past 24 h using Penman–Monteith or Hargreaves.
4. **Compute** ETc = Kc × ETo.
