Applying Bernoulli's equation between the nozzle inlet (point 1, where velocity ≈ 0 in the pipe) and the jet exit (point 2, in air):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 &= \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the jet discharges into the atmosphere, $P_2 = 0$ (gauge), and assuming $z_1 = z_2$ (no elevation change at the nozzle):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 &= 0 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the theoretical exit velocity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2 &= \sqrt{2gh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual discharge using coefficient of discharge**

The ideal discharge is $q_{ideal} = A \cdot v_2$, but real fluids lose energy due to friction, turbulence, and vena-contracta effects. Hence we apply the discharge coefficient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= C_d \cdot A \cdot v_2 \\[4pt]
q &= C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental nozzle equation** that KTU examiners expect students to derive. Always state the assumption of $z_1 = z_2$ and $P_2 = 0$ (gauge) explicitly to secure full marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Precipitation Rate (Application Rate) of Sprinkler System

**Given:**
- $q$ = discharge of one sprinkler (litres per second, lps)
- $S_l$ = spacing between laterals (m)
- $S_m$ = spacing between sprinklers along the lateral (m)

**Step 1 — Volume of water applied per sprinkler per hour**

Each sprinkler discharges $q$ lps, so the volume per hour is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= q \times 3600 \quad \text{(litres/hour)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Area covered by one sprinkler**

The sprinklers are arranged on a rectangular grid, so the area effectively irrigated by one sprinkler is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sprinkler} &= S_l \times S_m \quad \text{(m²)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of water applied per hour (Precipitation Rate)**

Convert litres to depth in millimetres: $1 \text{ litre} = 1 \text{ mm depth over } 1 \text{ m}^2$ — therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{V}{A_{sprinkler}} \times \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{1 \text{ L/m}^2} \\[4pt]
P &= \frac{q \times 3600}{S_l \times S_m} \quad \text{(mm/hour)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Check:** $q$ is in **lps** (litres per second), $S_l$ and $S_m$ in **metres**, time in seconds converted to hours (×3600). The result is directly in **mm/hr** because 1 litre spread over 1 m² = 1 mm depth.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU)

For a sprinkler system with overlapping patterns, uniformity of water distribution is measured using Christiansen's Coefficient.

**Step 1 — Collect catch-can readings**

Let $x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n$ be the depth of water (mm) collected in $n$ equally spaced catch cans placed within the wetted area.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute mean absolute deviation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MAD &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CU &= \left( 1 - \frac{MAD}{\bar{x}} \right) \times 100 \\[4pt]
CU &= \left( 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{x}} \right) \times 100 \quad (\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A well-designed sprinkler system has **CU ≥ 85%**. A low CU indicates poor overlap, clogged nozzles, or excessive wind drift.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Pump Power Requirement

The power required to drive a centrifugal pump for an overhead irrigation system is:

**Step 1 — Total Discharge Required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= n \cdot q
\end{aligned}
$$

where $n$ = number of sprinklers operating simultaneously.

**Step 2 — Total Dynamic Head (TDH)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TDH &= H_{static} + H_{friction} + H_{fittings} + H_{sprinkler}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Water Power (Pump Output)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{water} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{total} \cdot TDH \quad \text{(W)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Shaft Power (Brake Horse Power)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{shaft} &= \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Electrical Input Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{electric} &= \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{total} \cdot TDH}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practical units:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{electric} \, (kW) &= \frac{Q_{total} \, (L/s) \times TDH \, (m)}{102 \cdot \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **102** comes from the conversion: $1 \text{ kW} = 102 \text{ kg·m/s}$ (i.e., $1000 \text{ W} / 9.81$). Some textbooks use **75** for horsepower and **102** for kW. Use **102** for SI units in KTU exams.

---

### 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example

**Problem:** A sprinkler irrigation system has the following specifications:

- Discharge per sprinkler: $q = 0.5$ lps
- Spacing of sprinklers along lateral: $S_m = 12$ m
- Spacing between laterals: $S_l = 12$ m
- Number of sprinklers operating: $n = 100$

**Find:**
1. Precipitation rate (mm/hr)
2. Total discharge of the system (lps)
3. Area irrigated in 1 hour by one sprinkler (m²·mm)

---

**Solution:**

**(i) Precipitation Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{q \times 3600}{S_l \times S_m} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{0.5 \times 3600}{12 \times 12} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{1800}{144} \\[4pt]
  &= 12.5 \text{ mm/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total System Discharge**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= n \times q \\[4pt]
          &= 100 \times 0.5 \\[4pt]
          &= 50 \text{ lps} = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Volume applied by one sprinkler in 1 hour**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{1\,sprinkler} &= q \times 3600 = 0.5 \times 3600 = 1800 \text{ litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is equivalent to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth over } (12 \times 12) \text{ m}^2 &= \frac{1800 \text{ L}}{144 \text{ m}^2} = 12.5 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The verification in (iii) confirms the dimensional correctness of the formula. Always perform this consistency check in KTU exams to gain the examiner's confidence.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Overhead vs. Surface vs. Drip

| Parameter | Surface Irrigation | Overhead (Sprinkler) | Drip (Micro) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Water Efficiency** | 30–50% | 60–75% | 80–95% |
| **Operating Pressure** | 0 (gravity) | 1–4 bar | 0.5–2 bar |
| **Energy Consumption** | Low | Medium–High | Low |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium–High | High |
| **Suitability (Soil)** | Level, heavy soils | Most soils | All soils |
| **Suitability (Topography)** | Level fields only | Undulating OK | Undulating OK |
| **Evaporation Losses** | Low | High (10–30%) | Very Low |
| **Wind Sensitivity** | None | High | None |
| **Frost Protection** | No | Yes | No |
| **Fertigation Capability** | Difficult | Yes | Yes (excellent) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Overhead Irrigation System Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the functional architecture of a complete overhead (sprinkler) irrigation system, with all instrumentation integration points relevant to **Instrumentation for Agriculture (PEICT753)**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Source: Well / Reservoir / Canal] --> B[Suction Pipe with Foot Valve]
    B --> C[Centrifugal Pump Unit]
    C --> D[Filter Unit: Sand / Screen / Disc]
    D --> E[Fertilizer Injector / Venturi]
    E --> F[Mainline Pipe HDPE / GI]
    F --> G[Pressure Gauge PG1]
    F --> H[Flow Meter FM]
    H --> I[Sub-Main Pipe]
    I --> J[Control Valve CV]
    J --> K[Lateral Pipe]
    K --> L1[Sprinkler Head 1]
    K --> L2[Sprinkler Head 2]
    K --> L3[Sprinkler Head 3]
    K --> L4[Sprinkler Head N]
    G --> M[Pressure Sensor / Transmitter PT]
    H --> N[Flow Transmitter FT]
    M --> O[Controller PLC / RTU]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Soil Moisture Sensor SMS]
    P --> O
    O --> Q[Actuator: Solenoid Valve / VFD Pump Control]
    Q --> C
    O --> R[SCADA / HMI Display]
    R --> S[Operator Decision / Auto Mode]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Instrumentation Note (PEICT753 Specific):** The block diagram shows the integration of **PT (Pressure Transmitter)**, **FT (Flow Transmitter)**, **SMS (Soil Moisture Sensor)**, **PLC/RTU (Controller)**, and **SCADA/HMI** — these are the **key instrumentation elements** in an automated overhead irrigation system.

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Water Path in Sprinkler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pressurized Water in Pipe] --> B[Nozzle Orifice]
    B --> C[High Velocity Jet]
    C --> D[Jet Breakup into Droplets]
    D --> E[Spray Pattern Wetting Circle]
    E --> F[Soil Infiltration to Root Zone]
    F --> G[Crop Uptake / Transpiration]
```

### 4.3 Control Loop Diagram — Automated Overhead Irrigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Setpoint: Target Soil Moisture %] --> CTRL[Controller PLC]
    SMS[Soil Moisture Sensor Feedback] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> CMP{Comparison: Actual vs Setpoint}
    CMP -->|Actual < Setpoint| ON[Pump ON / Solenoid Valve OPEN]
    CMP -->|Actual ≥ Setpoint| OFF[Pump OFF / Valve CLOSED]
    ON --> SPRINKLER[Sprinkler Network Activated]
    OFF --> STOP[System Idle]
    SPRINKLER --> INFIL[Water Infiltrates Soil]
    INFIL --> SMS
```

### 4.4 Component Hierarchy Diagram — Sprinkler Parts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SH[Sprinkler Head Assembly] --> BASE[Base / Body]
    SH --> NOZ[Nozzle: Main + Auxiliary]
    SH --> STEM[Stem Riser]
    SH --> ARM[Impact Arm with Spring]
    SH --> BEARING[Ball Bearing Assembly]
    SH --> SEAL[Seals and Washers]
    NOZ --> OUTFLOW[Water Exit and Atomization]
    ARM --> ROT[Rotation Mechanism]
    ROT --> OUTFLOW
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Follow arrows from input (water source) to output (crop root zone). The **dashed line** from controller back to SMS in the control loop indicates a **feedback signal path**, which is the cornerstone of any automated irrigation instrumentation system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme**, mapped to **Module 3 Learning Outcomes**, and follow the official mark distribution: **Part A (3 marks × 2)** and **Part B (14 marks × 1 choice)**.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define overhead irrigation. List any four advantages of sprinkler irrigation over surface irrigation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Overhead irrigation is a pressurized method of artificial water application in which water is sprayed into the air through small nozzles or sprinkler heads, allowing it to fall on the crop and soil surface in a manner similar to natural rainfall.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Suitable for undulating topography** — does not require leveled fields, unlike surface irrigation.
2. **Higher water use efficiency (60–75%)** compared to surface irrigation (30–50%).
3. **Uniform water distribution** with proper overlap of sprinkler patterns (CU ≥ 85%).
4. **Frost protection and microclimate control** possible by intermittent spraying during cold nights.

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Precipitation Rate? Write the formula and state the field condition that must be satisfied for a sprinkler system to avoid runoff.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Precipitation rate is the rate at which water is applied by the sprinkler system to the soil, expressed as **mm of depth per hour (mm/hr)**.

**Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \frac{q \times 3600}{S_l \times S_m} \quad \text{(mm/hr)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $q$ is sprinkler discharge in lps, $S_l$ and $S_m$ are spacings in metres.

**Field Condition:** The precipitation rate must be **less than the basic infiltration rate** of the soil. If $P > \text{Infiltration Rate}$, surface ponding and runoff will occur.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **components and working principle of a sprinkler irrigation system**. Discuss the **role of a nozzle** in the system. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A sprinkler system has a **discharge of 0.8 lps** per sprinkler, with **spacing of 12 m × 12 m**. Calculate the **(i) precipitation rate**, **(ii) total discharge of a 1-hectare field**, and **(iii) depth of water applied in 6 hours of operation**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Components and Working Principle [7 Marks]**

**Working Principle:** Water is drawn from a source, pressurized by a pump, conveyed through a pipe network, and sprayed into the atmosphere through sprinkler nozzles. The pressurized jet breaks into fine droplets that fall under gravity, wetting the soil and crop uniformly.

**Components (with diagram — refer SECTION 4.1):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Water source | Supplies raw water |
| Pump unit | Provides pressure head |
| Filter | Removes debris to prevent nozzle clogging |
| Mainline / Sub-main | Carries pressurized water |
| Laterals | Distribute water to sprinklers |
| Sprinkler head | Atomizes water into droplets |
| Control valves | Regulate flow zone-wise |
| Fertilizer injector | Enables fertigation |
| Pressure gauge | Monitors operating pressure |
| Flow meter | Measures total water supplied |

**Role of Nozzle:** The nozzle is the **heart of the sprinkler** that converts **pressure energy into kinetic energy**, producing a high-velocity jet that atomizes into droplets. Nozzle diameter and shape directly govern discharge, droplet size, and wetted radius.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $q = 0.8$ lps, $S_l = 12$ m, $S_m = 12$ m, Area $A = 1$ ha $= 10000 \text{ m}^2$, Time $t = 6$ hr.

**(i) Precipitation Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{q \times 3600}{S_l \times S_m} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{0.8 \times 3600}{12 \times 12} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{2880}{144} \\[4pt]
  &= 20 \text{ mm/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Discharge for 1 hectare:**

Number of sprinklers in 1 ha:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{10000}{12 \times 12} = 69.44 \approx 70 \text{ sprinklers}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= n \times q = 70 \times 0.8 = 56 \text{ lps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Depth of water applied in 6 hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= P \times t = 20 \times 6 = 120 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024] (Alternative Choice)`**

**(a)** Explain **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU)** for sprinkler systems. A field test yields the following catch-can readings (in mm): **18, 22, 16, 20, 24, 19, 21, 17, 23, 20**. Calculate the **CU** and comment on the design. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare **overhead irrigation with drip irrigation** in terms of water efficiency, suitability, cost, instrumentation complexity, and typical applications. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient [7 Marks]**

**Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CU = \left( 1 - \frac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{x}} \right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Sum and Mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i &= 18+22+16+20+24+19+21+17+23+20 = 200 \\[4pt]
\bar{x} &= \frac{200}{10} = 20 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Absolute Deviations:**

| $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - 20 \vert$ |
|---|---|
| 18 | 2 |
| 22 | 2 |
| 16 | 4 |
| 20 | 0 |
| 24 | 4 |
| 19 | 1 |
| 21 | 1 |
| 17 | 3 |
| 23 | 3 |
| 20 | 0 |
| **Total** | **20** |

**Step 3 — Calculate CU:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CU &= \left( 1 - \frac{20}{10 \times 20} \right) \times 100 \\[4pt]
   &= \left( 1 - \frac{20}{200} \right) \times 100 \\[4pt]
   &= (1 - 0.1) \times 100 \\[4pt]
   &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Since $CU = 90\% \geq 85\%$, the sprinkler design is **excellent** and meets KTU design standards.

---

**(b) Comparison: Overhead vs. Drip Irrigation [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | Overhead (Sprinkler) | Drip (Micro) |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Efficiency** | 60–75% | 80–95% |
| **Suitability (Topography)** | Undulating OK | Undulating OK |
| **Suitability (Crops)** | Cereals, pulses, vegetables, lawns | Row crops, orchards, vineyards |
| **Capital Cost** | Medium to High | High |
| **Operating Cost** | Medium (energy for pumping) | Low to Medium |
| **Evaporation Loss** | High (10–30%) | Very Low |
| **Instrumentation Complexity** | Moderate (pressure, flow, soil moisture) | High (per-emitter flow monitoring, filters) |
| **Filtration Requirement** | Medium | Very High |
| **Typical Application** | Field crops, orchards, frost control | Horticulture, greenhouses, water-scarce zones |
| **Frost Protection** | Yes | No |
| **Suitability with Wastewater** | Limited | Good |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT LOSE MARKS HERE:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** precipitation rate formula's units. Use $q$ in **lps** and $S_l, S_m$ in **metres** to get $P$ in **mm/hr** directly.
> 2. **Always show the derivation steps** for the nozzle discharge equation — merely writing the formula fetches only 2 of 5 marks; the **derivation from Bernoulli's equation** is mandatory.
> 3. In **CU calculations**, students often forget to take **absolute values** of deviations — this is a **fatal arithmetic error** that drops the answer below 50% and forfeits marks.
> 4. **Avoid mixing up** wetted diameter and wetted radius; $D = 2R$. The wetted circle area is $\pi R^2 = \pi D^2 / 4$, not $\pi D^2$.
> 5. **Mention assumptions** explicitly: $z_1 = z_2$, $P_2 = 0$ (atmospheric gauge), and that $C_d$ accounts for energy losses.
> 6. In **comparison questions**, students often give **one-sided answers**; the KTU examiner rewards **balanced tabulated comparisons** with at least **5–6 differentiating parameters**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 3: Overhead Irrigation**

- **Definition:** Overhead (sprinkler) irrigation = artificial rainfall via pressurized spray through nozzles.
- **System Components:** Pump, mainline, sub-main, laterals, sprinklers, valves, filters, fertigation unit, instrumentation.
- **Working Pressure:** Typically **1–4 bar**; higher pressure → larger wetted radius but more drift losses.
- **Core Equation:** $q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ — derive from Bernoulli's law.
- **Precipitation Rate:** $P = \dfrac{q \times 3600}{S_l \times S_m}$ — must be ≤ soil infiltration rate.
- **Christiansen's CU:** Acceptable ≥ **85%**; excellent ≥ **90%**.
- **Distribution Uniformity (DU):** Lower quarter method; practical design check.
- **Application Efficiency:** 60–75% for sprinkler; 80–95% for drip.
- **Pump Power:** Use constant **102** for kW (SI) or **75** for HP.
- **Hazen-Williams formula** is standard for friction head loss in irrigation pipes.
- **Suitability:** Best for **undulating terrain, sandy soils, field crops, frost control**.
- **Limitations:** High wind reduces uniformity; not ideal for heavy clay or tall crops.
- **Instrumentation Links:** Soil moisture sensor, pressure transmitter, flow meter, RTU/PLC, SCADA — all feed back to automated controller.
- **Difference from drip:** Sprinkler wets **entire field**; drip wets **only root zone** of each plant.
- **Numerical Safety Tip:** Always cross-check by computing depth = volume / area; must match.

> **End of Notes — Module 3: Irrigation Methods: Overhead | PEICT753**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Centre Pivot # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Centre Pivot Irrigation System (CPIS)** is a self-propelled, mechanized, pressurized irrigation assembly in which a **lateral pipeline** — supported by a series of mobile **tower structures** — rotates about a fixed **central pivot point (hub)**. Water is delivered through **sprinkler nozzles** mounted on the lateral, providing uniform water distribution to a circular area of cultivated land. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** > *"A centre pivot is a pressurized irrigation apparatus in which a sprinkler lateral, supported across multiple A-frame towers, rotates around a fixed central water-supply hub, thereby irrigating a circular field with high uniformity and minimal manual labour."* The system is governed by: - **Standard system length:** typically **400 m to 800 m** - **Standard wetted radius:** up to **800 m** - **Common flow rate range:** **15 L/s to 100 L/s** - **Operating pressure:** **1.4 bar to 2.8 bar** (low-pressure CPIS) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Giant Garden Watering Clock"** > > Imagine placing a **hose nozzle on a tall pole at the centre of a circular lawn** and attaching a long rotating arm with spray heads along its length. As the arm sweeps around the pole (like the **minute hand of a clock**), every patch of grass gets watered exactly once per revolution. The pivot point is the "clock's centre," the lateral pipeline is the "minute hand," and the towers are the "rolling feet" that push the arm forward. That is, in essence, a Centre Pivot Irrigation System. **Why this matters for Agriculture:** Centre pivots are the workhorse of modern **large-scale mechanized farming** (especially in the U.S. Great Plains, Israel, Spain, and increasingly in India — Karnataka, Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, and Punjab). They can irrigate **130 to 500+ acres per single unit** with a **labour requirement of less than 1 person per 1000 acres**. ## 1.3 Classification of Centre Pivot Systems | Type | Description | Typical Application | |------|-------------|---------------------| | **Fixed (Permanent) Centre Pivot** | Anchored permanently on a single field | Long-duration single-crop fields | | **Towable / Portable Pivot** | Pivots can be dragged to multiple fields | Multi-field farms | | **Corner / Span Pivot** | Adds an outer swinging arm to irrigate field corners | Square/rectangular fields | | **Linear / Lateral Move** | Lateral moves linearly (technically not a pivot) | Rectangular fields | ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Operating Parameters > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Board Numerical Problems:** > - **Application efficiency:** 85–95 % > - **Christiansen's Coefficient of Uniformity (CU):** ≥ 85 % (acceptable), ≥ 90 % (excellent) > - **Application rate (typical):** 5 mm/hr to 25 mm/hr > - **End-gun radius (optional):** 15 m to 30 m # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Block Architecture A Centre Pivot system is a **closed-loop feedback irrigation machine**. Its operational sequence is: 1. **Water intake** — Pump delivers water from a reservoir/bore-well to the **central pivot hub**. 2. **Pressure regulation** — A **pressure regulator** and **control valves** maintain constant lateral pressure. 3. **Distribution** — Water flows down the **lateral pipeline** (typically **6 5/8″, 8 5/8″, or 10″** diameter steel or aluminium pipe). 4. **Application** — **Sprinkler / spray nozzles** mounted on drop tubes release water at preset flow rates. 5. **Rotation** — Each **drive tower** is powered by an **electric or hydraulic motor** (typically **0.5–1.5 HP** per tower) geared to a wheel. Towers are sequenced at **slow speeds** (full revolution in **12 to 100 hours**). 6. **Alignment control** — An **alignment sensor** (micro-switch, mechanical contactor, or proximity sensor) at each tower keeps the lateral perfectly straight. 7. **Safety & shutdown** — **Pressure switches, tower misalignment sensors, and over-current relays** automatically stop the system in fault conditions. ## 2.2 Core Components — Engineering Inventory | S.No. | Component | Function | Instrumentation Relevance | |------:|-----------|----------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Central Pivot Hub** | Water entry, swivel joint | Pressure gauge, flow meter | | 2 | **Lateral Pipeline** | Water conveyance | Pipe pressure sensors | | 3 | **Span (Bay)** | A 30–65 m section between towers | Structural integrity sensors | | 4 | **Drive Towers (A-frame)** | Movement propulsion | Motor current, wheel rotation encoder | | 5 | **Drive Motor (Electric/Hydraulic)** | Power the wheels | Voltage, current sensors | | 6 | **Drive Train (Gearbox + Wheel)** | Reduce motor speed to wheel rotation | Speed encoder, torque sensor | | 7 | **Sprinkler Nozzles / Spray Heads** | Atomize and distribute water | Soil-moisture feedback | | 8 | **Drop Tubes** | Lower sprinklers closer to crop canopy | — | | 9 | **End Gun (optional)** | Extends wetted radius by 15–30 m | Pressure switch | | 10 | **Control Panel / PLC** | Centralized automation | PLC, HMI, SCADA link | | 11 | **Alignment Sensor** | Maintains lateral straightness | Micro-switch / proximity sensor | | 12 | **Pressure Regulator** | Maintains constant lateral pressure | Pressure transducer | | 13 | **Safety Shutdown System** | Stops operation on fault | Pressure, tilt, voltage sensors | ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Geometry — Circular Field Coverage A single full-rotation pivot covers a **circular area**, leaving **four uncultivable corner zones** in a square/rectangular field. This is the **fundamental geometric limitation** of CPIS: $$\text{Corner loss} = \left( 1 - \frac{\pi r^{2}}{4 r^{2}} \right) \times 100\% \approx 21.46\%$$ > [!NOTE] > For a **square field of side 2r**, the pivot (radius r) leaves **≈ 21.46 % area un-irrigated** in the four corners. This loss is mitigated using **Corner Span / Swing Arm** attachments that fold out only at field corners. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Every formula below is examinable in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the units carefully.** | # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Variables | |---|----------|---------|----------|-----------| | 1 | **Area covered (full circle)** | $A = \pi r^{2}$ | m² | $r$ = wetted radius | | 2 | **Area covered (partial circle, angle θ°)** | $A = \dfrac{\theta}{360}\cdot \pi r^{2}$ | m² | $\theta$ in degrees | | 3 | **Area (annular ring at distance R, width ΔR)** | $A_{ring} = 2\pi R \cdot \Delta R$ | m² | $R$ = distance from pivot | | 4 | **Application rate (depth)** | $d = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{A}$ | mm | $Q$ in L/s, $A$ in m², $t$ in hr | | 5 | **Application rate (intensity)** | $I = \dfrac{3600 \cdot Q}{A}$ | mm/hr | $Q$ in m³/s, $A$ in m² | | 6 | **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient** | $C_{U} = 100\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert d_{i} - \bar{d}\vert}{n \bar{d}}\right)$ | % | $d_i$ = individual catch depth, $\bar{d}$ = mean | | 7 | **System Capacity (per unit area)** | $SC = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | L/s per ha | — | | 8 | **Time for one full revolution** | $T = \dfrac{2\pi r}{v}$ | hr | $v$ = lateral tip speed (m/hr) | | 9 | **Application depth per revolution** | $d_{rev} = \dfrac{Q \cdot T}{A}$ | mm | — | | 10 | **Sprinkler discharge (single nozzle)** | $q = C_{d} A_{n} \sqrt{2 g h}$ | m³/s | $C_d$ = 0.95–0.98, $h$ = nozzle head | | 11 | **Nozzle spacing along lateral** | Equal to **wetted diameter** of one nozzle | m | — | | 12 | **Effective wetted diameter (nozzle)** | $D_{w} \approx 1.35 \cdot d_{nozzle}$ (approx.) | m | $d_{nozzle}$ = nozzle bore | | 13 | **Motor speed at wheel** | $N_{wheel} = \dfrac{60 \cdot v_{tip}}{\pi D_{wheel}}$ | rpm | $D_{wheel}$ = wheel diameter | **Critical KTU Pitfall — Pipe vs. Nozzle Units:** When using the nozzle discharge formula, $h$ must be in **metres of water column** and $A_n$ in **m²**, otherwise the answer will be off by 1–3 orders of magnitude. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility Centre pivots are the **backbone of precision agriculture** in: - **Commercial grain farms** (wheat, maize, soybean in the US Midwest) - **High-value horticultural crops** (potato, onion, alfalfa) - **Drought-prone regions** (Rajasthan, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh under PMKSY schemes) - **Integration with IoT** — modern pivots stream telemetry to cloud dashboards for variable-rate irrigation (VRI) per **1° sector. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Application Depth Per Revolution **Problem Context:** A centre pivot has a wetted radius $r$ = 400 m, discharges a total flow $Q$ = 50 L/s, and the outermost tower advances at $v_{tip}$ = 60 m/hr. Derive the **gross application depth per revolution**. **Step 1 — Area covered by full circle:** $$A = \pi r^{2} = \pi \times (400)^{2} = 502654.82 \ \text{m}^{2} \approx 50.27 \ \text{ha}$$ **Step 2 — Time for one full revolution:** $$T = \frac{2\pi r}{v_{tip}} = \frac{2\pi \times 400}{60}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} T &= \frac{2513.274}{60} \\[2mm] T &= 41.888 \ \text{hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total volume applied per revolution:** Convert $Q$ to m³/hr: $$Q = 50 \ \text{L/s} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 180 \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{hr}$$ Total volume: $$ \begin{aligned} V &= Q \times T \\[2mm] V &= 180 \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{hr} \times 41.888 \ \text{hr} \\[2mm] V &= 7539.85 \ \text{m}^{3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Gross application depth:** $$ \begin{aligned} d_{gross} &= \frac{V}{A} \\[2mm] d_{gross} &= \frac{7539.85 \ \text{m}^{3}}{502654.82 \ \text{m}^{2}} \\[2mm] d_{gross} &= 0.015 \ \text{m} = 15 \ \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** Each full revolution of this pivot applies **≈ 15 mm** of water — a value that matches typical maize evapotranspiration demand in semi-arid climates. **Step 5 — Net application depth (accounting for efficiency):** Assuming application efficiency $\eta = 90\%$: $$d_{net} = d_{gross} \times \eta = 15 \times 0.9 = 13.5 \ \text{mm}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU) **Problem Context:** Four catch-cans placed along the lateral radius of a pivot collect the following depths (in mm) during a test run: **18, 20, 22, 16**. Compute $C_U$. **Step 1 — Mean depth:** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{d} &= \frac{d_{1} + d_{2} + d_{3} + d_{4}}{n} \\[2mm] \bar{d} &= \frac{18 + 20 + 22 + 16}{4} \\[2mm] \bar{d} &= \frac{76}{4} = 19 \ \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Absolute deviations from mean:** | Can $i$ | $d_i$ (mm) | $\vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert$ | |:-------:|:----------:|:--------------------------:| | 1 | 18 | $\vert 18 - 19 \vert = 1$ | | 2 | 20 | $\vert 20 - 19 \vert = 1$ | | 3 | 22 | $\vert 22 - 19 \vert = 3$ | | 4 | 16 | $\vert 16 - 19 \vert = 3$ | | **Sum** | — | **8** | **Step 3 — Substitute into CU formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{U} &= 100 \left( 1 - \frac{\sum \vert d_{i} - \bar{d} \vert}{n \bar{d}} \right) \\[2mm] C_{U} &= 100 \left( 1 - \frac{8}{4 \times 19} \right) \\[2mm] C_{U} &= 100 \left( 1 - \frac{8}{76} \right) \\[2mm] C_{U} &= 100 \times (1 - 0.10526) \\[2mm] C_{U} &= 100 \times 0.89474 \\[2mm] C_{U} &= 89.47 \ \% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** $C_U = 89.47\% > 85\%$ → the system is rated **ACCEPTABLE** by KTU / ASAE standards. Anything below 85% indicates poor nozzle sizing or pressure regulation. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lateral Tip Speed for a Target Application Depth **Problem Context:** A 400 m radius pivot must deliver a **net depth of 12 mm per pass**. Pump flow $Q$ = 50 L/s, efficiency $\eta$ = 90 %. Find the required lateral tip speed. **Step 1 — Area:** $$A = \pi r^{2} = \pi \times 160000 = 502654.82 \ \text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Required gross volume:** $$V = \frac{d_{net} \times A}{\eta} = \frac{0.012 \times 502654.82}{0.90} = 6702.06 \ \text{m}^{3}$$ **Step 3 — Time for one revolution:** $$T = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{6702.06 \ \text{m}^{3}}{180 \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{hr}} = 37.23 \ \text{hr}$$ **Step 4 — Required tip speed:** $$v_{tip} = \frac{2\pi r}{T} = \frac{2\pi \times 400}{37.23} = 67.51 \ \text{m/hr}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor ↔ Component Mapping | Sensor / Instrument | Mounted On | Purpose | Failure Consequence | |---------------------|-----------|---------|---------------------| | **Pressure Transducer (0–10 bar)** | Lateral at 3 locations | Monitors line pressure | Pressure drop → under-watered crops | | **Flow Meter (Electromagnetic)** | At pivot hub | Measures total Q | Over/under irrigation | | **Proximity / Micro-switch** | At each tower joint | Alignment of spans | Lateral kinks and collapse | | **Soil Moisture Sensor (TDR / Capacitive)** | At field | Real-time feedback loop | Wrong irrigation timing | | **Anemometer** | Top of pivot tower | Shut off in high wind (> 25 km/h) | Wind drift, evaporation loss | | **Tilt / Inclinometer** | End tower | Detects slope instability | System rollover | | **Current Sensor (CT)** | Motor circuit | Detects motor stall | Tower stuck, no rotation | | **Temperature Sensor (RTD)** | Motor housing | Thermal protection | Motor burnout | | **Rain Gauge (Tipping Bucket)** | Near pivot | Rain shutoff | Wasting water | | **GPS / RTK-GPS** | End tower | Precision sector control (VRI) | Non-uniform application | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid Flow Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Water Source
Borewell or Reservoir] --> B[Pump Station
with VFD] B --> C[Pressure Regulator
and Sand Filter] C --> D[Central Pivot Hub
Swivel Joint] D --> E1[Span 1
Lateral Pipe 6.6 inch] D --> E2[Span 2
Lateral Pipe 6.6 inch] D --> E3[Span 3
Lateral Pipe 5.5 inch] D --> E4[Span N
End Span with End Gun] E1 --> F1[Tower 1
Drive Motor 0.75 HP] E2 --> F2[Tower 2
Drive Motor 0.75 HP] E3 --> F3[Tower 3
Drive Motor 1.0 HP] E4 --> F4[End Tower
Drive Motor 1.5 HP] E1 --> G1[Sprinkler Nozzles
Drop Tube Assembly] E2 --> G2[Sprinkler Nozzles
Drop Tube Assembly] E3 --> G3[Sprinkler Nozzles
Drop Tube Assembly] E4 --> G4[End Gun
High Pressure] F1 --> H[Alignment Sensor
Micro Switch] F2 --> H F3 --> H F4 --> H H --> I[PLC Control Panel
and HMI] B --> I I --> J[SCADA / Cloud
Telemetry Link] K[Soil Moisture Sensor] --> I L[Weather Station] --> I ``` ## 4.2 Operational State Machine — Tower Alignment & Safety Logic ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> IDLE IDLE --> START : Operator command via HMI START --> PRESSURE_CHECK : Pump ON PRESSURE_CHECK --> ROTATING : Pressure within 1.4 to 2.8 bar PRESSURE_CHECK --> ALARM : Pressure out of range ROTATING --> ROTATING : Towers aligned, all sensors OK ROTATING --> MISALIGN : Alignment sensor triggered MISALIGN --> ROTATING : Auto correction within 30 s MISALIGN --> SHUTDOWN : Misalignment persists 60 s ROTATING --> SHUTDOWN : Overcurrent, tilt, or wind alarm SHUTDOWN --> IDLE : Manual reset after fault cleared ALARM --> SHUTDOWN : Pressure fault unresolved SHUTDOWN --> [*] : Maintenance mode ``` ## 4.3 Sensor-to-Actuator Feedback Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Soil Moisture Sensor TDR] --> SC[PLC / SCADA Controller] S2[Pressure Transducer] --> SC S3[Flow Meter] --> SC S4[Rain Gauge] --> SC S5[Wind Anemometer] --> SC SC --> DEC{Decision Logic
Compare to Setpoint} DEC -->|Below Threshold| A1[Open Solenoid Valves
Increase Flow] DEC -->|Above Threshold| A2[Close Valves
Halt Rotation] A1 --> M[Drive Motor on Tower] A2 --> M M --> F[Field Sprinkler Output] F --> S1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note on Diagrams:** Since Centre Pivot systems involve physical hardware (pipes, sprinklers, wheels, towers), the diagrams above model the **functional architecture and feedback logic** rather than the physical geometry. The student should mentally overlay these blocks onto a **top-view map of a circular field** for board-exam answers. ## 4.4 Span Layout (Top-View Schematic) ``` End Gun | Span N ---- O (End Tower - 1.5 HP) Span N-1 -- O Span 2 ---- O (Tower 2) Span 1 ---- O (Tower 1) | [ PIVOT HUB ] <-- Water + Power Inlet | Span 1 ---- O Span 2 ---- O Span N-1 -- O Span N ---- O | End Gun ``` *Each `O` = a drive tower; the lateral is symmetric about the pivot hub. The end gun extends coverage beyond the last tower.* # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** **Define a Centre Pivot Irrigation System. List any three advantages over surface flood irrigation.** **Model Answer:** A Centre Pivot Irrigation System is a self-propelled, mechanized sprinkler irrigation system in which a lateral pipeline supported on a series of A-frame towers rotates about a fixed central pivot hub, distributing water uniformly to a circular cropped area. **Three advantages over surface flood irrigation:** 1. **Higher water-use efficiency** — 85–95 % application efficiency versus 30–50 % in flood irrigation. 2. **Labour saving** — One operator can manage 500+ acres; flood irrigation needs continuous manual supervision. 3. **Suitability for undulating/sloping terrain** — No land levelling required, whereas flood irrigation demands precise grading. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **State Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU) formula. What is the minimum acceptable value of CU for a well-designed sprinkler system?** **Model Answer:** $$C_{U} = 100 \left( 1 - \frac{\sum \vert d_{i} - \bar{d} \vert}{n \bar{d}} \right)$$ where $d_i$ = individual catch-can depth, $\bar{d}$ = mean depth, $n$ = number of observations. **Minimum acceptable $C_U$ = 85 %** (values above 90 % are considered excellent per ASAE standards adopted in KTU evaluation). --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **main components and working of a Centre Pivot Irrigation System**. Discuss the role of the **alignment sensor** and **pressure regulator** in its safe operation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Centre Pivot has a **wetted radius of 400 m**, a **total discharge of 50 L/s**, and an **outermost tower tip speed of 60 m/hr**. Calculate: 1. The **area irrigated** in hectares. 2. The **gross application depth per revolution** in mm. 3. The **net application depth** if application efficiency is 90 %. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution — Part (a):** A Centre Pivot system consists of the following major sub-systems, organized as a **centralized hub with a radiating lateral pipeline**: **1. Water Supply & Hub Subsystem:** The pump station delivers water through a buried mainline to the **central pivot hub**, which contains a **swivel joint** (multi-channel rotary seal) that allows the lateral to rotate continuously without water leakage. A **pressure regulator** downstream of the hub maintains a constant lateral pressure of typically **1.4–2.8 bar**, irrespective of flow demand. **Instrumentation:** Pressure gauge, pressure transducer, and main shut-off valve are mounted at the hub. **2. Lateral Pipeline Subsystem:** The lateral is constructed of **galvanized steel or aluminium pipes** (commonly 168 mm / 219 mm outer diameter) bolted in spans of 30–65 m. Pipe diameter reduces with distance from the hub to maintain nearly constant flow velocity, thereby reducing friction losses and ensuring uniform nozzle pressure. **Instrumentation:** Pressure sensors at 1/3, 2/3, and end of the lateral. **3. Tower & Drive Subsystem:** Each span is supported by an **A-frame tower** running on rubber tyres (typical diameter 1.1 m). Each tower carries a **gearmotor** (electric 480 V three-phase, 0.5–1.5 HP) that drives the wheels at 0.5–3 rpm. Towers are spaced 30–65 m apart. **Instrumentation:** Current sensor (CT) on each motor, wheel-rotation encoder. **4. Sprinkler / Spray Head Subsystem:** Water is distributed through **impact sprinklers, rotary sprinklers, or fixed spray heads** on **drop tubes** suspended 1–3 m below the lateral. Sprinkler spacing along the lateral equals the **wetted diameter** of a single nozzle. **Instrumentation:** Soil-moisture sensors in the wetted footprint. **5. Control & Safety Subsystem:** A **PLC** (Programmable Logic Controller) housed in the panel at the pivot hub receives inputs from the alignment, pressure, flow, and current sensors. The PLC drives the contactors that energize the tower motors. **Instrumentation:** HMI touchscreen, SCADA link. **Role of the Alignment Sensor:** The **alignment sensor** (typically a **micro-switch with a cam follower**, or a **proximity sensor with a target plate**) is mounted on the **outboard side of every tower except the outermost one**. It monitors the **angle between adjacent spans**: - If a leading tower gets ahead, its switch closes, signalling the lagging tower to **energize its motor** to catch up. - If a tower lags excessively (kink in the lateral), the system triggers a **safety shutdown** to prevent structural failure. **Role of the Pressure Regulator:** The **pressure regulator** is a **spring-loaded diaphragm valve** that holds the downstream (lateral) pressure constant within ±5 % of the setpoint, despite upstream pump fluctuations. This is **critical because sprinkler discharge depends on the square root of nozzle head** — a 10 % drop in pressure causes ~5 % drop in discharge, destroying uniformity. **Working Principle:** Water enters the hub → flows down the lateral → emerges through sprinklers as the lateral rotates slowly (one revolution in 12–100 hours depending on desired depth). The system is **fully automated**; once started, it stops only at the set point, on fault, or on rain detection. ### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Given:** - $r = 400$ m - $Q = 50$ L/s $= 180$ m³/hr - $v_{tip} = 60$ m/hr - $\eta = 90\%$ **1. Area irrigated:** $$A = \pi r^{2} = \pi \times (400)^{2} = 502654.82 \ \text{m}^{2}$$ Converting to hectares (1 ha = 10000 m²): $$A = \frac{502654.82}{10000} = 50.27 \ \text{ha}$$ **2. Time for one revolution:** $$T = \frac{2\pi r}{v_{tip}} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 400}{60} = \frac{2513.27}{60} = 41.89 \ \text{hr}$$ **3. Gross application depth per revolution:** $$d_{gross} = \frac{Q \times T}{A} = \frac{180 \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{hr} \times 41.89 \ \text{hr}}{502654.82 \ \text{m}^{2}}$$ $$d_{gross} = \frac{7540.18}{502654.82} = 0.01500 \ \text{m} = 15.00 \ \text{mm}$$ **4. Net application depth:** $$d_{net} = d_{gross} \times \eta = 15.00 \times 0.90 = 13.50 \ \text{mm}$$ | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Area irrigated | **50.27 ha** | | Time per revolution | **41.89 hr** | | Gross depth | **15.00 mm** | | Net depth | **13.50 mm** | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU)**. Four catch-cans placed along the lateral of a centre pivot collect depths of **18, 20, 22, and 16 mm**. Calculate $C_U$ and comment on the uniformity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss **Variable Rate Irrigation (VRI)** as a precision-agriculture upgrade to conventional centre pivot systems. List the **sensors and actuators** involved. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution — Part (a):** (Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.2** of these notes.) **Result:** $C_U = 89.47\%$ **Comment:** Since $C_U > 85\%$, the system is rated **ACCEPTABLE** by KTU / ASAE standards. To improve uniformity, one should: - Verify that nozzle pressure at each can is within ±10 % of design pressure. - Inspect for clogged nozzles. - Check end-gun pressure if the lowest depth occurs at the outer radius. ### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Variable Rate Irrigation (VRI):** VRI is a precision-agriculture technique in which a centre pivot applies **different depths of water to different sectors (or even sub-zones) of a circular field**, based on **prescription maps** derived from soil, crop, and topography data. **Two Implementation Strategies:** 1. **Sector Control VRI** — uses **GPS-position-aware control valves** to switch sectors ON/OFF, typically dividing the field into 1°–10° pie slices. (Up to 360 sectors possible.) 2. **Zone Control VRI (Speed Control)** — varies the **lateral rotation speed** zone-wise; faster rotation = less water per zone. **Sensors and Actuators Involved:** | Type | Devices | |------|---------| | **Position Sensors** | GPS receiver, RTK-GPS for cm-level accuracy, rotary encoder on the pivot hub | | **Field Mapping Sensors** | Soil-moisture sensors (TDR / capacitive), EC sensors, soil temperature probes | | **Remote Sensing Inputs** | Satellite NDVI / drone multispectral imagery | | **Weather Sensors** | Rain gauge, anemometer, temperature & humidity probe | | **Actuators** | Solenoid valves per sector, VFD on drive motor, individual sprinkler shut-off valves | | **Decision System** | PLC / industrial PC running VRI prescription software, SCADA / cloud link | **KTU Exam Tip:** When answering VRI questions, always emphasize the **GPS-based sector identification** as the key enabler — without knowing *where* the lateral is on the field, sector control is impossible. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Centre Pivot Problems:** > > 1. **Unit mismatch in flow rate:** Many students plug $Q$ in L/s directly into a formula requiring m³/s. This produces answers off by a factor of 1000. **Always convert.** > 2. **Forgetting to apply application efficiency:** The question often asks for **net** depth, but students compute only gross. Both must appear in the answer key. > 3. **Wrong formula for partial circle:** $A = (\theta / 360) \cdot \pi r^{2}$, NOT $A = \pi r^{2}$ when $\theta < 360°$. > 4. **Confusing Christiansen's CU with Distribution Uniformity (DU):** $C_U \ne DU$. $DU = 100 \cdot d_{25\%} / \bar{d}$ (lower quarter ratio). $C_U$ uses **mean absolute deviation**. > 5. **Skipping the "Comment" in CU problems:** A computed value of $C_U$ must always be followed by a sentence interpreting it (acceptable / excellent / poor). > 6. **Diagram mistakes:** Drawing the pivot as a stationary sprinkler instead of a **rotating lateral with multiple towers**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin to your wall before the exam):** > > - **Definition:** A Centre Pivot is a rotating lateral-pipeline sprinkler system pivoting about a fixed hub. > - **Geometry:** Wetted area is a circle; **square-field corner loss ≈ 21.46 %** (mitigated by corner spans). > - **Standard dimensions:** Radius 200–800 m; pipe diameters 6 5/8″–10″; towers spaced 30–65 m. > - **Operating pressure:** 1.4–2.8 bar (low-pressure modern systems); higher pressure for impact sprinklers. > - **Key formula 1:** $A = \pi r^{2}$ (or $(\theta/360)\pi r^{2}$ for partial). > - **Key formula 2:** $d = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{A}$ — gross application depth (watch units!). > - **Key formula 3:** $T = \dfrac{2\pi r}{v_{tip}}$ — revolution time. > - **Key formula 4:** $C_U = 100\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert}{n \bar{d}}\right)$ — minimum acceptable value 85 %. > - **Components to memorize for diagram questions:** Hub, lateral, spans, A-frame towers, gear motors, drive wheels, alignment sensors, sprinklers, drop tubes, end gun, control panel / PLC. > - **Instrumentation emphasis:** Pressure transducer, flow meter, soil-moisture sensor, alignment switch, anemometer, GPS — all are KTU-favoured sensor topics. > - **Modern extensions:** VRI (Sector Control & Speed Control), SCADA/cloud telemetry, integration with IoT dashboards. > - **Comparison hooks:** Always compare CPIS with **drip** (higher efficiency, higher cost) and **flood** (lower efficiency, lower cost) — board questions often ask comparative answers. > - **Nozzle hydraulics:** $q = C_d A_n \sqrt{2gh}$ — remember $h$ in metres of water column.

topics.title: Lateral Move
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lateral Move

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Lateral Move (or Linear Move) Irrigation System** is a self-propelled, continuously-moving sprinkler irrigation machine in which the **main pipeline (lateral)** is carried on a series of aligned towers that travel in a straight path perpendicular to the lateral's length. The system irrigates rectangular fields by progressing in discrete, parallel "runs" until the entire area is covered, with water being delivered either from a **pressurized hose** (hose-fed) or a **concrete-lined open channel/canal** (canal-fed) running along one field edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Lateral Move systems fall under the classification of **Mechanized Sprinkler Irrigation Systems** and are studied alongside Center Pivot and Traveling Gun systems. The KTU examiner expects a clear comparison of *drive mechanism*, *water source*, *field shape suitability*, and *guidance systems*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Moving Watering Trolley"**
>
> Imagine a long horizontal water pipe with shower heads attached, mounted on a row of wheeled trolleys (called **towers**). This pipe is connected to a huge garden hose that supplies pressurized water. Now, instead of dragging the entire pipe across the field by hand, each trolley has its own small electric motor and wheels. All the trolleys walk in a perfectly straight line, at exactly the same speed, like a synchronized marching band. As they walk, they spray water from the shower heads. When they reach the end of the field, the operator disconnects the hose, shifts the entire system sideways by one lane-width, and begins a new pass — just like mowing a lawn in straight strips.
>
> *The "lateral" is the pipe + towers; the "move" is the synchronized lateral travel.*

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Mandated)

| Term | Definition | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Lateral Pipe** | Main water-carrying pipeline supported on multiple towers | — |
| **Tower** | A-frame mobile structure with drive wheels and motor | — |
| **Span** | Pipe section between two adjacent towers | L (m) |
| **Tower Drive** | Geared motor unit that propels the wheel | — |
| **Hose-Drag (Drag Hose)** | Flexible hose connecting lateral to hydrant | — |
| **Guide Cable / Furrow** | Physical alignment reference on field | — |
| **End Gun** | Large sprinkler at far end for extended coverage | — |
| **Stiffener / Truss** | Triangulated bracing between pipe and cable | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlights:**
> - Typical travel speed: **0.5 to 3 m/min**
> - Typical span length: **30 to 50 m**
> - Typical wetted nozzle pressure: **100 to 400 kPa (10–40 m head)**
> - System length: **100 m to 800+ m**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "How" Behind the System

The Lateral Move system operates on a deceptively simple principle: **continuous synchronized translation** of a long lateral pipeline across the field at a controlled rate while discharging water through closely-spaced sprinklers.

**Step 1 — Pressurized Water Supply**
Water enters the lateral either:
- (a) Through a **flexible drag hose** from a hydrant on the field edge (hose-fed), OR
- (b) Through a **scoop/diverting pipe** that dips into an open canal running parallel to the direction of travel (canal-fed).

**Step 2 — Distribution Along the Lateral**
The pipe is a **closed conduit under pressure** (typically 2.5–4.0 bar at the inlet). Sprinkler nozzles are spaced at regular intervals (typically 3–6 m) along the lateral so that overlapping wetted patterns create **uniform application**.

**Step 3 — Synchronized Tower Movement**
Each tower is independently driven. A **micro-switch or alignment sensor** at every span detects the angle between adjacent spans. When the lateral bends, the trailing tower is commanded to "catch up," restoring straightness. This is the heart of the **automatic alignment system**.

**Step 4 — Forward Travel & Irrigation**
The lateral advances at a constant rate $v$. As it moves, every point on the field receives water for a **dwell time** $t = W / v$, where $W$ is the wetted width of a single nozzle.

**Step 5 — Field Coverage (Multiple Passes)**
Because the lateral only covers one "strip" per pass, the system must be repositioned (laterally shifted by strip width $W$) and a new pass started. This continues until the rectangular field is fully irrigated.

### 2.2 Why This Works — The Engineering Rationale

- **Constant Speed** = **Constant Application Depth**: Because each point of soil receives water for a fixed duration, the depth applied is mathematically deterministic.
- **Synchronization** = **Mechanical Integrity**: A 400-m lateral pipe full of water, if not perfectly aligned, would snap. Hence alignment control is critical.
- **Pressure Regulation**: A **pressure regulator valve** at each nozzle ensures spray pattern uniformity regardless of position along the lateral.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{A}$ | Gross application depth (where $Q$ is discharge, $t$ is time, $A$ is area) | mm |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{W \cdot L}$ | Travel speed = total flow ÷ (wetted width × lateral length) | m/min |
| 3 | $t_{dwell} = \dfrac{W}{v}$ | Dwell time of lateral over a point | min |
| 4 | $d = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{v \cdot L}$ | Net application depth (along travel direction) | mm |
| 5 | $CU = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{x}}\right) \times 100$ | **Christiansen's Coefficient of Uniformity** | % |
| 6 | $H_{f} = K \cdot \dfrac{L \cdot Q^{1.75}}{D^{4.75}}$ | Hazen-Williams head loss in lateral | m |
| 7 | $P_{req} = H_{f} + h_{nozzle} + h_{elev} + h_{friction,hose}$ | Required pump pressure | m head |
| 8 | $A_{field} = L_{lateral} \times n_{passes} \times W$ | Total field area covered | m² |
| 9 | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{A_{field}}{v \cdot L_{lateral}}$ | Time per irrigation cycle | hours |
| 10 | $E_{a} = \dfrac{d_{stored}}{d_{applied}} \times 100$ | Application efficiency (typical 75–85%) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Formula 5 (Christiansen's CU) is the single most-tested formula under "uniformity" questions. Memorize it as written, with the sum of **absolute deviations** in the numerator (no negative signs). Note the use of `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown tables.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Lateral Move systems are deployed globally on:
- **Center-pivot-incompatible rectangular fields** (where a center pivot would leave ~21% corners un-irrigated)
- **Large-scale commercial farms** (e.g., US Midwest, Australian wheat belt, Indian sugarcane estates)
- **High-value crops** requiring precise water control (potato, onion, alfalfa)
- **Fields with shallow water tables** where canal supply is feasible

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Net Application Depth from First Principles

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the net depth of water applied by a continuous-move lateral system at a single point.

**Step 1 — Define the problem geometrically.**
Consider a single sprinkler nozzle on the lateral, projecting water over a circular wetted area of diameter $W$. As the lateral moves forward at speed $v$, every point under the lateral's path experiences sprinkler coverage once.

**Step 2 — Express the time a point is irrigated.**
The lateral takes time $t$ to traverse a distance equal to the wetted diameter $W$.

$$
t = \frac{W}{v}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the volume delivered.**
The single nozzle discharges at flow rate $q$ (m³/s). In time $t$, it delivers:

$$
V = q \cdot t = q \cdot \frac{W}{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the area irrigated by that nozzle.**
The nozzle wets a strip of width $W$ and length $L$ (the lateral's length). Per unit lateral length, the wetted area per nozzle is $W \times 1$ m = $W$ m². So the volume per unit length is:

$$
\frac{V}{L_{span}} = \frac{q \cdot W}{v \cdot L_{span}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert volume per area to depth.**

$$
d = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{q \cdot W / v}{W \cdot 1} = \frac{q}{v \cdot 1}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalize for the full lateral.**
For the entire lateral of length $L$ with $N$ nozzles each discharging $q_i$, the **total system flow** is $Q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} q_i$. The total application depth is:

$$
\boxed{d = \frac{Q_{total}}{v \cdot L}}
$$

> This is the **master equation** for continuous-move systems. It applies identically to lateral move AND center pivot systems (with $L$ replaced by wetted arc length for the latter).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Travel Speed for a Target Application Depth

**Given:** A lateral move system of length $L = 400$ m with total flow $Q_{total} = 25$ L/s. The target net application depth is $d = 12$ mm. Find the required travel speed $v$.

**Step 1 — Convert units to SI.**

$$
Q_{total} = 25 \text{ L/s} = 0.025 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad d = 0.012 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master equation, solving for $v$.**

$$
d = \frac{Q_{total}}{v \cdot L} \implies v = \frac{Q_{total}}{d \cdot L}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
v = \frac{0.025}{0.012 \times 400} = \frac{0.025}{4.8}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$
\boxed{v = 0.00521 \text{ m/s} = 31.25 \text{ mm/s} = 1.875 \text{ m/min}}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check (KTU standard).**
Typical lateral move speeds are **0.5–3 m/min**, so 1.875 m/min is realistic. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Irrigation Cycle Time for a Rectangular Field

**Given:** Field dimensions 400 m × 800 m. Lateral length = 400 m. Wetted width $W = 50$ m. Travel speed $v = 1.875$ m/min. Find the time to irrigate the entire field.

**Step 1 — Compute number of passes.**

$$
n_{passes} = \frac{\text{Field width}}{W} = \frac{800}{50} = 16 \text{ passes}
$$

**Step 2 — Time per pass.**

$$
t_{pass} = \frac{L_{lateral}}{v} = \frac{400}{1.875} = 213.33 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Time between passes (repositioning).**
Assume $t_{repos} = 30$ min (15 passes require repositioning).

$$
t_{repos,total} = 15 \times 30 = 450 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Total cycle time.**

$$
T_{cycle} = (16 \times 213.33) + 450 = 3413.33 + 450 = 3863.33 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{cycle} \approx 64.4 \text{ hours} \approx 2.7 \text{ days}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Lateral Move vs. Center Pivot

| Parameter | Lateral Move | Center Pivot |
|---|---|---|
| **Field shape** | Rectangular (best fit) | Square / circular |
| **Corner coverage** | Better (with end gun + corner arm) | Poor (~21% corners missed) |
| **Water source** | Hose-fed OR canal-fed | Single central well/pipe |
| **Drive system** | All towers driven | All towers driven |
| **Alignment** | Cable/furrow guide | N/A (rotational symmetry) |
| **Land leveling** | Required (for canal-fed) | Not required |
| **Capital cost** | Higher (per ha) | Lower |
| **Labor** | Higher (repositioning) | Lower (continuous rotation) |
| **Application uniformity** | 80–92% | 80–90% |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the comparison matters:** The KTU board almost always asks "Compare Lateral Move with Center Pivot" for 7-mark questions. Memorize this matrix.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** A hose-fed lateral move system has 10 spans, each 40 m long, with nozzle spacing 4 m. Each nozzle discharges 0.4 L/s. The system moves at 2 m/min. Calculate:
(i) Total system length
(ii) Total discharge
(iii) Wetted width (assume nozzle radius = 6 m)
(iv) Net application depth
(v) Time to complete one pass

**Solution:**

**(i) Total length:**

$$
L = 10 \times 40 = 400 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Total discharge:**

$$
N_{nozzles} = \frac{L}{spacing} = \frac{400}{4} = 100
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 100 \times 0.4 = 40 \text{ L/s} = 0.040 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Wetted width:**

$$
W = 2 \times 6 = 12 \text{ m}
$$

**(iv) Net application depth:**
Convert $v$ to m/s: $v = 2/60 = 0.0333$ m/s.

$$
d = \frac{Q_{total}}{v \cdot L} = \frac{0.040}{0.0333 \times 400} = \frac{0.040}{13.333} = 0.003 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ mm}
$$

**(v) Time per pass:**

$$
t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \text{ min} \approx 3.33 \text{ hours}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Field-Level Block Architecture of a Lateral Move System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Water Source / Pump Station] --> B[Pressurized Mainline]
    B --> C[Drag Hose Connection]
    C --> D[Tower 1 - Drive Unit]
    D --> E[Tower 2 - Drive Unit]
    E --> F[Tower 3 - Drive Unit]
    F --> G[Tower N - Drive Unit]
    G --> H[End Gun - Corner Coverage]
    D -.sprinklers.-> I[Sprinkler Bank 1]
    E -.sprinklers.-> J[Sprinkler Bank 2]
    F -.sprinklers.-> K[Sprinkler Bank 3]
    G -.sprinklers.-> L[Sprinkler Bank N]
    M[Guide Cable / Furrow Sensor] --> D
    M --> E
    M --> F
    M --> G
    N[Central Control Panel] --> D
    N --> E
    N --> F
    N --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The lateral is a sequence of towers (D → E → F → G) connected by pipe spans. Each tower has its own drive motor and alignment sensor. A central control panel (N) coordinates all towers based on feedback from the guide cable sensor (M). The drag hose (C) supplies pressurized water from a fixed pump station.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Functional Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph HYD[Hydraulic Subsystem]
        HYD1[Canal / Pump] --> HYD2[Filtration Unit] --> HYD3[Drag Hose] --> HYD4[Inlet Manifold]
    end
    subgraph STR[Structural Subsystem]
        STR1[Tower A-Frames] --> STR2[Trussed Pipe Spans] --> STR3[Wheel Bogies]
    end
    subgraph DRV[Drive & Alignment Subsystem]
        DRV1[Electric / Hydraulic Motor] --> DRV2[Gearbox] --> DRV3[Drive Wheels]
        ALN1[Span Angle Sensor] --> ALN2[Control Logic] --> DRV1
    end
    subgraph CTR[Control & Telemetry Subsystem]
        CTR1[Central Controller] --> CTR2[Speed Setpoint] --> DRV1
        CTR1 --> CTR3[Pressure Regulator] --> HYD4
        CTR1 --> CTR4[End Gun Actuator] --> STR2
    end
    HYD4 --> STR2
    STR2 --> DRV3
    DRV1 --> ALN2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Reading:** The system is a closed-loop mechatronic system. The **Hydraulic Subsystem** delivers water; the **Structural Subsystem** provides mechanical support; the **Drive & Alignment Subsystem** ensures straight travel via span-angle feedback; the **Control & Telemetry Subsystem** ties everything together with setpoints and pressure regulation.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Alignment Control Logic (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Aligning: Operator starts lateral
    Aligning --> Driving: All spans within ±0.5 deg
    Driving --> Aligning: Span angle exceeds limit
    Driving --> EndOfRun: Lateral reaches field edge
    EndOfRun --> Repositioning: Operator disconnects hose
    Repositioning --> Idle: Hose reconnected, new pass ready
    Aligning --> Fault: Sensor or motor fault
    Driving --> Fault: Motor stall / overcurrent
    Fault --> Idle: Manual reset
```

> [!NOTE]
> **State Machine Reading:** This is the operational lifecycle of a lateral move system. The system constantly toggles between `Aligning` and `Driving` modes during a pass (called "creep-align cycle"). A `Fault` state halts the machine and requires operator intervention.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Define a **Lateral Move Irrigation System**. Mention any two situations where it is preferred over a center pivot system.

**Model Answer:**
A **Lateral Move (Linear Move) Irrigation System** is a self-propelled sprinkler irrigation machine in which the main lateral pipeline is mounted on multiple aligned towers that travel in a straight line across a rectangular field, applying water through a series of evenly-spaced nozzles.

It is preferred over a center pivot when:
1. **The field is rectangular** (length >> width), where a center pivot would waste up to 21% of land in corners.
2. **An open canal runs along one edge** of the field, enabling canal-fed (lower-cost) operation.
3. **High uniformity is required** in high-value crops where corner coverage is critical.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
State the formula for **net application depth** of a lateral move system and explain the significance of **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU)**.

**Model Answer:**

$$
d = \frac{Q_{total}}{v \cdot L}
$$

where $Q_{total}$ is total system discharge, $v$ is travel speed, and $L$ is lateral length.

**Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient** quantifies how evenly water is distributed across the irrigated area:

$$
CU = \left(1 - \frac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{x}}\right) \times 100
$$

A CU of **85% or higher** is considered acceptable for most crops. Below 75%, the system needs maintenance (clogged nozzles, pressure loss, mis-alignment).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **components and working** of a hose-fed lateral move irrigation system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **automatic alignment mechanism** in detail.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A lateral move system of length **450 m** discharges a total of **30 L/s** uniformly along its length. If the system travels at **1.5 m/min**, calculate:
(i) Net application depth.
(ii) Time to complete one pass across the field.
(iii) Dwell time of the system over any point (assume wetted width = 15 m).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Components of a Hose-Fed Lateral Move System:**

1. **Water Source and Pump Station** — supplies pressurized water (2.5–4.0 bar).
2. **Drag Hose** — flexible, large-diameter hose (typically 100–150 mm dia) connecting the hydrant to the moving lateral.
3. **Lateral Pipeline** — the main pipe (aluminum or galvanized steel) supported on multiple towers.
4. **Towers / Drive Units** — A-frame structures with motors, gearboxes, and rubber-tired wheels.
5. **Sprinkler Nozzles** — fixed or rotating impact sprinklers at 3–6 m spacing.
6. **Pressure Regulators** — at each nozzle to maintain uniform discharge.
7. **End Gun** — large-radius sprinkler at the far end for extended coverage.
8. **Alignment System** — span-angle sensors + guide cable/furrow.
9. **Central Control Panel** — speed setpoint, fault detection, telemetry.

**Working Principle:**
- The pump delivers pressurized water through the drag hose into the lateral.
- The lateral moves perpendicular to its length at a constant speed.
- Each tower is driven by its own motor, synchronized via the alignment control system.
- Water is sprayed uniformly along the lateral; the field is covered in multiple parallel passes.

**Automatic Alignment Mechanism:**
- A **span-angle sensor** (potentiometer or Hall-effect) measures the angle between adjacent pipe spans.
- A **microcontroller** in the central panel monitors the angle setpoint (typically ±0.5° from straight).
- When the trailing span falls behind, the controller briefly **stops the leading tower's motor**, allowing the trailing tower to "catch up."
- Once aligned, all motors resume synchronized motion.
- A **guide cable** or **shallow furrow** is laid across the field, with **mechanical probe sensors** at the front tower ensuring the entire system follows a true straight line.
- This alignment cycle repeats every few meters, called the **creep-align cycle** (typically 1–2 m of travel between alignments).

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
$L = 450$ m, $Q_{total} = 30$ L/s = 0.030 m³/s, $v = 1.5$ m/min = 0.025 m/s, $W = 15$ m.

**(i) Net Application Depth:**

$$
d = \frac{Q_{total}}{v \cdot L} = \frac{0.030}{0.025 \times 450} = \frac{0.030}{11.25} = 0.00267 \text{ m} = 2.67 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Time per Pass:**

$$
t_{pass} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{450}{1.5} = 300 \text{ min} = 5 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Dwell Time:**

$$
t_{dwell} = \frac{W}{v} = \frac{15}{0.025} = 600 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ min}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare **Lateral Move** and **Center Pivot** irrigation systems under the following heads: (i) Field shape suitability, (ii) Water source, (iii) Capital cost, (iv) Labor requirement, (v) Uniformity, (vi) Corner coverage, (vii) Power consumption.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for **net application depth** of a continuous-move sprinkler system. A canal-fed lateral move system has a total discharge of **40 L/s**, a lateral length of **500 m**, and a wetted width of **20 m**. If the application depth required is **8 mm**, find the travel speed and the time to complete one pass.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Head | Lateral Move | Center Pivot |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Field shape | Rectangular | Square / circular |
| (ii) Water source | Hose OR canal (flexible) | Central well / single supply |
| (iii) Capital cost | Higher (per ha) due to long lateral | Lower |
| (iv) Labor | Higher (repositioning between passes) | Lower (continuous) |
| (v) Uniformity | 80–92% | 80–90% |
| (vi) Corner coverage | Better with end gun / corner arm | Poor (~21% missed) |
| (vii) Power | Higher (longer pipe, more towers) | Lower |

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes)

**Given:** $Q = 40$ L/s = 0.040 m³/s, $L = 500$ m, $W = 20$ m, $d = 8$ mm = 0.008 m.

**Step 1 — Find travel speed $v$:**

$$
d = \frac{Q}{v \cdot L} \implies v = \frac{Q}{d \cdot L} = \frac{0.040}{0.008 \times 500} = \frac{0.040}{4.0} = 0.01 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{v = 0.01 \text{ m/s} = 0.6 \text{ m/min}}
$$

**Step 2 — Time per pass:**

$$
t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{500}{0.01} = 50000 \text{ s} = 833.3 \text{ min} \approx 13.89 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** are the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always convert L/s → m³/s AND m/min → m/s BEFORE plugging into the formula. Mixing units gives wrong answers by a factor of 60 or 1000.
> 2. In Christiansen's CU, do NOT use squares or signed deviations — use **absolute deviations** (the $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbol).
> 3. In the comparison table, do not just list points — the KTU board rewards **contrasting pairs** (e.g., "Lateral Move uses hose OR canal; Center Pivot uses a single central supply"). A one-sided list loses marks.
> 4. When asked to "derive," do NOT just state the formula. Show at least 3–4 algebraic steps starting from a logical premise (volume = flow × time, etc.). Direct formula substitution = **0 marks** for the derivation part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **Lateral Move** = self-propelled straight-travel sprinkler system; ideal for **rectangular fields**.
- ✅ Two main types: **Hose-fed** (from hydrant) and **Canal-fed** (from open channel).
- ✅ Master equation (HIGH PRIORITY): $d = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{v \cdot L}$ — also valid for center pivot with $L$ replaced by wetted arc length.
- ✅ Travel speed formula: $v = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{d \cdot L}$
- ✅ Cycle time: $T_{cycle} = n_{passes} \times t_{pass} + t_{repositioning}$.
- ✅ **Automatic alignment** uses span-angle sensors + guide cable; the "creep-align cycle" keeps the lateral straight.
- ✅ **Christiansen's CU** = $100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{x}}\right)$; acceptable value ≥ **85%**.
- ✅ Typical speed: **0.5–3 m/min**; typical span length: **30–50 m**; typical system length: **100–800 m**.
- ✅ **Hazen-Williams equation** for head loss: $H_f = K \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.75} / D^{4.75}$.
- ✅ End guns extend coverage at the field edge; **pressure regulators** ensure nozzle uniformity.
- ✅ Compared to center pivot: better **corner coverage**, more **labor-intensive**, higher **capital cost**, suitable for **rectangular fields** only.
- ✅ **Always** convert units to SI (m, s, m³/s) before calculation — KTU loses many marks on this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Irrigation Systems and Comparison of Different Irrigation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro Irrigation Systems and Comparison of Different Irrigation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Irrigation System?

An **Irrigation System** is an engineered arrangement of hydraulic, mechanical, and electronic components designed to artificially supply controlled quantities of water to agricultural crops at the right time, in the right amount, and at the right place, supplementing natural rainfall.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Sense):**
> Irrigation is the controlled application of water to crops to supplement rainfall, while an **Irrigation System** is the integrated set of hardware (pumps, pipes, valves, emitters) and software (controllers, sensors, automation logic) used to deliver that water efficiently.

## 1.2 What is a Micro Irrigation System (MIS)?

A **Micro Irrigation System (MIS)** is a pressurized, low-volume, low-pressure irrigation methodology that delivers water directly to the **root zone** of each plant through a network of pipes, valves, filters, and emitters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro Irrigation is defined by THREE rigid KTU criteria:**
> 1. **Pressure**: Operates at low head (**1–3 kg/cm²**, i.e., **10–30 m of water column**).
> 2. **Discharge**: Each emission point delivers **less than 300 litres/hour**.
> 3. **Placement**: Water is delivered **near or below the soil surface**, directly wetting the root zone.

The two principal families of MIS recognized under KTU syllabus are:
- **Drip (Trickle) Irrigation System**
- **Sprinkler / Micro-Sprinkler Irrigation System**

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Plant Hospital IV Drip"

Imagine you are sick in a hospital:

| Patient Scenario | Plant Irrigation Analogy |
|---|---|
| Drinking a bucket of water at once | Flood / Furrow irrigation (high volume, low frequency, wasteful) |
| A nurse slowly feeding saline through IV drip, drop by drop, exactly when needed | **Micro Irrigation** — slow, precise, root-zone-targeted |
| Water drips on the floor of the ward | Conveyance / deep percolation losses |

**The Core Insight:**
A Micro Irrigation System functions like an **IV drip for plants** — water is delivered in **small, frequent, precise doses** directly into the **active root zone**, minimizing losses to evaporation, runoff, wind drift, and deep percolation.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Overview of Irrigation System Categories

```
Broad Classification of Irrigation Systems
│
├── 1. Surface Irrigation (Gravity-driven)
│     ├── Flooding
│     ├── Furrow
│     └── Basin / Border
│
├── 2. Pressurized Irrigation
│     ├── Sprinkler Irrigation
│     ├── Micro-Sprinkler
│     └── Drip (Trickle) Irrigation
│
├── 3. Sub-surface Irrigation
│     └── Sub-surface Drip Irrigation (SDI)
│
└── 4. Manual / Manual-lift (Traditional)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Efficiency Hierarchy:**
> The general order of **Application Efficiency (Ea)** in descending order is:
> **Drip > Sprinkler > Furrow > Flood Irrigation**
> This is the single most asked comparison in KTU exams.

---

## 1.5 Why do we Need Micro Irrigation?

| Problem in Conventional Irrigation | How Micro Irrigation Solves It |
|---|---|
| Heavy water wastage (40–60% loss) | Saves **30–70% water** vs surface methods |
| Uneven water distribution | Emitter uniformity > **90%** |
| High labour cost | Fully automated via solenoid valves + timers |
| Soil erosion & nutrient leaching | Localized wetted bulb, no overland flow |
| Weed growth between rows | Dry inter-row, only root zone is wetted |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Micro Irrigation System

A complete **Drip Irrigation System** (the most important sub-type under KTU Module 3) consists of the following functional blocks:

### (A) Head / Pump Station Unit
- **Pump unit** (centrifugal / submersible) — provides operating head
- **Fertigation tank** — for injecting fertilizers
- **Filters** — sand filter, screen filter, disc filter (critical to prevent emitter clogging)
- **Pressure gauges** and **flow meters** (instrumentation elements)
- **Control valves** — manual gate valve + automatic solenoid valve

### (B) Main Distribution Network
- **Main pipe** — PVC, conveys water from pump to sub-main
- **Sub-main pipe** — PE/PVC, distributes to manifolds
- **Manifold pipe** — feeds laterals

### (C) Laterals & Emission Devices
- **Laterals** — small diameter PE pipes (12 mm, 16 mm, 20 mm)
- **Emitters / Drippers** — regulate discharge at low pressure
- **Micro-tubes / spaghetti tubes** — for point-source delivery

### (D) Accessories
- **Air vacuum relief valve**
- **Non-return valve**
- **Pressure regulator**
- **Flush valve** (end-of-line)

---

## 2.2 Types of Emitters — KTU Critical Topic

| Emitter Type | Working Principle | Discharge Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Online (point-source) dripper** | Labyrinth / turbulent flow path | 2–8 L/h |
| **Inline dripper** (in-line tubing) | Moulded inside lateral wall | 1–4 L/h |
| **Micro-sprinkler** | Rotating nozzle | 30–150 L/h |
| **Bubbler** | Low-pressure fountain | < 250 L/h |
| **Micro-jet / Fogger** | Fine mist for greenhouse | 30–60 L/h |

---

## 2.3 The Hydraulic Relationship — Emitter Discharge Equation

The discharge of an emitter is governed by the classic **pressure–discharge relationship**:

$$q = k \cdot H^{x}$$

Where:
- $q$ = emitter discharge (L/h)
- $k$ = emitter discharge coefficient (constant)
- $H$ = operating pressure head at emitter inlet (m)
- $x$ = **emitter discharge exponent** (dimensionless)

### Physical Meaning of 'x'

The exponent $x$ determines how sensitive the emitter is to pressure variations:

| Value of x | Behaviour | Emitter Category |
|---|---|---|
| $x < 0.5$ | Very pressure-compensating | **Pressure Compensating (PC) dripper** |
| $0.5 \leq x \leq 0.7$ | Moderately compensating | Long-path / labyrinth |
| $x \approx 1.0$ | Fully linear (orifice type) | Simple orifice |
| $x \approx 0.5$ | Ideal square-root law | **Ideal pressure-compensating** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Memory Hook:**
> For **pressure-compensating emitters**, the goal is $x \rightarrow 0$ (i.e., $q$ is independent of $H$). Manufacturers aim for $x < 0.2$ in practice.

---

## 2.4 Performance Metrics — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All the following formulas are EXAM-FREQUENT and have been asked multiple times in KTU board examinations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Emitter discharge | $q = k \cdot H^{x}$ | Emitter selection |
| 2 | Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient | $CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}}\right]$ | Field uniformity |
| 3 | Statistical Uniformity | $US = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{s}{\bar{q}}\right]$ | Statistical evaluation |
| 4 | Distribution Uniformity | $DU = 100 \cdot \dfrac{q_{25\%}}{\bar{q}}$ | Low-quarter ratio |
| 5 | Application Efficiency | $E_a = 100 \cdot \dfrac{W_s}{W_a}$ | Water balance |
