| 6 | Distribution Efficiency | $E_d = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}}\right]$ | Engineering design |
| 7 | Water Requirement | $WR = ET_c - P_e$ | Irrigation scheduling |
| 8 | Wetted Diameter (empirical) | $D_w = a \cdot q^{0.5}$ | Soil wetted area |
| 9 | Lateral friction head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ | Hydraulic design |
| 10 | Reynolds Number (lateral flow) | $Re = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ | Flow regime check |

**Notation Notes for KTU Board:**
- $\bar{q}$ = mean emitter discharge
- $q_i$ = individual emitter discharge
- $n$ = number of emitters sampled
- $s$ = standard deviation of discharges
- $q_{25\%}$ = mean of lowest 25% emitter discharges
- $W_s$ = water stored in root zone
- $W_a$ = water applied
- $P_e$ = effective rainfall
- $ET_c$ = crop evapotranspiration

---

## 2.5 Comparison of Different Irrigation Systems — Conceptual Hierarchy

### (A) Surface Irrigation
- Water distributed by **gravity over the soil surface**
- Types: **Flood, Furrow, Basin, Border**
- Wastage: **30–50%** due to runoff, evaporation, deep percolation
- Application Efficiency: **30–60%**

### (B) Sprinkler Irrigation
- Water sprayed through pressurized nozzles into air, falls like rainfall
- Operating pressure: **2–5 kg/cm²**
- Application Efficiency: **60–75%**
- Losses: wind drift, evaporation from droplets

### (C) Micro / Drip Irrigation
- Water applied at **point-source** directly to root zone
- Operating pressure: **1–2 kg/cm²**
- Application Efficiency: **80–95%**
- Losses: minimal (clogging is the main operational hazard)

### (D) Sub-surface Drip Irrigation (SDI)
- Drip laterals buried **10–40 cm** below soil surface
- Highest efficiency (up to **95%+**)
- Eliminates evaporation loss completely
- High installation cost

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Pearl:**
> If a question asks "Which is the most efficient irrigation system?" — the textbook-correct KTU answer is **Drip Irrigation** (with **Sub-surface Drip Irrigation (SDI)** as the highest-tier variant).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Micro Irrigation Systems are extensively used in:
- **Horticulture** (grape, pomegranate, banana, mango orchards)
- **Greenhouse precision farming**
- **High-value cash crops** (sugarcane, cotton)
- **Landscape irrigation** (golf courses, municipal parks)
- **Drought-prone zones** (Rajasthan, Marathwada, Vidarbha — supported by PMKSY scheme in India)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Emitter Discharge Equation

The standard empirical discharge equation for an emitter emerges from the **orifice flow principle** combined with a **pressure-compensation factor**.

### Step 1 — Start with the General Orifice Equation

For any orifice-type emitter, the discharge follows the classical Bernoulli-based orifice equation:

$$q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}$$

Where:
- $q$ = discharge (m³/s)
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (dimensionless)
- $A$ = effective cross-sectional area of orifice (m²)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $H$ = pressure head at the orifice (m)

### Step 2 — Restate with Discharge Coefficient

Group $C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2g}$ into a single lumped constant:

$$q = (C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2g}) \cdot \sqrt{H}$$

Now, recognize that $\sqrt{H} = H^{0.5}$, so the equation becomes:

$$q = (C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2g}) \cdot H^{0.5}$$

### Step 3 — Generalize for Non-Ideal Orifices

For labyrinth-type, long-path, or pressure-compensating emitters, the exponent is not exactly 0.5. Therefore, generalize:

$$q = k \cdot H^{x}$$

Where:
- $k$ = lumped geometric + hydraulic constant
- $x$ = emitter discharge exponent

### Step 4 — Apply Logarithm to Both Sides (Useful for Field Calibration)

$$\log(q) = \log(k) + x \cdot \log(H)$$

This is a **straight-line equation** in the $\log(q)$ vs $\log(H)$ plane:
- **Slope** = $x$
- **Y-intercept** = $\log(k)$

In the KTU board exam, this logarithmic form is used to determine emitter constants $k$ and $x$ experimentally.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** An emitter has $k = 1.2$ (units consistent) and $x = 0.5$. Find the discharge at operating heads of (a) $H = 10$ m and (b) $H = 20$ m.

**Solution (a):**

$$q_1 = 1.2 \cdot (10)^{0.5}$$

$$q_1 = 1.2 \cdot 3.1623$$

$$q_1 = 3.7948 \text{ L/h}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$q_2 = 1.2 \cdot (20)^{0.5}$$

$$q_2 = 1.2 \cdot 4.4721$$

$$q_2 = 5.3665 \text{ L/h}$$

**Conclusion:** Discharge increases by only $\dfrac{5.37 - 3.79}{3.79} \cdot 100\% \approx 41.5\%$ when head **doubles**, because of the square-root relationship.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU)

### Step 1 — Define the Concept

The **Uniformity Coefficient** quantifies how evenly water is distributed across all emitters in a drip system.

### Step 2 — Define Deviations

Let $q_i$ be the discharge of the $i$-th emitter, and $\bar{q}$ the arithmetic mean discharge of $n$ emitters:

$$\bar{q} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i$$

Define the absolute deviation of each emitter from the mean:

$$d_i = \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert$$

### Step 3 — Average Absolute Deviation

The mean absolute deviation across all $n$ emitters:

$$\bar{d} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert$$

### Step 4 — Form the Uniformity Ratio

Christiansen defined uniformity as one minus the ratio of mean deviation to mean discharge:

$$UC = 1 - \frac{\bar{d}}{\bar{q}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Back

$$UC = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{1}{n} \sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{\bar{q}}$$

### Step 6 — Multiply Through

$$UC = 1 - \frac{\sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}}$$

### Step 7 — Express as a Percentage (KTU Convention)

$$\boxed{CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \frac{\sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}}\right]}$$

### Step 8 — Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** 5 emitters with discharges $q_1 = 4, q_2 = 4.2, q_3 = 3.8, q_4 = 4.1, q_5 = 3.9$ L/h.

**Step 8a — Mean discharge:**

$$\bar{q} = \frac{4 + 4.2 + 3.8 + 4.1 + 3.9}{5} = \frac{20}{5} = 4.0 \text{ L/h}$$

**Step 8b — Absolute deviations:**

$$\vert 4.0 - 4.0 \vert = 0.0$$
$$\vert 4.2 - 4.0 \vert = 0.2$$
$$\vert 3.8 - 4.0 \vert = 0.2$$
$$\vert 4.1 - 4.0 \vert = 0.1$$
$$\vert 3.9 - 4.0 \vert = 0.1$$

Sum: $\sum \vert d_i \vert = 0 + 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.6$

**Step 8c — Compute CU:**

$$CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \frac{0.6}{5 \cdot 4.0}\right]$$

$$CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \frac{0.6}{20}\right]$$

$$CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - 0.03\right]$$

$$\boxed{CU = 97\%}$$

### Step 9 — KTU Quality Grading

| CU Value | Field Performance | Grade |
|---|---|---|
| $> 90\%$ | Excellent | A |
| 80–90% | Good | B |
| 70–80% | Fair | C |
| $< 70\%$ | Poor — re-design needed | D |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Irrigation Systems

The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU compilation** and is frequently asked as a direct 14-mark comparison question.

| S.No | Parameter | Surface (Flood) | Surface (Furrow) | Sprinkler | Drip / Micro |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Application Efficiency | 30–50% | 40–60% | 60–75% | **80–95%** |
| 2 | Conveyance Efficiency | 60% | 70% | 100% | 100% |
| 3 | Water Saving | Baseline | 10–20% | 30–50% | **50–70%** |
| 4 | Operating Pressure | Gravity | Gravity | 2–5 kg/cm² | **0.5–2.5 kg/cm²** |
| 5 | Suitability for Sloping Land | Poor | Poor | Good | **Excellent** |
| 6 | Wind Drift Loss | Nil | Nil | 5–15% | Nil |
| 7 | Evaporation Loss | High | High | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| 8 | Soil Erosion Risk | High | High | Low | **Negligible** |
| 9 | Weed Growth | High | High | Moderate | **Lowest (dry inter-row)** |
| 10 | Initial Cost | Very Low | Low | Medium | **High** |
| 11 | Operating Cost | Low | Low | Medium | Low (high efficiency offset) |
| 12 | Fertilizer Injection (Fertigation) | Difficult | Difficult | Possible | **Easiest & Best** |
| 13 | Suitability for Saline Water | Poor | Poor | Moderate | **Good** |
| 14 | Labour Requirement | High | High | Low | **Lowest** |
| 15 | Field Shape Requirement | Levelled only | Levelled | Flexible | **Most flexible** |
| 16 | Disease Risk (Fungal) | High | High | Moderate | **Low** |
| 17 | Energy Consumption | Lowest | Lowest | High | Moderate |
| 18 | Crop Yield Increase | Baseline | +10% | +20–30% | **+30–100%** |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example — Comparative Water Saving

**Problem:** A farmer uses **Furrow Irrigation** with $E_a = 50\%$ to apply **100 mm** of water. How much water can he save per hectare by switching to Drip Irrigation with $E_a = 90\%$?

**Solution:**

**Water effectively stored in root zone (same in both cases):**

$$W_s = E_a \times W_a$$

For furrow: $W_s = 0.50 \times 100 = 50$ mm

For drip (to store same 50 mm effective):

$$W_a^{drip} = \frac{W_s}{E_a^{drip}} = \frac{50}{0.90} = 55.56 \text{ mm}$$

**Water saved per application:**

$$\Delta W = 100 - 55.56 = 44.44 \text{ mm}$$

**Per hectare (1 ha = 10,000 m²):**

$$\Delta V = 44.44 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\Delta V = 444.4 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per hectare}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V \approx 4{,}44{,}400 \text{ litres/hectare saved per cycle}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Drip Irrigation System Architecture

```
flowchart LR
    A[Water Source\nWell / Borewell / Canal] --> B[Pump Station\nCentrifugal Pump]
    B --> C[Fertigation Tank\nFertilizer Injector]
    C --> D[Filtration Unit\nSand + Screen + Disc Filters]
    D --> E[Pressure Gauge\nand Flow Meter]
    E --> F[Control Valve\nManual + Solenoid]
    F --> G[Air Vacuum Relief Valve]
    G --> H[Main Pipe\nPVC]
    H --> I[Sub-Main Pipe\nPVC]
    I --> J[Manifold\nPE Pipe]
    J --> K[Laterals\nPE Drip Line 16 mm]
    K --> L[Emitter / Dripper\nq = kH^x]
    L --> M[Root Zone\nSoil Wetted Bulb]
    M --> N[Plant Uptake\nEvapotranspiration]
```

## 4.2 Functional Comparison Flow Diagram — Irrigation System Selection Logic

```
flowchart TD
    Start([Crop Selection]) --> Q1{Available Water\nResource?}
    Q1 -->|Abundant| Q2{Topography?}
    Q1 -->|Scarce| D1[Drip Irrigation]
    Q2 -->|Flat Levelled| Surf[Surface Irrigation\nFlood or Furrow]
    Q2 -->|Undulating| Q3{Crop Type?}
    Q3 -->|Cereals / Field Crops| Sprink[Sprinkler Irrigation]
    Q3 -->|Orchards / Row Crops| D1
    Q3 -->|Greenhouse / Precision| D1
    Q2 -->|Very Sloping| D1
    Sprinkler2[Sprinkler] --> FertigationCheck{Fertigation\nRequired?}
    FertigationCheck -->|Yes| D1
    FertigationCheck -->|No| Sprink
    D1 --> EndOpt([Optimal Micro Irrigation Selected])
    Surf --> EndOpt2([Conventional Selected])
    Sprink --> EndOpt2
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Cross-Sectional View of a Drip Emitter

```
flowchart LR
    Inlet[Lateral Pipe\nPressurized Water Inlet] --> Diaphragm[Pressure Regulating\nSilicone Diaphragm]
    Diaphragm --> Labyrinth[Labyrinth Channel\nTurbulent Flow Path]
    Labyrinth --> Orifice[Exit Orifice\nCalibrated Opening]
    Orifice --> Outlet[Water drips\nto Soil]
    Outlet --> Soil[Root Zone Wetting\nForms Onion-shaped Bulb]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Reading Tip for KTU Exams:**
> The **flow direction** in your Mermaid block diagram should match the actual field layout — water always flows **Source → Pump → Filter → Control → Distribution → Emission**.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Micro Irrigation Automation

```
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensors [Sensor Layer]
        S1[Soil Moisture Sensor\nVolumetric Water Content]
        S2[Pressure Transmitter\nat Manifold]
        S3[Flow Meter\nElectromagnetic Type]
        S4[Rain Sensor\nTipping Bucket]
    end

    subgraph Controller [Controller Layer]
        PLC[Programmable Logic Controller\nor Microcontroller]
        RTU[RTU / IoT Gateway]
    end

    subgraph Actuators [Actuator Layer]
        V1[Main Solenoid Valve]
        V2[Sectional Valves V1..Vn]
        P1[Variable Frequency Drive\nfor Pump]
        F1[Fertilizer Injector Pump]
    end

    S1 --> PLC
    S2 --> PLC
    S3 --> PLC
    S4 --> PLC
    PLC --> V1
    PLC --> V2
    PLC --> P1
    PLC --> F1
    PLC --> RTU
    RTU --> Cloud[(Cloud Dashboard\nTelemetry and SCADA)]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define **Micro Irrigation**. List any **two advantages** of micro irrigation over conventional surface irrigation.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Micro Irrigation is a pressurized, low-volume irrigation methodology that delivers water directly to the root zone of plants through a network of pipes, valves, and emitters, operating typically at 0.5–2.5 kg/cm² with each emission point delivering less than 300 litres/hour.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Higher Water Use Efficiency (80–95%)** compared to 30–60% in surface irrigation, reducing water consumption by 30–70%.
2. **Improved crop yield and quality** due to uniform moisture distribution in the root zone and reduced weed, disease, and pest pressure.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Write the **emitter discharge equation** and explain the significance of the **discharge exponent 'x'**.

**Model Answer:**

**Equation:**

$$q = k \cdot H^{x}$$

Where $q$ is emitter discharge (L/h), $k$ is the emitter discharge coefficient, $H$ is the operating pressure head (m), and $x$ is the emitter discharge exponent.

**Significance of x:**
- The exponent $x$ indicates the **sensitivity of discharge to pressure variations**.
- For **pressure-compensating (PC) emitters**, $x \rightarrow 0$, meaning discharge remains nearly constant regardless of pressure changes — ideal for hilly terrain.
- For **non-compensating emitters**, $x \approx 0.5$ (laminar-turbulent flow), making discharge moderately pressure-dependent.
- For **simple orifice-type emitters**, $x = 1$ (fully linear), making them highly sensitive to pressure fluctuations.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, describe the **components and working of a Drip Irrigation System**. Explain the role of the **filtration unit** and **pressure regulator** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient** formula. A drip system has **6 emitters** with discharges **4.0, 4.2, 3.8, 4.1, 3.9, and 4.0 L/h**. Calculate the uniformity coefficient and comment on the field performance. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A (a)

**Components of a Drip Irrigation System:**

A drip irrigation system has the following functional components:

**1. Head / Pump Station:**
- Centrifugal or submersible pump supplies water at required pressure.
- Includes a non-return valve to prevent backflow.

**2. Fertigation Tank:**
- Abyass tank with injection pump for adding water-soluble fertilizers to irrigation water.

**3. Filtration Unit:**
- **Sand Filter:** Removes organic debris, algae, fine sand.
- **Screen Filter:** Removes fine particles (100–200 micron).
- **Disc Filter:** Removes very fine particles using grooved discs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of Filtration Unit:**
> Filters are the **life-line** of a drip system. Without filtration, emitters (with narrow labyrinth channels of 0.5–1.0 mm) get **clogged within hours**, leading to non-uniform discharge and system failure. **The cost of filters is 10–15% of total system cost** but they prevent 90% of operational failures.

**4. Pressure Regulator:**
- Maintains constant downstream pressure (typically **1.0–2.0 kg/cm²**).
- Critical for pressure-compensating emitter performance.
- Without it, laterals at lower elevation get higher pressure, causing over-irrigation.

**5. Distribution Network:**
- Main pipe (PVC) → Sub-main pipe (PVC) → Manifold (PE) → Lateral (PE 16 mm)

**6. Emitters / Drippers:**
- Discharge water at point locations near plant root zone.
- Discharge equation: $q = k \cdot H^{x}$.

**7. Accessories:**
- Air vacuum relief valve (prevents suction of dirt into laterals).
- Flush valve (end-of-line flushing).

**Working Principle:**
Water from source is pumped → passed through filters → pressure regulated → distributed via mains → sub-mains → manifolds → laterals → emitted through drippers → delivered to root zone in **small, frequent doses**.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A (b)

**Derivation of Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU):**

See Section 3.2 of these notes for the **complete 9-step derivation**. Final formula:

$$CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \frac{\sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}}\right]$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Mean Discharge:**

$$\bar{q} = \frac{4.0 + 4.2 + 3.8 + 4.1 + 3.9 + 4.0}{6} = \frac{24.0}{6} = 4.0 \text{ L/h}$$

**Step 2 — Absolute Deviations:**

$$\vert 4.0 - 4.0 \vert = 0.0$$
$$\vert 4.2 - 4.0 \vert = 0.2$$
$$\vert 3.8 - 4.0 \vert = 0.2$$
$$\vert 4.1 - 4.0 \vert = 0.1$$
$$\vert 3.9 - 4.0 \vert = 0.1$$
$$\vert 4.0 - 4.0 \vert = 0.0$$

Sum: $\sum \vert d_i \vert = 0 + 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.1 + 0.1 + 0 = 0.6$

**Step 3 — Compute CU:**

$$CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \frac{0.6}{6 \cdot 4.0}\right]$$

$$CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \frac{0.6}{24}\right]$$

$$CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - 0.025\right]$$

$$\boxed{CU = 97.5\%}$$

**Comment on Field Performance:**
Since $CU = 97.5\% > 90\%$, the system is rated **Grade A (Excellent)**. The discharge variation across emitters is negligible (< 5%), indicating a well-designed drip system with properly selected emitters and adequate pressure regulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently make the following mistakes in CU problems:
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply $n$ with $\bar{q}$** in the denominator. Write it as $n\bar{q}$ clearly, not just $\bar{q}$.
> 2. **Using $q_i^2$ instead of $\vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert$** — that is the **statistical uniformity** formula, not Christiansen's. Both are different.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert to percentage** at the end — KTU expects the **final answer as %**, not as a fraction.
> 4. **Skipping the absolute value bars** — deviations must be **positive**; always use $\vert \cdot \vert$.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Compare **Surface, Sprinkler, and Drip Irrigation Systems** with respect to **any eight parameters**, clearly stating which system is most efficient. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A farmer irrigates a **1-hectare field** using furrow irrigation with an application efficiency of **45%**. Calculate the volume of water (in cubic metres and litres) saved if he switches to a drip system with **90% application efficiency**, applying the same effective root-zone water depth of **80 mm**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (a)

| S.No | Parameter | Surface Irrigation | Sprinkler Irrigation | Drip Irrigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operating Pressure | Gravity (0 head) | 2–5 kg/cm² | 0.5–2.5 kg/cm² |
| 2 | Application Efficiency | 30–60% | 60–75% | **80–95%** |
| 3 | Water Saving | Baseline | 30–50% | **50–70%** |
| 4 | Soil Erosion Risk | High | Moderate | **Negligible** |
| 5 | Suitability for Slopes | Poor | Good | **Excellent** |
| 6 | Wind Drift Loss | None | 5–15% | **None** |
| 7 | Initial Cost | Very Low | Medium | **High** |
| 8 | Labour Requirement | High | Low | **Very Low** |

**Conclusion:**
The **Drip Irrigation System** is the most efficient, with the highest application efficiency (80–95%) and the lowest water loss among the three systems.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (b)

**Given:**
- Area = 1 hectare = **10,000 m²**
- Effective root-zone water depth (same in both systems): $d = 80$ mm = **0.08 m**
- Furrow $E_a = 45\%$
- Drip $E_a = 90\%$

**Step 1 — Effective Water Stored in Root Zone (Same for Both):**

$$W_s = d \times A = 0.08 \times 10{,}000 = 800 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per hectare}$$

**Step 2 — Water Applied by Furrow System:**

$$W_a^{furrow} = \frac{W_s}{E_a^{furrow}} = \frac{800}{0.45} = 1777.78 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Water Applied by Drip System:**

$$W_a^{drip} = \frac{W_s}{E_a^{drip}} = \frac{800}{0.90} = 888.89 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Water Saved:**

$$\Delta V = 1777.78 - 888.89 = 888.89 \text{ m}^3$$

**In Litres:**

$$\boxed{\Delta V \approx 8{,}88{,}889 \text{ litres/hectare saved per cycle}}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Saving:**

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{888.89}{1777.78} \times 100 = 50\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors**: 80 mm = 0.08 m, NOT 80 m. **Always convert mm to m** before multiplying with area in m².
> 2. **Hectare confusion**: 1 hectare = 10,000 m². KTU commonly tests whether students remember this.
> 3. **Efficiency misinterpretation**: Higher $E_a$ means **less water applied** for the same $W_s$. Do NOT assume higher $E_a$ means more water.
> 4. **Forgetting to state final units** — always mention both m³ and litres.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Micro Irrigation**: Pressurized, low-volume, low-pressure (< 3 kg/cm²) irrigation delivering water to root zone with each emission point < 300 L/h.
- ✅ **Emitter**: Device regulating discharge from a lateral pipe.
- ✅ **Dripper**: Another name for point-source emitter.

### Master Formulae
- ✅ Emitter discharge: $q = k \cdot H^{x}$
- ✅ Christiansen's Uniformity: $CU = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{n \cdot \bar{q}}\right]$
- ✅ Statistical Uniformity: $US = 100 \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{s}{\bar{q}}\right]$
- ✅ Distribution Uniformity: $DU = 100 \cdot \dfrac{q_{25\%}}{\bar{q}}$
- ✅ Water Requirement: $WR = ET_c - P_e$
- ✅ Application Efficiency: $E_a = 100 \cdot \dfrac{W_s}{W_a}$

### Key Numerical Benchmarks
- ✅ Application Efficiency Order: **Drip (90%) > Sprinkler (70%) > Furrow (50%) > Flood (35%)**
- ✅ Operating Pressure: Drip (0.5–2.5) < Sprinkler (2–5) kg/cm²
- ✅ Water Saving: Drip saves **30–70%** vs surface
- ✅ CU Grading: >90% = A (Excellent), 80–90% = B (Good), 70–80% = C (Fair), <70% = D (Poor)

### Components to Memorize (Order in System)
- ✅ Source → Pump → Fertigation → Filter → Pressure Gauge → Control Valve → Air Valve → Main → Sub-main → Manifold → Lateral → Emitter → Root Zone

### Common KTU Examiner Questions
- ✅ "Compare any 4 irrigation systems" — always mention efficiency, pressure, water saving, suitability.
- ✅ "Derive CU formula" — 9 steps expected.
- ✅ "Numerical on water saving between furrow and drip" — always show m³ and litres.
- ✅ "Significance of exponent $x$ in emitter equation" — PC vs non-PC distinction.

### Critical Field Knowledge
- ✅ **Filters are non-negotiable** — clogging is the #1 cause of drip system failure.
- ✅ **Pressure Compensating (PC) emitters** have $x < 0.5$ — used for undulating/hilly terrain.
- ✅ **Sub-surface Drip Irrigation (SDI)** is the most efficient variant — laterals buried 10–40 cm below soil.
- ✅ **Fertigation** = fertilizer + irrigation, easiest and most efficient through drip.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing $x = 1$ (orifice) with $x = 0.5$ (ideal pressure-compensating).
- ❌ Using **Christiansen's** formula when **Statistical Uniformity** is asked.
- ❌ Forgetting to convert mm to m in volume calculations.
- ❌ Stating efficiency as a fraction when KTU expects percentage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil Moisture Measurement Methods: Resistance-based Method
Voltage-based Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Voltage-based Method # Voltage-based Method in Agricultural Instrumentation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEICT753 – Module 3):** > **Voltage-based Methods** refer to a class of agricultural sensing techniques in which a physical, chemical, or biological parameter of the soil, plant, water, or produce is converted into a measurable **voltage signal** using transducers such as electrodes, resistive/capacitive bridges, potentiometric probes, and ion-selective sensors. The output voltage is then conditioned, amplified, and digitized for crop monitoring, irrigation control, and fertigation decisions. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, voltage-based methods sit at the heart of **Process Parameter Measurement**, where the *agronomic variable* (moisture, pH, nutrient concentration, salinity) is mapped to a corresponding *electrical potential difference* that obeys a known physical law (Ohm's law, Nernst equation, bridge balance equation, or dielectric polarization principle). ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Plant Thermometer with a Voltage Dial" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine you place a special leaf-shaped probe in soil. Depending on how much water is present, how acidic the soil is, or how much nitrate is dissolved, the probe develops a tiny voltage — much like how a clinical thermometer's mercury rises with body temperature. A voltmeter simply "reads" this voltage and tells you whether the field needs water, lime, or fertilizer. **The plant/soil is the signal source; the voltmeter is the interpreter.** ### Classification of Voltage-Based Methods (KTU Board-Standard) | **Category** | **Physical Law Used** | **Typical Output** | **Agricultural Parameter** | |---|---|---|---| | Resistive / Conductivity | Ohm's Law | $V = IR$ | Soil moisture, salinity, EC | | Capacitive / Dielectric | $C = \varepsilon A/d$ | $V_{out} \propto C$ | Volumetric water content | | Potentiometric (ISE) | Nernst Equation | $E = E^0 + (RT/zF)\ln a$ | pH, N, P, K | | Bridge Methods | Wheatstone Balance | $V_{AB} = 0$ (null) | Strain, moisture, temperature | | Semiconductor / FET | Threshold shift | $\Delta V_{GS}$ | pH (ISFET), ion activity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Lab Manual Defaults):** > - Faraday Constant: $F = 96485 \;\text{C mol}^{-1}$ > - Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314 \;\text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$ > - Absolute Temperature for soil at 25 °C: $T = 298.15 \;\text{K}$ > - Nernst Slope factor at 25 °C: $\dfrac{RT}{F} \approx 25.693 \;\text{mV}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Voltage Pipeline" Every voltage-based agricultural sensor follows a **three-stage signal chain**: 1. **Transduction Stage** — The agronomic parameter alters a primary electrical property (resistance, capacitance, or chemical potential). 2. **Voltage Generation Stage** — A reference/excitation source drives the transducer so the property change manifests as a measurable $V_{out}$. 3. **Signal Conditioning Stage** — Amplification, filtering, and linearization (cold-junction compensation for pH, temperature compensation for EC) prepare the signal for ADC. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" of Voltage:** Voltage is preferred because it is *easily amplified by op-amps*, *immune to transmission losses over cables*, and *directly digitizable by ADC*. Modern KTU lab-grade systems digitize the conditioned voltage using 12-bit or 16-bit ADCs with reference spans of **0 – 5 V** or **0 – 3.3 V**. ## 2.2 Sub-Method 1 — Wheatstone Bridge (Foundation of Most Voltage Methods) The **Wheatstone bridge** is the canonical voltage-comparison circuit. For four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ supplied by excitation voltage $V_s$: $$V_{AB} = V_s \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right)$$ **Balance Condition** (zero detector voltage): $$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$$ When one arm is a sensing resistor (e.g., gypsum block for soil moisture), an imbalance voltage $\Delta V$ appears that is proportional to the parameter change. ## 2.3 Sub-Method 2 — Potentiometric / Ion-Selective Electrodes (ISE) For pH glass electrodes, ion-selective field-effect transistors (ISFETs), and NPK sensors, the governing relation is the **Nernst Equation**: $$E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln(a_{ion})$$ where: - $E$ = measured cell voltage (V) - $E^0$ = standard electrode potential (V) - $z$ = ionic charge number - $a_{ion}$ = activity of the target ion For pH ($z = +1$, $a_{H^+} = 10^{-pH}$): $$E = E^0 - 2.303 \cdot \dfrac{RT}{F} \cdot pH$$ At **25 °C**, the Nernst slope is **−59.16 mV/pH**, meaning each pH unit shifts the electrode voltage by ~59 mV. ## 2.4 Sub-Method 3 — Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor A capacitive sensor embedded in soil forms a **cylindrical capacitor** whose dielectric depends on water content. Because water has $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ (vs dry soil $\varepsilon_r \approx 4$), capacitance shifts strongly with moisture. $$C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$$ where $L$ = electrode length, $a$ = inner radius, $b$ = outer radius. The capacitance is converted to voltage by an **LC oscillator** or an **AC bridge rectifier**, where frequency $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ is mapped to $V_{out}$ via a frequency-to-voltage converter (e.g., LM2907). ## 2.5 Sub-Method 4 — Conductivity / Salinity Method Soil electrical conductivity (EC) is measured by inserting two electrodes and applying an AC excitation (to avoid polarization). The measured voltage across a known series resistor gives: $$\sigma = \dfrac{K_{cell}}{R_{solution}} = \dfrac{K_{cell} \cdot I}{V}$$ where $K_{cell}$ = cell constant (cm$^{-1}$). Typical agricultural EC range: **0 – 4 mS/cm** for healthy soils. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — High-Yield Formula Block (Voltage-based Methods).** *All quantities in SI units unless otherwise stated.* | **Formula** | **Name / Application** | **Variables & Units** | |---|---|---| | $V_{out} = V_s\!\left(\dfrac{R_x}{R_x+R_f}-\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\right)$ | Unbalanced Wheatstone bridge | $V_s$: excitation (V); $R_x$: sensor ($\Omega$) | | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ | Bridge balance (null) | All resistances in $\Omega$ | | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln(a)$ | Nernst Equation (ISE/pH) | $E^0$: standard potential (V); $a$: activity | | $E = E^0 - 0.05916 \cdot pH$ (at 25 °C) | pH electrode voltage | Slope: V per pH unit | | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Cylindrical capacitive moisture probe | $\varepsilon_r$: relative permittivity | | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC oscillator frequency | $f$: Hz; $L$: H; $C$: F | | $V_{out} = k_f \cdot f$ | F-to-V converter | $k_f$: gain (V/Hz) | | $\sigma = \dfrac{K_{cell}}{R_{soln}}$ | Solution conductivity | $\sigma$: S/cm; $K_{cell}$: cm$^{-1}$ | | $\theta_v = a + b \cdot \dfrac{V_{out}-V_{dry}}{V_{wet}-V_{dry}}$ | Volumetric water content calibration | $\theta_v$: m$^3$/m$^3$; $a, b$: linear coefficients | | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_s}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Small-signal bridge approximation | $\Delta R$: small resistance change | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Precision Agriculture:** Drone-mounted capacitive sensors scan fields for irrigation zoning. - **Greenhouse Automation:** pH + EC probes regulate fertigation in real time (e.g., tomato hydroponics at EC 2.5 mS/cm, pH 5.8). - **Soil Health Mapping:** Veris-style on-the-go EC mappers feed prescription maps to variable-rate fertilizer spreaders. - **Food Quality:** Voltage-based ripeness meters (dielectric spectroscopy) grade mangoes, apples, and tomatoes non-destructively. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage **Statement:** For a bridge with arms $R_1, R_2$ in the left leg and $R_3, R_4$ in the right leg, derive the differential output voltage $V_{AB}$ seen by the detector. **Step 1 — Identify node voltages.** The excitation $V_s$ is applied across the bridge. The top node is at $V_s$, the bottom node is at ground (0 V). By the voltage divider rule: $$V_A = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{(left-middle node)}$$ $$V_B = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \quad \text{(right-middle node)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the differential output.** $$\begin{aligned} V_{AB} &= V_A - V_B \\ &= V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \\ &= V_s \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to a single fraction.** $$\begin{aligned} V_{AB} &= V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_2(R_3+R_4) - R_4(R_1+R_2)}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)} \\ &= V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_2 R_3 + R_2 R_4 - R_1 R_4 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)} \\ &= V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Identify balance condition.** The detector reads zero ($V_{AB} = 0$) iff: $$R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$$ This is the **null-balance** condition. When $R_1$ is replaced by a soil moisture-dependent resistor, any deviation produces a non-zero $V_{AB}$ proportional to moisture content. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Nernst Slope at 25 °C (pH Probe) **Step 1 — Start with the general Nernst equation.** $$E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln(a_{ion})$$ **Step 2 — Substitute for the hydrogen ion.** For pH, $z = +1$ and $a_{H^+} = 10^{-pH}$, so $\ln(a_{H^+}) = -pH \cdot \ln(10) = -2.303 \cdot pH$. $$E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{F} \cdot (-2.303 \cdot pH) = E^0 - \dfrac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} \cdot pH$$ **Step 3 — Plug in numerical constants at $T = 298.15 \;\text{K}$.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Slope} &= \dfrac{2.303 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 298.15}{96485} \\ &= \dfrac{5710.5}{96485} \\ &= 0.05916 \;\text{V per pH unit} \\ &= 59.16 \;\text{mV/pH} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Final working equation for pH probes.** $$E_{cell} = E^0 - 0.05916 \cdot pH \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Numerical Insight:** A 1-unit change in soil pH produces a 59.16 mV change at 25 °C. At 30 °C the slope becomes **60.15 mV/pH** — this is why pH transmitters have **automatic temperature compensation (ATC)** in KTU lab experiments. ## 3.3 Derivation of Capacitive Sensor → Voltage Mapping **Step 1 — Capacitance of a cylindrical probe.** $$C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$$ **Step 2 — The probe is part of an LC tank** with fixed inductance $L_0$. $$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_0 C}}$$ **Step 3 — Using a frequency-to-voltage converter** with transfer constant $k_f$: $$V_{out} = k_f \cdot f = \dfrac{k_f}{2\pi\sqrt{L_0 C}}$$ **Step 4 — Substituting $C$:** $$V_{out} = \dfrac{k_f}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L_0 L}}$$ Because $\varepsilon_r$ rises monotonically with water content, $C$ rises, $f$ falls, and $V_{out}$ falls — a **monotonic inverse** relationship. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for KTU Viva | **Parameter** | **Best Voltage Method** | **Typical Range** | **Resolution** | **KTU Lab Setup** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Soil moisture (vol.) | Capacitive / FDR | 0 – 60 % VWC | ±2 % | Arduino + capacitive probe | | Soil pH | Glass ISE | 3 – 9 pH | ±0.01 | pH electrode + BNC + ADC | | Soil EC | 4-electrode AC | 0 – 5 mS/cm | ±0.01 mS/cm | Conductivity probe + LM358 | | NPK (Nitrate) | Nitrate ISE | 1 – 1000 ppm | ±5 % | ISE + REF electrode | | Leaf wetness | Resistive grid | 0 / 1 (binary) | Boolean | Resistive mesh + comparator | | Fruit firmness | Piezo / voltage | 0 – 100 N | ±0.5 N | Load cell + INA125 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Voltage-based Soil Sensor Node ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Soil / Plant
Process Variable] --> nodeB[Transducer Stage
R, C or ISE] nodeB --> nodeC[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier + Filter] nodeC --> nodeD[ADC
12-bit, 0-3.3 V] nodeD --> nodeE[Microcontroller
Arduino / STM32] nodeE --> nodeF[Display / LoRa / WiFi] nodeE --> nodeG[Decision Logic
Threshold Compare] nodeG --> nodeH[Actuator
Pump / Valve] nodeX[Excitation Source
AC 1 kHz or DC 5 V] --> nodeB nodeY[Reference Electrode
for ISE only] --> nodeB ``` ## 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge with Soil Moisture Sensor ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bridge[Wheatstone Bridge] nodeVs[Vs = 5 V DC] --- nodeR1[R1 Fixed] nodeVs --- nodeR3[R3 Fixed] nodeR1 --- nodeA[Node A] nodeR3 --- nodeB[Node B] nodeA --- nodeRx[Rx Soil Sensor] nodeB --- nodeR2[R2 Fixed] nodeRx --- nodeGND[GND] nodeR2 --- nodeGND end nodeA --- nodeDiff[INA125 / AD620
Instrumentation Amplifier] nodeB --- nodeDiff nodeDiff --> nodeADC[16-bit ADC] nodeADC --> nodeMCU[Microcontroller] ``` ## 4.3 pH Measurement Signal Chain (ISE → Voltage → ADC) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeSoil[Soil Slurry] --> nodeGlass[Glass pH Electrode
High Impedance ~ 50 Mohm] nodeGlass --> nodeRef[Reference Electrode
KCl Saturated] nodeGlass --> nodePre[FET Input Op-Amp
LMC662 / OPA129] nodeRef --> nodePre nodePre --> nodeATC[Temperature Compensation
PT100 + Lookup Table] nodeATC --> nodeAMP[Gain Stage x5] nodeAMP --> nodeFilter[2nd Order LPF fc=10 Hz] nodeFilter --> nodeADC[ADC 0-3.3 V] nodeADC --> nodeLCD[LCD Display pH Value] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Decision Flow for Choosing a Voltage Method ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart[Agricultural Parameter?] --> nodeQ1{Solid or Liquid?} nodeQ1 -- Solid Soil --> nodeQ2{Need moisture content?} nodeQ1 -- Liquid Nutrient --> nodeQ3{pH or ions?} nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeR1[Capacitive FDR Probe] nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeR2[4-Electrode EC Probe] nodeQ3 -- pH --> nodeR3[Glass ISE with Nernst Readout] nodeQ3 -- Specific Ion --> nodeR4[Ion-Selective FET ISFET] nodeQ3 -- Total Salts --> nodeR5[Conductivity Cell] nodeR1 --> nodeEnd[Condition + ADC + Calibrate] nodeR2 --> nodeEnd nodeR3 --> nodeEnd nodeR4 --> nodeEnd nodeR5 --> nodeEnd ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question] *State the Nernst equation and calculate the theoretical slope (mV/pH) of a glass pH electrode at 30 °C.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Nernst equation for a glass pH electrode is: $$E = E^0 - \dfrac{2.303\, RT}{F} \cdot pH$$ At $T = 303.15\;\text{K}$: $$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 303.15}{96485} = 60.15 \;\text{mV/pH}$$ Hence each unit change in pH produces a **60.15 mV** change in electrode voltage. **(1 mark — equation, 1 mark — substitution, 1 mark — final value.)** **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question] *List any three advantages of capacitive voltage-based soil moisture sensors over resistive gypsum-block sensors.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **No galvanic corrosion** — capacitive sensors do not pass DC current through soil, avoiding electrode degradation. **(1 mark)** 2. **Insensitive to soil salinity** — capacitive measurement depends on dielectric permittivity, not ionic conductivity. **(1 mark)** 3. **Better long-term stability and faster response** — no electrolyte to dissolve, hence suitable for continuous field deployment. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-3 Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the working principle of a Wheatstone bridge with a neat diagram. Derive the balance condition and the expression for the off-balance output voltage when one arm changes by $\Delta R$.* **Model Solution:** A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistive arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ connected in a diamond configuration with an excitation source $V_s$ across one diagonal and a detector across the other. **(1 mark — definition + diagram)** The differential output voltage is: $$V_{AB} = V_s \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4} \right) = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$$ Derivation (as in §3.1). **(3 marks — derivation)** Balance condition: $V_{AB}=0$ when $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$. **(1 mark)** Off-balance case: Let $R_1 \to R_1 + \Delta R$. For small $\Delta R$ and a symmetric bridge ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$): $$\Delta V_{AB} \approx \dfrac{V_s}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$$ **(2 marks — approximation and final expression)** **Q.A(b)** [7 Marks] *A capacitive soil moisture sensor is modeled as a cylindrical capacitor with $a = 2\;\text{mm}$, $b = 4\;\text{mm}$, $L = 50\;\text{mm}$. When dry, $\varepsilon_r = 4$; when saturated, $\varepsilon_r = 28$. Compute the dry and saturated capacitances and the corresponding frequencies of an $L_0 = 10\;\text{mH}$ LC oscillator.* **Model Solution:** Capacitance formula: $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ **(1 mark)** $\ln(b/a) = \ln(4/2) = \ln 2 = 0.6931$ $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}$, $L = 0.05\;\text{m}$ **Dry:** $\varepsilon_r = 4$ $$C_{dry} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 8.854\times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 0.05}{0.6931} = \dfrac{1.112\times 10^{-11}}{0.6931} = 1.604 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{F} \approx 16.0\;\text{pF}$$ **Saturated:** $\varepsilon_r = 28$ $$C_{sat} = 7 \times C_{dry} = 1.123 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{F} \approx 112.3\;\text{pF}$$ **(2 marks — both capacitances)** Oscillator frequency: $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_0 C}}$ with $L_0 = 10\;\text{mH} = 0.01\;\text{H}$ $$f_{dry} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.01 \times 1.604\times 10^{-11}}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 4.005 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 397.6\;\text{kHz}$$ $$f_{sat} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.01 \times 1.123\times 10^{-10}}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1.060 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 150.2\;\text{kHz}$$ **(2 marks — both frequencies)** **Conclusion:** Frequency drops by ~62 % from dry to saturated soil — easily measurable with a microcontroller timer-capture input, enabling **2 % VWC resolution** with simple calibration. **(2 marks — engineering interpretation)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **Q.B(a)** [7 Marks] *With a block diagram, describe a pH measurement system for agricultural soil. Derive the electrode voltage at pH 6.5 and pH 8.0 given $E^0 = 0.414\;\text{V}$ at 25 °C. Comment on the temperature compensation requirement.* **Model Solution:** Block diagram (3 blocks minimum — KTU marking norm): **pH Electrode → High-impedance FET input amplifier → ADC → Microcontroller → Display** **(2 marks — diagram and explanation)** Nernst equation: $E = E^0 - 0.05916 \cdot pH$ At pH 6.5: $$E_1 = 0.414 - 0.05916 \times 6.5 = 0.414 - 0.3845 = 0.0295\;\text{V} = 29.5\;\text{mV}$$ At pH 8.0: $$E_2 = 0.414 - 0.05916 \times 8.0 = 0.414 - 0.4733 = -0.0593\;\text{V} = -59.3\;\text{mV}$$ **(3 marks — calculations)** **Temperature Compensation:** The Nernst slope $2.303\,RT/F$ depends linearly on $T$. At 25 °C it is 59.16 mV/pH; at 35 °C it is 60.15 mV/pH; at 10 °C it is 56.18 mV/pH. Without compensation, a soil temperature shift of 10 °C introduces an apparent pH error of ~0.1 pH units — significant for crop decisions. A **PT100/PT1000** sensor and a lookup-table correction in firmware are mandatory. **(2 marks)** **Q.B(b)** [7 Marks] *Discuss the 4-electrode soil electrical conductivity (EC) measurement technique. Why is AC excitation preferred over DC? A soil sample has measured resistance $R = 1.2\;\text{k}\Omega$ using a conductivity cell with $K_{cell} = 1.05\;\text{cm}^{-1}$. Compute the EC and comment on the suitability of this soil for paddy cultivation.* **Model Solution:** **4-Electrode Method:** Two outer electrodes inject AC current; two inner electrodes sense the voltage drop. This separates the current-carrying and voltage-sensing paths, eliminating contact-resistance and polarization errors. **(2 marks)** **Why AC Excitation:** DC current polarizes the electrode-electrolyte interface, forming a double layer that adds an error voltage and corrodes electrodes. AC (typically 1 kHz) alternates polarity fast enough to prevent net polarization, while remaining slow enough that the resistance is still accurately measured. **(2 marks)** **Calculation:** $$\sigma = \dfrac{K_{cell}}{R} = \dfrac{1.05}{1200} = 8.75 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{S/cm} = 0.875\;\text{mS/cm}$$ **(2 marks)** **Agricultural Interpretation:** Paddy (rice) requires standing water and tolerates EC up to 2 mS/cm; ideal range is **0.5 – 1.5 mS/cm**. The measured 0.875 mS/cm indicates a **slightly low-salinity soil, well-suited for paddy cultivation**. For salt-sensitive crops like grapes (threshold 1.0 mS/cm), this soil would also be acceptable. **(1 mark)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Voltage-based Methods):** > 1. **Sign Error in Nernst Equation:** Many students write $E = E^0 + 0.059\,pH$ instead of $E = E^0 - 0.059\,pH$. This leads to inverted pH readings and **complete loss of 3 marks** in pH problems. > 2. **Unit Mismatch in Bridge:** Mixing $\Omega$ and k$\Omega$, or forgetting to convert mS/cm to S/cm. Always write units at the end of every numerical step. > 3. **Forgetting ln(b/a) in Cylindrical Capacitance:** Using parallel-plate formula $C = \varepsilon A/d$ for a soil probe leads to gross errors. > 4. **Neglecting Temperature Compensation:** pH and EC probes both drift with temperature; KTU expects explicit ATC discussion. > 5. **Missing Calibration Steps:** Capacitive sensors require **two-point calibration** (air = 0 %, water = 100 %). Skip this in the lab record and lose 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Voltage-based Methods** convert soil/plant parameters (moisture, pH, EC, ions) into measurable voltages using **Ohm's law**, **Nernst equation**, or **capacitive/inductive** principles. - **Wheatstone Bridge** is the foundational circuit; balance condition $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ gives zero detector voltage; off-balance gives parameter-proportional $V_{out}$. - **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^0 - 0.05916 \cdot pH$ at 25 °C; slope changes to **60.15 mV/pH at 30 °C** and **56.18 mV/pH at 10 °C** — **always include temperature compensation**. - **Capacitive Sensors:** $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$; converted to voltage via **LC oscillator + F-to-V converter**. - **Conductivity:** $\sigma = K_{cell}/R$; use **AC excitation** to avoid polarization. - **KTU Lab Equipment:** pH electrode, conductivity cell, capacitive probe, INA125/AD620 instrumentation amplifier, Arduino/STM32 MCU, 12–16 bit ADC. - **Calibration Mandatories:** Two-point calibration (dry/wet) for moisture; buffer solutions pH 4, 7, 10 for pH; KCl standard for EC. - **Design Trade-offs:** Glass pH electrodes are accurate but fragile; ISFETs are rugged but require pH-Correction firmware; capacitive moisture sensors are cheap but need temperature correction above 40 °C. - **Typical KTU Numeric Values to Memorize:** - Faraday constant $F = 96485\;\text{C/mol}$ - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}$ - Water relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ - Dry soil $\varepsilon_r \approx 4$ - Nernst slope at 25 °C = $59.16\;\text{mV/pH}$

topics.title: Thermal-based Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal-based Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal-based Method** in agricultural instrumentation refers to the set of measurement, sensing, and analytical techniques that exploit **temperature, heat flow, thermal radiation, or thermal properties** of agricultural entities (soil, plants, animals, grains, and microclimate) for monitoring, control, and decision-making.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this topic is broadly classified into four engineering sub-domains:

1. **Contact Temperature Sensing** — Thermocouples, Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs), Thermistors, and Semiconductor sensors used to measure soil, air, canopy, grain, and animal body temperature.
2. **Non-Contact (Radiation) Pyrometry** — Infrared (IR) thermometers and thermal cameras that infer surface temperature from emitted thermal radiation.
3. **Soil Thermal Property Measurement** — Determination of **thermal conductivity ($k$)**, **thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$)**, and **volumetric heat capacity ($C_v$)** of soils using transient line heat source (probe) and guarded hot plate methods.
4. **Thermal-Time / Energy-Balance Applications** — Growing Degree Days (GDD), Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI), soil heat flux estimation, and frost prediction.

The governing physical law across all these methods is the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law of Thermal Radiation** (for non-contact) and **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** (for contact/property measurement), both of which the KTU board frequently tests as 2- and 7-mark conceptual items.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Mandatory for KTU Numerical Questions):**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant ($\sigma$):** $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W} \, \text{m}^{-2} \, \text{K}^{-4}$
> - **Typical soil thermal conductivity range:** $0.2 - 2.0 \, \text{W} \, \text{m}^{-1} \, \text{K}^{-1}$
> - **Typical plant canopy temperature range (mid-day):** $20 - 35^{\circ}\text{C}$ in tropical Kerala conditions
> - **Standard reference temperature for CWSI:** $T_{\text{air}}$ (dry-bulb) and **$T_{\text{wet}}$ (wet-bulb)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Farmer's Hand Test":**
> Imagine a traditional Kuttanad farmer walking into his paddy field at noon. He intuitively places his hand on the soil — if it feels *cool*, he knows irrigation is working (transpiration is cooling the canopy/soil). If it feels *hot*, the crop is likely water-stressed. This **touch-based heat-sensing** is exactly the principle behind thermal-based methods — they simply replace the farmer's hand with calibrated **thermocouples, IR sensors, and heat flux plates** that quantify what the hand feels qualitatively. The instrument's job is to convert *thermal intuition* into *numerical, repeatable, loggable data* for precision agriculture.

In short: **Thermal methods turn "warm vs. cool" judgments into engineering measurements** with $\pm 0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Four Pillars of Thermal Instrumentation

### Pillar 1: Contact Temperature Sensing (The "Thermometer Family")

- **Thermocouple (Seebeck Effect):** Two dissimilar metals joined at a *hot junction* produce an EMF ($E$) proportional to the temperature difference ($\Delta T$) between the hot and cold (reference) junction. **Type T (Cu–Constantan)** and **Type K (Chromel–Alumel)** are the most common in agriculture.
  - *Why it matters:* Cheap, rugged, wide range ($-200^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $1350^{\circ}\text{C}$), ideal for **soil probes at multiple depths** and **grain silo thermometry**.
- **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):** Pure metal (usually platinum) whose resistance increases linearly with temperature. Pt100 and Pt1000 are standards.
  - *Why it matters:* High accuracy ($\pm 0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$), excellent long-term stability — used in **greenhouse climate control** and **research-grade soil temperature monitoring**.
- **Thermistor (NTC type):** Semiconductor bead with resistance that *decreases* non-linearly (exponentially) with temperature.
  - *Why it matters:* Extremely high sensitivity ($\sim 10 \times$ more than RTD per °C), used in **leaf-temperature clips, poultry-house ambient sensors, and frost alarms**.
- **Semiconductor IC Sensor (LM35, DS18B20):** Bandgap-based, outputs voltage or digital count proportional to temperature.
  - *Why it matters:* Integrated, low-power, ideal for **IoT-based field sensor nodes** (very important for KTU's 2024 NEP-aligned IoT agriculture modules).

### Pillar 2: Non-Contact Infrared (IR) Thermometry

- All bodies above **0 K** emit electromagnetic radiation governed by the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**.
- An IR thermometer focuses emitted radiation from a target onto a thermopile detector, producing a voltage proportional to the **4th power of absolute temperature**.
- **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** is the critical agricultural parameter — wet soil: $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$, dry soil: $\varepsilon \approx 0.93$, green leaf: $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$, animal hide: $\varepsilon \approx 0.95 - 0.98$.
- *Real-world use:* **Canopy temperature measurement** to compute **CWSI**, irrigation scheduling in coconut/arecanut plantations of Kerala.

### Pillar 3: Soil Thermal Property Measurement (Probe Method)

- A **line heat source probe** (single or dual needle, typically 1.3 mm diameter, 60–150 mm long) is inserted into the soil.
- A constant heat flux ($q$ in W/m) is applied, and the **temperature rise at the probe surface** is recorded as a function of time ($t$).
- The temperature response, fitted to Carslaw & Jaeger's infinite-line-source solution, yields the **thermal conductivity $k$** and **thermal diffusivity $\alpha$**.

### Pillar 4: Thermal-Time (GDD) and Energy-Balance Indices

- **Growing Degree Days (GDD)** is a heat-unit index used to predict crop phenology (flowering, maturity).
- **Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI)** integrates canopy and air temperature with vapor pressure deficit to flag irrigation needs.
- **Soil Heat Flux ($G$)** is measured using **heat flux plates** buried at 5–10 cm depth — feeds into the **surface energy balance equation** $R_n = H + LE + G$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference for Thermal-Based Methods in Agriculture (KTU High-Yield)**

| # | Quantity / Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Boundary / Use Condition |
|---|------------------------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Stefan–Boltzmann Radiated Power | $E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (0–1), $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W} \, \text{m}^{-2} \, \text{K}^{-4}$, $T$ in **K** | Used in IR thermometry; convert °C to K: $T_K = T_{^{\circ}C} + 273.15$ |
| 2 | Thermocouple EMF | $E = S(T) \cdot \Delta T$ | $S(T)$ = Seebeck coefficient ($\mu$V/°C) | $\Delta T = T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}}$ (cold-junction compensation required) |
| 3 | RTD Resistance | $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$ | $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$, $\alpha_{\text{Pt}} = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$ | Linear approximation valid $0 - 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 4 | Thermistor (NTC, Steinhart–Hart) | $1/T = A + B \ln R + C (\ln R)^{3}$ | $T$ in K, $R$ in $\Omega$ | High accuracy; $A, B, C$ calibrated by manufacturer |
| 5 | Line Heat Source Probe | $T(r, t) - T_0 = \frac{q}{4 \pi k} \left[ \ln t + \gamma + \ln \left(\frac{4 \alpha}{r^{2}}\right) - E_1 \right]$ | $q$ = heat input per unit length (W/m), $k$ = thermal conductivity (W m⁻¹ K⁻¹), $\alpha$ = diffusivity (m²/s), $r$ = probe radius, $\gamma = 0.5772$ (Euler constant) | Valid for $t \gg r^{2} / (4\alpha)$ (typically $t \geq 60$ s) |
| 6 | Soil Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = k / (\rho \, c_p)$ | $\rho$ = bulk density (kg/m³), $c_p$ = specific heat (J kg⁻¹ K⁻¹) | Used to compute $\alpha$ once $k$ and $\rho c_p$ are known |
| 7 | Growing Degree Days | $GDD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{T_{\max,i} + T_{\min,i}}{2} - T_{\text{base}} \right]$ | $T_{\text{base}}$ = crop base temp (e.g., rice: $10^{\circ}\text{C}$, maize: $10^{\circ}\text{C}$, coconut: $15^{\circ}\text{C}$) | Kerala rice Kharif: $T_{\text{base}} = 10^{\circ}\text{C}$, GDD-to-maturity $\approx 2000$ |
| 8 | Crop Water Stress Index | $CWSI = \frac{(T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}}) - D_1}{D_2 - D_1}$ | $D_1$ = lower bound (well-watered baseline), $D_2$ = upper bound (non-transpiring baseline) | $0 \leq CWSI \leq 1$; values $> 0.5$ indicate severe stress |
| 9 | Surface Energy Balance | $R_n = H + LE + G$ | $R_n$ = net radiation, $H$ = sensible heat, $LE$ = latent heat, $G$ = soil heat flux (all in W/m²) | Used in remote-sensing ET models (SEBAL, METRIC) |
| 10 | IR Thermometer Output | $V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot (T_{\text{target}}^{4} - T_{\text{det}}^{4})$ | $K$ = instrument constant, $T_{\text{det}}$ = detector (internal) temperature | Emissivity correction: $T_{\text{true}} = T_{\text{meas}} / \varepsilon^{1/4}$ (approx.) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Irrigation Scheduling:** CWSI from handheld IR thermometers saves 25–40% water in drip-irrigated banana, coconut, and mango orchards in Kerala.
- **Greenhouse Climate Control:** RTD-based air temperature sensors drive ventilation, fogging, and heating actuators in polyhouse vegetable production.
- **Post-Harvest Grain Storage:** Multi-point thermocouple arrays in silos detect hotspots that indicate fungal/insect activity — critical for FCI godowns.
- **Frost & Heat-Stress Warning:** Threshold alarms based on canopy/air temperature protect tea (Munnar) and cardamom plantations.
- **Soil Thermal Regime Mapping:** For design of underground biogas digesters, mushroom cultivation beds, and drip-line burial depth selection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI) from Energy Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Favourite (Dec 2023 / June 2024 pattern):** The CWSI derivation appears almost every cycle. Master the full derivation.

**Step 1 — Energy balance for a single leaf (or canopy):**

The net radiation $R_n$ absorbed by a leaf is partitioned into sensible heat ($H$), latent heat ($LE$), and a small storage term (neglected):

$$R_n = H + LE$$

**Step 2 — Express sensible and latent heat fluxes in terms of temperature difference and vapor pressure deficit (VPD):**

$$
H = \rho \, c_p \, \frac{(T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})}{r_a}
$$

$$
LE = \frac{\rho \, c_p \, VPD}{\gamma \, (r_a + r_c)}
$$

where $\rho$ = air density, $c_p$ = specific heat of air, $r_a$ = aerodynamic resistance, $r_c$ = canopy (stomatal) resistance, $\gamma$ = psychrometric constant, $VPD$ = vapor pressure deficit.

**Step 3 — Define the lower (well-watered) and upper (non-transpiring) baselines:**

- **Lower baseline ($D_1$):** When the plant is well-watered, $r_c \to 0$ (stomata fully open), and the canopy is at its coolest possible temperature for that $VPD$:

$$D_1 = (T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})_{\min} = \frac{r_a \, R_n}{\rho \, c_p} - \frac{VPD}{\gamma}$$

- **Upper baseline ($D_2$):** When the plant is severely stressed, $r_c \to \infty$ (stomata fully closed), $LE \to 0$, and the canopy reaches its maximum possible temperature:

$$D_2 = (T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})_{\max} = \frac{r_a \, R_n}{\rho \, c_p}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into the dimensionless CWSI:**

$$
CWSI = \frac{(T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})_{\text{measured}} - D_1}{D_2 - D_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**
- $CWSI = 0 \Rightarrow$ no water stress
- $CWSI = 1 \Rightarrow$ maximum stress (transpiration halted)
- Typical irrigation trigger: $CWSI \geq 0.3 - 0.5$ depending on crop.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Conductivity from Line Heat Source Probe

**Step 1 — Governing equation:** The transient heat conduction in a homogeneous, isotropic medium around an infinite line source is:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial r^{2}} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial T}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{\alpha}\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply initial and boundary conditions:**
- Initially, $T(r, 0) = T_0$ for all $r > 0$.
- For $t > 0$, heat is injected at a constant rate $q$ per unit length of the line source.
- The far-field boundary is $T(\infty, t) = T_0$.

**Step 3 — Use the instantaneous point source (or line source) Green's function solution.** For an infinite line source, the standard Carslaw–Jaeger solution is:

$$
\Delta T(r, t) = T(r, t) - T_0 = \frac{q}{4 \pi k} \left[ -\gamma - \ln\!\left(\frac{r^{2}}{4 \alpha t}\right) + E_1\!\left(\frac{r^{2}}{4 \alpha t}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify for the long-time limit.** When $t \gg r^{2}/(4\alpha)$ (e.g., $t > 60$ s for a 1.3 mm probe in typical soil), the exponential integral $E_1 \to 0$, and the expression reduces to the **working equation**:

$$
\Delta T(t) = T(r, t) - T_0 = \frac{q}{4 \pi k} \left[ \ln t + \gamma + \ln\!\left(\frac{4 \alpha}{r^{2}}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to extract $k$ and $\alpha$:**

$$
\Delta T(t) = \frac{q}{4 \pi k} \ln t + \frac{q}{4 \pi k}\left[ \gamma + \ln\!\left(\frac{4 \alpha}{r^{2}}\right) \right]
$$

This is a **linear equation** of the form $y = m \cdot x + c$ where:
- $y = \Delta T(t)$, $x = \ln t$
- **Slope** $m = \dfrac{q}{4 \pi k} \implies \boxed{k = \dfrac{q}{4 \pi m}}$
- **Intercept** $c = m\left[\gamma + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 \alpha}{r^{2}}\right)\right] \implies \boxed{\alpha = \dfrac{r^{2}}{4} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{c}{m} - \gamma\right)}$

**Step 6 — Field procedure:** Plot measured $\Delta T$ vs. $\ln t$, fit a straight line, and compute $k$ and $\alpha$ from slope and intercept.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem (PEICT753 Model — Dec 2024 style):**
> A line heat source probe of radius $r = 1.3 \, \text{mm}$ is embedded in a sandy loam soil. A constant heat input $q = 12.0 \, \text{W/m}$ is applied. The recorded temperature rise is:
> $t_1 = 60 \, \text{s}, \; \Delta T_1 = 1.80^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $t_2 = 600 \, \text{s}, \; \Delta T_2 = 3.65^{\circ}\text{C}$.
> Calculate the **thermal conductivity $k$** and **thermal diffusivity $\alpha$** of the soil.

**Step 1 — Slope of $\Delta T$ vs. $\ln t$:**

$$
m = \frac{\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1}{\ln t_2 - \ln t_1} = \frac{3.65 - 1.80}{\ln(600) - \ln(60)}
$$

$$
m = \frac{1.85}{6.3969 - 4.0943} = \frac{1.85}{2.3026} = 0.8034 \, \text{K} \quad (\text{per unit ln-s})
$$

**Step 2 — Thermal conductivity:**

$$
k = \frac{q}{4 \pi m} = \frac{12.0}{4 \pi \times 0.8034} = \frac{12.0}{10.097}
$$

$$
\boxed{k \approx 1.188 \, \text{W} \, \text{m}^{-1} \, \text{K}^{-1}}
$$

This is consistent with **sandy loam** (typical $k \approx 1.0 - 1.5 \, \text{W} \, \text{m}^{-1} \, \text{K}^{-1}$).

**Step 3 — Intercept and diffusivity:**

Using point 1: $c = \Delta T_1 - m \ln t_1 = 1.80 - 0.8034 \times 4.0943 = 1.80 - 3.289 = -1.489 \, \text{K}$

$$
\alpha = \frac{r^{2}}{4} \exp\!\left(\frac{c}{m} - \gamma\right) = \frac{(1.3 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} \exp\!\left(\frac{-1.489}{0.8034} - 0.5772\right)
$$

$$
\alpha = 4.225 \times 10^{-7} \exp(-1.853 - 0.577) = 4.225 \times 10^{-7} \exp(-2.430)
$$

$$
\alpha = 4.225 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.0880 = 3.72 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^{2} \text{/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha \approx 3.72 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^{2} \text{s}^{-1}}
$$

This matches sandy-loam $\alpha \approx 3 - 6 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ order.

**Step 4 — Volumetric heat capacity (bonus):**

$$
\rho c_p = \frac{k}{\alpha} = \frac{1.188}{3.72 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 3.19 \times 10^{7} \, \text{J} \, \text{m}^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Contact Temperature Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.1 — KTU-Exam-Oriented Sensor Comparison (Typical 7-Mark Question)**

| Parameter | Thermocouple | RTD (Pt100) | Thermistor (NTC) | Semiconductor IC |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|
| Principle | Seebeck EMF | Resistance ∝ T | Exponential R–T | Bandgap voltage |
| Range (°C) | $-200$ to $+1350$ | $-200$ to $+850$ | $-50$ to $+150$ | $-55$ to $+150$ |
| Accuracy | $\pm 1 - 2^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.1 - 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.1 - 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.5 - 2^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Sensitivity | $40 - 60 \, \mu\text{V}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\sim 0.385 \, \Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $-4\%/^{\circ}\text{C}$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $10 \, \text{mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ (LM35) |
| Linearity | Moderate (poly-fit) | Excellent | Poor (needs Steinhart) | Good |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High | Low | Low |
| Best Ag Use | Soil profile, silos, compost | Greenhouse air, lab | Leaf clip, frost alarm | IoT node, drone |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Thermal-Based Measurement System for Precision Agriculture

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping the data flow from the physical thermal signal in the agricultural environment to the final actionable output (irrigation trigger, climate control, phenology prediction).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Domain: Soil / Plant / Air / Grain / Animal] --> B[Thermal Signal: Temperature T or Heat Flux q]
    B --> C{Sensor Type Selection}
    C --> D1[Contact Sensor: Thermocouple]
    C --> D2[Contact Sensor: RTD Pt100]
    C --> D3[Contact Sensor: NTC Thermistor]
    C --> D4[Contact Sensor: Semiconductor LM35]
    C --> D5[Non-Contact Sensor: IR Thermometer / Thermal Camera]
    C --> D6[Soil Probe: Line Heat Source for k and alpha]
    D1 --> E[Signal Conditioning: Cold Junction Comp + Amplifier]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> F[Emissivity Correction Block: T_true = T_meas / eps^0.25]
    D6 --> G[Transient Solver: Carslaw-Jaeger Equation]
    E --> H[ADC: Analog to Digital Conversion]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Microcontroller / Data Logger]
    I --> J1[Display and Local Storage]
    I --> J2[Wireless IoT Module: LoRa or GSM]
    I --> J3[Cloud Database and Dashboard]
    I --> K1[Algorithm 1: GDD Computation]
    I --> K2[Algorithm 2: CWSI Computation]
    I --> K3[Algorithm 3: Soil Heat Flux Mapping]
    K1 --> L[Decision Support Output]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M1[Automated Irrigation Valve Control]
    L --> M2[Greenhouse Fan and Fogger Trigger]
    L --> M3[Frost or Heat Stress SMS Alert to Farmer]
    J2 --> M4[Remote Mobile App Notification to KTU Researcher]
    J3 --> M4
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Infrared Thermometer for Canopy Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Canopy / Leaf Surface at T_canopy] --> B[IR Optics: Germanium Lens, FOV 5 to 20 deg]
    B --> C[Thermopile Detector: Absorbs Long-Wave IR 8 to 14 micrometer]
    C --> D[Low-Noise Preamplifier with T_det Compensation]
    D --> E[Microcontroller: Apply Stefan-Boltzmann and Emissivity]
    E --> F[LCD Display of T_canopy in degree C]
    E --> G[Serial Output to Data Logger]
    G --> H[CWSI Calculator: Combine with T_air and VPD]
    H --> I[Irrigation Decision: Trigger if CWSI exceeds 0.4]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Line Heat Source Probe Measurement Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Insert Probe into Soil at Field Capacity] --> S1[Record Initial Equilibrium Temperature T0 over 60 s]
    S1 --> S2[Apply Constant Heat Input q W per m for 600 s]
    S2 --> S3[Log Temperature T versus Time t at 1 Hz Sampling]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Delta T versus ln t]
    S4 --> S5[Fit Linear Regression: y = m x + c]
    S5 --> S6[Compute k = q over 4 pi m]
    S5 --> S7[Compute alpha from intercept c and gamma = 0.5772]
    S6 --> S8[Report Soil Thermal Conductivity in W per m K]
    S7 --> S9[Report Soil Thermal Diffusivity in m squared per s]
    S8 --> S10[End: Store in Farm Database]
    S9 --> S10
```

---
