<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 style]**

### **Q1. Define Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI) and explain its significance in irrigation scheduling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CWSI is a **dimensionless thermal index** that quantifies the water status of a crop based on the **difference between canopy and air temperature** ($T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}}$), normalized by the theoretical maximum and minimum temperature differences for the prevailing vapor pressure deficit (VPD).

$$
CWSI = \frac{(T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}})_{\text{measured}} - D_1}{D_2 - D_1}
$$

where $D_1$ and $D_2$ are the lower (well-watered) and upper (severely stressed) baselines.

**Significance:**
- Non-destructive, real-time indicator of plant water status.
- Values of $CWSI \geq 0.3 - 0.5$ are used as **automatic irrigation triggers** in drip and sprinkler systems, enabling **20–40% water savings** — particularly relevant for Kerala's water-scarce summer months.
- Integrates plant physiological response (stomatal closure) with micro-meteorology (VPD, $R_n$, $r_a$).

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — significance/application)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 style]**

### **Q2. List and briefly explain any three contact-type temperature sensors used in agricultural instrumentation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Thermocouple:** Two dissimilar metals (e.g., Chromel–Alumel Type K) joined at a *hot junction* produce a Seebeck EMF proportional to the temperature difference with the *cold junction*. Robust, wide range, ideal for **soil profile probes at multiple depths** and **grain silo hot-spot detection**.

2. **RTD (Pt100):** Pure platinum wire whose resistance increases linearly with temperature ($R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha T]$). High accuracy ($\pm 0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$), used in **greenhouse air-temperature control** and **research-grade soil temperature monitoring**.

3. **Thermistor (NTC):** Semiconductor with resistance that *decreases exponentially* with temperature (Steinhart–Hart equation). Very high sensitivity ($\sim -4\%/^{\circ}\text{C}$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$), used in **leaf-temperature clips**, **frost-warning alarms**, and **poultry house ambient sensors**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - End Semester Pattern, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of an **infrared (IR) thermometer** used for canopy temperature measurement in precision agriculture. Discuss the role of **emissivity** and **Stefan–Boltzmann's law** in its operation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Crop Water Stress Index (CWSI)** starting from the leaf energy balance equation. A coconut plantation in Thrissur records the following on a summer afternoon: $T_{\text{canopy}} = 33.5^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_{\text{air}} = 31.0^{\circ}\text{C}$, $D_1 = -1.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $D_2 = 4.5^{\circ}\text{C}$. Compute CWSI and recommend an irrigation action. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Block Diagram & Working:**

An IR thermometer consists of the following cascaded functional blocks:

1. **IR Optics (Germanium or Silicon Lens):** Collects the long-wave infrared radiation (8–14 μm atmospheric window) emitted by the canopy and focuses it onto the detector. A defined **field of view (FOV)**, typically 5°–20°, determines the target spot size.
2. **Thermopile Detector (Hot–Cold Junction Array):** Converts the absorbed IR radiation into a small EMF via the Seebeck effect. Output is of the order of microvolts to millivolts.
3. **Reference Temperature Sensor (Internal Thermistor/Si Diode):** Measures the detector body temperature $T_{\text{det}}$ for cold-junction compensation.
4. **Low-Noise Preamplifier & Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies the thermopile output and subtracts the ambient $T_{\text{det}}$ contribution.
5. **Microcontroller / ADC:** Applies the inverse Stefan–Boltzmann equation and emissivity correction, then displays the result on an LCD or outputs via serial/UART.

**Governing Physics — Stefan–Boltzmann Law:**

The total radiant exitance from a target surface is:

$$
E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the **emissivity** (ratio of actual emission to blackbody emission), $\sigma$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant ($5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-4}$), and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin.

The IR thermometer measures the difference between target and detector radiation:

$$
V_{\text{out}} \propto \varepsilon \, \sigma \left( T_{\text{target}}^{4} - T_{\text{det}}^{4} \right)
$$

**Role of Emissivity:**

- Emissivity of **green leaves** is typically $\varepsilon \approx 0.97 - 0.98$ (nearly blackbody in the 8–14 μm band).
- Wet soil: $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$; dry soil: $\varepsilon \approx 0.93$.
- If the instrument is set to $\varepsilon = 1$ but the target is $\varepsilon < 1$, the thermometer **under-reads** the temperature.
- Correction: $T_{\text{true}} \approx T_{\text{meas}} / \varepsilon^{1/4}$ (for low $T_{\text{det}}$).
- For precision work, field calibration with a contact thermocouple and a blackbody tape ($\varepsilon \approx 0.96$) is recommended.

**Typical Use:** Handheld IR thermometers (e.g., Apogee MI-210, FLIR TG-series) measure canopy temperature from 1–5 m distance for irrigation scheduling.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Derivation of CWSI:**

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final working equation is:

$$
CWSI = \frac{(T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}}) - D_1}{D_2 - D_1}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $T_{\text{canopy}} = 33.5^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_{\text{air}} = 31.0^{\circ}\text{C}$, $D_1 = -1.5^{\circ}\text{C}$, $D_2 = 4.5^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Compute measured temperature difference:**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{meas}} = T_{\text{canopy}} - T_{\text{air}} = 33.5 - 31.0 = 2.5^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator ($D_2 - D_1$):**

$$
D_2 - D_1 = 4.5 - (-1.5) = 6.0^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{meas}} - D_1 = 2.5 - (-1.5) = 4.0^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute CWSI:**

$$
CWSI = \frac{4.0}{6.0} = 0.667
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation & Recommendation:**

- $CWSI = 0.667$ lies in the **moderate-to-severe stress** range (0.5–0.8).
- **Recommendation:** Trigger **immediate drip irrigation** for the coconut palms. For adult palms, apply 150–200 L/palm through the drip ring. Re-measure canopy temperature after 24 hours to confirm recovery ($CWSI$ should drop below 0.3).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **line heat source probe method** for the measurement of **soil thermal conductivity** and **thermal diffusivity**. State clearly the assumptions and governing equation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A probe of radius $1.0 \, \text{mm}$ is inserted into a clay loam soil. Heat is supplied at $q = 10.0 \, \text{W/m}$. The following temperature rise data is recorded:

| Time $t$ (s) | 60 | 120 | 300 | 600 | 1200 |
|--------------|----|----|-----|-----|------|
| $\Delta T$ (°C) | 1.45 | 1.95 | 2.55 | 3.05 | 3.55 |

Plot $\Delta T$ vs. $\ln t$, find the best-fit slope, and hence determine $k$ and $\alpha$. Take Euler's constant $\gamma = 0.5772$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Principle:** A long, thin, cylindrical heater (the "line source") is inserted into the soil. A constant heat flux $q$ per unit length is applied, and the temperature rise at the probe-soil interface is recorded as a function of time. The thermal properties of the soil are back-calculated from the temperature-time response.

**Diagram (Textual Description):**

A horizontal probe consisting of:
- An **inner heater wire** (Constantan or Nichrome) running the full length of the probe.
- A **central thermocouple** (Type T or K) at the midpoint measuring the probe surface temperature.
- **Outer stainless-steel sheath** (radius $r$) for mechanical rigidity and waterproofing.
- Lead wires exiting at one end, connected to a constant-current source (for $q$) and a data logger.

**Assumptions:**
1. Soil is **homogeneous, isotropic, and infinite** in extent (probe is far from surface and other boundaries).
2. Soil thermal properties ($k, \alpha$) are **constant** during the test (no phase change, no water movement).
3. The probe has **negligible thermal mass** compared to the soil (or is compensated).
4. Heat is applied as a **constant, continuous** line source for time $t$.
5. Valid only for **long times** $t \gg r^{2}/(4\alpha)$, typically $t > 60$ s.

**Governing Equation (working form, long-time limit):**

$$
\Delta T(t) = \frac{q}{4 \pi k} \left[ \ln t + \gamma + \ln\!\left(\frac{4 \alpha}{r^{2}}\right) \right]
$$

Plotting $\Delta T$ vs. $\ln t$ yields a straight line with **slope $m = q / (4\pi k)$** and **intercept $c = m[\gamma + \ln(4\alpha/r^{2})]$**. Hence:

$$
k = \frac{q}{4 \pi m}, \quad \alpha = \frac{r^{2}}{4} \exp\!\left(\frac{c}{m} - \gamma\right)
$$

**Field Procedure:**
1. Pre-insert the probe into pre-drilled pilot hole of correct depth.
2. Allow thermal equilibrium (5–10 min) and record $T_0$.
3. Switch on constant-power supply and log $\Delta T$ every second for 10 minutes.
4. Plot $\Delta T$ vs. $\ln t$ and perform linear regression to extract $m$ and $c$.
5. Compute $k$ and $\alpha$.

**Advantages:** Fast, minimally destructive, suitable for in-situ field measurements in cropped soils.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Step 1 — Convert time to $\ln t$:**

| $t$ (s) | 60 | 120 | 300 | 600 | 1200 |
|--------|----|-----|-----|-----|------|
| $\ln t$ | 4.094 | 4.787 | 5.704 | 6.397 | 7.090 |
| $\Delta T$ (°C) | 1.45 | 1.95 | 2.55 | 3.05 | 3.55 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression for slope $m$:**

Using least-squares fit (or simply $\Delta T$ vs $\ln t$ being nearly linear):

$$
m \approx \frac{\Delta T_{\text{last}} - \Delta T_{\text{first}}}{\ln t_{\text{last}} - \ln t_{\text{first}}} = \frac{3.55 - 1.45}{7.090 - 4.094} = \frac{2.10}{2.996} \approx 0.701 \, \text{K} \text{ per unit ln-s}
$$

A more rigorous least-squares fit (using all 5 points) yields $m \approx 0.70 \, \text{K}$ and intercept $c \approx \Delta T_1 - m \ln t_1 = 1.45 - 0.701 \times 4.094 = -1.420 \, \text{K}$.

**Step 3 — Thermal conductivity:**

$$
k = \frac{q}{4 \pi m} = \frac{10.0}{4 \pi \times 0.701} = \frac{10.0}{8.808}
$$

$$
\boxed{k \approx 1.135 \, \text{W} \, \text{m}^{-1} \, \text{K}^{-1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Thermal diffusivity:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{r^{2}}{4} \exp\!\left(\frac{c}{m} - \gamma\right) = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} \exp\!\left(\frac{-1.420}{0.701} - 0.5772\right)
$$

$$
\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \exp(-2.026 - 0.577) = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \exp(-2.603)
$$

$$
\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.0741 = 1.85 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^{2} \text{/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha \approx 1.85 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^{2} \text{s}^{-1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric heat capacity (bonus):**

$$
\rho c_p = \frac{k}{\alpha} = \frac{1.135}{1.85 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 6.13 \times 10^{7} \, \text{J} \, \text{m}^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Interpretation:** This $\rho c_p$ value is consistent with a wet clay loam — the high water content increases the volumetric heat capacity significantly. The relatively low $k$ and $\alpha$ confirm clay's role as a **thermal insulator** — important for designing subsurface drip-line burial depth and biogas digester insulation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (PEICT753 Module 3):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in Stefan–Boltzmann calculations. Examiner's deduction: **1 mark minimum**.
> 2. **Mixing up CWSI numerator and denominator** — the formula is **(measured − lower)/(upper − lower)**, NOT the reverse.
> 3. **Ignoring the long-time validity condition** $t \gg r^{2}/(4\alpha)$ for the probe method. Examiners often specifically ask this; failing to state the condition loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using diameter instead of radius** of the probe in the diffusivity equation.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply emissivity correction** when computing true canopy temperature from IR thermometer readings.
> 6. **Not stating units clearly** in numerical answers (always write W m⁻¹ K⁻¹ for $k$, m² s⁻¹ for $\alpha$).
> 7. **Confusing Growing Degree Days base temperature** for different crops — coconut: 15°C, rice/maize: 10°C, tea: 12°C. Wrong base = full 2 marks lost.
> 8. **In CWSI problems, the examiner expects both the numerical value AND the interpretation/action** — a numerical answer without "trigger irrigation" loses marks.
> 9. **Not drawing/mentioning the field of view (FOV) of IR thermometers** when explaining the optical arrangement.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Glance Summary — Module 3: Thermal-Based Methods**

- ✅ Thermal-based methods convert **temperature, heat flux, or thermal radiation** into engineering measurements for agriculture.
- ✅ **Four pillars:** Contact sensors (TC/RTD/Thermistor/IC), IR Pyrometry, Soil Property Probe, and Thermal-Time/Energy-Balance Indices.
- ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann Law:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ — non-contact thermometry. **$\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-4}$.** Always use **K**, not °C.
- ✅ **Thermocouple:** Cheap, rugged, wide range. **Type K** = Chromel–Alumel. Needs **cold-junction compensation**.
- ✅ **RTD:** $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha T]$; Pt100 ($R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$, $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$). Best accuracy, used in greenhouse control.
- ✅ **Thermistor (NTC):** Exponential R–T, Steinhart–Hart equation. High sensitivity, leaf clips, frost alarms.
- ✅ **IR Thermometer:** Measures $\varepsilon \sigma (T_{\text{target}}^{4} - T_{\text{det}}^{4})$. Emissivity correction **mandatory** ($\varepsilon_{\text{green leaf}} \approx 0.97 - 0.98$).
- ✅ **CWSI:** $CWSI = \dfrac{(T_c - T_a) - D_1}{D_2 - D_1}$. Trigger irrigation at $CWSI \geq 0.3 - 0.5$.
- ✅ **Growing Degree Days:** $GDD = \sum \left[\frac{T_{\max} + T_{\min}}{2} - T_{\text{base}}\right]$. Base temp: rice/maize 10°C, coconut 15°C, tea 12°C.
- ✅ **Probe Method (Transient Line Source):** Plot $\Delta T$ vs. $\ln t$, get slope $m$ and intercept $c$. Then $k = q/(4\pi m)$ and $\alpha = (r^{2}/4) \exp(c/m - \gamma)$ with $\gamma = 0.5772$.
- ✅ **Long-time validity:** $t \gg r^{2}/(4\alpha)$, typically $t > 60 \, \text{s}$ for a 1.3 mm probe.
- ✅ **Energy Balance:** $R_n = H + LE + G$ — drives remote-sensing ET models (SEBAL, METRIC).
- ✅ **Typical $k$ values:** Sand: $1.5 - 2.5$, Sandy loam: $1.0 - 1.5$, Clay loam: $0.8 - 1.2$, Peat: $0.2 - 0.5$ (all in $\text{W m}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$).
- ✅ **Typical $\alpha$ values:** Sand $\sim 10^{-6}$, Clay loam $\sim 10^{-7}$, Peat $\sim 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-8}$ (all in $\text{m}^{2} \text{s}^{-1}$).
- ✅ **Volumetric heat capacity:** $\rho c_p = k / \alpha$ — high water content = high $\rho c_p$.
- ✅ **Fourier's Law of Conduction:** $q = -k \nabla T$ — fundamental for all contact/property measurements.
- ✅ **KTU Favourite Topics (high-yield):** CWSI derivation, Probe method derivation + numerical, GDD formula, IR thermometer block diagram with emissivity.
- ✅ **IoT Linkage:** Semiconductor IC sensors (DS18B20, LM35) + LoRa/GSM modules = modern agricultural thermal IoT nodes — a KTU 2024 NEP-aligned trend.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Distribution and Management Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Water Distribution and Management Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Water Distribution and Management Control** in agricultural instrumentation refers to the integrated use of **sensors, actuators, controllers, and communication networks** to monitor, regulate, and optimize the conveyance and application of irrigation water from its source (well, canal, reservoir) to the crop root zone, ensuring **precise volumetric delivery**, minimal conveyance losses, and adherence to crop-water-demand schedules.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic falls under **Module 3** of *Instrumentation for Agriculture* (PEICT753) and is mapped to the following Course Outcomes:

| CO ID | Course Outcome (Bloom Level) |
|---|---|
| **CO3** | Design and analyse instrumentation schemes for water management (Apply / Analyse) |
| **CO4** | Evaluate the performance of automated irrigation systems (Evaluate) |

The three sub-systems that constitute this domain are:

1. **Water Conveyance & Distribution Network** — pipes, channels, emitters, sprinklers.
2. **Monitoring Sub-system** — soil-moisture sensors, flow meters, pressure transducers, weather stations.
3. **Control Sub-system** — solenoid valves, motorized valves, VFD-driven pumps, PLC/MCU/SCADA controllers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital IV Drip"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of a farm's water-distribution system exactly like the **IV drip used in a hospital**. The saline bag is the *water source*, the drip chamber regulates flow rate, the tube is the *pipe network*, the cannula is the *emitter/dripper*, and the nurse (who checks the patient and adjusts the drip) is the *controller + sensor loop*. Just as a nurse never pours the entire bottle at once, an automated irrigation system never floods the field — it supplies water in **measured pulses** dictated by the **soil-moisture feedback** (the patient's vitals).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of water at 20 °C**: $\rho = 998 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C**: $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Crop Water Requirement (CWR)** is normally expressed in **mm/day** or **m³/ha/day**.
- **Application Efficiency ($E_a$)**: typically **70–90 %** for drip, **60–75 %** for sprinkler, **30–50 %** for surface flood irrigation.
- **Standard emitter discharge**: **2, 4, 8 L/h** (most common in Indian KTU problem statements).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students are expected to compare **drip, sprinkler, and surface** irrigation on the basis of *discharge uniformity*, *application efficiency*, *energy consumed per m³ delivered*, and *suitability for different crops*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Automated Water Distribution System

The instrumentation chain operates as a closed-loop **Sense → Decide → Actuate** cycle:

1. **Sense** — Soil-moisture probes (TDR, capacitance, tensiometer), flow meters (electromagnetic / ultrasonic), pressure transmitters (piezoresistive), and weather stations feed raw analog / digital signals to the controller.
2. **Decide** — The PLC / Arduino / Raspberry-Pi compares the sensed value against the **set-point** (e.g., 25 % VWC for tomato) and computes the **ON-duration** of valves using a scheduling algorithm (time-based, volume-based, or feedback-based).
3. **Actuate** — Solenoid valves (12 V DC / 24 V AC) or motorized ball-valves open/close, while **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** modulates pump speed to maintain the design pressure at the farthest emitter.

### 2.1.1 Operational Modes

| Mode | Trigger | Control Variable | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual** | Operator | Valve position | Small farms, backup |
| **Time-Based** | RTC clock | Irrigation duration | Open-loop, scheduled |
| **Volume-Based** | Flow meter pulse count | Litres delivered | Greenhouses |
| **Feedback (Closed-Loop)** | Soil-moisture sensor | Volumetric Water Content (VWC) | Precision agriculture |
| **Evapotranspiration (ET)-Based** | Penman–Monteith / Hargreaves | mm/day deficit | Open field, orchards |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Code)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table verbatim — at least **one formula from this table appears in every KTU ESE paper from this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gravimetric Soil Moisture Content** ($\theta_g$) | $\theta_g = \dfrac{M_{\text{wet}} - M_{\text{dry}}}{M_{\text{dry}}}$ | dimensionless (g/g) |
| 2 | **Volumetric Water Content (VWC)** ($\theta_v$) | $\theta_v = \theta_g \times \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_w}$ | $\rho_b$ = bulk density (kg/m³), $\rho_w$ = water density |
| 3 | **Depth of Irrigation (Gross)** ($d_g$) | $d_g = \dfrac{d_n}{E_a}$ | $d_n$ = net depth (mm), $E_a$ = application efficiency |
| 4 | **Volume of Water per Hectare** ($V$) | $V = d_g \times 10 \; \text{m}^3/\text{ha}$ per 1 mm | converts mm-depth to m³/ha |
| 5 | **Emitter Discharge Variation** ($q_{\text{var}}$) | $q_{\text{var}} = \dfrac{q_{\max} - q_{\min}}{q_{\max}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient** ($C_u$) | $C_u = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\overline{\Delta q}}{\bar{q}}\right)$ | $\overline{\Delta q}$ = mean absolute deviation, $\bar{q}$ = mean discharge |
| 7 | **Distribution Uniformity** ($DU$) | $DU = \dfrac{q_{25\%}}{\bar{q}} \times 100$ | $q_{25\%}$ = mean of lowest quartile |
| 8 | **Darcy–Weisbach Head Loss** ($h_f$) | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | $f$ = friction factor, $L,D$ in m, $v$ in m/s |
| 9 | **Hazen-Williams Head Loss** ($h_f$) | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \, L \, Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \, D^{4.87}}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $D$ in m, $C \approx 140$ for PVC |
| 10 | **Emitter Flow–Pressure Relation** ($q$) | $q = k \, h^{x}$ | $k$ = discharge coeff., $h$ = pressure head (m), $x$ = exponent (0.5 turbulent, 1.0 laminar) |
| 11 | **Pump Power Requirement** ($P$) | $P = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, Q \, H_{\text{total}}}{\eta}$ | $H_{\text{total}}$ = total head (m), $\eta$ = pump efficiency |
| 12 | **Reference ET (Hargreaves)** ($ET_0$) | $ET_0 = 0.0023 (T_{\text{mean}} + 17.8) \sqrt{T_{\max} - T_{\min}} \, R_a$ | $R_a$ = extraterrestrial radiation (mm/day) |

> **Markdown Safeguard Note:** Any absolute-value notations in the derivations below are written as $\vert x \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command to prevent breakage of the markdown pipe-table structure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application | Benefit Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| **Greenhouse Cultivation** | Closed-loop drip with VWC sensor | 40–60 % water saving |
| **Open-field row crops** | Sprinkler + ET-based scheduling | Yield increase up to 25 % |
| **Orchards / Plantation** | Micro-jet with pressure-compensating drippers | Uniform distribution on slopes |
| **Hydroponics** | Recirculating NFT with EC/pH control | Zero discharge, 90 % water recycle |
| **Smart Village (KTU Project)** | IoT + LoRa + solar pump + cloud dashboard | Off-grid autonomous irrigation |

---

## 2.4 Why a "Closed-Loop" Beats an Open-Loop System

In an **open-loop** (timer-based) system, the controller has *no knowledge* of whether the rain has just soaked the field or whether a rodent has chewed through a lateral. It will irrigate on schedule regardless, wasting water and energy. In a **closed-loop** system, the soil-moisture sensor continuously reports VWC. When VWC $>$ **field capacity** the controller keeps valves shut; when VWC drops below the **permanent wilting point (PWP)**, the controller triggers an irrigation cycle whose duration is computed from the deficit. This is exactly the **negative-feedback principle** of classical control theory applied to agriculture.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CU)

The **Coefficient of Uniformity** is the single most important performance index for a drip-irrigation system and is **guaranteed to be asked for 7 marks** in KTU 2024 ESE.

### Step 1 — Define the Mean Discharge

Let $q_1, q_2, q_3, \ldots, q_n$ be the discharges (L/h) measured at $n$ emitters uniformly spaced along a lateral. The arithmetic mean is:

$$
\bar{q} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i
$$

### Step 2 — Define Absolute Deviations

For each emitter, compute the absolute deviation of its discharge from the mean:

$$
\Delta q_i = \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Mean Absolute Deviation

Average the absolute deviations across all emitters:

$$
\overline{\Delta q} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta q_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert
$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Uniformity Coefficient

Christiansen (1942) defined $C_u$ as the percentage of the mean that remains **after subtracting the mean relative deviation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_u &= 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\overline{\Delta q}}{\bar{q}}\right) \\[6pt]
    &= 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i}\right) \\[6pt]
    &= 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert q_i - \bar{q} \vert}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **[KTU-Style Solved Example]** Discharges measured at 5 emitters on a lateral are: $q_1=3.8$, $q_2=4.1$, $q_3=3.6$, $q_4=4.0$, $q_5=3.5$ L/h. Compute $C_u$.

**Step 5.1 — Mean discharge:**

$$
\bar{q} = \frac{3.8 + 4.1 + 3.6 + 4.0 + 3.5}{5} = \frac{19.0}{5} = 3.8 \;\text{L/h}
$$

**Step 5.2 — Absolute deviations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta q_1 &= \vert 3.8 - 3.8 \vert = 0.0 \\
\Delta q_2 &= \vert 4.1 - 3.8 \vert = 0.3 \\
\Delta q_3 &= \vert 3.6 - 3.8 \vert = 0.2 \\
\Delta q_4 &= \vert 4.0 - 3.8 \vert = 0.2 \\
\Delta q_5 &= \vert 3.5 - 3.8 \vert = 0.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.3 — Mean absolute deviation:**

$$
\overline{\Delta q} = \frac{0.0 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.3}{5} = \frac{1.0}{5} = 0.2 \;\text{L/h}
$$

**Step 5.4 — Apply $C_u$ formula:**

$$
C_u = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.2}{3.8}\right) = 100 \times (1 - 0.05263) = 94.74 \; \%
$$

Since $C_u > 90 \,\%$, the system is graded as **"Excellent"** under KTU/IS standards.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Net and Gross Depth of Irrigation

### Step 1 — Available Moisture in Root Zone

$$
d_{\text{available}} = (\theta_{FC} - \theta_{PWP}) \times D_r
$$

where $D_r$ = effective root-zone depth (mm), $\theta_{FC}$ = VWC at field capacity, $\theta_{PWP}$ = VWC at permanent wilting point.

### Step 2 — Readily Available Moisture (RAM)

A management-allowed deficit (MAD) of typically 30–50 % is introduced:

$$
d_{\text{RAM}} = d_{\text{available}} \times \text{MAD}
$$

### Step 3 — Net Depth to be Applied

$$
d_n = d_{\text{RAM}}
$$

### Step 4 — Account for Application Efficiency

The gross depth that the system must deliver compensates for losses (evaporation, wind drift, deep percolation):

$$
d_g = \frac{d_n}{E_a}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

> **[KTU-Style Solved Example]** For a tomato crop: $\theta_{FC}=0.32$, $\theta_{PWP}=0.14$, $D_r = 600$ mm, MAD = 0.40, $E_a = 0.85$. Compute the gross irrigation depth.

**Step 5.1 — Available moisture:**

$$
d_{\text{available}} = (0.32 - 0.14) \times 600 = 0.18 \times 600 = 108 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5.2 — Readily available moisture:**

$$
d_{\text{RAM}} = 108 \times 0.40 = 43.2 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5.3 — Net depth:**

$$
d_n = 43.2 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5.4 — Gross depth:**

$$
d_g = \frac{43.2}{0.85} = 50.82 \;\text{mm}
$$

The farmer must therefore command the system to deliver **50.82 mm** of water so that **43.2 mm** is *effectively* stored in the root zone.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Total Dynamic Head of a Pumping System

For a pump lifting water from a bore-well to a field, the total head is:

$$
H_{\text{total}} = H_s + H_f + H_v + \frac{v^2}{2g}
$$

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $H_s$ | Static suction + delivery lift | 15–50 m |
| $H_f$ | Friction head loss in pipes | 2–10 m |
| $H_v$ | Velocity head at outlets | 0.3–1.0 m |
| $v^2/2g$ | Kinetic head at discharge | small |

### Step 1 — Friction Loss (Hazen-Williams)

For a PVC main of length $L = 200$ m, diameter $D = 0.063$ m (63 mm OD class-2), carrying $Q = 5 \; \text{L/s} = 0.005 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, with Hazen-Williams coefficient $C = 140$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \frac{10.67 \times 200 \times (0.005)^{1.852}}{(140)^{1.852} \times (0.063)^{4.87}} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{2134 \times 4.886 \times 10^{-5}}{11953.7 \times 5.21 \times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{0.1043}{0.0623} \approx 1.67 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Head

If static lift $H_s = 25$ m, velocity head $H_v = 0.5$ m:

$$
H_{\text{total}} = 25 + 1.67 + 0.5 = 27.17 \;\text{m}
$$

### Step 3 — Power Required (Assuming 70 % pump + motor efficiency)

$$
P = \frac{998 \times 9.81 \times 0.005 \times 27.17}{0.70} = \frac{1329.3}{0.70} = 1899 \;\text{W} \approx 1.90 \;\text{kW}
$$

The student must specify a standard **2.2 kW** pump (next standard rating).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Irrigation Methods (Board-Exam Favourite)

| Parameter | Surface (Flood) | Sprinkler | Drip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application Efficiency ($E_a$) | 30–50 % | 60–75 % | 70–90 % |
| Water Saving vs. Flood | 0 % | 30–40 % | 50–70 % |
| Energy (kWh per m³) | 0.05–0.15 | 0.20–0.40 | 0.10–0.25 |
| Suitable Crops | Paddy, wheat | Cereals, pulses | Vegetables, orchards |
| Suitable Soil | Heavy / medium | Medium / light | All |
| Suitable Topography | Levelled | Sloped up to 15 % | Sloped up to 30 % |
| Capital Cost (₹/ha) | Low | Medium | High |
| Weed Control | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Fertigation Feasibility | Difficult | Possible | Highly efficient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever the question asks *"Compare drip and sprinkler"*, draw a **two-column table with AT LEAST 6 parameters** — efficiency, cost, energy, terrain suitability, crop suitability, weed control. Examiners reward **6-point comparisons with at least 2 numerical values**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automated Water Distribution System

The diagram below maps the **Sense → Decide → Actuate** loop with all instrumentation blocks explicitly labelled. This is the architecture examiners expect you to redraw in every 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[Water Source Layer]
        nodeA[Borewell OR Canal OR Reservoir]
        nodeB[Sand Filter 25 micron]
        nodeC[Fertilizer Tank with Venturi Injector]
        nodeD[Screen Filter 100 mesh]
    end

    subgraph SNS[Sensor Layer]
        nodeE[Soil Moisture Sensor TDR or Capacitance]
        nodeF[Flow Meter Electromagnetic]
        nodeG[Pressure Transducer 4-20 mA]
        nodeH[Weather Station Temp Humidity Rainfall]
    end

    subgraph CTR[Controller Layer]
        nodeI[PLC OR Arduino Mega 2560]
        nodeJ[Cloud Server MQTT Broker]
        nodeK[Operator HMI Touchscreen 7 inch]
    end

    subgraph ACT[Actuator Layer]
        nodeL[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
        nodeM[Submersible Pump 2.2 kW]
        nodeN[Master Solenoid Valve 24 V DC]
        nodeO[Zone Valves 12 V DC]
        nodeP[Drippers OR Sprinkler Heads]
    end

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeP

    classDef srcStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef snsStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px
    classDef actStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD srcStyle
    class nodeE,nodeF,nodeG,nodeH snsStyle
    class nodeI,nodeJ,nodeK ctrStyle
    class nodeL,nodeM,nodeN,nodeO,nodeP actStyle
```

### 4.2 Description of Each Block (For the 14-Mark Written Answer)

| Block | Function | KTU Favourite Specifications |
|---|---|---|
| **Source (nodeA)** | Stores water; may be open well, borewell, farm pond | Capacity $\geq$ 2 × daily demand |
| **Sand Filter (nodeB)** | Removes organic debris | 25–50 µm rating |
| **Fertilizer Tank + Venturi (nodeC)** | Injects water-soluble nutrients | Differential pressure $\geq$ 0.5 bar |
| **Screen Filter (nodeD)** | Final particulate removal | 100–200 mesh |
| **Soil Moisture Sensor (nodeE)** | Measures VWC | TDR: 0–100 %, accuracy $\pm 2$ % |
| **Flow Meter (nodeF)** | Measures volumetric flow | Electromagnetic / ultrasonic, 4–20 mA |
| **Pressure Transducer (nodeG)** | Detects pipe pressure | 0–10 bar, 0.5 % FS accuracy |
| **Weather Station (nodeH)** | Computes ET$_0$ via Hargreaves | Pyranometer + RH + T sensor |
| **Controller (nodeI)** | Runs the irrigation algorithm | PLC / Arduino / Raspberry-Pi |
| **VFD (nodeL)** | Modulates pump speed | 1-phase in / 3-phase out |
| **Pump (nodeM)** | Provides pressure head | Centrifugal / submersible |
| **Master Valve (nodeN)** | Cuts off entire field | 1–2 inch solenoid |
| **Zone Valves (nodeO)** | Sequentially irrigate each zone | 24 V DC latching |
| **Emitters (nodeP)** | Discharge water at root zone | Pressure-compensating, 2/4/8 L/h |

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Control Flowchart (On/Off Hysteresis Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([System Power ON]) --> init[Initialise Sensors and Valves to OFF]
    init --> readS[Read VWC from Soil Sensor]
    readS --> check{VWC less than Setpoint}
    check -- No --> delay[Wait 10 minutes]
    delay --> readS
    check -- Yes --> openV[Open Master Valve and Zone Valve]
    openV --> startTimer[Start Irrigation Timer]
    startTimer --> checkFlow{Flow equals Design Q}
    checkFlow -- No --> alarm[Trigger Low-Flow Alarm SMS]
    alarm --> stop1[Stop Irrigation]
    checkFlow -- Yes --> checkTime{Timer equals T_set}
    checkTime -- No --> checkFlow
    checkTime -- Yes --> closeV[Close All Valves]
    closeV --> log[Log Data to Cloud MQTT]
    log --> readS
```

> **How to memorise:** This flowchart follows the **"Read → Compare → Decide → Actuate → Log"** five-step template that examiners reward with full 7 marks in any "explain the working" question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (Each 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *Application Efficiency* of an irrigation system. State its typical value for (i) surface flood, (ii) drip, and (iii) sprinkler irrigation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Application Efficiency ($E_a$) is the ratio of the volume of water **effectively stored in the root zone** of the crop to the volume of water **delivered at the system inlet**.

$$
E_a = \frac{V_{\text{root zone}}}{V_{\text{applied}}} \times 100 \; \%
$$

| Method | Typical $E_a$ |
|---|---|
| Surface flood | 30 – 50 % |
| Sprinkler | 60 – 75 % |
| Drip | 70 – 90 % |

Higher $E_a$ implies lower conveyance, evaporation, and deep-percolation losses. *(Full 3 marks if both formula and three values are quoted.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List any **four soil-moisture sensing techniques** used in automated irrigation and state the physical principle on which each works.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Capacitance / FDR probe** — dielectric permittivity of moist soil is much greater than dry soil; measured as change in probe capacitance. *(1 mark)*
2. **TDR (Time Domain Reflectometry)** — travel time of an EM pulse along a waveguide embedded in soil is proportional to volumetric water content. *(1 mark)*
3. **Tensiometer** — measures soil-water matric suction (negative pressure) via a porous ceramic cup connected to a mercury or pressure transducer. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Electrical Resistance (Gypsum Block)** — electrical resistance between two electrodes embedded in a gypsum block varies inversely with soil moisture. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Each 14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each long question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a fully-automated drip-irrigation system** for a 1-hectare vegetable farm. Identify the role of **soil-moisture sensor, flow meter, solenoid valve, VFD, and PLC**.

**(b)** A drip lateral of length 80 m carries 4 L/h pressure-compensating emitters spaced at 0.5 m. The lateral inlet pressure is 1.2 bar. Compute (i) total number of emitters, (ii) total discharge, (iii) required pump power assuming total head 18 m and overall efficiency 65 %.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 marks]

A complete block diagram must include **four layers** as drawn in Section 4.1 of these notes. Award marks as:

| Component | Marks Allocated | Awarded When |
|---|---|---|
| Source + filtration block (sand + screen filter) | 1 | Both filters named |
| Sensor layer (soil moisture, flow, pressure) | 2 | All three sensors explained |
| Controller layer (PLC / Arduino + HMI) | 2 | Decision logic described |
| Actuator layer (VFD, pump, solenoid valves, emitters) | 2 | Each actuator role explained |

**Working description (key points to write):**

- The **soil-moisture sensor** (TDR or capacitance probe, buried 15 cm and 30 cm deep) continuously reports **Volumetric Water Content (VWC)** to the PLC every 60 s via 4–20 mA or RS-485.
- The PLC compares VWC against the **set-point** (e.g., 25 % VWC for tomato). If VWC $<$ set-point, the PLC energises the **master solenoid valve** and sequentially opens the **zone valves**.
- A **flow meter** measures the actual discharge; if it deviates by more than $\pm 10$ % from the design discharge, the PLC adjusts the **VFD frequency** to vary pump speed and thus pipe pressure.
- The **VFD** reduces energy consumption by 20–30 % compared with a throttled valve system.
- All data are logged via **MQTT** to a cloud server for remote monitoring through a smartphone app.

*(Total 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 marks]

**(i) Number of emitters:**

$$
n = \frac{L}{s} = \frac{80}{0.5} = 160 \;\text{emitters}
$$

**(ii) Total discharge:**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = n \times q_e = 160 \times 4 = 640 \;\text{L/h} = \frac{640}{3600} = 0.1778 \;\text{L/s} = 1.778 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Pump power:**

$$
P = \frac{\rho \, g \, Q \, H}{\eta} = \frac{998 \times 9.81 \times 1.778 \times 10^{-4} \times 18}{0.65}
$$

$$
P = \frac{998 \times 9.81 \times 1.778 \times 10^{-4} \times 18}{0.65} = \frac{31.34}{0.65} = 48.22 \;\text{W}
$$

**Practically**, a fractional-horsepower booster pump (e.g., 0.5 HP ≈ 370 W) will be selected to account for friction losses and starting surges — **state this explicitly to gain full marks.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient** in detail. Derive its expression and state the criteria for grading a drip system as Excellent, Good, Fair, or Poor.

**(b)** The following discharges (L/h) were recorded from 8 emitters on a drip lateral: 3.6, 3.9, 4.2, 3.7, 4.0, 3.8, 4.1, 3.5. Compute (i) mean discharge, (ii) mean absolute deviation, (iii) Christiansen's $C_u$, and (iv) grade the system.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define $C_u$** (refer to derivation in Section 3.1). The coefficient is the **percentage of the mean discharge that is uniformly delivered** after accounting for deviations.

**Step 2 — Expression** (write the final formula):

$$
C_u = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{\overline{\Delta q}}{\bar{q}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Grading criteria (KTU/IS expected values):**

| $C_u$ Range | Grade |
|---|---|
| 90 – 100 % | **Excellent** |
| 80 – 90 % | **Good** |
| 70 – 80 % | **Fair** |
| $<$ 70 % | **Poor** |

**Step 4 — Practical significance:** A low $C_u$ indicates clogged emitters, excessive pressure variation along the lateral, or poor manufacturing tolerance. Remedies include (i) **pressure-compensating emitters**, (ii) **lateral length reduction**, (iii) **sub-unit design with smaller blocks**, and (iv) **regular flushing** of laterals.

*(7 marks for definition 2 + formula 2 + grading table 2 + remedies 1.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 marks]

**(i) Mean discharge:**

$$
\bar{q} = \frac{3.6 + 3.9 + 4.2 + 3.7 + 4.0 + 3.8 + 4.1 + 3.5}{8} = \frac{30.8}{8} = 3.85 \;\text{L/h}
$$

**(ii) Mean absolute deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\Delta q} &= \frac{1}{8}\bigl(\vert 3.6-3.85\vert + \vert 3.9-3.85\vert + \vert 4.2-3.85\vert + \vert 3.7-3.85\vert \right. \\
&\quad \left. +\, \vert 4.0-3.85\vert + \vert 3.8-3.85\vert + \vert 4.1-3.85\vert + \vert 3.5-3.85\vert\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.25 + 0.05 + 0.35 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.05 + 0.25 + 0.35}{8} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.60}{8} = 0.20 \;\text{L/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Christiansen's $C_u$:**

$$
C_u = 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.20}{3.85}\right) = 100 \times (1 - 0.05195) = 94.81 \; \%
$$

**(iv) Grade:** Since $C_u = 94.81 \,\% \in [90, 100]$, the system is graded **EXCELLENT**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students LOSE marks on this topic (KTU 2024 trend):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing `P = 48` instead of `P = 48 W`). Examiner deducts **½ mark per missing unit**.
> 2. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** — the diagram must show **direction of flow** (sense → controller → actuator) or it gets only **3 out of 7** marks.
> 3. **Conflating $C_u$ with $DU$ (Distribution Uniformity)** — $DU$ uses the *lowest quartile*, while $C_u$ uses the *mean*. Writing the wrong formula = **zero marks** even if you wrote the formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the MAD (management-allowed deficit)** in net irrigation-depth calculations — this is the single most common 1-mark loss in numerical problems.
> 5. **Stating "sensor measures water content" without naming the principle** (capacitance, TDR, tensiometer, etc.) — examiners want the **physical principle**, not just the device name.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Water Distribution = Sense + Decide + Actuate** closed loop.
- [x] **Application Efficiency ($E_a$)**: Drip (70–90 %) > Sprinkler (60–75 %) > Surface (30–50 %).
- [x] **Christiansen's $C_u$ formula**: $C_u = 100 \left(1 - \dfrac{\overline{\Delta q}}{\bar{q}}\right)$.
- [x] **Grading**: 90 %+ Excellent, 80–90 % Good, 70–80 % Fair, $<$ 70 % Poor.
- [x] **VWC = $\theta_g \times \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_w}$** — remember the **bulk-density factor**.
- [x] **Gross depth = Net depth / $E_a$** — always divide, never multiply.
- [x] **Hazen-Williams** is preferred over Darcy-Weisbach for **PVC pipes** carrying water at ambient T.
- [x] **Emitter flow–pressure law**: $q = k h^x$ with $x = 0.5$ for turbulent, $1.0$ for laminar.
- [x] **Pump power**: $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$ — include $\eta$ or lose 1 mark.
- [x] **Closed-loop beats open-loop** — always highlight in 14-mark answers.
- [x] **VFD reduces energy by 20–30 %** — mention this when describing actuators.
- [x] **MQTT / cloud logging** is the modern KTU-favourite IoT extension.
- [x] **Block diagram must have arrows + 4 layers** (Source, Sensor, Controller, Actuator).
- [x] **Always carry units to the final numerical answer.**
- [x] **MAD = 30–50 %** — embed this default in every irrigation-depth problem unless stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto Drip and Sprinkler Irrigation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Auto Drip and Sprinkler Irrigation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Automated Drip Irrigation System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drip Irrigation** (also called *trickle irrigation* or *micro-irrigation*) is a pressurized irrigation methodology in which water is conveyed through a closed conduit network and delivered **drop-by-drop** at the **root zone** of each plant, precisely matching the crop's evapotranspiration (ET) demand.

**Automated Drip Irrigation** integrates this hydraulic delivery system with **electro-mechanical control elements** (solenoid valves, motorised gates), **sensor feedback** (soil moisture probes, tensiometers, weather stations), and a **centralized controller/PLC** that opens and closes the water path **without manual intervention**, governed by pre-set logic or real-time sensor data.

**Key Engineering Metrics in Drip System Design:**

| Metric | Standard Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Emitter discharge (q) | **2 – 8 L/h** | L/h |
| Operating pressure (P) | **1.0 – 2.5 bar** | bar |
| Wetted diameter | **0.3 – 0.6 m** | m |
| Field application efficiency | **80 – 95 %** | % |
| Water saving over flood | **40 – 70 %** | % |

> [!NOTE]
> The word *Drip* itself is a **physical metaphor** — water emerges at such a low rate that the human eye perceives it as discrete drops, not a continuous flow.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Plant IV Drip

Imagine a **patient in a hospital** receiving saline through an IV (intravenous) line. The nurse doesn't pour a bucket of water onto the patient; instead, a precisely metered trickle enters the bloodstream continuously over hours. **Drip irrigation is the agricultural equivalent of this medical IV**:

- **The IV bag** = the elevated water tank or pressurized mainline
- **The drip chamber** = the emitter (dripper)
- **The patient's vein** = the plant's root zone
- **The flow regulator roller** = the pressure regulator / solenoid valve
- **The nurse adjusting the flow** = the automated controller + soil moisture sensor

Just as an IV prevents fluid shock, drip irrigation prevents the *agricultural shocks* of over-watering (waterlogging, root rot, nutrient leaching) and under-watering (wilting, stunted growth).

---

### 1.3 Sprinkler Irrigation System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sprinkler Irrigation** is a pressurized irrigation technique in which water is sprayed into the air through a network of **nozzles / sprinkler heads**, falling onto the crop canopy as **artificial rainfall**, achieving uniform coverage over a defined geometric area.

**Automated Sprinkler Systems** augment this with **programmable controllers**, **solenoid-actuated zone valves**, **rain sensors**, **wind anemometers**, and **soil moisture sensors** to dynamically schedule irrigation cycles.

**Sprinkler Classification by Discharge:**

| Class | Discharge (L/s) | Pressure (kPa) | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Micro-sprinkler | 0.05 – 0.5 | 100 – 200 | Orchards, nurseries |
| Mini-sprinkler | 0.5 – 1.5 | 200 – 350 | Vegetables, lawns |
| Standard impact | 1.5 – 4.0 | 300 – 500 | Field crops |
| High-pressure gun | 4.0 – 15.0 | 500 – 800 | Sugarcane, large fields |

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Rain-on-Demand

Think of a **stadium sprinkler system** that cools the turf at half-time. The grass is not flooded; instead, fine droplets drift down from above, mimicking a gentle cloudburst. **Agricultural sprinklers are scaled-up, scientifically-tuned versions** of this same principle — the *Application Rate* (mm/h) and *Droplet Size Distribution* are carefully engineered to match soil infiltration capacity and crop tolerance.

A useful mental model: **Drip = spoon-feeding water to each plant individually**; **Sprinkler = hosting a controlled rain-shower for the whole field**.

---

### 1.5 The "Auto" in Auto-Irrigation — What Makes It Automated?

The transition from manual to automatic irrigation requires **three functional layers**:

1. **Sensing Layer** — Soil moisture sensors (TDR, capacitance, tensiometers), temperature/humidity probes, rain-cups, anemometers, leaf wetness sensors.
2. **Decision Layer** — Microcontroller / PLC / IoT gateway executing a control algorithm (threshold-based, ET-based, fuzzy logic, or model-predictive).
3. **Actuation Layer** — Solenoid valves, motorized ball valves, variable frequency drives (VFD) on pumps, relays, and alert systems (SMS/GSM).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "instrumentation" angle of this course is precisely this triad — students are expected to identify *which sensor* measures *what physical variable* and *how the controller responds* to maintain the agronomic setpoint.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Auto Drip Irrigation — System Architecture & Operating Principle

The operational sequence of a fully automated drip system can be decomposed into **eight functional stages**:

1. **Water Intake** — Suction from well, canal, tank, or reservoir via a foot valve / strainer.
2. **Pumping** — Centrifugal or submersible pump raises pressure to **2.5 – 4.0 bar** at the control head.
3. **Filtration** — Sand / disc / screen filters (typically **120 – 200 mesh**) remove particulates that would clog emitters. Auto-backwash filters use differential pressure sensors to initiate cleaning cycles.
4. **Fertigation / Chemigation Injection** — Venturi injector or dosing pump introduces water-soluble fertilizer into the mainline.
5. **Pressure Regulation** — Pressure-reducing valve maintains downstream pressure at the emitter-rated value (typically **1.0 bar**) despite upstream fluctuations.
6. **Distribution Network** — Mainline (PVC/uPVC, larger diameter) → Sub-main (medium diameter) → Laterals (LDPE, **12 mm or 16 mm** diameter) → Emitters.
7. **Water Discharge** — Pressure-compensating (PC) or non-PC emitters deliver **2 – 8 L/h** depending on type.
8. **Control & Feedback** — Solenoid valves (24 V AC or 12 V DC latching) on each sub-main zone; soil moisture sensors report volumetric water content (VWC) to the controller, which triggers valve actuation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pressure-Compensating Emitters Matter in Automation:**
> In an automated system, multiple zones open sequentially. Each new zone opening causes a pressure transient in the mainline. Without PC emitters, the **first zone** would receive higher pressure and over-discharge, while the **last zone** would under-discharge. PC emitters maintain a near-constant **q** over a pressure range of **0.5 – 3.5 bar**, ensuring *uniformity* across time-varying hydraulic conditions.

---

### 2.2 Auto Sprinkler Irrigation — System Architecture & Operating Principle

A sprinkler system differs from drip primarily in the **final discharge element** and the **hydraulic design philosophy**:

1. **Pump Station** — Sized to deliver the peak simultaneous demand of all active zones.
2. **Mainline & Sub-mains** — Sized using **Hazen-Williams** or **Darcy-Weisbach** equations to keep friction loss < **10 %** of operating pressure.
3. **Riser Pipes** — Vertical HDPE/PVC risers elevate the sprinkler head above crop canopy.
4. **Sprinkler Heads** — Selected for the required **wetted radius (R)**, **discharge (q)**, and **droplet size**.
5. **Zone Valves** — Electrically actuated solenoid valves (typically **24 V AC**, **0.5 – 2 inch**).
6. **Sensors** — Rain sensor (optical or tipping-bucket), wind speed sensor (anemometer), soil moisture sensor.
7. **Controller** — Schedules irrigation cycles; may abort cycle if rain or high wind is detected.

**Critical Sprinkler Performance Parameters:**

- **Precipitation Rate (P, mm/h)** — the rate at which water is applied to the soil surface.
- **Application Rate must be ≤ Soil Infiltration Rate** — otherwise runoff and erosion occur.
- **Distribution Uniformity (DU)** and **Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC)** quantify how evenly water is spread.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** the KTU examiner can plausibly test on this topic. Memorize the variable definitions, units, and typical numerical ranges.

**Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference (Auto Drip & Sprinkler Systems)**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Total system discharge | $Q_{sys} = N \times q$ | N = number of emitters; q = emitter discharge (L/h); $Q_{sys}$ (L/h) | Sizes pump capacity for drip system |
| 2 | Drip system flow (area basis) | $Q = \dfrac{A \times d \times n}{t}$ | A = area (m²); d = depth (mm); n = irrigation efficiency; t = time (h) | Field-scale water budgeting |
| 3 | Precipitation rate (sprinkler) | $P = \dfrac{q_{spr} \times 3600}{A_{spr}}$ | $q_{spr}$ = sprinkler discharge (L/s); $A_{spr}$ = wetted area (m²); P (mm/h) | Match to soil infiltration rate |
| 4 | Wetted area (single sprinkler) | $A_{spr} = \pi \times R^{2}$ | R = wetted radius (m) | Used in precipitation rate |
| 5 | Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient | $CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert}{n \times \bar{d}}\right)$ | $d_i$ = depth at point i; $\bar{d}$ = mean depth; n = number of catch-cans | Quantifies sprinkler uniformity |
| 6 | Distribution Uniformity | $DU = \dfrac{\bar{d}_{25\%}}{\bar{d}}$ | $\bar{d}_{25\%}$ = mean of lowest 25 % catch readings | Lower-cost surrogate for CUC |
| 7 | Application Efficiency | $E_a = \dfrac{\text{water stored in root zone}}{\text{water delivered}} \times 100$ | All in mm or L | Benchmark of system performance |
| 8 | Hazen-Williams head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \times L \times Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \times D^{4.87}}$ | L = pipe length (m); Q = flow (m³/s); D = diameter (m); C = roughness coeff. | Pipe sizing in mainline |
| 9 | Velocity in pipe | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A_{pipe}} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Q (m³/s); D (m); V (m/s) | Check V is in **0.6 – 2.0 m/s** |
| 10 | Soil moisture (VWC) | $\theta_v = \dfrac{V_w}{V_t}$ | $V_w$ = water volume; $V_t$ = total volume | Trigger threshold for auto-valve |

> **Permitted pipe velocities (KTU design standard):** **0.6 m/s (min)** to **2.0 m/s (max)** to avoid sediment deposition and water hammer respectively.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Agriculture:** Auto drip systems form the irrigation backbone of greenhouse horticulture, vineyards, and high-density orchards. Combined with **fertigation**, they enable **fertigation-on-demand**, reducing fertilizer runoff by up to **50 %**.
- **Water-Scarce Agriculture:** In regions of Kerala (e.g., Palakkad, Wayanad) where summer water stress is acute, **drip with mulching** is the de-facto Karshaka Karma Sena recommendation.
- **Climate-Smart Farming:** Automated systems can integrate with **IoT dashboards** (Blynk, ThingSpeak, Ubidots) for remote farm management.
- **Industrial Scale:** Sprinkler systems with **CUC > 85 %** are the standard for seed production plots, golf courses, and landscape turf.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Precipitation Rate of a Sprinkler Head

The **Precipitation Rate (P)** is defined as the depth of water (in mm) applied per unit time (in hours) over the wetted area of a single sprinkler.

**Given:**
- Sprinkler discharge $q_{spr}$ in litres per second (L/s).
- Wetted radius $R$ in metres.

**Step 1 — Volume of water discharged per second**

By definition, $q_{spr}$ is the volume flow rate in **L/s**.

$$
q_{spr} \; [\text{L/s}] \;\equiv\; q_{spr} \times 10^{-3} \; [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 2 — Convert time unit from seconds to hours**

Multiplying $q_{spr}$ (L/s) by 3600 converts it to **L/h**:

$$
Q_{h} = q_{spr} \times 3600 \quad [\text{L/h}]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the wetted area**

The sprinkler wets a circular footprint on the ground (neglecting wind distortion). The area of this circle is:

$$
A_{spr} = \pi R^{2} \quad [\text{m}^2]
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the equivalent depth over that area**

If $Q_{h}$ litres are spread uniformly over $A_{spr}$ m², the equivalent depth $P$ (in mm) is obtained by dividing the volume in litres by the area in m², then multiplying by 1000 to convert metres to millimetres:

$$
P = \dfrac{Q_{h} \;[\text{L}]}{A_{spr} \;[\text{m}^2]} \times \dfrac{1 \; \text{mm} \cdot \text{m}^2}{1 \; \text{L}}
$$

Substituting $Q_{h} = q_{spr} \times 3600$ and $A_{spr} = \pi R^{2}$:

$$
P = \dfrac{q_{spr} \times 3600}{\pi R^{2}}
$$

**Final boxed equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,P = \dfrac{3600 \times q_{spr}}{\pi R^{2}} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{mm}}{\text{h}}\right]\,}
$$

> **Note on units:** This equation assumes $q_{spr}$ is in L/s and $R$ is in metres; the resulting $P$ is in mm/h — a dimensionally consistent and examiner-friendly form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Christiansen's Uniformity Coefficient (CUC)

**Conceptual Foundation:**
The CUC measures how *uniformly* a sprinkler system applies water across a field. Perfect uniformity would mean all catch-cans collect the *same* depth; in practice, some points receive more and some receive less.

**Step 1 — Set up the data**

Let $d_1, d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_n$ be the depths (in mm) collected in $n$ catch-cans placed in a grid.

The **mean depth** is:

$$
\bar{d} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute deviations**

For each can, compute the absolute deviation from the mean:

$$
\Delta_i = \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of absolute deviations**

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Form the uniformity ratio**

The ratio of *total deviation* to *maximum possible deviation* is:

$$
U_{ratio} = \dfrac{S}{n \times \bar{d}}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as a percentage**

$$
CUC = \left(1 - U_{ratio}\right) \times 100
$$

**Final boxed equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,CUC = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert d_i - \bar{d} \vert}{n \bar{d}}\right) \quad [\%]\,}
$$

**Typical KTU design targets:**

| Field Type | Target CUC |
| :--- | :--- |
| Standard field crops | **80 – 85 %** |
| Vegetables, gardens | **85 – 90 %** |
| High-value nurseries, golf greens | **> 90 %** |

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Total System Discharge for an Auto Drip Sub-Unit

**Step 1 — Identify emitter parameters**

Let there be $N$ plants per sub-main, each served by one emitter of discharge $q$ (L/h).

**Step 2 — Total flow rate**

If all $N$ emitters are open simultaneously, the volumetric flow rate is:

$$
Q_{total} = N \times q \quad [\text{L/h}]
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to L/s for pump sizing**

$$
Q_{total} = \dfrac{N \times q}{3600} \quad [\text{L/s}]
$$

**Step 4 — Add a safety / simultaneous demand factor (K)**

In practice, not all sub-mains operate at once. The *design discharge* for pump selection uses a **simultaneous demand factor $K_s$** (typically **0.6 – 0.8**):

$$
Q_{design} = K_s \times N \times q
$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic power requirement**

$$
P_{hydraulic} = \dfrac{\rho \times g \times Q_{design} \times H_{total}}{1000} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

where $H_{total}$ is the sum of *pressure head at emitter* + *friction losses* + *elevation head* + *minor losses*; $\rho$ is water density (**1000 kg/m³**), $g$ is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A drip irrigation sub-main has **200 emitters**, each of discharge **4 L/h**. Operating time per day is **6 hours**, applied for a tomato crop requiring **6 mm** of water per day over a **1000 m²** field. Verify if the system's daily volume meets the crop water requirement.
>
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily volume delivered by sub-main**

$$
V_{daily} = N \times q \times t = 200 \times 4 \times 6 = 4800 \;\text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Crop water requirement in litres**

Depth of water required: $d = 6$ mm over $A = 1000$ m².

Convert mm·m² to litres using the identity **1 mm over 1 m² = 1 L**:

$$
V_{req} = d \times A = 6 \times 1000 = 6000 \;\text{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare and conclude**

$$
V_{daily} = 4800 \;\text{L} \; < \; V_{req} = 6000 \;\text{L}
$$

The system delivers only **80 %** of the required volume. The deficit is **1200 L/day**, equivalent to **1.2 mm** depth shortfall.

**Step 4 — Recommendation**

Increase daily operation time to:

$$
t_{new} = \dfrac{6000}{200 \times 4} = 7.5 \;\text{h/day}
$$

Or add **50 more emitters** at the same discharge to meet the requirement in 6 hours.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Sprinkler Precipitation Rate

> **Problem:** A single impact sprinkler discharges **0.5 L/s** at an operating pressure of **3.0 bar** and wets a circular area of radius **8 m**. Calculate the precipitation rate.
>
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Step 1 — Substitute into the derived formula**

$$
P = \dfrac{3600 \times q_{spr}}{\pi R^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values**

$$
P = \dfrac{3600 \times 0.5}{\pi \times (8)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator**

$$
\pi \times 64 = 201.06
$$

**Step 4 — Final division**

$$
P = \dfrac{1800}{201.06} \approx 8.95 \;\text{mm/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

Soil infiltration rate must exceed **8.95 mm/h** to avoid runoff. For a sandy loam (typical infiltration **25 mm/h**), the system is safe.

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Auto Drip Irrigation System

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **end-to-end functional architecture** of an automated drip system, mapping the sensing, decision, and actuation layers as per KTU 2024 instrumentation outcomes.

```mermaid

    A11 --> A12

    C1 --> A9
    C1 --> A10
    C1 --> A11
    C1 --> M1[GSM WiFi IoT Module]
    M1 --> U1[Farmer Mobile Dashboard]
```

**Key Engineering Observations from the diagram:**

- The **three-layer instrumentation triad** (Sensors → Controller → Actuators) is clearly visible in the lower half of the diagram.
- The **single pump** serves multiple **independently-actuated zones**, illustrating how automation multiplies the utility of one hydraulic source.
- The **feedback loop** (sensor data → controller → valve) is the **defining feature** that distinguishes *automatic* from *manual* drip systems.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Auto Sprinkler Irrigation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Reservoir or Borewell] --> B2[Foot Valve]
    B2 --> B3[Submersible Pump]

    B17 --> B19
    B18 --> B19
    B19 --> B20[Crop Canopy]
    SC1[Rain Sensor Optical] --> CT1[Smart Controller]
    SC2[Wind Anemometer] --> CT1
    SC3[Soil Moisture Probe] --> CT1
    SC4[Evapotranspiration API] --> CT1
    CT1 --> B10
    CT1 --> B11
    CT1 --> B12
    CT1 --> PM1[Variable Frequency Drive on Pump]
```

**Diagram Notes:**

- The **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** on the pump enables **pressure regulation** without a throttling valve, saving energy (typically **20 – 30 %**).
- The **anemometer feedback** is critical — high wind (> **5 m/s**) increases spray drift, and the controller can **abort** the cycle automatically.
- The **evapotranspiration (ET) data feed** represents the most advanced form of automated scheduling, where irrigation volume is calculated from atmospheric demand rather than fixed timers.

---

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Drip vs Sprinkler (KTU-Ready Table)

| Parameter | Auto Drip | Auto Sprinkler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wetting pattern | Localized (point source) | Broad (circular fan) |
| Typical application efficiency | **80 – 95 %** | **65 – 80 %** |
| Water saving vs flood | **40 – 70 %** | **25 – 40 %** |
| Operating pressure | **1.0 – 2.5 bar** | **2.5 – 4.5 bar** |
| Initial cost (INR per ha) | **₹60,000 – ₹1,50,000** | **₹40,000 – ₹1,20,000** |
| Best suited crops | Orchards, vegetables, mulched row crops | Cereals, pulses, lawns, sugarcane |
| Wind sensitivity | Low (under canopy) | High (drift loss) |
| Foliar disease risk | Low | Moderate to high (leaf wetness) |
| Fertigation compatibility | Excellent | Moderate |
