# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define SCADA. List any four components of a SCADA system. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
SCADA stands for **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition**. It is a computer-based industrial automation system that monitors and controls geographically dispersed assets from a centralized location.

**Four components:**
1. **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)** – Central server running SCADA software.
2. **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** – Remote device collecting field data.
3. **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** – Local high-speed logic controller.
4. **Communication Network** – Wired/wireless link (Modbus, Ethernet, GSM).

---

### Q2. Differentiate between RTU and PLC. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RTU | PLC |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Design Focus | Telemetry & remote monitoring | High-speed logic control |
| Environment | Rugged, outdoor | Industrial cabinet |
| Communication | Master-slave polling | Real-time fieldbus |
| Typical Use | Pump station, weather station | Greenhouse, conveyor |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a) Explain the architecture of a SCADA system with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of MTU, RTU, and HMI. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A SCADA system has a **layered architecture** (ANSI/ISA-95 inspired):

1. **Field Level (Level 0):** Sensors (analog/digital) and actuators (valves, motors) interface with the physical process (e.g., soil, water, air).
2. **Control Level (Level 1):** RTUs and PLCs digitize sensor signals and provide local control logic.
3. **Supervisory Level (Level 2):** The MTU (Master Terminal Unit) polls all RTUs, stores data in a historian, displays on HMI, and handles alarms.
4. **Operations Level (Level 3-4):** ERP, MES, cloud dashboards for management reporting.

**Functions of Key Blocks:**

- **MTU (Master Terminal Unit):** Central computer running SCADA software (e.g., WinCC, Ignition). It performs **polling, data logging, alarming, trending, and recipe management**.
- **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit):** Microprocessor-based field device that acquires analog/digital inputs, performs local processing, and responds to MTU commands. **It is the "eyes and ears"** of SCADA at remote sites.
- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** Graphical screen mimicking the physical plant. Allows operators to **monitor, acknowledge alarms, and issue control commands** by clicking icons.

**Application Note for Agriculture:** In an automated drip irrigation SCADA, the **MTU** runs on a farm PC, **RTUs** are deployed at each valve cluster, and the **HMI** displays soil moisture maps on a tablet.

**(b) With a suitable example from agriculture, explain how data flows from a sensor to the operator's screen in a SCADA system. Calculate the polling cycle time for 128 tags if each transaction takes 15 ms. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Example: Automated Drip Irrigation System**

A soil moisture sensor (e.g., Decagon EC-5) is buried in **Field Block A**. Its analog output (0–3 V proportional to VWC) is wired to **RTU-1**.

**Step 1 — Acquisition:** RTU-1's ADC digitizes the voltage into a 12-bit integer (0–4095).

**Step 2 — Encoding:** RTU-1 stores the value in a Modbus Holding Register (e.g., Register 40001 = 2048).

**Step 3 — Polling:** The MTU sends a Modbus request: *"Read Holding Register 40001 from Slave ID 1."*

**Step 4 — Transmission:** Over RS-485 / LoRaWAN, the packet travels to RTU-1.

**Step 5 — Response:** RTU-1 returns the value (2048 = 50% moisture).

**Step 6 — Display:** SCADA software maps the value to the tag `FieldA_Moisture` and updates the HMI gauge from red (<30%) to green (>40%).

**Step 7 — Alarm:** If moisture < 25%, an alarm popup appears. The operator clicks "OPEN VALVE 3".

**Step 8 — Command:** MTU sends Modbus write to RTU-1 coil → relay energizes → solenoid opens.

**Calculation:**

Given: $N = 128$ tags, $t_{tag} = 15 \text{ ms}$

$$
T_{poll} = N \times t_{tag} = 128 \times 15 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{poll} = 1920 \text{ ms} = 1.92 \text{ seconds}
$$

Refresh rate:

$$
f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{1.92} \approx 0.52 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Conclusion:** The HMI refreshes every ~2 seconds — perfectly adequate for slow agricultural processes.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) Discuss the role of communication protocols in SCADA. Compare Modbus, DNP3, and IEC 61850 with respect to their agricultural applicability. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Communication Protocols:**
Communication protocols define the **language** between MTU, RTUs, PLCs, and sensors. They ensure:
- **Interoperability** between vendors.
- **Data integrity** via CRC/checksums.
- **Deterministic timing** for time-critical control.
- **Security** via authentication and encryption.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Modbus RTU/TCP | DNP3 | IEC 61850 |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Origin | Modicon (1979) | IEEE (1990s) | IEC (2004) |
| Topology | Master-slave | Master-slave + peer | Peer-to-peer |
| Data Model | Simple registers | Objects + variants | Logical nodes (LD0, MMXU) |
| Error Handling | Basic CRC | Robust with confirmations | High reliability |
| Speed | Moderate | Moderate-High | Very High |
| Agricultural Fit | ★★★★★ (most common) | ★★★ (utilities) | ★ (substations) |

**Agricultural Verdict:**
- **Modbus TCP/RTU** dominates agriculture due to simplicity, low cost, and broad RTU/PLC support.
- **DNP3** is occasionally used in large irrigation canal automation (inherited from water utility SCADA).
- **IEC 61850** is rarely used in agriculture; it is specialized for **electrical substations**.

**(b) An RTU uses a 10-bit ADC to digitize a temperature sensor with a 0–5 V output range. Calculate (i) the quantization step in mV, and (ii) the % error when measuring 2.5 V. Comment on whether a 10-bit ADC is suitable for greenhouse temperature control (accuracy required: ±0.5 °C, sensor sensitivity: 10 mV/°C). [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Quantization Step:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{5}{1024}
$$

$$
Q \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Error at 2.5 V:**

$$
\%E = \dfrac{Q}{V_{measured}} \times 100 = \dfrac{4.88}{2500} \times 100
$$

$$
\%E \approx 0.195\%
$$

**Suitability Analysis:**

- Required temperature accuracy: $\pm 0.5 \text{ °C}$
- Sensor sensitivity: $10 \text{ mV/°C}$
- Required voltage resolution: $0.5 \text{ °C} \times 10 \text{ mV/°C} = 5 \text{ mV}$

Since the quantization step ($4.88 \text{ mV}$) is **slightly smaller** than the required resolution ($5 \text{ mV}$), a 10-bit ADC **just barely meets** the requirement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often compute %E using full-scale voltage instead of measured value — this is WRONG and loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Failing to convert the temperature accuracy requirement into voltage resolution before comparing with Q. Always show the **unit-consistency step**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing a vague conclusion like "ADC is fine" without numerical justification. **Always state: "Q (4.88 mV) < Required Resolution (5 mV), hence SUITABLE with margin < 0.12 mV."**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting that **greenhouse temperature accuracy ±0.5°C** also depends on sensor linearity and noise, not just ADC bits alone.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SCADA = Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition** — a *centralized* monitoring + control system for *distributed* assets.
- ✅ Four pillars: **Field Instrumentation → RTU/PLC → Communication → MTU (HMI + Historian).**
- ✅ **MTU** = the "brain"; **RTU/PLC** = the "local muscles"; **HMI** = the "face"; **Communication** = the "nervous system".
- ✅ Master-slave polling is the **dominant architecture**; Modbus is the **most common agricultural protocol**.
- ✅ **Polling cycle** $T_{poll} = N \times t_{tag}$ — directly affects HMI refresh rate.
- ✅ **ADC quantization** $Q = V_{FS}/2^n$ — must be < 1/10 of required measurement resolution.
- ✅ **System Availability** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — target > 99.9% for farm SCADA.
- ✅ SCADA ≠ DCS (Distributed Control System) — DCS has **tightly coupled** control loops; SCADA has **loosely coupled** supervisory loops over wide geography.
- ✅ Agricultural applications: **Drip irrigation, greenhouse climate, fertigation, cold storage, weather stations.**
- ✅ Modern trend: **Web SCADA + Cloud + Mobile** — but on-prem SCADA remains standard for food safety / cybersecurity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Exams:** Always relate SCADA concepts to an **agricultural example** (e.g., drip irrigation, greenhouse, dairy automation) to score full marks — generic industrial examples are accepted but agri-specific answers earn higher credit under NEP 2020 outcome-based evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of SCADA in Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of SCADA in Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized, computer-based automation architecture that continuously monitors, acquires, and controls geographically distributed agricultural field devices (sensors, motors, valves, pumps) in real time using a layered hierarchy of **RTUs (Remote Terminal Units)**, **PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers)**, **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)**, and **HMI (Human Machine Interface)** over wired/wireless telemetry links.

In the context of **Instrumentation for Agriculture (PEICT753 – Module 3)**, SCADA forms the **digital backbone of Precision Agriculture**, enabling closed-loop control of irrigation, fertigation, greenhouse micro-climate, and livestock parameters by translating analog field signals into actionable supervisory decisions.

### Key Terminology Used in the Module

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| RTU | Remote Terminal Unit — rugged field data collector |
| PLC | Programmable Logic Controller — local sequence/control engine |
| MTU / SCADA Server | Central supervisory computer running the SCADA software |
| HMI | Human Machine Interface — graphical operator dashboard |
| Telemetry | Bidirectional data transmission between field & control room |
| PLC Scan Time | Time required by PLC to read all inputs (typically 10–50 ms) |
| PID Controller | Proportional-Integral-Derivative closed-loop control law |
| Modbus / IEC 60870 | Industrial communication protocols |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Farm Manager"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine a single experienced farm supervisor sitting in an air-conditioned control room. Instead of physically running to every corner of a 100-acre farm, he has a **walkie-talkie link** with workers stationed near every pump, valve, and sensor. The workers shout numbers ("soil moisture = 18 %", "tray 4 temperature = 32 °C"), the supervisor writes them in a register, thinks for a moment, and replies ("Open Valve 7 for 10 minutes", "Switch off Heater 3"). **That supervisor is the SCADA Master Terminal Unit**, the workers are the RTUs/PLCs, the walkie-talkies are the communication network, and the register is the **data historian**.

This is the essence of SCADA — **supervisory control** (human-decided or automated logic) over **distributed assets** using **real-time data acquisition**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Field device baud rates: 9600 bps to 115200 bps (Modbus RTU standard)**
- **Typical SCADA poll cycle: 1 s to 60 s** for agricultural applications
- **Soil moisture measurement range: 0 % VWC to 60 % VWC** (Volumetric Water Content)
- **Greenhouse optimal temperature band: 18 °C to 28 °C** (crop dependent)
- **Reference Evapotranspiration (ET₀): 3 mm/day to 7 mm/day** in tropical Kerala climate
- **Standard sensor accuracy: ±2 % FS** (Full Scale)

---

## 1.4 Why SCADA is Essential in Modern Agriculture

- Replaces manual scouting → continuous, high-resolution monitoring.
- Reduces water usage by **30 %–50 %** via automated drip irrigation.
- Enables remote control of pumps across vast distances using **GSM/GPRS, LoRaWAN, Zigbee**.
- Provides historical trends (data historian) for **decision support systems (DSS)** and **crop modeling**.
- Integrates heterogeneous subsystems (irrigation, fertigation, environment, livestock) under a **single supervisory umbrella**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of Agricultural SCADA

A SCADA system in agriculture is organized into **four hierarchical levels**. Understanding this hierarchy is the most scoring conceptual point in KTU questions.

### Level 1 — Field Instrumentation Layer (Sensors & Actuators)
- **Sensors:** Soil moisture probes (TDR, capacitive), soil temperature, air temperature/humidity (DHT22, SHT31), pH, EC (electrical conductivity), wind speed, solar radiation, leaf wetness, rain gauge.
- **Actuators:** Solenoid valves, motorized ball valves, submersible pumps, misters, foggers, exhaust fans, shade nets, grow lights, dosing pumps for fertigation.

### Level 2 — Control Layer (RTUs / PLCs)
- **Function:** Local data acquisition (analog/digital inputs), local closed-loop control (PID), alarm generation, communication with the master.
- **Examples:** Schneider M221, Siemens LOGO!, ABB AC500, custom Arduino/ESP32 RTUs for low-cost agri applications.
- **I/O Count:** Typically **8 AI + 8 DI + 8 DO + 4 AO** for a mid-sized farm RTU.

### Level 3 — Communication / Network Layer
- **Wired:** RS-485 (Modbus RTU), CAN bus, Ethernet/IP, fiber.
- **Wireless Short Range:** Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4), Bluetooth, Wi-Fi.
- **Wireless Long Range:** LoRaWAN (up to 15 km), GSM/GPRS (4G LTE), NB-IoT, satellite (for remote farms).

### Level 4 — Supervisory Layer (MTU + HMI)
- **MTU:** Industrial PC running SCADA software (WinCC, iFIX, Ignition, open-source like OpenSCADA, ScadaBR).
- **HMI:** Touchscreen panels (10"–22") for on-site operators + web-based dashboards for remote access.
- **Functions:** Trend visualization, alarm logs, recipe management, report generation, historian database.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** If a question asks "What is the role of RTU vs MTU in agricultural SCADA?", answer: *RTU is the field data concentrator with limited local control; MTU is the central supervisory computer that runs the HMI, historian, and global control logic.*

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Closed-Loop Irrigation Control (Step-by-Step)

Modern SCADA-controlled drip irrigation follows this **structured bulleted logic**:

1. **Acquire** soil moisture (θ, in % VWC) from capacitive probes inserted at 15 cm and 30 cm depths.
2. **Acquire** weather data (rainfall, ET₀, temperature) from the on-farm weather station.
3. **Transmit** values to RTU via RS-485 / LoRa every **T_poll** seconds.
4. **Compare** θ against a configurable **setpoint θ_sp** (e.g., 22 % for tomato in sandy loam).
5. **Compute** deficit Δθ = θ_sp − θ_measured (positive → water required).
6. **Convert** Δθ to irrigation runtime using the formula in §2.3.
7. **Actuate** solenoid valves and booster pumps via digital output relays.
8. **Log** the event in the historian with timestamp (ISO 8601).
9. **Trigger alarms** if Δθ exceeds a critical threshold or sensors fail (open-circuit detection).
10. **Update** HMI trend graph and push notification to farmer's mobile via GSM.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield equations for SCADA-based agricultural control are consolidated below.** Master these for KTU 14-mark derivation questions.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{sp} = \theta_{FC} \cdot MAD$ | Setpoint moisture = Field Capacity × Management Allowed Depletion |
| 2 | $\Delta\theta = \theta_{sp} - \theta_{measured}$ | Moisture deficit triggering irrigation |
| 3 | $d_{gross} = \dfrac{(\theta_{FC} - \theta_{WP})}{100} \cdot D_r \cdot A$ | Gross irrigation depth (mm), $D_r$ = root depth, $A$ = area |
| 4 | $T_{run} = \dfrac{60 \cdot d_{gross}}{q_{emitter} \cdot n_{emitters}}$ | Valve runtime (min) where $q$ = emitter discharge (L/h) |
| 5 | $ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_0$ | Crop evapotranspiration (mm/day); $K_c$ = crop coefficient |
| 6 | $ET_0 = \dfrac{0.408 \cdot \Delta (R_n - G) + \gamma \cdot \dfrac{900}{T+273} \cdot u_2 \cdot (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma (1 + 0.34 \cdot u_2)}$ | FAO Penman-Monteith reference ET (mm/day) |
| 7 | $u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID control law for valve positioner |
| 8 | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Control error = Setpoint − Process Variable |
| 9 | $MV(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | PID in Laplace form |
| 10 | $VWC = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric Water Content definition |
| 11 | $EC_{corrected} = EC_{measured} \cdot \left(1 + 0.019 \cdot (T - 25)\right)$ | Temperature compensation for EC probes |
| 12 | $K_{sat} = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta h}$ | Saturated hydraulic conductivity (Darcy's law) |
| 13 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Sensor calibration goodness-of-fit |
| 14 | $N = \dfrac{SF \cdot V}{C_{min}}$ | Minimum number of samples (Nyquist for SCADA poll) |
| 15 | $P_{loss} (dB) = 32.5 + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}(d)$ | Free-space path loss for LoRa link budget |

> **Standard units to memorize:** VWC (%), EC (dS/m or mS/cm), pH (unitless 0–14), ET (mm/day), $K_c$ (dimensionless 0.3–1.2), $K_p$ (proportional gain, dimensionless or %/%), $T_i, T_d$ (seconds).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Conservation (Kerala Paddy):** SCADA-controlled drip reduces water use by **40 %**.
- **Greenhouse Tomato Cultivation:** Maintains **±1 °C** temperature stability, boosting yield by **20 %**.
- **Dairy Farm SCADA:** Monitors rumination, milk yield, and barn ammonia (NH₃) for herd health.
- **Aquaponics:** SCADA balances pH, dissolved oxygen, and feed dosing.
- **Smart Village (KTU FYP hot-topic):** Centralized SCADA for community irrigation pumps using **LoRaWAN + cloud SCADA (AWS IoT, ThingsBoard).**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Soil Moisture Deficit into Valve Run-Time

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**. The examiner expects the candidate to start from first principles, list assumptions, and end with a clean, deployable equation.

### Problem Setup
A 1-hectare tomato field in Palakkad, Kerala, uses drip irrigation. Soil moisture readings show θ_measured = 15 % VWC. Set-point θ_sp = 22 % VWC. Root depth D_r = 0.4 m. Emitter discharge q = 4 L/h. Plant spacing 0.5 m × 0.5 m (one emitter per plant). Compute the **valve run-time T_run in minutes**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the moisture deficit.**
The deficit is the difference between desired and actual soil moisture at the root zone.

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta_{sp} - \theta_{measured}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\Delta\theta = 22\% - 15\% = 7\% \text{ VWC}
$$

This means we must add water equivalent to 7 % of the soil volume within the root zone.

**Step 2 — Convert volumetric deficit to a depth of water.**

The volume of soil in the root zone per unit field area is:

$$
V_{soil} = D_r \times A
$$

For 1 hectare, A = 10,000 m², D_r = 0.4 m:

$$
V_{soil} = 0.4 \text{ m} \times 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 4{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

Volume of water needed (since 1 m³ = 1000 L):

$$
V_{water} = \frac{\Delta\theta}{100} \times V_{soil} = \frac{7}{100} \times 4{,}000 = 280 \text{ m}^3 = 280{,}000 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Express depth in millimetres (preferred agronomic unit).**

$$
d_{gross} = \frac{\Delta\theta}{100} \times D_r \times 1000 \text{ mm/m} = \frac{7}{100} \times 0.4 \times 1000 = 28 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute number of emitters in 1 hectare.**

With 0.5 m × 0.5 m spacing, each emitter covers 0.25 m²:

$$
n_{emitters} = \frac{A}{\text{spacing area}} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ m}^2}{0.25 \text{ m}^2/\text{emitter}} = 40{,}000 \text{ emitters}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total system flow rate.**

$$
Q_{total} = n_{emitters} \times q = 40{,}000 \times 4 \text{ L/h} = 160{,}000 \text{ L/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute valve run-time.**

$$
T_{run} = \frac{V_{water}}{Q_{total}} \text{ in hours} = \frac{280{,}000}{160{,}000} = 1.75 \text{ h}
$$

Convert to minutes:

$$
T_{run} = 1.75 \times 60 = 105 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{T_{run} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \Delta\theta \cdot D_r \cdot A}{100 \cdot q \cdot n_{emitters}} \approx 105 \text{ min}}
$$

The SCADA controller will open the master solenoid valve for **105 minutes** to refill the root zone to the set-point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Simplified Penman–Monteith ET₀ (FAO-56 Reference Form)

The Penman-Monteith equation is the gold standard for reference evapotranspiration. Below is the **simplified FAO-56 form** used in agricultural SCADA weather stations.

### Starting Equation

$$
ET_0 = \frac{0.408 \, \Delta \, (R_n - G) + \gamma \, \frac{900}{T + 273} \, u_2 \, (e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma \, (1 + 0.34 \, u_2)}
$$

### Variable Definitions

- $\Delta$ = slope of saturation vapor pressure curve (kPa/°C)
- $R_n$ = net radiation (MJ/m²/day)
- $G$ = soil heat flux (MJ/m²/day)
- $\gamma$ = psychrometric constant (≈ 0.066 kPa/°C at sea level Kerala)
- $T$ = mean daily air temperature (°C)
- $u_2$ = wind speed at 2 m height (m/s)
- $e_s$ = saturation vapor pressure (kPa)
- $e_a$ = actual vapor pressure (kPa)

### Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation (Sample Day in Thrissur, Kerala)

**Assumed inputs from weather station SCADA:**

- $T$ = 28 °C, $RH$ = 70 %, $u_2$ = 2.1 m/s, $R_n$ = 14 MJ/m²/day, $G$ = 0.1 MJ/m²/day.

**Step A — Saturation vapor pressure $e_s$.**

$$
e_s = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \cdot T}{T + 237.3}\right)
$$

$$
e_s = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 28}{28 + 237.3}\right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.820) \approx 3.78 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step B — Actual vapor pressure $e_a$.**

$$
e_a = e_s \cdot \frac{RH}{100} = 3.78 \times 0.70 = 2.65 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step C — Vapor pressure deficit.**

$$
VPD = e_s - e_a = 3.78 - 2.65 = 1.13 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step D — Slope of saturation curve $\Delta$.**

$$
\Delta = \frac{4098 \cdot e_s}{(T + 237.3)^2} = \frac{4098 \times 3.78}{(28 + 237.3)^2} \approx 0.221 \text{ kPa/°C}
$$

**Step E — Substitute in numerator.**

$$
N_1 = 0.408 \times 0.221 \times (14 - 0.1) = 1.251
$$

$$
N_2 = 0.066 \times \frac{900}{28 + 273} \times 2.1 \times 1.13 = 0.066 \times 2.99 \times 2.1 \times 1.13 \approx 0.469
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1.251 + 0.469 = 1.720
$$

**Step F — Substitute in denominator.**

$$
D = 0.221 + 0.066 \times (1 + 0.34 \times 2.1) = 0.221 + 0.066 \times 1.714 = 0.221 + 0.1131 = 0.334
$$

**Step G — Compute ET₀.**

$$
ET_0 = \frac{1.720}{0.334} \approx 5.15 \text{ mm/day}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{ET_0 \approx 5.15 \text{ mm/day for the sampled day in Thrissur}}
$$

### Step H — Crop Evapotranspiration (For Tomato, $K_c = 1.15$ at mid-season)

$$
ET_c = K_c \cdot ET_0 = 1.15 \times 5.15 \approx 5.92 \text{ mm/day}
$$

The SCADA system can now use $ET_c$ in a **water-balance model** to schedule the next irrigation cycle, instead of relying solely on soil-moisture thresholds.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing SCADA Communication Technologies

| Feature | RS-485 (Modbus RTU) | Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4) | LoRaWAN | GSM/GPRS (4G) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 1.2 km | 100 m | 15 km | Cellular coverage |
| Data Rate | 10 Mbps | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps | 1–100 Mbps |
| Power | Low | Very Low | Very Low | High |
| Cost/node | Low | Low | Low | Medium (SIM) |
| Use Case | Greenhouse | In-field sensor mesh | Remote farm | Cloud SCADA gateway |
| Latency | <10 ms | <100 ms | 1–10 s | 100–500 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trend:** Questions increasingly prefer **LoRaWAN + Cloud SCADA (ThingsBoard/Blynk)** as the answer for "design a SCADA system for a 50-acre rubber plantation in Idukki."

---

## 3.4 Worked PID Example — Greenhouse Ventilation Fan Speed

Let the inside temperature PV (process variable) be T(t). Set-point SP = 25 °C. PID gains: K_p = 30, T_i = 60 s, T_d = 5 s.

### Error Signal

$$
e(t) = SP - PV = 25 - T(t)
$$

### PID Output (Position Form)

$$
u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]
$$

Assuming at t = 0, T = 30 °C, e = −5 °C:

- **P-term:** $K_p \cdot e = 30 \times (-5) = -150$ (negative → fan runs slow)
- **I-term:** initially 0
- **D-term:** initially 0

As temperature drops, e approaches 0, and the integral term eliminates steady-state offset. The **SCADA HMI** displays u(t) scaled to 0–100 % fan speed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical SCADA Architecture for a Smart Farm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    fieldA[Field Layer: Soil Moisture, pH, EC, DHT22, Rain Gauge, Pyranometer]
    actA[Field Layer: Solenoid Valves, Pumps, Dosing Unit, Exhaust Fans]
    rtuA[RTU Layer: PLC / ESP32 RTU Local PID Control]
    rtuB[RTU Layer: LoRa Node for Remote Plots]
    commA[Network Layer: RS-485 Modbus RTU Bus]
    commB[Network Layer: LoRaWAN Gateway]
    commC[Network Layer: GSM LTE 4G Modem]
    mtuA[Supervisory Layer: MTU Industrial PC WinCC Ignition]
    hmiA[Supervisory Layer: HMI Touchscreen 15 inch]
    cloudA[Cloud Layer: ThingsBoard Historian MQTT]
    userA[End User: Farmer Mobile App Web Dashboard]

    fieldA --> rtuA
    actA --> rtuA
    fieldA --> rtuB
    rtuA --> commA
    rtuB --> commB
    commB --> mtuA
    commA --> mtuA
    mtuA --> commC
    commC --> cloudA
    mtuA --> hmiA
    cloudA --> userA
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Drip Irrigation Control Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([SCADA Poll Cycle Start every 60 s])
    readS[Read Soil Moisture Sensor 15 cm Depth]
    readT[Read Temperature and ET0 from Weather Station]
    compA{Compare theta with Setpoint}
    calcDelta[Compute Deficit Delta Theta]
    calcRun[Compute Run Time T_run from formula]
    openV[Open Master Solenoid Valve DO1]
    runPump[Start Booster Pump DO2]
    timer[Timer: Countdown T_run Minutes]
    closeV[Close Master Solenoid Valve]
    stopPump[Stop Booster Pump]
    logD[Log Event to Historian with Timestamp]
    alarm[Send SMS via GSM if Sensor Failure]

    start --> readS --> readT --> compA
    compA -- theta less than setpoint --> calcDelta --> calcRun --> openV --> runPump --> timer --> closeV --> stopPump --> logD
    compA -- theta greater or equal --> logD
    readS -.sensor fault.-> alarm
    logD --> start
```

## 4.3 Greenhouse Micro-Climate SCADA Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensG[Temperature and Humidity Sensor DHT22 SHT31]
    sensL[Light Sensor PAR Lux]
    sensC[CO2 Sensor K30 NDIR]
    plcG[Greenhouse PLC Siemens LOGO]
    fan1[Exhaust Fan 1]
    fan2[Exhaust Fan 2]
    heater[Heater Coil]
    fogger[High Pressure Fogger]
    shade[Shade Net Motorized]
    growL[Grow Light LED Panel]
    hmiG[HMI Touchscreen Inside Greenhouse]
    mtug[MTU WinCC Supervisory PC]

    sensG --> plcG
    sensL --> plcG
    sensC --> plcG
    plcG --> fan1
    plcG --> fan2
    plcG --> heater
    plcG --> fogger
    plcG --> shade
    plcG --> growL
    plcG --> hmiG
    plcG --> mtug
```

## 4.4 Data Flow — Field Sensor to Cloud Historian

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorX[Sensor Analog Output 4 to 20 mA]
    rtux[RTU Analog Input Channel AI1]
    convx[ADC Conversion 12 bit Resolution]
    modx[Modbus Register Map Holding Register 40001]
    tx[RS-485 Transceiver to MTU]
    mtuX[SCADA Server Polls Register every 1 s]
    histX[Historian Time Series Database InfluxDB]
    vizX[Grafana Dashboard Trends and KPIs]
    smsX[Alarm Engine Sends SMS on Threshold]

    sensorX --> rtux --> convx --> modx --> tx --> mtuX --> histX --> vizX
    mtuX -.threshold breach.-> smsX
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**  
> **Define SCADA. List the four major hardware components of a SCADA system used in an automated greenhouse.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

SCADA stands for **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition**. It is a computer-based automation architecture that monitors and controls distributed field devices from a central location in real time.

The four major hardware components are:

1. **Field Sensors and Actuators** (e.g., DHT22, soil moisture probes, solenoid valves, exhaust fans).
2. **Remote Terminal Units / PLCs** (e.g., Siemens LOGO!, Schneider M221) for local data acquisition and control.
3. **Communication Network** (RS-485, Zigbee, LoRaWAN, GSM).
4. **Master Terminal Unit (MTU) with HMI** (e.g., industrial PC running WinCC or Ignition) for supervisory visualization and historian storage.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> **Differentiate between RTU and PLC in the context of agricultural SCADA.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | RTU | PLC |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Designed for remote, geographically distributed telemetry | Designed for factory-floor discrete control |
| Environment | Ruggedized for outdoor field (IP65/IP67) | Indoor panel-mount (IP20) |
| Communication | Master-slave telemetry (Modbus, DNP3) | Often proprietary fieldbus, also Modbus |
| Local Control | Limited logic, mostly data relay | Rich ladder logic, PID, sequencing |
| Typical Use in Agri | Weather station, soil-moisture nodes | Greenhouse climate controller, feed mill |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the functional architecture of a SCADA system for a **50-acre drip-irrigated banana plantation in Wayanad**. Describe the role of each layer with at least two examples of field devices.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** A SCADA system reads soil moisture θ_measured = 18 % VWC in a 0.8 hectare tomato plot. The set-point is 25 %, root depth is 0.5 m, emitter spacing is 0.4 m × 0.6 m, and emitter discharge is 2 L/h. Compute the **valve run-time in minutes** to restore the set-point.

---

**Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks**

The four-layer architecture is as follows:

1. **Field Layer (Sensors and Actuators):**
   - Soil moisture probes (capacitive or TDR) at 15 cm and 30 cm depths.
   - Soil temperature sensors, pH, and EC sensors for fertigation.
   - Weather station: tipping-bucket rain gauge, pyranometer, anemometer, DHT22.
   - Actuators: 16 motorized solenoid valves (zoned), 50 HP submersible pump, fertilizer dosing pumps.

2. **Control Layer (RTU/PLC):**
   - One master PLC (Siemens S7-1200) at the pump house.
   - 8 remote RTUs (ESP32 + RS-485) each handling 2 irrigation zones.
   - Performs local PID on pressure, schedules fertigation pH-EC loops.

3. **Communication Layer:**
   - RS-485 backbone (Modbus RTU, 9600 bps) connecting RTUs to master PLC.
   - LoRaWAN gateway on the hilltop for the most distant plot (5 km away).
   - GSM/LTE router for cloud telemetry to the farmer's mobile.

4. **Supervisory Layer (MTU + HMI):**
   - Industrial PC running Ignition SCADA with 21" HMI.
   - Web dashboard via ThingsBoard cloud for remote monitoring.
   - Historian in PostgreSQL stores 10 years of trend data.

---

**Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** A = 0.8 ha = 8,000 m², θ_sp = 25 %, θ_measured = 18 %, D_r = 0.5 m, q = 2 L/h, spacing = 0.4 m × 0.6 m.

**Step 1 — Moisture deficit:**

$$
\Delta\theta = 25 - 18 = 7\% \text{ VWC}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of emitters:**

$$
n_{emitters} = \frac{A}{0.4 \times 0.6} = \frac{8{,}000}{0.24} = 33{,}333 \text{ emitters}
$$

**Step 3 — Total system flow:**

$$
Q_{total} = 33{,}333 \times 2 = 66{,}666 \text{ L/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Water volume needed:**

$$
V_{water} = \frac{7}{100} \times 0.5 \times 8{,}000 = 280 \text{ m}^3 = 280{,}000 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 5 — Run time:**

$$
T_{run} = \frac{280{,}000}{66{,}666} \times 60 = 252 \text{ minutes} \approx 4.2 \text{ hours}
$$

**Answer:** The SCADA must keep the master valve open for approximately **252 minutes (4.2 hours)** to restore the root zone to the 25 % set-point.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe how SCADA is used for **greenhouse micro-climate control**. Mention at least four control loops.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A greenhouse SCADA uses a PID controller to maintain inside temperature at 25 °C. The current temperature is 30 °C. Given K_p = 25 %/°C, T_i = 90 s, T_d = 4 s, compute the **proportional, integral, and derivative contributions** for the first 5 seconds assuming the error decreases linearly at 1 °C/s.

---

**Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks**

Greenhouse micro-climate SCADA has the following control loops:

1. **Temperature Control Loop:** DHT22 → PLC → Exhaust Fan and Heater Coil.
2. **Humidity Control Loop:** SHT31 → PLC → Fogger and Dehumidifier.
3. **CO₂ Enrichment Loop:** K30 NDIR sensor → PLC → CO₂ solenoid valve (target 800–1200 ppm).
4. **Light / PAR Loop:** Quantum sensor → PLC → LED Grow Lights and Motorized Shade Net.
5. **Ventilation / Airflow Loop:** Anemometer inside + differential pressure → PLC → Roof vent motors.

The PLC executes four parallel PID loops and writes actuator outputs (0–100 %) via analog/digital outputs. The HMI displays live trends.

---

**Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** SP = 25 °C, T(0) = 30 °C, so e(0) = 25 − 30 = −5 °C. Error decreases linearly at 1 °C/s, i.e. e(t) = −5 + t.

**Proportional Term:**

$$
P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) = 25 \cdot (-5 + t)
$$

At t = 5 s: $P(5) = 25 \times 0 = 0\%$

**Integral Term:**

$$
I(t) = \frac{K_p}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau = \frac{25}{90} \int_0^t (-5 + \tau) d\tau
$$

$$
I(t) = \frac{25}{90} \left[-5t + \frac{t^2}{2}\right]
$$

At t = 5 s: $I(5) = \frac{25}{90} \times [-25 + 12.5] = \frac{25}{90} \times (-12.5) \approx -3.47\%$

**Derivative Term:**

$$
D(t) = K_p \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{de}{dt} = 25 \times 4 \times 1 = 100\%
$$

(Rate of change of error is constant = +1 °C/s)

**Total Output at t = 5 s:**

$$
u(5) = P + I + D = 0 + (-3.47) + 100 = 96.53\% \approx 97\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The fan runs at near full speed (97 %), dropping inside temperature sharply toward the set-point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in SCADA-Agriculture Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing RTU and PLC** — Examiner deducts 1 mark if roles are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Always state VWC (%), mm/day, °C, L/h. Naked numbers = ½ mark cut.
> 3. **Skipping assumptions** in derivations — State "Assume field capacity at root zone, no runoff, irrigation efficiency = 90 %" before substituting.
> 4. **Writing Modbus register addresses wrong** — Input registers are 3xxxx, Holding registers 4xxxx.
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** — Direction of data flow must be shown; otherwise −1 mark.
> 6. **Missing alarm management** in long answers — Always mention alarm thresholds, SMS/email, and historian logging.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **SCADA = Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition**; the **Master-Slave hierarchy** is RTU/PLC → MTU → HMI.
- **Four layers:** Field → Control → Communication → Supervisory.
- **Typical agricultural sensors:** Capacitive soil moisture, DHT22, pH, EC, rain gauge, pyranometer.
- **Common communication protocols:** **Modbus RTU (RS-485), Zigbee, LoRaWAN, GSM/GPRS, MQTT for cloud.**
- **PID is the workhorse** for valve/fan control; gains K_p, T_i, T_d must be tuned on-site.
- **ET₀ from Penman-Monteith** drives irrigation scheduling in water-scarce regions.
- **Setpoint rule:** $\theta_{sp} = \theta_{FC} \times MAD$ (e.g., 22 % for tomato in sandy loam).
- **Run-time formula:** $T_{run} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \Delta\theta \cdot D_r \cdot A}{100 \cdot q \cdot n_{emitters}}$ minutes.
- **Cybersecurity in modern SCADA:** Use VPN, firewalls, role-based HMI access; never expose port 502 directly to the internet.
- **Open-source SCADA platforms:** ScadaBR, OpenSCADA, ThingsBoard (cloud-native) — popular in KTU projects.
- **Future direction:** AI/ML-driven SCADA, Digital Twin of the farm, 5G URLLC for low-latency valve control.

---

✅ **End of Module 3 Notes — Applications of SCADA in Agriculture (PEICT753).** Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse Parameters and Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Parameters and Instrumentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Greenhouse – Formal KTU Definition

A **greenhouse** is a closed, transparent, or semi-transparent enclosed structure designed to create a controlled micro-climate for optimal plant growth by regulating key environmental parameters — namely **temperature, relative humidity, light intensity, carbon dioxide (CO₂) concentration, and soil moisture** — independent of the external ambient conditions.

**Greenhouse Instrumentation** refers to the integrated suite of **sensors, transducers, signal conditioners, data acquisition systems (DAQ), controllers, and actuators** used to measure these parameters and automatically actuate climate-control devices (heaters, fans, foggers, shade nets, CO₂ injectors, irrigation valves) to maintain them at agronomically optimal setpoints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 4 expects students to understand *what* is measured, *how* it is measured, *which sensor* is used, and *what control action* follows. Memorize parameter ranges — examiners love numerical questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Plant Hospital ICU"

Think of a greenhouse as an **Intensive Care Unit (ICU) for plants**. Just as a human ICU continuously monitors a patient's **heart rate (≈ light/CO₂ for photosynthesis)**, **body temperature (≈ air temperature)**, **oxygen saturation (≈ CO₂ level)**, and **IV fluids (≈ soil moisture)**, the greenhouse uses analogous sensors to keep the plants in a "Goldilocks Zone" — never too hot, never too cold, never too dry, never starved of light or CO₂. The **PLC / microcontroller** acts as the doctor-on-call, deciding when to switch on the ventilator (fan), the humidifier (fogger), or the IV drip (drip irrigation).

### 1.3 The Five Primary Greenhouse Parameters

| # | Parameter | Standard Unit | Typical Agronomic Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air Temperature | °C | **15 °C – 30 °C** |
| 2 | Relative Humidity (RH) | % | **60 % – 80 %** |
| 3 | Light Intensity (PAR / PPFD) | µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | **200 – 800 µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹** |
| 4 | CO₂ Concentration | ppm | **350 – 1500 ppm** |
| 5 | Soil Moisture | % VWC (Volumetric Water Content) | **20 % – 60 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> Two **secondary but critical** parameters are also assessed: **Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD)** in kPa and **Electrical Conductivity (EC)** of soil in dS/m.

### 1.4 Why Instrumentation? — The "Why" Behind Automation

Without instrumentation:
- A sudden **+5 °C** rise in noon temperature can wilt crops within **30 minutes**.
- **RH > 90 %** triggers fungal pathogens (*Botrytis cinerea* — gray mold).
- **CO₂ < 200 ppm** halts photosynthesis (compensation point).

Instrumentation converts these biological needs into **closed-loop electrical control signals**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Parameter 1 — Air Temperature (Tₐ)

**Why it matters:** Governs the **rate of enzyme activity** in photosynthesis (Q₁₀ rule — metabolic rate doubles per 10 °C rise, up to an optimum).

**Measurement Methods:**
- **Thermocouples (Type T, Type K):** Seebeck-effect based; rugged, cheap, range **−200 °C to +1350 °C**.
- **RTDs (Pt100 / Pt1000):** Platinum resistance changes linearly with temperature. Accuracy ±0.1 °C.
- **Thermistors (NTC):** Highly sensitive (ΔR per °C), but non-linear — requires Steinhart-Hart equation.
- **Infrared (IR) Thermometers:** Non-contact; measure canopy leaf temperature directly via **Stefan-Boltzmann radiation law**.

$$P = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$$

where $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of leaf (≈ 0.97 – 0.98), $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.

### 2.2 Parameter 2 — Relative Humidity (RH)

**Definition:** Ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapor ($P_v$) to saturation vapor pressure at the same temperature ($P_{vs}$).

$$RH = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}} \times 100\%$$

**Saturation Vapor Pressure** is computed via the **Magnus-Tetens equation** (standard meteorological formula):

$$P_{vs}(T) = 0.61078 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \, T}{T + 237.3}\right) \quad \text{[kPa]}$$

where $T$ is in °C.

**Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD)** — the *true* driver of plant transpiration:

$$VPD = P_{vs} - P_v = P_{vs}\!\left(1 - \frac{RH}{100}\right) \quad \text{[kPa]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **VPD is the gold standard** for transpiration control. Optimal vegetative VPD = **0.8 – 1.2 kPa**. KTU expects you to compute VPD given T and RH.

**Sensors:** Capacitive polymer humidity sensors (e.g., **DHT22, SHT31, HIH-4000**); Chilled-mirror dew-point hygrometers (most accurate).

### 2.3 Parameter 3 — Light Intensity (PAR / PPFD)

Plants use only the **400 nm – 700 nm** waveband — **Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR)**.

**Quantum sensor units:**
- **PPFD** (Photosynthetic Photon Flux Density): µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹
- **Daily Light Integral (DLI):** mol·m⁻²·day⁻¹ → $DLI = PPFD \times \text{photoperiod (seconds)} / 10^6$

**Sensors:** **Quantum sensors** (LI-COR LI-190R) with silicon photodiodes filtered to match the PAR spectrum.

### 2.4 Parameter 4 — CO₂ Concentration

Measured by:
- **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) sensors**: Gold standard — measures IR absorption at **4.26 µm** band unique to CO₂. Range **0 – 5000 ppm**, accuracy ±30 ppm.
- **EC (Electrochemical) sensors**: Cheaper, slower response.

### 2.5 Parameter 5 — Soil Moisture

**Volumetric Water Content (VWC):**

$$\theta_v = \frac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} \times 100\%$$

**Sensors:**
- **TDR (Time Domain Reflectometry)** — most accurate; measures dielectric permittivity via propagation time of EM pulse.
- **Capacitive probes** — cheap, Arduino-compatible.
- **Tensiometers** — measure soil water tension (kPa).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relative Humidity | $RH = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{vs}} \times 100$ | % | 60 – 80 % |
| Sat. Vapor Pressure (Magnus) | $P_{vs} = 0.61078 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27 T}{T + 237.3}}$ | kPa | 1.0 – 5.0 kPa |
| Vapor Pressure Deficit | $VPD = P_{vs}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{RH}{100}\right)$ | kPa | 0.8 – 1.2 kPa |
| Stefan-Boltzmann (IR Temp.) | $P = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | W·m⁻² | — |
| DLI | $DLI = \dfrac{PPFD \times t_{sec}}{10^{6}}$ | mol·m⁻²·d⁻¹ | 12 – 20 |
| Dew Point (Magnus) | $\gamma(T,RH) = \dfrac{b \cdot \alpha(T,RH)}{a - \alpha(T,RH)}$ | °C | 10 – 20 °C |
| VWC | $\theta_v = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s} \times 100$ | % | 20 – 60 % |
| Net Radiation Balance | $R_n = R_s\!\left(1 - \alpha\right) + R_l\!\downarrow - R_l\!\uparrow$ | W·m⁻² | — |
| Q₁₀ Temperature Coeff. | $R_2 = R_1 \cdot Q_{10}^{(T_2 - T_1)/10}$ | ratio | 2.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all formulas above: $T$ is in °C unless stated; $\alpha$ (albedo) for greenhouse cover ≈ **0.10 – 0.15**; $\sigma$ (Stefan-Boltzmann const.) = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

Greenhouse instrumentation enables **precision agriculture** in **Dutch-style high-tech glasshouses** (yielding 70 kg/m² of tomatoes), in **polyhouses** (low-cost Indian variant), and in **vertical farms**. Without it, year-round cultivation of capsicum, cucumber, gerbera, and strawberry in Kerala's monsoon climate would be impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Saturation Vapor Pressure via Clausius-Clapeyron

Starting from the first-principles **Clausius-Clapeyron relation** for liquid-vapor equilibrium:

$$\frac{dP_{vs}}{dT} = \frac{L}{T \, \Delta v} \approx \frac{L \, P_{vs}}{R_v \, T^{2}}$$

where $L$ = latent heat of vaporization (J·kg⁻¹), $R_v$ = specific gas constant for water vapor (461.5 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹).

**Step 1:** Separate variables and integrate between reference state $(T_0, P_{vs,0})$ and $(T, P_{vs})$:

$$\int_{P_{vs,0}}^{P_{vs}} \frac{dP_{vs}}{P_{vs}} = \frac{L}{R_v} \int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate both integrals (assuming L is approximately constant over small T range):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{vs}}{P_{vs,0}}\right) = \frac{L}{R_v}\left(\frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T}\right) = \frac{L}{R_v}\!\left(\frac{T - T_0}{T \cdot T_0}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$P_{vs}(T) = P_{vs,0} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{L}{R_v}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute empirical constants (Murray, 1967) and convert T to °C by fitting to standard tables → **Magnus-Tetens form**:

$$\boxed{\,P_{vs}(T) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \, T}{T + 237.3}\right) \;\text{kPa}\,}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — VPD Calculation

**Problem:** Inside a polyhouse, $T_{air} = 28\,°C$ and $RH = 70\%$. Compute the Saturation Vapor Pressure, Actual Vapor Pressure, and Vapor Pressure Deficit.

**Step 1 — Saturation Vapor Pressure at 28 °C:**

$$P_{vs} = 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 28}{28 + 237.3}\right)$$

Compute the exponent numerator: $17.27 \times 28 = 483.56$

Compute the denominator: $28 + 237.3 = 265.3$

Fraction: $483.56 / 265.3 = 1.8227$

Exponentiate: $e^{1.8227} = 6.1936$

Multiply by 0.61078: $P_{vs} = 0.61078 \times 6.1936 = 3.783 \;\text{kPa}$

**Step 2 — Actual Vapor Pressure:**

$$P_v = RH \times P_{vs} = 0.70 \times 3.783 = 2.648 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Vapor Pressure Deficit:**

$$VPD = P_{vs} - P_v = 3.783 - 2.648 = 1.135 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Interpretation:** $VPD = 1.135$ kPa lies in the **optimal vegetative range (0.8 – 1.2 kPa)** → transpiration is healthy; **no fogger needed**.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Daily Light Integral (DLI)

A tomato greenhouse receives an average PPFD of **450 µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹** during a **12-hour photoperiod**.

$$DLI = \frac{PPFD \times t_{sec}}{10^{6}} = \frac{450 \times (12 \times 3600)}{10^{6}} = \frac{450 \times 43200}{10^{6}} = 19.44 \;\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·d}^{-1}$$

This exceeds the **tomato optimum (≥ 12 mol·m⁻²·d⁻¹)** — supplemental lighting unnecessary.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor vs Parameter Decision Table

| Parameter | Primary Sensor | Secondary Sensor | Output Signal | Cost Tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Air Temp | Pt100 RTD | Thermocouple K | 4–20 mA / 0–10 V | Low |
| Leaf Temp | IR Pyrometer | Thermistor | 0–5 V | Mid |
| Humidity | SHT31 (capacitive) | Chilled-mirror | I²C / SDI-12 | Low–Mid |
| Light (PAR) | Quantum sensor (LI-190R) | Lux meter (approx.) | 0–5 V | Mid–High |
| CO₂ | NDIR (K30) | Electrochemical | 0–10 V / RS485 | Mid |
| Soil Moisture | TDR (CS616) | Capacitive probe | 4–20 mA / SDI-12 | Mid |
| Soil pH | Glass electrode | ISFET | mV / BNC | Mid |
| EC | 4-electrode conductivity | — | mS/cm | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Greenhouse Climate Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Greenhouse Microclimate] --> B[Sensor Array Bank]
    B --> B1[Temperature RTD]
    B --> B2[Humidity SHT31]
    B --> B3[PAR Quantum Sensor]
    B --> B4[CO2 NDIR Sensor]
    B --> B5[Soil Moisture TDR]
    B1 --> C[Signal Conditioning Unit]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Data Acquisition DAQ]
    D --> E[Microcontroller PLC]
    E --> F[Decision Algorithm PID]
    F --> G[Actuator Bank]
    G --> G1[Exhaust Fan]
    G --> G2[Heater]
    G --> G3[Fogger Misting]
    G --> G4[Shade Net Motor]
    G --> G5[CO2 Injector Valve]
    G --> G6[Drip Irrigation Pump]
    G1 --> A
    G2 --> A
    G3 --> A
    G4 --> A
    G5 --> A
    G6 --> A
    E --> H[Cloud IoT Dashboard]
    H --> I[Farmer Mobile Alert]
```

### 4.2 Sensor-to-Actuator Closed-Loop Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Setpoint Input eg 25C] --> SUM[Summer Comparator Junction]
    SENSE[Sensor Measurement] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal e]
    ERR --> PID[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    PID --> CTRL[Control Signal 4-20mA]
    CTRL --> ACT[Actuator Fan Heater Valve]
    ACT --> PROC[Greenhouse Process Plant]
    PROC --> SENSE
    PROC --> DIST[Disturbance External Sun Wind]
    DIST -.-> PROC
```

### 4.3 Vapor Pressure Deficit Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Read T and RH from sensors] --> CALC[Calculate Pvs via Magnus Eqn]
    CALC --> PV[Compute Pv equals RH times Pvs]
    PV --> V[Compute VPD equals Pvs minus Pv]
    V --> Q{VPD check}
    Q -->|VPD less than 0.4 kPa| A1[Action Activate Fogger and Dehumidifier]
    Q -->|VPD between 0.8 and 1.2 kPa| A2[Action Optimal Maintain Status]
    Q -->|VPD greater than 1.6 kPa| A3[Action Activate Fogger and Reduce Ventilation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block 4.1 above depicts the canonical **greenhouse climate-control architecture** expected in KTU 14-mark answers. Memorize the feedback direction: Sensor → DAQ → PLC → Actuator → Greenhouse → (back to Sensor).
