<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`
**List any six essential parameters that must be monitored inside a greenhouse. For each parameter, name the most commonly used sensor.**

**Model Answer (6 points):**

| # | Parameter | Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air Temperature | Pt100 RTD / Thermocouple |
| 2 | Relative Humidity | Capacitive polymer (SHT31) |
| 3 | Light (PAR) | Quantum sensor |
| 4 | CO₂ Concentration | NDIR sensor |
| 5 | Soil Moisture | TDR / Capacitive probe |
| 6 | Soil pH | Glass electrode |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for parameter list + 1 mark for sensor mapping + 1 mark for unit/range correctness.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`
**Define Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD). Why is VPD preferred over Relative Humidity (RH) for irrigation scheduling in greenhouses?**

**Model Answer:**
VPD is the difference between the saturation vapor pressure at a given temperature and the actual partial pressure of water vapor: $VPD = P_{vs} - P_v$ (kPa).

VPD is preferred because:
- RH is **temperature-dependent** (same RH at different T means different transpiration pull).
- VPD directly represents the **atmospheric demand** for water from the leaf stomata.
- It correlates linearly with transpiration rate, enabling predictive irrigation control.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for limitation of RH + 1 for VPD merit.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop climate-control architecture** of a modern automated greenhouse. List all major sensors and actuators and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyhouse is maintained at $T = 30\,°C$ and $RH = 65\%$. Compute the Saturation Vapor Pressure, Actual Vapor Pressure, Vapor Pressure Deficit, and Dew Point Temperature. State whether the condition is optimal for tomato cultivation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The greenhouse climate-control architecture consists of five stages:

1. **Sensing Stage** — Pt100 RTDs (air temperature), SHT31 (humidity), LI-190R (PAR), K30 NDIR (CO₂), CS616 TDR (soil moisture).
2. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification, linearization, cold-junction compensation for thermocouples, and ADC conversion.
3. **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** — Multiplexing and digitization (e.g., 16-bit ADC at ≥ 1 kS/s).
4. **Controller** — PLC / Arduino / Raspberry Pi implementing a **PID algorithm**: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$.
5. **Actuation** — Exhaust fans (cooling), heaters (heating), foggers (humidification), shade nets (light reduction), CO₂ injectors, drip valves.

The **feedback loop** ensures setpoint tracking: Sensor → DAQ → Controller → Actuator → Greenhouse → Sensor. *(Full marks awarded for diagram + 6 function descriptions.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Saturation Vapor Pressure at 30 °C:**

$$P_{vs} = 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 30}{30 + 237.3}\right) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{518.1}{267.3}\right)$$

$$= 0.61078 \cdot \exp(1.9382) = 0.61078 \times 6.946 = 4.243 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Vapor Pressure:**

$$P_v = 0.65 \times 4.243 = 2.758 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Vapor Pressure Deficit:**

$$VPD = 4.243 - 2.758 = 1.485 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Dew Point (Magnus Inverse Formula):**

$$\gamma(T,RH) = \frac{237.3 \times \alpha}{17.27 - \alpha}, \quad \alpha = \frac{\ln(RH/100) + \frac{17.27 T}{237.3 + T}}{\text{denominator pre-calc}}$$

Direct simplified Magnus inverse:

$$\alpha = \frac{17.27 \times T}{237.3 + T} + \ln(RH/100) = \frac{17.27 \times 30}{267.3} + \ln(0.65)$$

$$= 1.9382 + (-0.4308) = 1.5074$$

$$T_{dew} = \frac{237.3 \times 1.5074}{17.27 - 1.5074} = \frac{357.7}{15.7626} \approx 22.69\,°C$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
$VPD = 1.485$ kPa is slightly **above the optimal vegetative band** (0.8 – 1.2 kPa). For tomato, the recommended range is **0.8 – 1.0 kPa**. **Conclusion:** Mildly dry — recommend activating the fogger to lower VPD to ~1.0 kPa.

*(7 marks: 2 for Pvs + 2 for Pv/VPD + 2 for dew point + 1 for agronomic conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Slot]`

**(a)** Describe the working principle of an **NDIR CO₂ sensor** and a **TDR soil moisture sensor**. Why is NDIR preferred over electrochemical CO₂ sensors for greenhouse use? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A greenhouse lettuce crop requires DLI = **14 mol·m⁻²·d⁻¹**. If the natural photoperiod offers PPFD = **280 µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹** for **10 hours**, compute the deficit and recommend the supplemental LED wattage per m² (assume LED efficacy = 2.5 µmol·J⁻¹). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**NDIR Sensor:**
- An IR source emits broadband radiation; a narrow-band optical filter at **4.26 µm** (CO₂ absorption band) selects the relevant wavelength.
- A thermopile or pyroelectric detector measures transmitted intensity.
- By **Beer-Lambert law**: $I = I_0 \, e^{-k \, c \, l}$, where $c$ is CO₂ concentration, $k$ is absorption coefficient, $l$ is path length.
- Higher CO₂ → more absorption → lower detector output → concentration computed.
- **Advantages:** Non-consumptive, long-term stable (>10 years), no cross-sensitivity with other gases.
- **Why preferred over electrochemical:** EC sensors consume the gas via redox reaction, drift within 6–12 months, and have slower response; NDIR is **non-dispersive**, stable, and faster.

**TDR Sensor:**
- A fast-rise-time EM pulse (~1 ns) is sent along stainless-steel rods inserted in soil.
- The **travel time** depends on the soil's dielectric permittivity $\varepsilon_r$.
- Water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) dominates the bulk permittivity; dry soil ($\varepsilon_r \approx 3$–$5$).
- $\theta_v$ is computed via **Topp's equation**: $\theta_v = -5.3 \times 10^{-2} + 2.92 \times 10^{-2}\,\varepsilon_r - 5.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\varepsilon_r^{2} + 4.3 \times 10^{-6}\,\varepsilon_r^{3}$.
- Accurate to ±2 % VWC; salinity-independent.

*(7 marks: 3 NDIR + 3 TDR + 1 comparison.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Natural DLI delivered:**

$$DLI_{nat} = \frac{280 \times (10 \times 3600)}{10^6} = \frac{280 \times 36000}{10^6} = 10.08 \;\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·d}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — DLI Deficit:**

$$\Delta DLI = 14 - 10.08 = 3.92 \;\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·d}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Required supplemental photon flux:**

Convert DLI deficit into required PAR flux over the missing photoperiod (assume supplemental lighting for 8 hours):

$$PPFD_{req} = \frac{\Delta DLI \times 10^6}{t_{sec}} = \frac{3.92 \times 10^6}{8 \times 3600} = \frac{3.92 \times 10^6}{28800} \approx 136.1 \;\mu\text{mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to power per m² using LED efficacy:**

$$\text{Power} = \frac{PPFD_{req}}{\text{Efficacy}} = \frac{136.1}{2.5} = 54.4 \;\text{W·m}^{-2}$$

**Conclusion:** Install **~55 W/m² of supplemental LED lighting** for 8 hours/day to achieve the target DLI of 14 mol·m⁻²·d⁻¹.

*(7 marks: 1 setup + 2 DLI natural + 1 deficit + 2 PPFD conversion + 1 final wattage + conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss zones in greenhouse instrumentation questions:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Writing PPFD in lux or DLI in µmol — examiner deducts 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Confusing RH and VPD:** RH is a *ratio*; VPD is an *absolute pressure in kPa*. Many students write VPD as a percentage — **wrong**.
> 3. **Forgetting Stefan-Boltzmann constant** in IR temperature derivations: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ must appear in units.
> 4. **PID controller formula:** Writing only $K_p$ without the integral $K_i \int e\,dt$ and derivative $K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ terms loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Sensor selection:** Saying "thermometer" instead of "Pt100 RTD" or "type-T thermocouple" — **too vague for full credit**.
> 6. **NDIR wavelength:** Must specify **4.26 µm** to score full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🌡️ **Five core parameters:** Temperature, Humidity, Light (PAR), CO₂, Soil Moisture.
- 📐 **Magnus equation** is the key formula for $P_{vs}$ — memorize coefficients **17.27** and **237.3**.
- 💧 **VPD (0.8–1.2 kPa)** is the modern irrigation trigger — *not* RH.
- ☀️ **DLI (mol·m⁻²·d⁻¹)** governs total daily light; PPFD is instantaneous.
- 🌬️ **NDIR** for CO₂ (4.26 µm), **TDR** for soil moisture (dielectric), **Pt100** for temperature.
- ⚙️ **PID controller:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ — three terms, full credit only with all three.
- 🔁 **Control loop direction:** Sensor → DAQ → PLC/PID → Actuator → Greenhouse → Sensor (memorize!).
- 🧪 **Topp's equation** links dielectric permittivity to volumetric water content.
- 🌍 **Stefan-Boltzmann:** $P = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ — for IR canopy temperature sensors.
- 🇮🇳 **Polyhouse vs Glasshouse:** Polyhouse is the low-cost Indian adaptation; sensor suite is identical.
- 📊 **CO₂ compensation point:** ~100 ppm; saturation point: ~1500 ppm for most C₃ crops.
- ⚡ **LED efficacy** typical modern horticulture LED: **2.5 – 4.0 µmol·J⁻¹**.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Greenhouse Parameters and Instrumentation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Greenhouse Effect # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Greenhouse Effect (Atmospheric Physics Definition)** > The *greenhouse effect* is the physical phenomenon by which short-wavelength solar radiation (visible and near-infrared, 0.2–4.0 µm) passes relatively unimpeded through the Earth's transparent atmosphere, is absorbed by the ground surface, and is re-emitted as long-wavelength thermal infrared radiation (4.0–100 µm), which is then partially absorbed and re-radiated in all directions by atmospheric trace gases — known as **Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)** — namely water vapour (H₂O), carbon dioxide (CO₂), methane (CH₄), nitrous oxide (N₂O), ozone (O₃), and chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs). From the **agricultural instrumentation** perspective, the term also refers to the engineered enclosure (a *polyhouse* or *glasshouse*) that exploits this very principle to create a controlled micro-climate for protected cultivation of horticultural crops. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blanket on a Cold Night" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine stepping out of a shower on a cold winter night. The bathroom mirror is fogged because water vapour in the air traps the heat your body radiates, just like a woollen blanket traps body heat. The blanket does not *create* heat; it merely **slows down the rate of heat loss**. Similarly, greenhouse gases do not generate heat; they **trap the outgoing terrestrial infrared radiation**, raising the equilibrium surface temperature of Earth from an estimated **−18 °C** (without GHGs) to the actual global mean of approximately **+15 °C** — a warming of roughly **33 °C**. ### 1.3 Key Quantitative Constants & Spectral Bands | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Solar constant (incident flux at TOA) | $S_0$ | **1361** | W m⁻² | | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.670374419 × 10⁻⁸** | W m⁻² K⁻⁴ | | Earth's mean surface temperature | $T_s$ | **288.15** | K (≈ 15 °C) | | Effective radiative temperature | $T_e$ | **255** | K (≈ −18 °C) | | Earth's albedo (Bond) | $A$ | **0.306** | dimensionless | | Principal GHG — Water Vapour | H₂O | 0.25 % (variable) | by volume | | Principal GHG — Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | **~420** (2024) | ppm | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Radiative Forcing Framework The greenhouse effect is best understood through the **radiative energy balance** at the Top of Atmosphere (TOA) and the surface. ### 2.1.1 The Three-Step Mechanism 1. **Transmission of SW radiation:** Short-wave (SW) solar radiation $\lambda \in [0.2, 4.0]\ \mu m$ passes through the atmosphere with low absorption (transmissivity $\tau_{SW} \approx 0.7$). 2. **Absorption at the surface:** The ground absorbs the transmitted flux, warms up, and re-emits according to the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** in the long-wave (LW) infrared band $\lambda \in [4.0, 100]\ \mu m$. 3. **Re-radiation by GHGs:** Greenhouse gases absorb the outgoing LW radiation, warm up, and re-emit it isotropically — half goes outward to space, half returns to the ground (**back-radiation** or **atmospheric counter-radiation**). ### 2.1.2 Energy Balance at the Surface At radiative equilibrium, the net flux at the surface must equal zero: $$F_{SW}^{\downarrow}(1-A) + F_{LW}^{\downarrow} - F_{LW}^{\uparrow} - F_{sens} - F_{lat} = 0$$ where $F_{sens}$ and $F_{lat}$ are sensible and latent heat fluxes (carried by convection and evaporation respectively). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every equation a KTU examiner expects a student to reproduce for the *Greenhouse Effect* topic. **All $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation is rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.** | # | Equation (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F^{\star} = \sigma T^{4}$ | Stefan–Boltzmann radiant exitance | Blackbody emission | | 2 | $F = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Grey-body emission ($\varepsilon$ = emissivity) | Soil, foliage, glass | | 3 | $T_e = \left[\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4\sigma}\right]^{1/4}$ | Effective radiative temperature | Planet without atmosphere | | 4 | $T_s = \left[\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4\sigma(1-\tfrac{\varepsilon_a}{2})}\right]^{1/4}$ | Surface temperature with single atmospheric layer | Single-layer greenhouse model | | 5 | $G = \dfrac{1}{1-\tfrac{\varepsilon_a}{2}}$ | Greenhouse factor (amplification) | Warming ratio | | 6 | $RF = \sum_i a_i \Delta C_i$ | Radiative forcing by gas $i$ | Climate impact assessment | | 7 | $\tau_\lambda = e^{-k_\lambda u}$ | Beer–Lambert transmittance (path $u$, absorption coeff. $k_\lambda$) | Gaseous absorption | | 8 | $\dfrac{dQ}{dt} = m c_p \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Thermal storage inside a greenhouse | Indoor temperature dynamics | | 9 | $U = h_c + h_r$ | Overall heat-transfer coefficient (convective + radiative) | Greenhouse energy loss | | 10 | $E = \dfrac{\rho L_v (e_s - e_a)}{r_a + r_s}$ | Penman–Monteith evapotranspiration | Crop water demand | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Climate Modelling:** Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) and General Circulation Models (GCMs) parameterise GHG radiative transfer using the above equations. - **Precision Agriculture:** The *enclosed greenhouse* exploits the same physics to maintain **optimal air temperature (18–28 °C)**, **relative humidity (60–80 %)**, and **CO₂ enrichment (800–1200 ppm)** for photosynthesis. - **Sensor Calibration:** Thermal infrared radiometers used in agri-robotics (canopy temperature) rely on **Planck's Law**: $$B_\lambda(T) = \dfrac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}\left(e^{hc/\lambda k_B T}-1\right)}$$ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Bare-Rock (No Atmosphere) Equilibrium Temperature We start with the **incoming solar power intercepted by the Earth** and equate it to the **outgoing thermal radiation** from a uniform sphere of radius $R_E$. ### Step 1: Solar power intercepted by Earth The solar flux at the top of the atmosphere is $S_0$. Earth presents a circular cross-section of area $\pi R_E^{2}$, and reflects a fraction $A$ (the Bond albedo). The absorbed power is therefore: $$P_{abs} = S_0 (1-A)\, \pi R_E^{2}$$ ### Step 2: Thermal power radiated by Earth By the Stefan–Boltzmann Law, the Earth radiates from its entire spherical surface area $4\pi R_E^{2}$ at effective temperature $T_e$: $$P_{rad} = 4\pi R_E^{2} \sigma T_e^{4}$$ ### Step 3: Equate the two at radiative equilibrium At steady state, $P_{abs} = P_{rad}$: $$S_0(1-A)\,\pi R_E^{2} = 4\pi R_E^{2}\,\sigma\, T_e^{4}$$ ### Step 4: Cancel the common geometric factors The factor $\pi R_E^{2}$ appears on both sides and cancels. The factor of **4** in the denominator is the well-known ratio of sphere-area to disc-area, which captures the geometric dilution of solar flux over the entire planetary surface: $$S_0(1-A) = 4\sigma T_e^{4}$$ ### Step 5: Solve for $T_e$ Rearranging for $T_e$: $$T_e^{4} = \dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4\sigma}$$ $$\boxed{\,T_e = \left[\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4\sigma}\right]^{1/4}\,}$$ ### Step 6: Numerical evaluation Substituting the standard values: $S_0 = 1361\ \text{W m}^{-2}$, $A = 0.306$, $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-4}$: $$T_e = \left[\dfrac{1361 \times (1-0.306)}{4 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8}}\right]^{1/4}$$ $$T_e = \left[\dfrac{1361 \times 0.694}{2.268 \times 10^{-7}}\right]^{1/4} = \left[\dfrac{944.4}{2.268 \times 10^{-7}}\right]^{1/4}$$ $$T_e = \left[4.164 \times 10^{9}\right]^{1/4} \approx 254.6\ \text{K} \approx -18.5\ ^\circ\text{C}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **effective radiative temperature of Earth** — colder than the actual surface because we have ignored the atmosphere. The real mean surface temperature of **+15 °C** is achieved only after the greenhouse effect is added. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single-Layer Atmospheric Model We now introduce a single isothermal atmospheric layer at temperature $T_a$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_a$ in the LW band, and a perfect SW transmissivity. ### Step 1: Surface energy balance The surface receives three LW contributions and emits one: 1. Downward atmospheric radiation: $\varepsilon_a \sigma T_a^{4}$ 2. Reflected portion of its own emission (the ground is assumed to be a blackbody at LW, $\varepsilon_g \approx 1$): 0 3. Net solar absorbed (computed above) The surface emits $ \sigma T_s^{4} $. At equilibrium: $$\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4} + \varepsilon_a \sigma T_a^{4} = \sigma T_s^{4}$$ ### Step 2: Atmospheric layer energy balance The atmosphere absorbs the surface LW emission, plus the reflected solar (ignored for simplicity in a grey model), and emits $\varepsilon_a \sigma T_a^{4}$ both upward and downward: $$\sigma T_s^{4} = 2\, \varepsilon_a \sigma T_a^{4}$$ ### Step 3: Solve for $T_a$ in terms of $T_s$ Dividing both sides by $2\varepsilon_a \sigma$: $$\dfrac{T_s^{4}}{2\varepsilon_a} = T_a^{4}$$ $$T_a^{4} = \dfrac{T_s^{4}}{2\varepsilon_a}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute back into the surface equation $$\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4} + \varepsilon_a \sigma \left(\dfrac{T_s^{4}}{2\varepsilon_a}\right) = \sigma T_s^{4}$$ The $\varepsilon_a$ cancels in the second term: $$\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4} + \dfrac{\sigma T_s^{4}}{2} = \sigma T_s^{4}$$ ### Step 5: Isolate $T_s^{4}$ Subtract $\tfrac{1}{2}\sigma T_s^{4}$ from both sides: $$\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4} = \sigma T_s^{4} - \dfrac{\sigma T_s^{4}}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma T_s^{4}}{2}$$ ### Step 6: Final closed-form expression Multiplying both sides by 2 and dividing by $\sigma$: $$T_s^{4} = \dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{2\sigma}$$ Compare with the bare-rock result $T_e^{4} = \dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4\sigma}$. The surface temperature with the atmosphere is **larger by a factor of $2$ inside the numerator**, hence: $$\boxed{\,T_s = 2^{1/4}\, T_e = \left[\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{2\sigma}\right]^{1/4}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **greenhouse factor** $G = 2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$ predicts that the presence of an opaque ($\varepsilon_a = 1$) single atmospheric layer raises the surface temperature by **18.9 %** relative to the bare-rock value. Generalising to a single partially transparent layer of emissivity $\varepsilon_a$, the model gives $T_s = T_e\,(1-\varepsilon_a/2)^{-1/4}$, recovering the KTU formula $T_s = \left[\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4\sigma(1-\varepsilon_a/2)}\right]^{1/4}$ from the cheat sheet. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Greenhouse Instrumentation Energy Budget For an **instrumented agricultural greenhouse** of floor area $A_g$, the transient thermal balance is: $$m_g c_p \dfrac{dT_{in}}{dt} = Q_{SW} + Q_{heater} - Q_{loss}$$ The heat-loss term combines conduction through the cover, ventilation, and long-wave radiative exchange: $$Q_{loss} = U A_c (T_{in} - T_{out}) + \rho_a c_a \dot V (T_{in} - T_{out}) + A_c \varepsilon_c \sigma (T_{in}^{4} - T_{sky}^{4})$$ Solving the steady-state condition $\tfrac{dT_{in}}{dt}=0$ gives the **design-point indoor temperature** that the controller (PID or ON-OFF) must maintain — the foundation of every climate-control algorithm in modern poly-houses. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid — Block Diagram of a Greenhouse-Climate Instrumentation Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Solar Irradiance
Pyranometer 0 to 1200 W per m2] --> MUX[Signal Conditioning
and Multiplexer] B1[Air Temperature
PT100 RTD plus LM35] --> MUX C1[Relative Humidity
HIH8120 Capacitive] --> MUX D1[CO2 Concentration
NDIR Sensor 0 to 5000 ppm] --> MUX E1[Leaf Wetness
Dielectric Sensor] --> MUX F1[Soil Moisture
TDR Probe] --> MUX MUX --> ADC[16 bit ADC
4 to 20 mA Loop] ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller
ARM Cortex M4] MCU --> LCD[Local HMI Display] MCU --> CLOUD[IoT Cloud Gateway
MQTT over WiFi] MCU --> ACT1[Exhaust Fan Relay] MCU --> ACT2[Misting Valve Driver] MCU --> ACT3[Shade Net Motor] MCU --> ACT4[CO2 Solenoid Valve] CLOUD --> DAQ[Data Logger and
PC Dashboard] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid — Radiative Energy Flow Inside an Agricultural Greenhouse ```mermaid flowchart LR SOL[Incoming Solar
Short Wave 0.4 to 2.5 micron] -->|transmitted| COV[Polyethylene Cover
tau SW about 0.85] COV -->|absorbed fraction| PLANT[Crop Canopy
and Soil Surface] PLANT -->|emitted LW 4 to 50 micron| COV2[Cover Material
epsilon LW about 0.3 to 0.5] COV2 -->|partial re emission back| PLANT COV2 -->|escape to sky| SKY[Outside Atmosphere] PLANT -->|sensible heat| AIR[Greenhouse Air Mass] PLANT -->|latent heat| AIR AIR -->|controlled ventilation| VENT[Roof Vent and
Fan Assembly] AIR -->|measured by sensors| SENS[Instrumentation Cluster] ``` ## 4.3 Sensor-to-Actuator Mapping Matrix (Compact Text Diagram) ``` +-----------------------+-------------------+--------------------------+ | SENSOR (MEASUREMENT) | RANGE / UNIT | ACTUATOR (RESPONSE) | +-----------------------+-------------------+--------------------------+ | Pyranometer | 0-1200 W m-2 | Shade-net deployment | | Air Temp RTD | -10 to 60 deg C | Heater / Fan / Vent | | RH sensor | 0 to 100 percent | Misting / Dehumidifier | | CO2 NDIR | 0 to 5000 ppm | CO2 injector solenoid | | Soil moisture TDR | 0 to 60 percent | Drip irrigation valve | | Leaf wetness | 0 / 1 digital | Disease-warning module | +-----------------------+-------------------+--------------------------+ ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the greenhouse effect. List any four major greenhouse gases with their typical atmospheric concentrations.** **Model Answer (≈ 90 words):** The greenhouse effect is the trapping of outgoing long-wave terrestrial infrared radiation by trace atmospheric gases, raising Earth's mean surface temperature from the bare-rock radiative temperature of **−18 °C** to **+15 °C**. The four principal GHGs are: 1. **Water vapour (H₂O)** — variable, ~0.25 % by volume; strongest absorber. 2. **Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — **~420 ppm** (2024); doubled since pre-industrial era. 3. **Methane (CH₄)** — **~1930 ppb**; GWP ≈ 28. 4. **Nitrous oxide (N₂O)** — **~336 ppb**; GWP ≈ 265. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for list + 1 for concentration values.)* ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** **Distinguish between the *natural* greenhouse effect and the *enhanced* (anthropogenic) greenhouse effect.** **Model Answer (≈ 80 words):** The *natural* greenhouse effect is the baseline warming produced by water vapour, CO₂, and other GHGs that have always existed in the atmosphere; it is essential for life, keeping Earth habitable. The *enhanced* greenhouse effect refers to the **additional** warming caused by human activities — fossil-fuel combustion, deforestation, and industrial processes — that have increased atmospheric CO₂ by **>50 %** since 1750, raising the global mean temperature by **~1.1 °C** above pre-industrial levels. *(3 marks: 1 each for natural definition, enhanced definition, and one quantitative distinction.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Omitting the factor of 4 (geometric dilution)** in the Stefan–Boltzmann derivation. Most students write $T = (S_0/\sigma)^{1/4}$ and lose 2 marks instantly. > 2. **Mixing up *albedo* and *emissivity*** — they look similar but albedo $A$ is a SW reflectance whereas emissivity $\varepsilon$ is a LW radiative efficiency. > 3. **Writing units inconsistently** — KTU strictly expects SI units (W m⁻², K, ppm) inside the derivation; mixing °C and K costs 1 mark. > 4. **Forgetting the ±4 arithmetic in numerical substitution** — show the *intermediate* values, not only the final box. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive, from first principles, the expression for the **effective radiative temperature of Earth** in the absence of any atmosphere. Clearly state all assumptions and evaluate the final numerical value using $S_0 = 1361\ \text{W m}^{-2}$, $A = 0.306$, and $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-4}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the aid of a suitable single-layer atmospheric model, derive the **surface temperature** of Earth in the presence of an atmosphere with long-wave emissivity $\varepsilon_a$. Show that the result reduces to a **greenhouse factor of $2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$** when the atmosphere is fully opaque to terrestrial LW radiation. Comment on the implication for agricultural greenhouse design. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] *Step 1 — State the problem and list assumptions (1 mark):* Assumptions: (i) Earth is a perfect blackbody in the LW; (ii) Earth is a sphere of uniform temperature; (iii) the Sun acts as a point source of flux $S_0$ at the orbital distance; (iv) no atmosphere, so all absorbed SW is balanced by LW emission. *Step 2 — Absorbed solar power (1.5 marks):* $$P_{abs} = S_0 (1-A)\,\pi R_E^{2}$$ Geometric cross-section = $\pi R_E^{2}$; albedo reflection = $A$. *Step 3 — Emitted thermal power (1.5 marks):* $$P_{rad} = 4\pi R_E^{2}\,\sigma\, T_e^{4}$$ Full spherical surface, Stefan–Boltzmann Law. *Step 4 — Equate and simplify (1.5 marks):* $$S_0(1-A) = 4\sigma T_e^{4} \;\Rightarrow\; T_e = \left[\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4\sigma}\right]^{1/4}$$ *Step 5 — Numerical evaluation (1.5 marks):* $$T_e = \left[\dfrac{1361 \times 0.694}{4 \times 5.67\times 10^{-8}}\right]^{1/4} = \left[4.164 \times 10^{9}\right]^{1/4} \approx \mathbf{254.6\ K}\;(\mathbf{-18.5\ ^\circ C})$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] *Step 1 — Write surface and atmospheric energy balances (2 marks):* Surface: $$\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4} + \varepsilon_a \sigma T_a^{4} = \sigma T_s^{4}$$ Atmosphere (grey, isothermal, $\varepsilon_a$): $$\sigma T_s^{4} = 2\varepsilon_a \sigma T_a^{4} \;\Rightarrow\; T_a^{4} = \dfrac{T_s^{4}}{2\varepsilon_a}$$ *Step 2 — Substitute (1.5 marks):* $$\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4} + \dfrac{\sigma T_s^{4}}{2} = \sigma T_s^{4} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{4} = \dfrac{\sigma T_s^{4}}{2}$$ *Step 3 — Solve (1.5 marks):* $$T_s = \left[\dfrac{S_0(1-A)}{2\sigma}\right]^{1/4} = 2^{1/4} T_e$$ With $\varepsilon_a = 1$, $G = (1-\tfrac{1}{2})^{-1/4} = 2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$. Surface temperature = $1.189 \times 254.6 \approx 302.7\ \text{K}$ ≈ 29.5 °C. *Step 4 — Implication for greenhouse design (2 marks):* Modern polyethylene covers are engineered with $\varepsilon_a^{\text{LW}} \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ to **enhance** the back-radiation effect, raising inside temperature 5–10 °C above ambient and providing the protected-cultivation micro-climate needed for high-value horticultural crops (tomato, capsicum, gerbera). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **instrumentation architecture** of a computerised agricultural greenhouse. Identify at least **five sensors** and **four actuators** and justify their selection. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A $240\ \text{m}^{2}$ polyethylene greenhouse (cover emissivity $\varepsilon_c = 0.4$, overall heat-loss coefficient $U = 4.5\ \text{W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-1}$) is maintained at $T_{in} = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$ when $T_{out} = 12\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and the effective sky temperature is $T_{sky} = 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Estimate (i) the convective-conductive loss, (ii) the radiative loss to the sky, and (iii) the total heater power required. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] *Architecture description (2 marks):* Sensors → Signal Conditioning → Multiplexer → ADC → Microcontroller → HMI + IoT gateway + Actuator Drivers (refer to Section 4.1 diagram). *Five sensors (2.5 marks):* 1. **Pyranometer** — quantifies incoming PAR for shade control. 2. **PT100 RTD** — high accuracy (±0.3 °C) air-temperature sensing. 3. **HIH8120 capacitive RH sensor** — non-condensing humidity for misting. 4. **NDIR CO₂ sensor** — feedback for CO₂ enrichment. 5. **TDR soil-moisture probe** — volumetric water content for irrigation scheduling. *Four actuators (2.5 marks):* 1. **Exhaust fan + roof vent** — temperature and humidity removal. 2. **Fogger / misting nozzle** — evaporative cooling and humidity boost. 3. **Shade-net motor** — solar-load management. 4. **CO₂ injector solenoid** — photosynthetic enrichment up to 1200 ppm. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] Given: $A = 240\ \text{m}^{2}$, $\Delta T = 28 - 12 = 16\ \text{K}$, $\varepsilon_c = 0.4$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W m}^{-2}\text{K}^{-4}$, $T_{in} = 301.15\ \text{K}$, $T_{sky} = 278.15\ \text{K}$. *(i) Convective-conductive loss (2 marks):* $$Q_{cc} = U A \Delta T = 4.5 \times 240 \times 16 = \mathbf{17\,280\ W} \approx \mathbf{17.3\ kW}$$ *(ii) Radiative loss to sky (3 marks):* $$Q_{rad} = A \varepsilon_c \sigma (T_{in}^{4} - T_{sky}^{4})$$ $$T_{in}^{4} = 301.15^{4} = 8.222 \times 10^{9}\ \text{K}^{4}$$ $$T_{sky}^{4} = 278.15^{4} = 5.978 \times 10^{9}\ \text{K}^{4}$$ $$\Delta T^{4} = 2.244 \times 10^{9}\ \text{K}^{4}$$ $$Q_{rad} = 240 \times 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.244 \times 10^{9}$$ $$Q_{rad} = 240 \times 0.4 \times 127.2 = \mathbf{12\,211\ W} \approx \mathbf{12.2\ kW}$$ *(iii) Total heater power (2 marks):* $$Q_{heater} = Q_{cc} + Q_{rad} = 17\,280 + 12\,211 = \mathbf{29\,491\ W} \approx \mathbf{29.5\ kW}$$ *(Verification remark: this aligns with the 25–35 kW heater sizing typical for a 240 m² polyhouse in temperate Kerala winter conditions.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔑 The greenhouse effect **traps long-wave (LW)** radiation, not short-wave (SW) — the SW transmissivity of the atmosphere and cover material is *high*, but LW emissivity is what does the work. - 🔑 Always remember the **factor of 4** in $T_e = [S_0(1-A)/(4\sigma)]^{1/4}$; this arises from the sphere-to-disc geometric ratio and is a common KTU trap. - 🔑 The **greenhouse factor** for a fully opaque single layer is $2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$, raising $T_s$ by ~19 %. - 🔑 Principal GHGs in decreasing order of abundance (atmospheric): H₂O ≫ CO₂ ≫ CH₄ ≫ N₂O ≫ O₃ ≫ CFCs. - 🔑 In **agricultural polyhouses**, cover materials (LDPE film, twin-wall polycarbonate, glass) are selected on **transmissivity in PAR (400–700 nm)** and **emissivity in far-IR (4–50 µm)**. - 🔑 Five essential sensors: **Pyranometer, PT100 RTD, RH sensor, CO₂ NDIR, TDR soil probe**. - 🔑 Four essential actuators: **Vent/exhaust fan, fogger, shade net, CO₂ injector**. - 🔑 Radiative heat loss uses the **fourth power of absolute temperature** — a small temperature rise causes disproportionately large LW loss. - 🔑 The Beer–Lambert law $\tau_\lambda = e^{-k_\lambda u}$ governs spectral absorption of GHGs and is the starting point of any line-by-line radiative-transfer code. - 🔑 Modern greenhouses use **PID + IoT** control loops where the controller acts on the *error* between measured and set-point climate variables. - 🔑 **Kerala-specific context:** KTU examiners frequently use day–night tropical temperature swings (15 °C ΔT) and 70–95 % RH range in numerical problems.

topics.title: Concept and Construction of Green Houses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept and Construction of Green Houses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Greenhouse** (also called a *glasshouse*, *hothouse*, or *controlled environment agriculture (CEA) structure*) is a translucent-walled, roofed enclosure engineered to cultivate plants under precisely regulated microenvironmental conditions. The fundamental operating principle is the **Greenhouse Effect** — short-wavelength solar radiation penetrates the transparent covering, is absorbed by soil, plants, and interior surfaces, and is re-radiated as long-wavelength infrared energy that is partially trapped within the structure, elevating the internal air and root-zone temperature above ambient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> A greenhouse is a framed structure covered with transparent or translucent glazing material (glass, polyethylene film, or polycarbonate panels) in which environmental parameters such as **temperature (15°C–35°C optimum)**, **relative humidity (60%–80%)**, **CO₂ concentration (1000–1500 ppm)**, and **light intensity (20,000–50,000 lux)** are monitored and controlled using instrumentation systems for protected cultivation of horticultural and floricultural crops.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — "The Car-in-the-Sun" Effect**
> Imagine leaving a car with closed windows parked under direct sunlight. Even on a cold winter day, the interior becomes noticeably warm. Why? The glass windows let sunlight in but block the heat trying to escape. A greenhouse works on exactly the **same principle**, just scaled up for plants instead of passengers. The plants get a warm, sheltered "room" where they can grow even when outdoor weather is too cold, too windy, or too wet. Sensors inside the greenhouse act like the car's automatic climate control — opening vents, turning on fans, or activating heaters to keep conditions just right.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot (Standard KTU Categories)

| Greenhouse Type | Structural Style | Glazing Material | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Even-span** | Symmetric A-frame ridge | Glass / Polycarbonate | Research stations |
| **Uneven-span** | Asymmetric roof | Polyethylene film | Hilly terrain cultivation |
| **Quonset** | Semi-cylindrical hoop | UV-stabilized PE film | Commercial vegetable production |
| **Gutter-connected** | Multi-span (modular) | Twin-wall polycarbonate | Large-scale commercial farms |
| **Sawtooth** | Roof with ventilation slot | Polyethylene | Tropical climates |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants Used in Greenhouse Design

- **Solar constant (Iₛc) = 1367 W/m²** — extraterrestrial radiation intensity.
- **Typical transmissivity of clear glass (τ) = 0.85–0.90** (single pane, normal incidence).
- **Typical transmissivity of PE film (τ) = 0.70–0.80** (new, single layer).
- **Stephan-Boltzmann constant (σ) = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** (used for longwave radiation losses).
- **Thermal conductivity of glass (k) ≈ 0.81 W/m·K**; of PE film ≈ 0.33 W/m·K.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The greenhouse system operates through a chain of six sequential physical phenomena:

- **Step 1 — Incident Shortwave Radiation:** The sun emits radiation primarily in the **0.3–2.5 µm** wavelength band (visible + near-IR). This radiation passes almost unimpeded through transparent glazing because glass and PE films have a low absorption coefficient in this band.

- **Step 2 — Absorption by Internal Mass:** Floor, benches, soil, and plant canopies absorb the transmitted shortwave radiation. Black-body absorption is governed by **Kirchhoff's Law** — good absorbers are good emitters.

- **Step 3 — Re-emission as Longwave Radiation:** The heated interior surfaces re-radiate energy in the **3–100 µm** (far-IR) band. Glazing materials strongly absorb in this band (transmissivity < 0.10), so the longwave radiation is effectively trapped.

- **Step 4 — Convective Heat Loss:** Heated air near the glazing loses heat to the outdoor environment through **forced** (wind-driven) and **natural** (buoyancy-driven) convection. This is the *largest* heat loss pathway in single-glazed structures.

- **Step 5 — Conductive Heat Loss:** Through opaque structural members (aluminum frame, foundation, gutters) via Fourier's law of conduction.

- **Step 6 — Latent Heat Exchange:** Water vapor from plant transpiration and soil evaporation absorbs heat during phase change, transferring energy between the canopy and the air.

### 2.2 Why Controlled Environment Agriculture?

> The **Why**: Outdoor agriculture is constrained by seasonal variation, pest attack, and climatic extremes. A greenhouse decouples the plant from ambient weather, allowing year-round production, **higher yield per unit area (3–10× open-field yield)**, reduced water consumption (through closed-loop irrigation), and pesticide-free cultivation when combined with insect-proof netting.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solar Heat Gain** | $Q_{solar} = I_{sc} \cdot A_c \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha$ | $I_{sc}$ = solar intensity (W/m²), $A_c$ = covered area (m²), $\tau$ = transmissivity, $\alpha$ = absorptivity | Estimate daytime heat input |
| 2 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Longwave Loss** | $Q_{lw} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{in}^4 - T_{out}^4)$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma$ = 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴, $A_s$ = surface area (m²), $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | Nighttime radiative cooling |
| 3 | **Convective Heat Loss** | $Q_{conv} = h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | $h_c$ = convective coefficient (W/m²·K) ≈ 3–10 W/m²·K | Overall convection loss |
| 4 | **Conductive Heat Loss (Fourier)** | $Q_{cond} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $L$ = thickness (m) | Wall and roof conduction |
| 5 | **Ventilation Air Change Rate** | $Q_{v} = \dfrac{V}{\Delta t}$ | $V$ = greenhouse volume (m³), $\Delta t$ = time for one air change (s) | Required fan capacity |
| 6 | **Ventilation Heat Removal** | $Q_{v,loss} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot Q_v \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | $\rho$ = air density (1.2 kg/m³), $c_p$ = 1005 J/kg·K | Sensible cooling by fans |
| 7 | **Vapour Pressure Deficit (VPD)** | $VPD = e_s(T) - e_a$ | $e_s$ = saturation vapour pressure (kPa), $e_a$ = actual vapour pressure (kPa) | Irrigation/humidity control |
| 8 | **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value)** | $U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$ | $h_i, h_o$ = inside/outside film coefficients | Glazing selection |
| 9 | **Light Transmission (Multi-layer)** | $\tau_{total} = \tau_1 \cdot \tau_2 \cdot \tau_3$ | $\tau_n$ = transmissivity of each layer | Double/triple glazing |
| 10 | **CO₂ Enrichment Rate** | $Q_{CO_2} = V \cdot \Delta C / t$ | $\Delta C$ = desired CO₂ rise (ppm) | Pure CO₂ dosing |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tropical Kerala Context:** Sawtooth and naturally-ventilated greenhouses dominate, using **passive roof ventilation** to expel heat; no active heating is needed year-round, but **fan-and-pad evaporative cooling** is critical from March–May.
- **High-altitude (Munnar, Wayanad):** Polyethylene-clad Quonset structures with biomass/electric heating for high-value crops like strawberries, capsicum, and orchids.
- **Middle East & European exports of KTU curriculum:** Active environmental control with **HVAC**, **shade nets (50%–75% albedo)**, and **supplemental LED lighting (PPFD 200–600 µmol/m²/s)** for photoperiodic crops.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Greenhouse Heat Balance

**The Core Concept:** In a *steady-state* condition (typically night, with no solar input), the rate of heat generated inside must equal the rate of heat lost to the surroundings.

**Setting up the energy balance:**

$$Q_{heater} + Q_{solar} = Q_{cond} + Q_{conv} + Q_{lw} + Q_{vent} + Q_{latent}$$

For a *night-time winter scenario*, we assume $Q_{solar} = 0$, and the heating system must compensate all other losses. Let us derive a simplified nocturnal heat loss equation.

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant loss pathways:**
For a single-glazed greenhouse, by far the largest terms are $Q_{conv}$ and $Q_{lw}$ through the glazing. Combining these into a single equivalent coefficient gives us the *overall heat transfer coefficient U*.

**Step 2 — Combine convection and radiation into a single U-value:**

$$Q_{loss} = U \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

The derivation begins by writing each term separately.

$$Q_{conv} = h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

$$Q_{lw} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{in}^4 - T_{out}^4)$$

**Step 3 — Linearise the radiation term using a *linearised radiation coefficient* $h_r$:**

For a small temperature difference, the Stefan-Boltzmann term can be approximated using the **linearized radiation coefficient** $h_r$, which is defined as:

$$h_r = 4 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_m^3$$

where $T_m$ is the mean absolute temperature in Kelvin, given by:

$$T_m = \dfrac{T_{in} + T_{out}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the convective and linearised radiative coefficients:**

$$U = h_c + h_r$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the linearised forms back into the heat-loss equation:**

$$Q_{loss} = (h_c + h_r) \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

**Step 6 — Thus the required heater capacity is obtained by rearranging:**

$$Q_{heater} = U \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

**Given:** Single-glazed greenhouse, $A_s = 200$ m² (roof + walls), $T_{in} = 18°C$, $T_{out} = 5°C$, $\varepsilon = 0.85$, $h_c = 5$ W/m²·K.

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_{in} = 18 + 273 = 291 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{out} = 5 + 273 = 278 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean temperature:**

$$T_m = \dfrac{291 + 278}{2} = 284.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linearised radiation coefficient:**

$$h_r = 4 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot (284.5)^3$$

$$h_r = 3.4 \cdot 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 2.302 \times 10^{7}$$

$$h_r = 4.44 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the overall U-value:**

$$U = h_c + h_r = 5 + 4.44 = 9.44 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = 18 - 5 = 13 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the required heater capacity:**

$$Q_{heater} = 9.44 \cdot 200 \cdot 13 = 24{,}544 \text{ W} \approx 24.5 \text{ kW}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Ventilation Rate for Temperature Control

**The Core Concept:** To maintain internal temperature during peak summer, a minimum volumetric airflow must replace hot air with cooler (or evaporatively-cooled) outside air.

**Step 1 — Sensible heat to be removed by ventilation:**

$$Q_{sensible} = U \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out}) + q_{solar}$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat removed per unit mass of ventilation air:**

$$q_{mass} = c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate required:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{sensible}}{c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})}$$

**Step 4 — Convert mass flow rate to volumetric flow rate:**

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Air changes per hour (ACH):**

$$ACH = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot 3600}{V_{greenhouse}}$$

> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU-locked value):** A properly designed greenhouse in a tropical climate requires **20–30 ACH** during summer, and **5–10 ACH** in winter.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Parameter Selection Guide

| Design Parameter | Low-Cost Polyhouse | Mid-Range Glasshouse | High-Tech Dutch Greenhouse |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glazing | 200 µm UV-stabilized PE | 4 mm float glass | Diffuse AR-coated glass |
| Structural Frame | GI pipe (25 mm) | GI pipe (40–50 mm) | Galvanized steel truss |
| Ventilation | Manual roll-up side | Roof + side vents | Automated ridge + side |
| Cooling | Natural | Fan-and-pad | Fan-and-pad + fogging |
| Heating | None / biomass | Electric blower | Hot-water pipe network |
| Sensors | Thermometer | Thermometer + hygrometer | Full climate controller |
| Estimated Cost (₹/m²) | 600–900 | 1500–2500 | 4000–8000 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
