## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define a greenhouse. State the four most commonly controlled environmental parameters inside it.*

**Model Answer:**
A greenhouse is a framed, translucent-clad structure that uses solar energy trapped under transparent glazing to create a controlled microclimate for plant growth. The four most commonly controlled parameters are:
1. **Air temperature** (15°C–35°C, setpoint 25°C)
2. **Relative humidity** (60%–80%, VPD 0.8–1.2 kPa)
3. **CO₂ concentration** (ambient 400 ppm → enriched 1000 ppm)
4. **Photosynthetic Active Radiation / Light intensity** (200–600 µmol/m²/s PAR)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Explain the role of UV-stabilized polyethylene film as a greenhouse covering material.*

**Model Answer:**
UV-stabilized polyethylene (PE) film is the most widely used low-cost greenhouse cladding. Its properties are:
- **Thickness:** typically 100–200 µm.
- **Light transmissivity:** 0.70–0.80 for new film.
- **UV stabilization:** Additives like HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers) extend service life from <1 year (untreated) to **3–5 years**.
- **IR retention:** Special thermal films (EVA co-extruded) retain up to 80% of longwave radiation, reducing night-time fuel use by 20%–30%.
- **Diffusion:** Diffuse PE films scatter light, improving canopy penetration and reducing leaf scorch.
- **Drawback:** Lower rigidity than glass — requires closely-spaced GI pipe hoops (spacing ≤ 2 m).

---

### Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional components of a **fan-and-pad evaporative cooling system** used in greenhouses. State the working principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A glass-glazed greenhouse of floor area 100 m² and total glazed surface area 220 m² is maintained at 22°C when ambient is 8°C. Take $\varepsilon = 0.85$, $h_c = 6$ W/m²·K. Calculate the **night-time heating load**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A fan-and-pad system is the most efficient active cooling method for hot, dry climates. Its components are:
- **Cellulose/Asbestos Pad Wall (inlet side):** Water trickles down the pad; a small pump recirculates it.
- **Exhaust Fan Bank (opposite end):** Pulls outside air through the wet pad.
- **Water Tank, Pump, and Distribution Header:** Ensures uniform pad wetting.
- **Float Valve and Bleed-off Line:** Maintains water quality.

**Working Principle:** As hot, dry outside air is drawn through the wet pad, water evaporates by absorbing latent heat of vaporization (≈ 2430 kJ/kg). The temperature drop can reach **70–80% of the wet-bulb depression**. Air enters the greenhouse saturated and cooler; exhaust fans remove heated air at the opposite end, creating a continuous through-flow.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_{in} = 22 + 273 = 295 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{out} = 8 + 273 = 281 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Mean temperature:**

$$T_m = \dfrac{295 + 281}{2} = 288 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Linearised radiation coefficient:**

$$h_r = 4 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot (288)^3$$

$$h_r = 3.4 \cdot 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 2.387 \times 10^7$$

$$h_r = 4.60 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 4 — Overall U-value:**

$$U = h_c + h_r = 6 + 4.60 = 10.60 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 5 — Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = 22 - 8 = 14 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Heating load:**

$$Q = U \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T = 10.60 \cdot 220 \cdot 14$$

$$Q = 32{,}648 \text{ W} \approx 32.65 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** The required heating load is **≈ 32.65 kW**.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **PE film, glass, and polycarbonate** as greenhouse glazing materials on the basis of transmissivity, durability, cost, and weight. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **siting and orientation criteria** for a greenhouse in Kerala, including slope, drainage, wind exposure, and east–west ridge orientation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Property | PE Film (200 µm) | Glass (4 mm) | Polycarbonate (Twin-wall) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmissivity (PAR) | 0.70–0.80 | 0.85–0.90 | 0.75–0.82 |
| Service Life | 3–5 years | 20–25 years | 10–15 years |
| Cost (₹/m²) | 60–100 | 700–1200 | 500–900 |
| Weight (kg/m²) | ~0.2 | ~10 | ~1.5 |
| Impact Resistance | Low | Brittle | High |
| IR Retention | Low (EVA version high) | Moderate | Moderate-High |

Glass gives the highest optical clarity but is heavy, fragile, and expensive. PE is cheapest and lightest, ideal for low-cost polyhouses. Polycarbonate is the best compromise — durable, lightweight, with reasonable optical properties and good impact resistance (hail-safe).

**Model Solution (b):**

Siting and orientation are critical design decisions that determine a greenhouse's productivity:

- **Slope and Drainage:** Site must have a **gentle slope (1%–2% gradient)** to allow surface water runoff; low-lying water-logged areas must be avoided. A raised platform (≥ 30 cm above grade) is preferred.

- **Wind Exposure:** Kerala receives strong southwest monsoon (June–September); site selection should be **sheltered on the western side by natural windbreaks** (coconut rows, Casuarina, or artificial shelter belts) to prevent PE film uplift damage.

- **Sun Path and Orientation:** For year-round equatorial sun, the **ridge of an even-span greenhouse should run East–West**, so the long slope faces **South** to capture maximum winter solar radiation.

- **Accessibility:** Proximity to a water source (for irrigation and pad cooling) and an electricity connection (for fans, pumps) is essential.

- **Soil Quality:** Soil beneath must be well-drained, fertile loam, or the greenhouse must be built with raised benches using imported substrate.

- **Spacing:** Multiple greenhouses should be spaced at least **2× the ridge height apart** to avoid mutual shadowing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Forgetting to **convert °C to Kelvin** in radiation/Stefan-Boltzmann calculations — costs **1–2 marks instantly**.
> - Using $\sigma = 5.67$ without the $10^{-8}$ factor — entire answer becomes numerically wrong.
> - Confusing **solar constant (1367 W/m²)** with **terrestrial insolation (~1000 W/m² at noon)**.
> - Not stating the **assumed glazing emissivity** in heat-load derivations — examiner deducts **1 mark** for missing assumption.
> - Writing *"greenhouse gases"* instead of *"longwave IR radiation trapping"* — answer is then scientifically wrong.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **greenhouse** is a translucent-clad structure exploiting the **Greenhouse Effect** to create a controlled plant-growing environment.
- Key parameters: **Temperature (15–35°C)**, **RH (60–80%)**, **CO₂ (1000–1500 ppm)**, **PAR (200–600 µmol/m²/s)**.
- The dominant **heat-loss pathways** are convection, longwave radiation, and ventilation.
- **Glazing materials** ranked by cost: PE < Polycarbonate < Glass; ranked by transmissivity: Glass > Polycarbonate > PE.
- **Cooling:** fan-and-pad is the workhorse for hot dry climates; natural ventilation is preferred in humid Kerala.
- **Heating load formula:** $Q = U \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T$ where $U = h_c + h_r$ and $h_r = 4\varepsilon\sigma T_m^3$.
- **Ventilation rule of thumb:** 20–30 ACH in summer, 5–10 ACH in winter.
- **Orientation in Kerala:** East–West ridge for even-span; sheltered west side for monsoon wind protection.
- **Linearisation** of the Stefan-Boltzmann term is essential for hand-calculation; always convert to Kelvin first.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ — *never omit the $10^{-8}$*.
- **VPD** is the single most useful humidity index for irrigation scheduling in protected cultivation.
- **Instrumentation stack** always includes: RTD/thermistor, capacitive RH sensor, NDIR CO₂ sensor, quantum PAR sensor, and TDR soil moisture probe.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ventilation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** Ventilation is a critical sub-topic under *Environmental Control & Process Monitoring in Agriculture*. It deals with the controlled exchange of air between an enclosed agricultural environment (greenhouse, dairy barn, poultry shed, mushroom house, grain silo) and the outside atmosphere, with the primary objective of maintaining optimum **temperature**, **relative humidity**, **gas concentration (CO₂, NH₃, H₂S)**, and **air velocity** for healthy plant/animal growth and post-harvest storage.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ventilation**, in the context of agricultural instrumentation, is defined as the deliberate and measurable movement of air into, through, and out of an enclosed agricultural space, achieved through a combination of **driving forces** (wind, thermal buoyancy, or mechanical fans) and **control elements** (inlet dampers, exhaust fans, louvres, thermostats, and electronic controllers).

From an instrumentation standpoint, ventilation is not merely a fluid-mechanical process — it is a **closed-loop control problem** where physical variables (T, RH, CO₂, NH₃) are sensed, compared against setpoints, and used to actuate fans/dampers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Terminology:** Always use the terms *Air Exchange Rate (AER)*, *Air Changes per Hour (ACH)*, and *Static Pressure (ΔPs)* in your answers. They are high-frequency keywords in KTU valuation scripts.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Breathing Building"

Think of a greenhouse or poultry shed as a **living organism** that needs to *breathe*. Just like humans inhale oxygen-rich air and exhale CO₂, plants absorb CO₂ during photosynthesis and release O₂, while animals consume O₂ and release CO₂, moisture, and ammonia. Without *ventilation*, these "exhaled" gases accumulate and act as slow poison, while heat and humidity build up to lethal levels.

> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine being stuck in a closed car on a hot sunny day with three other people. Within minutes, the windows fog up (humidity rises), it gets unbearably hot (heat builds), and the air feels "thick" (CO₂ rises). You instinctively roll down the window — that is **natural ventilation**. Now imagine the car has a computer that automatically opens the window a precise amount based on a temperature sensor — that is **instrumented ventilation**.

---

## 1.3 Why Ventilation is Critical in Agriculture — Five Functional Goals

| S.No. | Functional Goal | Agricultural Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Temperature Control** | Remove sensible heat from solar load and animal metabolism |
| 2 | **Humidity Control** | Prevent condensation, fungal diseases, heat stress |
| 3 | **CO₂ Replenishment** | Maintain 800–1200 ppm for greenhouse photosynthesis |
| 4 | **Pollutant Removal** | Exhaust NH₃, H₂S, CH₄ from animal houses |
| 5 | **Air Velocity & Distribution** | Prevent stratification, ensure uniform microclimate |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Reference Metrics to remember for KTU exams:**
> - Greenhouse CO₂ setpoint: **1000 ppm** (daytime)
> - Poultry house NH₃ limit: **< 25 ppm**
> - Dairy barn air velocity in summer: **1.5 – 2.5 m/s**
> - Recommended ACH for broiler house: **10 – 20 ACH** (winter), **60 – 90 ACH** (summer)

---

## 1.4 Classification of Ventilation Systems

```
Ventilation
├── Natural Ventilation
│   ├── Wind-driven (cross-flow)
│   ├── Stack-effect (thermal buoyancy)
│   └── Side-wall + ridge opening
├── Mechanical (Forced) Ventilation
│   ├── Exhaust system (negative pressure)
│   ├── Pressurization (positive pressure)
│   └── Balanced (equal supply & exhaust)
└── Hybrid / Mixed Mode
    ├── Natural base + mechanical backup
    └── Sensor-triggered fan assistance
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Ventilation — Driving Forces

Ventilation airflow is generated by two fundamental physical mechanisms. Understanding these is essential because KTU questions often ask *"Why does a greenhouse with a higher ridge vent have better summer ventilation?"* — the answer lies in the **stack effect**.

### 2.1.1 Wind Effect (Wind-Driven Pressure Differential)
When wind strikes a building, it creates a **positive pressure (stagnation pressure)** on the windward side and a **negative pressure (suction)** on the leeward side. Air naturally flows from high to low pressure.

$$\Delta P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot v^2 \cdot C_d$$

where:
- $\Delta P_{wind}$ = wind-induced pressure differential (Pa)
- $\rho_{air}$ = air density ($\approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 20°C)
- $v$ = wind velocity (m/s)
- $C_d$ = pressure coefficient (dimensionless, depends on opening geometry, typically **0.4 to 0.8**)

### 2.1.2 Stack Effect (Thermal Buoyancy)
Warm air inside the structure is less dense than the cooler outside air. Buoyancy forces cause warm air to rise and escape through high openings (ridge vents), drawing cool air in through low openings (side vents).

$$\Delta P_{stack} = \rho_{o} \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \left( \frac{T_i - T_o}{T_i} \right)$$

where:
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $H$ = vertical distance between inlet and outlet (m)
- $T_i$, $T_o$ = inside and outside absolute temperatures (Kelvin)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the stack effect:** Hot air molecules have higher kinetic energy and move faster, pushing each other apart. This reduces the *mass per unit volume* (density). Gravity pulls the heavier cold air down, displacing the lighter warm air upward — a continuous chimney-like flow.

### 2.1.3 Combined Driving Force
In real conditions, both effects act simultaneously. The total pressure differential driving ventilation is:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{wind} + \Delta P_{stack} - \Delta P_{friction}$$

The friction loss $\Delta P_{friction}$ represents resistance to flow through screens, louvers, ducts, and inlet openings.

---

## 2.2 Ventilation Rate Equations — The Core of Sizing Calculations

### 2.2.1 Air Changes per Hour (ACH)
The most fundamental ventilation performance metric used in KTU numericals.

$$ACH = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V_{space}}$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $V_{space}$ = enclosed volume of the agricultural structure (m³)
- 3600 = conversion factor (seconds per hour)

Rearranging to find the required fan capacity:

$$Q_{required} = \frac{ACH \times V_{space}}{3600}$$

### 2.2.2 Heat Balance Based Ventilation (Sensible Heat Method)
For temperature-controlled spaces (broiler houses, greenhouses in summer), the required airflow is determined by the heat that must be removed.

$$Q_{sensible} = \frac{q_{total}}{\rho_{air} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})}$$

where:
- $q_{total}$ = total sensible heat load (W) = solar gain + animal metabolic heat + equipment heat
- $C_p$ = specific heat of air = **1005 J/(kg·K)**
- $(T_{in} - T_{out})$ = allowable temperature rise (K)

### 2.2.3 Moisture Balance Based Ventilation (Latent Heat Method)
For humidity-sensitive applications (mushroom houses, grain drying, greenhouses with high transpiration).

$$Q_{moisture} = \frac{\dot{m}_{water}}{\rho_{air} \cdot (W_i - W_o)}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_{water}$ = moisture to be removed (kg/s)
- $W_i$, $W_o$ = inside and outside humidity ratios (kg water/kg dry air)

### 2.2.4 CO₂ Balance Based Ventilation
For greenhouses, photosynthesis consumes CO₂. The required ventilation is:

$$Q_{CO_2} = \frac{R_{CO_2}}{C_{i} - C_{o}}$$

where:
- $R_{CO_2}$ = CO₂ consumption rate by plants (m³/s × ppm equivalent)
- $C_i$, $C_o$ = target indoor and outdoor CO₂ concentrations (ppm)

---

## 2.3 Fan Affinity Laws — High-Yield for KTU

Fans used in agricultural ventilation follow three **affinity laws** relating flow, pressure, and power to fan speed and impeller diameter. These are almost always asked in KTU numericals.

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3$$

$$\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{P_{power,2}}{P_{power,1}} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^5$$

where $N$ = rotational speed (RPM) and $D$ = impeller diameter (m).

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that doubling the fan speed ($N_2/N_1 = 2$) **doubles** the flow, **quadruples** the pressure, and **octuples** the power consumption. This is why variable frequency drives (VFDs) are used in modern agricultural ventilation for energy efficiency.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ACH = (Q \times 3600)/V$ | Q in m³/s, V in m³ | Minimum ventilation sizing |
| 2 | $Q = (ACH \times V)/3600$ | As above | Fan capacity calculation |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 C_d$ | Pa, kg/m³, m/s | Wind-driven natural vent |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{stack} = \rho g H (\Delta T / T_i)$ | Pa, K | Thermal buoyancy design |
| 5 | $Q_{sensible} = q / (\rho C_p \Delta T)$ | m³/s, W | Summer cooling load |
| 6 | $Q_{moisture} = \dot{m} / [\rho (W_i - W_o)]$ | m³/s, kg/s | Humidity control |
| 7 | $Q_{CO_2} = R_{CO_2} / (C_i - C_o)$ | m³/s, ppm | Greenhouse CO₂ dosing |
| 8 | $Q_2/Q_1 = N_2/N_1 = (D_2/D_1)^3$ | Dimensionless | Fan flow scaling |
| 9 | $\Delta P_2/\Delta P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$ | Dimensionless | Fan pressure scaling |
| 10 | $P_2/P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^3$ | Dimensionless | Fan power scaling |
| 11 | $H_{sensible} = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ | W | Heat removal check |
| 12 | $H_{latent} = \dot{m} h_{fg} \Delta W$ | W | Moisture removal check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Marks Distribution Note:** Numerical problems on ACH/fan capacity carry **7 marks** in Part B. Always show: (i) given data, (ii) formula, (iii) substitution, (iv) unit conversion, (v) final answer with unit. Missing unit = 0.5 mark cut guaranteed.

---

## 2.5 Instrumentation Sensors Used in Ventilation

A modern agricultural ventilation system is essentially a **multi-sensor feedback control loop**. The following sensors are integral to the Module 4 syllabus:

- **Temperature sensors:** Thermocouples (Type K, T), RTDs (PT100), thermistors, IC sensors (LM35, DHT22)
- **Humidity sensors:** Capacitive polymer (HS1101, SHT31), resistive, chilled-mirror hygrometers
- **Gas sensors:** NDIR CO₂ sensors (MH-Z19, K30), electrochemical NH₃/H₂S sensors
- **Air velocity sensors:** Hot-wire anemometers, vane anemometers, ultrasonic transit-time
- **Differential pressure sensors:** For duct flow measurement (Pitot-static, orifice)
- **Light sensors:** PAR sensors for photosynthesis-driven CO₂ control

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** In commercial broiler farms in Kerala, automated ventilation using **PID controllers** with DHT22 sensors and variable-speed fans reduces mortality by 15–20% and improves FCR (Feed Conversion Ratio) by 0.1–0.2, directly impacting farm profitability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Fan Capacity from ACH

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard Style):** A polyhouse used for capsicum cultivation has dimensions **30 m × 8 m × 4 m (L × W × H)**. The design recommendation is **20 air changes per hour**. Determine:
- (a) The required ventilation fan capacity in m³/s
- (b) The same capacity expressed in CFM (Cubic Feet per Minute)

### Step 1 — Identify Given Data
Length $L = 30$ m, Width $W = 8$ m, Height $H = 4$ m, $ACH = 20$ /h.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Internal Volume
$$V_{space} = L \times W \times H = 30 \times 8 \times 4 = 960 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Apply the ACH Formula
Rearranging for Q:
$$Q = \frac{ACH \times V_{space}}{3600}$$

Substituting the numerical values:
$$Q = \frac{20 \times 960}{3600}$$

### Step 4 — Perform the Arithmetic
$$Q = \frac{19200}{3600} = 5.333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to CFM
Using the conversion: **1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM**
$$Q = 5.333 \times 2118.88 = 11{,}300 \text{ CFM (approx.)}$$

### Final Answer
- (a) **Q = 5.33 m³/s**
- (b) **Q ≈ 11,300 CFM**

> **Examiner's Note:** Always state the conversion factor used. The CFM conversion factor (1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM) is derived from 1 m³ = 35.3147 ft³ and 60 s/min, hence 35.3147 × 60 = 2118.88.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fan Affinity Law Application

**Problem Statement:** A ventilation fan operating at 1450 RPM delivers 4.5 m³/s against a static pressure of 250 Pa, drawing 1.8 kW. If the fan speed is increased to 1750 RPM, calculate the new flow, pressure, and power.

### Step 1 — Identify the Speed Ratio
$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1750}{1450} = 1.2069$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Flow Affinity Law
$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$
$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times 1.2069 = 4.5 \times 1.2069 = 5.431 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Pressure Affinity Law
$$\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = (1.2069)^2 = 1.4566$$
$$\Delta P_2 = 250 \times 1.4566 = 364.16 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Power Affinity Law
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (1.2069)^3 = 1.7576$$
$$P_2 = 1.8 \times 1.7576 = 3.164 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5 — Summary Table

| Parameter | Original (1450 RPM) | New (1750 RPM) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow Q | 4.5 m³/s | 5.43 m³/s | +20.7% |
| Pressure ΔP | 250 Pa | 364.16 Pa | +45.7% |
| Power P | 1.8 kW | 3.16 kW | +75.8% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** A 20.7% increase in fan speed produced a 75.8% increase in power. This non-linear power scaling is why **VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives)** are used in modern precision-ventilated farms — they deliver the *exact* airflow required by the control loop without the cubic penalty in energy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stack Effect Pressure for Greenhouse Design

**Problem Statement:** A greenhouse has a side vent at 1.5 m height and a ridge vent at 5.0 m height. The inside air temperature is 35°C (308 K) and outside temperature is 28°C (301 K). Calculate the buoyancy-induced pressure difference driving ventilation. Take $\rho_o = 1.16$ kg/m³.

### Step 1 — Identify the Vertical Distance
$$H = h_{ridge} - h_{side} = 5.0 - 1.5 = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Stack Effect Equation
$$\Delta P_{stack} = \rho_o \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \left(\frac{T_i - T_o}{T_i}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values
$$\Delta P_{stack} = 1.16 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 \times \left(\frac{308 - 301}{308}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Compute Numerator of Bracket
$$T_i - T_o = 308 - 301 = 7 \text{ K}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Bracket
$$\frac{7}{308} = 0.02273$$

### Step 6 — Final Computation
$$\Delta P_{stack} = 1.16 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 \times 0.02273$$
$$\Delta P_{stack} = 0.9061 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 7 — Convert to a More Intuitive Unit
$$0.9061 \text{ Pa} = 0.9061 \text{ N/m}^2$$

> **Interpretation:** This tiny pressure (~0.91 Pa) is enough to drive natural ventilation in a properly designed greenhouse, equivalent to a wind speed of about **0.7 m/s** (using $\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$). The student should recognize that the **stack effect is gentle but persistent**, working 24/7, while wind effect is stronger but intermittent.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sensible Heat Ventilation Load

**Problem Statement:** A poultry shed houses 5000 broilers. Each bird generates 8.5 W of sensible heat. The maximum allowable indoor-outdoor temperature difference is 6 K. Outside temperature is 32°C. Calculate the required ventilation airflow.

### Step 1 — Total Heat Load
$$q_{total} = N_{birds} \times q_{per\ bird} = 5000 \times 8.5 = 42{,}500 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Sensible Heat Ventilation Equation
$$Q = \frac{q_{total}}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

Using $\rho = 1.16$ kg/m³, $C_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $\Delta T = 6$ K:

$$Q = \frac{42{,}500}{1.16 \times 1005 \times 6}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Denominator
$$1.16 \times 1005 \times 6 = 6994.8$$

### Step 4 — Final Calculation
$$Q = \frac{42{,}500}{6994.8} = 6.075 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 5 — Express in Practical Units
$$Q = 6.075 \times 3600 = 21{,}870 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

> **Practical Insight:** This corresponds to an ACH = $(21{,}870 \times 1) / V_{shed}$. For a 50 m × 12 m × 3 m shed (1800 m³), ACH ≈ 12.1/h, which falls within the KTU textbook range for summer broiler ventilation. Cross-validation is a hallmark of a top-scoring KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Instrumented Ventilation Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Setpoint T RH CO2 NH3] --> B[Microcontroller PLC PID]
    C[Temperature Sensor PT100 DHT22] --> B
    D[Humidity Sensor SHT31] --> B
    E[CO2 Sensor MHZ19 NDIR] --> B
    F[NH3 Electrochemical Sensor] --> B
    B --> G[Driver VFD Relay Module]
    G --> H[Exhaust Fan Variable Speed]
    G --> I[Inlet Damper Actuator]
    H --> J[Air Exchange in Greenhouse or Animal House]
    I --> J
    J --> C
    J --> D
    J --> E
    J --> F
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The sensors (C, D, E, F) continuously measure the indoor microclimate. The setpoint (A) is the desired target value. The microcontroller (B) executes a **PID algorithm** that compares measured vs. setpoint and outputs a control signal. This signal drives the actuator interface (G), which in turn adjusts the fan speed (H) and the damper position (I). The resulting air exchange (J) modifies the measured variables, closing the loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Ventilation System Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V[Ventilation System] --> N[Natural Ventilation]
    V --> M[Mechanical Ventilation]
    V --> H[Hybrid System]
    N --> N1[Wind Driven Cross Flow]
    N --> N2[Stack Effect Thermal Buoyancy]
    N --> N3[Side and Ridge Opening]
    M --> M1[Negative Pressure Exhaust]
    M --> M2[Positive Pressure Supply]
    M --> M3[Balanced Equal Flow]
    H --> H1[Natural Base plus Fan Backup]
    H --> H2[Sensor Triggered Mechanical Assist]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Greenhouse with Instrumented Ventilation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Greenhouse_Structure
        S1[Side Vent Low Level 1.5m] -->|Cool Air In| GH[Greenhouse Air Volume]
        S2[Roof Vent High Level 5.0m] -->|Warm Air Out| GH
        GH -->|Airflow Path| F1[Exhaust Fan 1 Variable Speed]
        GH --> F2[Exhaust Fan 2 Variable Speed]
    end
    subgraph Sensor_Network
        GH --> T1[Temp Sensor 1 Center]
        GH --> T2[Temp Sensor 2 Corner]
        GH --> H1[Humidity Sensor]
        GH --> G1[CO2 Sensor]
    end
    subgraph Control_Unit
        T1 --> PID[PID Controller]
        T2 --> PID
        H1 --> PID
        G1 --> PID
        PID -->|PWM Signal| F1
        PID -->|PWM Signal| F2
        PID -->|Analog 0 10V| S1
    end
```

> **Operational Logic:** When the central temperature $T_1$ exceeds the setpoint, the PID controller increases the PWM duty cycle to fans F1 and F2, raising the airflow rate. Simultaneously, the inlet damper S1 opens proportionally, maintaining a target static pressure differential of ~10 Pa to ensure uniform air distribution.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — PID Control Logic for Ventilation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Loop] --> B[Read T RH CO2]
    B --> C[Compute Error e equals Setpoint minus Measured]
    C --> D[Compute PID Output]
    D --> E{Output within Safe Limits}
    E -->|Yes| F[Apply PWM to Fan and Damper]
    E -->|No| G[Clamp to Min or Max Limit]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Wait Next Sample Time 1 to 5 seconds]
    H --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Practical Tip:** When asked to draw a control loop, always label the **error signal $e(t) = SP - PV$** where SP is setpoint and PV is process variable. The PID output is $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$. Examiners award marks for these explicit terms.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Avoid these traps):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing flow in m³/h when formula expects m³/s. Always check.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3600 conversion** between ACH and Q.
> 3. **Mixing up fan affinity exponents:** 1, 2, 3 for flow, pressure, power respectively.
> 4. **Stating "ventilation removes heat"** without specifying *sensible* vs *latent* heat.
> 5. **No diagram or poorly labeled diagrams** in 14-mark answers → 2-mark cut guaranteed.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** 
*Define ventilation. List any four objectives of ventilation in agricultural structures.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Ventilation is the controlled process of exchanging air between an enclosed agricultural environment and the outside atmosphere, achieved through natural or mechanical means, to maintain optimal temperature, humidity, and gas concentration for plant/animal health.

**Four Objectives:**
1. **Temperature regulation** — removal of sensible heat from solar load and metabolic activity.
2. **Humidity control** — preventing fungal diseases, condensation, and heat stress.
3. **CO₂ replenishment** — maintaining ~1000 ppm in greenhouses for photosynthesis.
4. **Pollutant removal** — exhausting harmful gases like NH₃, H₂S, and CH₄ from animal houses.

> **Marking:** Definition: 1 mark, Four objectives: 0.5 each = 2 marks, Total = 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between natural and mechanical ventilation systems with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Natural Ventilation | Mechanical Ventilation |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving force** | Wind pressure and stack (thermal) effect | Electric fans/blowers |
| **Control** | Manual adjustment of vent area | Automatic via sensors and controllers |
| **Cost** | Low (no power) | High (capital + operating) |
| **Airflow predictability** | Weather-dependent | Highly predictable |
| **Example** | Polyhouse with side and ridge vents | Tunnel-ventilated broiler house |
| **Best suited for** | Mild climates, low-density stocking | Hot/humid climates, high-density stocking |

> **Marking:** Any 3 valid distinguishing points with one example each = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each, with internal choice (either Q1 or Q2 to be answered). Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **closed-loop PID control of ventilation** in a greenhouse. Define the PID output equation and explain the role of $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$ in ventilation stability.

**Model Answer:**

A closed-loop ventilation control system continuously measures indoor environmental parameters and adjusts fan/damper actuators to maintain setpoints. The block diagram (drawn as shown in Section 4.1) consists of:

- **Sensors:** Temperature (PT100/DHT22), humidity (SHT31), CO₂ (MH-Z19).
- **Controller:** Microcontroller/PLC executing PID algorithm.
- **Actuators:** VFD-driven exhaust fans, motorized dampers.
- **Process:** The greenhouse air volume itself.

**PID Output Equation:**
$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $e(t) = SP - PV$ (setpoint minus process variable).

**Role of Each Gain:**

- **$K_p$ (Proportional):** Provides immediate corrective action proportional to current error. Higher $K_p$ gives faster response but causes oscillation if too high.
- **$K_i$ (Integral):** Eliminates steady-state offset by accumulating past errors. Essential for precise temperature holding.
- **$K_d$ (Derivative):** Anticipates future error by responding to rate of change. Dampens oscillations, improves stability.

**For agricultural ventilation:** Typical starting values are $K_p = 2$–$5$, $K_i = 0.1$–$0.5$ /min, $K_d = 0.01$–$0.1$ min, tuned via **Ziegler-Nichols** or **Cohen-Coon** methods.

> **Mark Split:** Diagram 3 marks, PID equation 2 marks, Explanation of gains 2 marks.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **mushroom growing room** measures **10 m × 6 m × 3.5 m**. The desired ACH is **15/h**. The CO₂ level must be maintained below **1000 ppm** while ambient CO₂ is 420 ppm. Mushroom respiration releases CO₂ at **0.05 L/s per m² of floor area**. Calculate: (i) Required fan capacity in m³/s, (ii) Whether this airflow is sufficient to keep CO₂ below 1000 ppm.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 10$ m, $W = 6$ m, $H = 3.5$ m, ACH = 15/h, Floor area $A = 10 \times 6 = 60$ m².

**Part (i) — Fan Capacity:**
$$V_{space} = 10 \times 6 \times 3.5 = 210 \text{ m}^3$$
$$Q = \frac{ACH \times V_{space}}{3600} = \frac{15 \times 210}{3600} = 0.875 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Part (ii) — CO₂ Sufficiency Check:**

CO₂ generation rate:
$$R_{CO_2} = 0.05 \text{ L/s/m}^2 \times 60 \text{ m}^2 = 3.0 \text{ L/s} = 0.003 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to ppm-equivalent volumetric concentration: $1$ m³ = $10^6$ mL = $10^6$ ppm·m³
$$R_{CO_2} = 0.003 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm} = 3000 \text{ ppm·m}^3/\text{s}$$

Steady-state CO₂ concentration using mass balance:
$$C_i = C_o + \frac{R_{CO_2}}{Q} = 420 + \frac{3000}{0.875 \times 10^6} \times 10^6$$

Let me recalculate carefully:
$$\frac{R_{CO_2}}{Q} = \frac{3000 \text{ ppm·m}^3/\text{s}}{0.875 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 3428.6 \text{ ppm}$$

$$C_i = 420 + 3428.6 = 3848.6 \text{ ppm}$$

**Since 3848.6 ppm ≫ 1000 ppm, the airflow of 0.875 m³/s is NOT sufficient.**

**Required airflow for 1000 ppm:**
$$\frac{R_{CO_2}}{Q_{new}} = 1000 - 420 = 580 \text{ ppm}$$
$$Q_{new} = \frac{3000}{580} = 5.17 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

This is approximately **6 times** the ACH-based requirement. Hence, for mushroom cultivation, **CO₂ control dominates** the sizing, not ACH.

> **Mark Split:** Part (i) 2 marks, Part (ii) calculation 4 marks, Conclusion 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **fan affinity laws** from first principles. State clearly the assumptions. Mention two agricultural applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Geometric similarity between fans (same impeller shape, scaled by $D$).
2. Air density remains constant.
3. No compressibility effects (incompressible flow at low speeds).
4. Fan operating in the same flow regime (same Reynolds number regime).

**Derivation:**

Consider two geometrically similar fans with impeller diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$ operating at speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$.

The flow velocity at the impeller is proportional to the tip speed:
$$v \propto N \cdot D$$

**Flow rate** $Q$ is proportional to velocity × area:
$$Q \propto (N \cdot D) \times D^2 = N \cdot D^3$$
$$\Rightarrow \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right) \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3$$

**Pressure** $\Delta P$ from Bernoulli/dynamic pressure:
$$\Delta P \propto \rho v^2 \propto (N \cdot D)^2$$
$$\Rightarrow \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

**Power** $P = \Delta P \times Q$:
$$P \propto (N D)^2 \times N D^3 = N^3 D^5$$
$$\Rightarrow \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^5$$

**Agricultural Applications:**
1. **Variable-speed exhaust fans** in poultry houses using VFDs to modulate airflow based on bird age and ambient temperature.
2. **Greenhouse circulation fans** sized using affinity laws to match crop-specific air velocity requirements.

> **Mark Split:** Assumptions 1 mark, Each law 1.5 marks, Applications 1 mark.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a **neat sketch**, explain the **stack effect** in a greenhouse. A greenhouse has a ridge height of 6 m and side vent height of 1.2 m. Inside temperature is 38°C and outside is 30°C. Calculate the stack pressure difference. Given $\rho_o = 1.14$ kg/m³.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch Description (Mermaid):**
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Greenhouse
        direction LR
        SV[Side Vent Inlet 1.2m Cool Air In] --> AIR[Greenhouse Airspace]
        AIR --> RV[Ridge Vent Outlet 6.0m Warm Air Out]
    end
    AIR -. Hot air rises .-> RV
    SV -. Cool air enters .-> AIR
```

**Calculation:**

Step 1: Vertical distance between openings:
$$H = 6.0 - 1.2 = 4.8 \text{ m}$$

Step 2: Convert temperatures to Kelvin:
$$T_i = 38 + 273 = 311 \text{ K}, \quad T_o = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$$

Step 3: Apply stack effect equation:
$$\Delta P_{stack} = \rho_o \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{T_i - T_o}{T_i}$$

Step 4: Substitute:
$$\Delta P_{stack} = 1.14 \times 9.81 \times 4.8 \times \frac{311 - 303}{311}$$

Step 5: Compute the bracket:
$$\frac{8}{311} = 0.02572$$

Step 6: Compute the product:
$$\Delta P_{stack} = 1.14 \times 9.81 \times 4.8 \times 0.02572$$
$$\Delta P_{stack} = 1.381 \text{ Pa}$$

**Conclusion:** The stack effect generates ~1.38 Pa driving pressure, sufficient to drive slow but continuous natural ventilation. For a wind effect of comparable magnitude, a wind speed of only ~1.5 m/s is required.

> **Mark Split:** Sketch 2 marks, Setup 2 marks, Calculation 2 marks, Conclusion 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the KTU exam.**

- ☐ **Ventilation = controlled air exchange** in greenhouses, poultry houses, dairy barns, mushroom rooms, grain silos.
- ☐ **Five goals:** Temperature ↓, Humidity ↓, CO₂ ↑, Pollutants ↓, Air velocity uniformity.
- ☐ **Two natural driving forces:** Wind ($\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 C_d$) and Stack effect ($\Delta P = \rho g H \Delta T / T_i$).
- ☐ **Master formula:** $Q = (ACH \times V) / 3600$ — learn it cold; use it in 70% of numericals.
- ☐ **Heat-based sizing:** $Q = q_{total} / (\rho C_p \Delta T)$, with $C_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K).
- ☐ **Fan affinity laws exponents are 1, 2, 3** for flow, pressure, power — never mix them.
- ☐ **PID equation:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \cdot de/dt$.
- ☐ **Key sensors:** PT100/DHT22 (T/RH), SHT31 (RH), MH-Z19 (CO₂), electrochemical NH₃ sensor, hot-wire anemometer.
- ☐ **Standard values to memorize:**
   - Greenhouse CO₂ setpoint: **1000 ppm**
   - Broiler house ACH: **10–20** (winter), **60–90** (summer)
   - Ammonia limit: **< 25 ppm**
   - Air density at 20°C: **1.2 kg/m³**
   - 1 m³/s = **2118.88 CFM**
- ☐ **Always draw a labeled block diagram** for 7+ mark questions — 2 marks saved every time.
- ☐ **Always include units** in every numerical step — examiners WILL deduct for missing units.
- ☐ **Always state the conversion** 3600 (s/h) when going between ACH and m³/s.
- ☐ **Industrial relevance:** Modern farms use **VFD + PID + IoT sensors** for 15–20% mortality reduction in broilers and 20–30% energy savings in greenhouses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling and Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTURE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cooling and Heating ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Cooling in Agriculture: Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Agricultural Cooling** refers to the engineered removal of sensible and latent heat from harvested produce, livestock shelters, and controlled-environment agricultural structures (greenhouses, mushroom houses, cold rooms) to suppress microbial activity, retard respiration, slow enzymatic degradation, and extend the marketable shelf life of biological products. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the *cooling* subsystem of agricultural instrumentation comprises the **load**, the **refrigeration plant (vapor compression / vapor absorption)**, the **heat exchange network**, and the **closed-loop instrumentation** (RTD/thermocouple sensors, PID controllers, solenoid valves, compressor contactors). **Core sub-domains of agricultural cooling:** 1. **Pre-cooling** — rapid removal of field heat immediately after harvest. 2. **Cold Storage** — long-term holding at prescribed temperature and RH. 3. **Refrigerated Transport (Reefer)** — mobile cold chain. 4. **Evaporative Cooling** — pad-and-fan systems for low-cost storage. ### 1.2 Heating in Agriculture: Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Agricultural Heating** is the controlled addition of thermal energy to the growing medium, ambient air, or product mass in order to maintain optimum temperatures for crop growth, germination, drying, disease prevention, and livestock comfort. It includes **greenhouse heating**, **soil/bed heating**, **brooding (poultry heating)**, and **product drying**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Refrigerator" Analogy** — Think of your home refrigerator. It does two jobs: (a) it *sucks* heat out of the food compartment using a refrigerant, and (b) it *rejects* that heat to your kitchen via the back coil. Agricultural cooling is the **same principle at farm scale** — your harvest is the "food," the cold room is the "fridge," and the condenser heat is dumped outside the storage shed. > > **The "Bath Heater" Analogy** — Greenhouse heating is essentially *giving a warm bath to plants*. Just as a geyser heats water which flows through pipes to warm your bathroom, a boiler heats water that flows through poly-tubes or finned pipes inside the greenhouse, transferring warmth to the air and roots. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in This Module - **1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR)** = **3.517 kW** = **50.4 MJ/hr** of heat removed. - **Temperature ranges** in agricultural storage: **0 °C to 13 °C** (horticulture), **−18 °C** (frozen produce), **18 °C to 27 °C** (tropical fruits like banana ripening). - **Relative Humidity (RH)** targets: **85–95 %** for leafy vegetables, **60–70 %** for apples. - **U-value** of cold-room walls: typically **0.20 to 0.40 W/m²·K** with PUF insulation. ### 1.5 Why This Topic is Exam-Critical > [!WARNING] > - Module 4 frequently appears as a **Part-B 14-mark question** in KTU ESE, typically asking for heat-load calculation or description of a refrigeration cycle. > - Numerical problems on **latent/sensible heat balance** and **cop calculation** are examiner favourites (Dec 2022, June 2023, Dec 2023 KTU pattern). > - Students frequently confuse **TR with kW** and **sensible heat with total heat** — these are the top two mark-deduction zones. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Components of the Cooling/Heating Subsystem The system is best understood as a **closed energy-control loop**: 1. **Sensors** — RTD (Pt100), Thermocouple (K-type), Thermistor, Hygrometer. 2. **Controller** — On-Off / Proportional / PID controller with set-point (SP). 3. **Actuators** — Compressor contactor, solenoid valve, damper motor, hot-water valve. 4. **Final Control Element** — Evaporator coil (cooling), boiler/burner (heating). 5. **Process** — Cold room, greenhouse, drying bin, livestock shed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Cognitive Link** — This maps to **CO4** of the syllabus: *“Apply instrumentation principles for cooling, heating, and environmental control in agriculture.”* ### 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why pre-cool?** Because field heat at 30 °C causes a mango to lose shelf-life **9× faster** than at 13 °C. Rapid removal flattens the respiration curve. - **Why refrigerate?** Lowering temperature from 25 °C to 0 °C reduces the respiration rate of apples by a factor of ~3 and microbial growth rate by ~10. - **Why heat a greenhouse?** Night-time radiation losses can drop internal temperature below the dew point, causing fungal disease; heating maintains photosynthesis and prevents chilling injury. - **Why PID and not On-Off?** A simple thermostat "chatters" the compressor (1–4 cycles/hour), causing wear and ±3 °C swing. PID gives smooth modulation within ±0.5 °C. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are the **KTU 2024 expected formulae** for this module. Use `\vert` style for any absolute value (not the literal pipe `|`). **Table 2.1 — Heat Load and Refrigeration Formula Sheet** | # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (product cooling) | W or kJ/hr | | 2 | $Q_l = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat (respiration moisture, infiltration) | W or kJ/hr | | 3 | $Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Heat gain through walls / roof | W | | 4 | $Q_{prod} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Heat to be removed from produce | kW | | 5 | $\text{1 TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$ | One ton of refrigeration | kW | | 6 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot (ideal) COP | dimensionless | | 8 | $Q_{total} = Q_{wall} + Q_{prod} + Q_{inf} + Q_{resp}$ | Total cold-room load | W | | 9 | $q_v = \dfrac{V_{room}}{\tau}$ | Volumetric air-flow requirement | m³/s | | 10 | $h_{moist} = 1.005\,T + \omega\,(2501 + 1.88\,T)$ | Moist-air enthalpy | kJ/kg dry air | | 11 | $\omega = 0.622\,\dfrac{P_v}{P_a - P_v}$ | Specific humidity | kg water / kg dry air | | 12 | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{vs}}$ | Relative humidity (ratio form) | dimensionless (0–1) | | 13 | $Q_{greenhouse} = U \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Greenhouse heat-loss | W | | 14 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{m_{fuel} \cdot CV}$ | Boiler efficiency | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Numericals** > - Air specific heat $C_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ > - Water latent heat $L = 2501\ \text{kJ/kg}$ at 0 °C > - PUF insulation $\lambda = 0.020$ to $0.025\ \text{W/m·K}$ > - Approximate respiration heat: apples ≈ **10–20 mW/kg** at 0 °C, bananas ≈ **80–120 mW/kg** at 13 °C. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Post-harvest loss reduction** — India loses ~₹92,000 crore/year in perishables; proper cold-chain instrumentation can cut this by **30–40 %**. - **Precision greenhouse** — Dutch greenhouse complexes use 100 % controlled heating + CO₂ enrichment, achieving 4× the open-field yield. - **Dairy and meat cold-chain** — Mandatory HACCP cold-chain compliance for export depends on RTD/logger + PID instrumentation. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Heat-Load of a Cold Room **Problem Setup:** A cold room stores 10,000 kg of apples at 0 °C. The ambient is 35 °C. Wall area $A = 80\ \text{m}^2$, overall $U = 0.30\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Door opening causes 1.5 air changes/hr. Respiration heat = 15 mW/kg. Compute the refrigeration capacity required in **kW and TR**. **Step 1 — Heat gain through walls (conduction load):** $$ Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T $$ $$ Q_{wall} = 0.30 \times 80 \times (35 - 0) $$ $$ Q_{wall} = 0.30 \times 80 \times 35 $$ $$ Q_{wall} = 840\ \text{W} = 0.840\ \text{kW} $$ *Text logic:* Multiply U-value (W/m²·K), area (m²), and temperature difference (K) to get heat leak in watts. **Step 2 — Product cooling load (sensible heat to be removed):** $$ Q_{prod} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T $$ $$ Q_{prod} = 10{,}000 \times 3.65 \times (28 - 0) / 3600 $$ Using apple $C_p \approx 3.65\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\Delta T = 28\ \text{K}$ (since fruit enters at ~28 °C, cooled to 0 °C) and converting kJ/hr → kW by dividing by 3600: $$ Q_{prod} = 10{,}000 \times 3.65 \times 28 / 3600 $$ $$ Q_{prod} = 1{,}022{,}000 / 3600 $$ $$ Q_{prod} = 283.9\ \text{kW} $$ > [!WARNING] > In KTU exams, **forgetting the /3600 conversion** when kJ/hr is given in the question is the #1 numerical mistake. **Step 3 — Infiltration (door-opening + air-change) load:** Volume of room $V = 5\ \text{m} \times 4\ \text{m} \times 3\ \text{m} = 60\ \text{m}^3$. Density of air $\rho = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Air changes/hr = 1.5. $$ \dot{m}_{air} = V \cdot \rho \cdot N = 60 \times 1.2 \times 1.5 $$ $$ \dot{m}_{air} = 108\ \text{kg/hr} $$ $$ Q_{inf} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 108 \times 1.005 \times 35 $$ $$ Q_{inf} = 3798.9\ \text{kJ/hr} = 3798.9 / 3600 = 1.055\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Respiration heat:** $$ Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_{resp} $$ $$ Q_{resp} = 10{,}000 \times 0.015 = 150\ \text{W} = 0.150\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 5 — Total cooling load:** $$ Q_{total} = Q_{wall} + Q_{prod} + Q_{inf} + Q_{resp} $$ $$ Q_{total} = 0.840 + 283.9 + 1.055 + 0.150 $$ $$ Q_{total} = 285.95\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to Tons of Refrigeration:** $$ \text{TR} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{3.517} = \dfrac{285.95}{3.517} \approx 81.3\ \text{TR} $$ *Final answer:* A **286 kW (~81 TR)** refrigeration plant is required. Add a **10–15 % safety margin** → spec a **90–95 TR** unit. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — COP of an Ideal Vapor-Compression Refrigeration Cycle **Cycle state points (typical):** - **1** → Saturated vapor at evaporator outlet. - **2** → Superheated vapor at compressor outlet. - **3** → Saturated liquid at condenser outlet. - **4** → Liquid-vapor mixture after throttling. **Energy balance on evaporator (refrigeration effect):** $$ q_L = h_1 - h_4\ \ [\text{kJ/kg}] $$ **Compressor work input:** $$ w_{comp} = h_2 - h_1\ \ [\text{kJ/kg}] $$ **Condenser heat rejection:** $$ q_H = h_2 - h_3\ \ [\text{kJ/kg}] $$ **Definition of COP:** $$ \text{COP} = \dfrac{q_L}{w_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} $$ **For Carnot (ideal) cycle between $T_L$ and $T_H$ (K):** $$ \text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Micro-Example:** For a freezer with $T_L = 258\ \text{K}$ (−15 °C) and ambient $T_H = 308\ \text{K}$ (35 °C): > > $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = 258 / (308 - 258) = 258 / 50 = 5.16$ > > A real system typically achieves **COP ≈ 1.5 to 2.5** for the same conditions, demonstrating irreversibility losses. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Greenhouse Heat-Loss Calculation **Net heat requirement** for a greenhouse to maintain $T_{in}$ when ambient is $T_{out}$: $$ Q_g = U \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out}) - Q_{solar} $$ where: - $A_g$ = total envelope area (roof + walls + gables) in $\text{m}^2$. - $Q_{solar}$ = incident solar radiation absorbed (W). **Pipe-heating system design — number of pipes required:** $$ N_{pipes} = \dfrac{Q_g}{q_{pipe} \cdot L_{pipe}} $$ where $q_{pipe}$ is heat output per metre of pipe (W/m), $L_{pipe}$ is length per pipe run. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Standard Pipe Output:** A 2-inch MS pipe carrying water at 80 °C in still air at 15 °C delivers approximately **350–450 W/m**. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Cold Storage Instrumentation Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Cold Room
Process] --> B1[Pt100 RTD Sensor
T measurement] B1 --> C1[Temperature Transmitter
4 to 20 mA] C1 --> D1[PID Controller
Set Point 0 degC] D1 --> E1[Solenoid Valve
Refrigerant flow] E1 --> F1[Compressor Contactor
ON OFF control] F1 --> G1[Evaporator Coil
Heat absorption] G1 --> A1 D1 --> H1[Data Logger SCADA
HMI Display] H1 --> D1 ``` *Reading the diagram:* The RTD senses the cold-room air, the transmitter linearizes it, the PID controller compares to set-point and actuates the solenoid → compressor → evaporator, closing the loop. The SCADA layer provides logging and remote alarm. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Greenhouse Heating System with Boiler + Pipe Network ```mermaid flowchart TD A2[Boiler Furnace
Fuel Coal Gas Biomass] --> B2[Hot Water Header
80 degC] B2 --> C2[Primary Circulation Pump] C2 --> D2[Heat Exchanger
Mixing Valve] D2 --> E2[Distribution Manifold] E2 --> F2[Heating Pipe Network
Root Zone Overhead] F2 --> G2[Greenhouse Air
Plant Canopy] G2 --> H2[Return Line
60 degC] H2 --> A2 G2 --> I2[Pt100 + RH Sensor] I2 --> J2[Climate Controller PID] J2 --> K2[3 Way Mixing Valve Actuator] K2 --> D2 ``` *Reading the diagram:* Fuel → Boiler → Hot water → Pump → Mixing valve (modulated by PID) → Distribution pipes → Greenhouse air → Return → Boiler. The PID uses RTD + RH feedback to throttle the mixing valve, giving closed-loop climate control. --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[State 1
Saturated Vapor
Evaporator Outlet] --> P2[State 2
Superheated Vapor] P2 --> P3[State 3
Saturated Liquid
Condenser Outlet] P3 --> P4[State 4
Two Phase Mix
After Throttle] P4 --> P1 P2 --- CMP[Compressor
W in] P3 --- COND[Condenser
Heat out Q H] P4 --- THV[Throttle Valve
Isenthalpic] P1 --- EVAP[Evaporator
Heat in Q L] ``` *Reading the diagram:* 1→2 = Compression, 2→3 = Condensation (heat rejected), 3→4 = Throttling, 4→1 = Evaporation (heat absorbed from cold room). --- ### 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Pre-cooling Methods **Table 4.1 — Pre-cooling Methods Compared** | Method | Mechanism | Suitable For | Cooling Speed | Cost | |--------|-----------|--------------|---------------|------| | Room Cooling | Still air | General produce | Slow (12–24 hr) | Low | | Forced-Air Cooling | High-velocity air through stack | Pome fruits, strawberry | Fast (4–8 hr) | Medium | | Hydrocooling | Ice-water immersion | Leafy veg, sweet corn | Very fast (1–2 hr) | Medium-High | | Vacuum Cooling | Evaporation under vacuum | Lettuce, mushroom | Ultra fast (15–30 min) | High | | Package Icing | Crushed ice in package | Broccoli, fish | Fast | Medium | ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] Define "Ton of Refrigeration" and state its SI equivalent. Why is this unit preferred over kW in the agricultural cold-chain industry?** **Model Answer:** A *Ton of Refrigeration (TR)* is the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb ≈ 907.18 kg)** of pure water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours. Numerically: $$ 1\ \text{TR} = \dfrac{907.18 \times 335}{24 \times 3600} \approx 3.517\ \text{kW} $$ In **MJ/hr**: $1\ \text{TR} = 50.4\ \text{MJ/hr}$. It is preferred in the cold-chain industry because (i) plant capacities are typically in the range 5–500 TR which avoids large kW numbers, (ii) it directly relates to historical ice-making economics, and (iii) it is the universally quoted rating in commercial refrigeration catalogues and tenders. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between "Sensible Heat" and "Latent Heat" with one agricultural example each.** **Model Answer:** - **Sensible Heat** — heat transfer that changes the *temperature* of a body without a phase change. *Example:* Cooling of freshly harvested mangoes from 30 °C to 13 °C removes sensible heat. - **Latent Heat** — heat transfer associated with a *phase change* at constant temperature. *Example:* Removal of moisture (water vapour condensing on evaporator coil) from inside a cold room, or ice melting during hydrocooling. Mathematically: $$ Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T $$ $$ Q_l = m \cdot L $$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Choose ONE of the two options.** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **vapor compression refrigeration system** used in an agricultural cold storage. State the function of each major component. **(b) [7 Marks]** A cold room stores 8,000 kg of oranges. Initial temperature of fruit = 27 °C, storage temperature = 5 °C, ambient = 38 °C. Wall area = 70 m², $U = 0.35\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. $C_p$ of orange ≈ 3.85 kJ/kg·K. Respiration heat = 12 mW/kg. Compute the total refrigeration load in kW and in TR. Assume 1.5 air changes per hour with room volume 50 m³. **Model Solution (a):** The vapor compression refrigeration cycle (VCRS) has four major components: 1. **Compressor** — Compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapour from evaporator pressure to condenser pressure. Raises both temperature and pressure of the refrigerant. 2. **Condenser** — The hot, high-pressure vapour rejects heat to the surroundings (ambient air or cooling water) and condenses into a saturated liquid. 3. **Throttling / Expansion Valve** — Reduces pressure of the liquid refrigerant isenthalpically, causing partial flash evaporation and significant cooling. 4. **Evaporator** — The cold low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture absorbs heat from the cold-room air, completing evaporation and providing the refrigeration effect. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor] --> B[Condenser] B --> C[Expansion Valve] C --> D[Evaporator Cold Room] D --> A ``` Refrigerants commonly used: R-134a, R-404A, R-22 (legacy), and ammonia (R-717) in large industrial plants. **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Wall heat gain:** $$ Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.35 \times 70 \times (38 - 5) $$ $$ Q_{wall} = 0.35 \times 70 \times 33 = 808.5\ \text{W} = 0.8085\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 2 — Product sensible heat:** $$ Q_{prod} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 8000 \times 3.85 \times (27 - 5) / 3600 $$ $$ Q_{prod} = 8000 \times 3.85 \times 22 / 3600 = 677{,}600 / 3600 $$ $$ Q_{prod} = 188.22\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 3 — Infiltration load:** $$ \dot{m}_{air} = V \cdot \rho \cdot N = 50 \times 1.2 \times 1.5 = 90\ \text{kg/hr} $$ $$ Q_{inf} = 90 \times 1.005 \times 33 / 3600 = 2984.85 / 3600 = 0.829\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Respiration heat:** $$ Q_{resp} = 8000 \times 0.012 = 96\ \text{W} = 0.096\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 5 — Total load:** $$ Q_{total} = 0.8085 + 188.22 + 0.829 + 0.096 = 189.95\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 6 — TR:** $$ \text{TR} = 189.95 / 3.517 \approx 54\ \text{TR} $$ **Final Answer:** **190 kW ≈ 54 TR** refrigeration plant is required. Adding 10 % safety → spec **60 TR**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **various methods of pre-cooling of horticultural produce**. Compare hydro-cooling, forced-air cooling, and vacuum cooling in terms of principle, suitable crops, and cooling time. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a labelled diagram, explain the **greenhouse heating system** using a hot-water boiler and pipe network. Derive the expression for total heat loss $Q = U A (T_{in} - T_{out})$ and compute the number of 2-inch MS pipes (each 6 m long, 400 W/m output) required if $Q_{g} = 60\ \text{kW}$ and the available pipe run is 40 m. **Model Solution (a):** Pre-cooling is the **rapid removal of field heat** from freshly harvested produce to reduce its temperature to the storage temperature as quickly as possible. **Methods:** 1. **Room Cooling** — Produce is placed in a cold room and cooled by still or slightly circulated air. Slow but inexpensive; suitable for less perishable items. 2. **Forced-Air Cooling** — A pressure differential is created across stacked produce using a fan, pulling cold air through the package vents. 4–8 times faster than room cooling. Used for strawberry, grape, pome fruits. 3. **Hydrocooling** — Immersion or showering of produce in ice-cold water (≈ 1 °C). Very high heat-transfer coefficient; cooling in 1–2 hours. Used for sweet corn, leafy vegetables, peaches. 4. **Vacuum Cooling** — Produce sealed in a vacuum chamber; water evaporates rapidly from its surface, withdrawing latent heat and dropping temperature quickly. 15–30 minutes. Used for lettuce, mushroom, cut flowers. 5. **Package Icing** — Crushed ice placed in package. Suitable for broccoli, spinach. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Hydrocooling | Forced-Air | Vacuum Cooling | |-----------|--------------|-----------|----------------| | Heat transfer medium | Cold water | Cold air | Water vapour | | Heat transfer coeff. | High (500–1000 W/m²K) | Medium (50–100) | Latent (very high) | | Cooling time | 1–2 hr | 4–8 hr | 15–30 min | | Suitable crops | Sweet corn, leafy veg | Strawberry, apple | Lettuce, mushroom | | Cost | Medium | Low–Medium | High | **Model Solution (b):** **Greenhouse Heating System:** The system consists of a **boiler (fired by coal, biomass, diesel, or natural gas)** that heats water to 80–90 °C, a **circulation pump**, a **3-way mixing valve** (modulated by climate controller), and a **network of finned pipes** running along the perimeter and inside the growing beds. ``` [Boiler] → [Hot water header] → [Pump] → [Mixing valve] → [Pipes] → [Greenhouse air] → [Return] → [Boiler] ``` **Derivation:** For a steady-state greenhouse, the heat supplied by the heating system equals the heat lost through the envelope (walls, roof, gables) minus the solar gain. Heat loss by conduction through each envelope element: $$ Q_{i} = U_i \cdot A_i \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out}) $$ Summing over all envelope elements: $$ Q_g = \sum_i U_i A_i (T_{in} - T_{out}) $$ Assuming an average U-value $U$ and total area $A_g$: $$ \boxed{Q_g = U \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out}) - Q_{solar}} $$ **Numerical Computation:** Given: $Q_g = 60\ \text{kW} = 60{,}000\ \text{W}$, pipe output $q_{pipe} = 400\ \text{W/m}$, pipe length per run $L_{pipe} = 6\ \text{m}$. Heat delivered by one pipe run: $$ Q_{one} = 400 \times 6 = 2400\ \text{W} $$ Number of pipe runs required: $$ N = \dfrac{60{,}000}{2400} = 25\ \text{pipe runs} $$ Total pipe length needed: $25 \times 6 = 150\ \text{m}$. If only 40 m of pipe-run length is available per loop, then number of parallel loops: $$ N_{loops} = \dfrac{150}{40} = 3.75 \rightarrow 4\ \text{loops} $$ **Final Answer:** **25 pipe runs of 6 m each (≈ 150 m total), arranged in 4 parallel loops** of 40 m each. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 4:** > > 1. **Unit mismatch** — Mixing kJ/hr with kW without converting. Always convert using ÷3600 or ×3600. > 2. **TR vs kW confusion** — Examiners will deduct a full mark if you state "refrigeration = 286 TR" when the answer expects kW, or vice versa. > 3. **Forgetting respiration heat** — It is small in absolute terms but examiners specifically allocate a 1-mark step for it. > 4. **No safety factor** — Real designs use a 10–15 % margin; failure to mention it loses the "design thinking" marks. > 5. **Diagram missing labels** — A block diagram without labels (compressor, condenser, etc.) on the arrows loses 2 marks even if the explanation is correct. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Cooling & Heating** - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 50.4 MJ/hr** — memorize this factor. - **VCRS** = Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator (closed loop). - **COP** = refrigeration effect / compressor work = $(h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$. - **Carnot COP** = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — only an ideal upper bound. - **Total cold-room load** = $Q_{wall} + Q_{prod} + Q_{inf} + Q_{resp}$. - **Sensible heat** changes temperature: $Q_s = m C_p \Delta T$. - **Latent heat** changes phase: $Q_l = m L$. - **Air-change infiltration load** = $V \rho N C_p \Delta T / 3600$. - **Respiration heat** of apples ≈ 15 mW/kg at 0 °C; bananas ≈ 100 mW/kg at 13 °C. - **Pre-cooling methods** — Room, Forced-Air, Hydro, Vacuum, Package Icing — know one crop each. - **Greenhouse heating** uses hot-water pipes; boiler efficiency $\eta = Q_{useful}/(m_{fuel} \cdot CV)$. - **PUF insulation** $U \approx 0.30\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$; bare GI sheet $U \approx 6.5\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ (≈ 20× worse). - **Psychrometric identity** — $h = 1.005 T + \omega(2501 + 1.88 T)$ kJ/kg dry air. - **RH targets** — leafy veg 85–95 %, apples 60–70 %, mangoes 85–90 %. - **Always add 10–15 % design margin** after computing total load. - **Sensor choice** — Pt100 for −50 to +200 °C; Type-K thermocouple above 200 °C; thermistor for narrow range high-accuracy. - **Control strategy** — On-Off for small sheds, PID for commercial cold rooms and greenhouses.

topics.title: Wind Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wind Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Wind Speed** is defined as the magnitude of the velocity of air parcels moving relative to a fixed reference frame on the Earth's surface. In the context of **Agricultural Instrumentation (PEICT753)**, wind speed refers to the scalar measurement of horizontal air motion expressed at a standard reference height (typically **10 meters** above ground level as per **WMO — World Meteorological Organization** standards), and is a critical parameter in micrometeorology, precision agriculture, agro-meteorological stations, greenhouse climate control, and spray-drift prediction.

Mathematically, wind speed $v$ is the magnitude of the wind velocity vector $\vec{V}$:

$$v = \vert \vec{V} \vert = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2 + w^2}$$

where:
- $u$ = zonal (east–west) wind component in $\text{m/s}$
- $v$ = meridional (north–south) wind component in $\text{m/s}$
- $w$ = vertical wind component in $\text{m/s}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For agricultural applications, only the *horizontal* wind speed ($u, v$) is practically measured, since vertical motion near the surface is negligible compared to horizontal flow. The instrument used is called an **Anemometer**.

### 1.2 Standard Units & Scales

| Quantity | SI Unit | Common Practical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Wind Speed | $\text{m/s}$ | $\text{km/h}$, $\text{knots}$, $\text{mph}$, $\text{Beaufort}$ |
| Wind Direction | degrees ($\circ$) from North | Cardinal points (N, NE, E…) |
| Wind Pressure (dynamic) | Pascal ($\text{Pa}$) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Constant to Memorize:**
> $1 \text{ m/s} = 3.6 \text{ km/h} = 1.944 \text{ knots} = 2.237 \text{ mph}$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine standing at the edge of a wheat field in Thrissur during the monsoon. The stalks bend — the *faster* they bend, the stronger the wind. Now imagine placing a tiny paddle-wheel (a children's pinwheel) into that wind. The faster the wind blows, the faster the wheel spins.

**That spinning paddle-wheel is exactly how a Cup Anemometer works.** It converts invisible, intangible air motion into a *countable* rotation (revolutions per second), which we then convert mathematically back into wind speed.

Think of wind speed as **"how much air is rushing past a fixed point every second"** — measured in *meters of air-column passing per second*.

> [!TIP]
> **Agricultural Why:** Wind speed affects pesticide spraying (drift), pollination, evapotranspiration, soil erosion, and frost damage. Knowing it precisely is essential for **smart farming**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wind Energy & Power Density — The Core Engineering Relationship

Wind is *kinetic energy in motion*. The instantaneous **Kinetic Energy (KE)** of an air parcel of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ is:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

Consider a cylindrical cross-section of air of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $L$ passing a point per unit time $t$. The mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

where $\rho$ = **air density** ($\approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level, ISA conditions).

The **Wind Power Density** $P / A$ (power per unit swept area) — the most important formula for any wind-based agricultural or renewable system — is:

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power in wind scales with the CUBE of velocity.** Doubling wind speed gives **8× more power**. This is why small measurement errors at low speeds matter enormously.

The actual extractable mechanical power (Betz limit) is:

$$P_{extracted} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

where $C_p$ = power coefficient (theoretical max $= 0.593$ — **Betz Limit**).

## 2.2 Wind Profile — Logarithmic Law (Why 10 m?)

Wind speed is **not constant with height** above ground. Near the surface, friction (from crops, trees, soil) slows the wind. The **Logarithmic Wind Profile Law** governs this:

$$v(z) = \frac{v_*}{\kappa} \ln\!\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$

where:
- $v(z)$ = wind speed at height $z$
- $v_*$ = friction velocity ($\text{m/s}$)
- $\kappa$ = von Kármán constant $\approx 0.41$
- $z_0$ = surface roughness length (depends on terrain)

### 2.3 Dynamic Pressure — Pitot-Static Anemometer Principle

When wind strikes a sensor, it exerts **dynamic pressure**:

$$q = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2$$

This is the principle behind **pressure-tube anemometers** and **Pitot-static tubes**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2 + w^2}$ | Vector magnitude of wind | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Wind power | $\text{W}$ |
| 3 | $\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^3$ | Wind power density | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 4 | $P_{extracted} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Actual extractable power | $\text{W}$ |
| 5 | $q = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2$ | Dynamic pressure (Pitot tube) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 6 | $v(z) = \frac{v_*}{\kappa} \ln(z/z_0)$ | Log wind profile | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 7 | $v_{\text{cup}} = a \cdot f + b$ | Cup anemometer linear calibration | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 8 | $N_s = \frac{\omega R}{v_\infty}$ | Cup anemometer rotation-speed ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_v = \frac{L}{v}$ | Transport time of air parcel over distance $L$ | $\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $1 \text{ m/s} = 3.6 \text{ km/h}$ | Unit conversion | — |
| 11 | $E_d = k \cdot v^2$ | Soil/erosion drift factor | — |
| 12 | $\text{Beaufort } B \Rightarrow v \approx 0.836 \cdot B^{3/2}$ | Beaufort to m/s (approx) | $\text{m/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always include **$\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and standard reference height **$z = 10 \text{ m}$** when solving numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Agriculture

1. **Pesticide/Herbicide Spray Drift Prediction:** Drift distance $d \approx k \cdot v \cdot h$ (where $h$ = release height).
2. **Greenhouse Ventilation Design:** Required airflow $Q = A \cdot v$ where $A$ = vent opening area.
3. **Wind-Pump / Wind-Mill Sizing:** Sizing blades for irrigation using $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$.
4. **Pollination & Dispersal Models:** Seed/pollen travel distance depends on $v^2$.
5. **Evapotranspiration (Penman–Monteith Equation):** Wind is a direct input term.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wind Power Density $\frac{1}{2} \rho v^3$

**Starting Premise:** Compute the kinetic energy of a parcel of air and divide by the time it takes to pass a fixed plane.

**Step 1 — Mass of air in a cylindrical volume:**
Consider a cylinder of cross-section $A$ (perpendicular to wind) and length $L$. Its volume is:

$$V_{\text{cyl}} = A \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Mass of this air parcel:**

$$m = \rho \cdot V_{\text{cyl}} = \rho A L$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy of this parcel (Standard mechanics):**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A L v^2$$

**Step 4 — Time for the entire parcel to pass the reference plane:**
The "back" of the cylinder reaches the plane after time $t = L / v$. Hence:

$$t = \frac{L}{v}$$

**Step 5 — Power = Energy / Time:**

$$P = \frac{E_k}{t} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \rho A L v^2}{L/v}$$

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A L v^2 \cdot \frac{v}{L}$$

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

**Step 6 — Power per unit swept area:**

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^3 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cup-Anemometer Linear Calibration Equation

A three-cup anemometer rotates at frequency $f$ (Hz). Empirically:

$$v = a f + b$$

**Derivation of constants (from physical reasoning):**

For an ideal cup anemometer, drag force on the convex cup differs from drag on the concave cup. Let:
- $F_c$ = drag on concave cup (moving away from wind)
- $F_x$ = drag on convex cup (moving into wind)

Net torque on rotor:

$$\tau_{\text{net}} = R(F_c - F_x)$$

where $R$ = arm length.

Drag force: $F_D = \frac{1}{2} C_D \rho A v_{\text{rel}}^2$

For a cup moving at tangential velocity $\omega R$ in a wind of velocity $v_\infty$:
- Concave cup relative velocity: $v_\infty - \omega R$
- Convex cup relative velocity: $v_\infty + \omega R$

Setting $\tau_{\text{net}} = 0$ at steady state (no friction assumed):

$$R \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_c C_{Dc}(v_\infty - \omega R)^2 = R \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_x C_{Dx}(v_\infty + \omega R)^2$$

Dividing both sides by $R \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A$:

$$\sqrt{C_{Dc}}(v_\infty - \omega R) = \sqrt{C_{Dx}}(v_\infty + \omega R)$$

Let $K = \sqrt{C_{Dc}/C_{Dx}}$:

$$K(v_\infty - \omega R) = (v_\infty + \omega R)$$

$$v_\infty(K - 1) = \omega R (K + 1)$$

$$\omega R = v_\infty \cdot \frac{K - 1}{K + 1}$$

Since $f = \omega / (2\pi)$:

$$v_\infty = \omega R \cdot \frac{K+1}{K-1} = \frac{2\pi f R (K+1)}{K-1}$$

This gives the linear form $v = a f + b$ where:

$$a = \frac{2\pi R (K+1)}{K-1}, \qquad b = 0$$

For real instruments with bearing friction, a small offset $b \neq 0$ appears, hence the **practical calibration** $v = af + b$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A site in Palakkad records an average wind speed of **6 m/s** at 10 m height. Air density is $1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$. A farmer wishes to install a small wind pump with rotor diameter 2 m and $C_p = 0.35$. Compute:
> (i) Wind power density
> (ii) Total wind power through rotor
> (iii) Extractable mechanical power

**Solution:**

**(i) Wind Power Density:**

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.20 \times 6^3$$

$$\frac{P}{A} = 0.6 \times 216 = 129.6 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**(ii) Swept area of rotor (diameter $D = 2$ m):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4}{4} = \pi \approx 3.1416 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.6 \times 3.1416 \times 216$$

$$P = 407.15 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Extractable power:**

$$P_{\text{mech}} = C_p \cdot P = 0.35 \times 407.15 = 142.5 \text{ W} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wind Speed Measurement Methods

| Method | Principle | Range (m/s) | Accuracy | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cup Anemometer** | Rotation $\propto v$ | 0.3 – 75 | $\pm 0.5$ m/s | Standard agro-met stations |
| **Vane Anemometer** | Wind-driven propeller | 0.4 – 30 | $\pm 3$ % | Portable field surveys |
| **Hot-Wire Anemometer** | Cooling of heated wire $\propto v$ | 0.05 – 30 | $\pm 1$ % | Lab, low speeds |
| **Ultrasonic Anemometer** | Transit-time of sound pulses | 0 – 60 | $\pm 0.1$ m/s | Research-grade |
| **Pitot-Static Tube** | Differential pressure $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | 2 – 80 | $\pm 2$ % | High-speed wind tunnels |
| **Sonic Resonance** | Frequency shift | 0 – 40 | $\pm 0.5$ % | Compact sensors |
| **Laser Doppler (LDA)** | Doppler shift of scattered light | 0.001 – 100 | $\pm 0.1$ % | Research / lab |

> [!NOTE]
> **Most common in KTU agriculture syllabus:** **Cup Anemometer** (rotational type) and **Vane Anemometer** (propeller type). Focus your exam preparation on these.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
