```mermaid
    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        ECU[Tractor ECU Microcontroller]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        D1[Analog Instrument Cluster]
        D2[Digital LCD Display]
        D3[Warning Lamps]
        D4[ISOBUS to Implement ECU]
        D5[Auto-Steer Controller]
        D6[Telematics Modem]
    end

    ENG --> T1
    ENG --> T2
    ENG --> T3
    ENG --> T4
    ENG --> T11
    ENG --> T5
    ENG --> T6
    ENG --> T7
    ENG --> T8
    ENG --> T9
    ENG --> T10
    EMAG --> T11

    T1 --> SC
    T2 --> SC
    T3 --> SC
    T4 --> SC
    T5 --> SC
    T6 --> SC
    T7 --> SC
    T8 --> SC
    T9 --> SC
    T10 --> SC
    T11 --> SC

    SC --> ECU

    ECU --> D1
    ECU --> D2
    ECU --> D3
    ECU --> D4
    ECU --> D5
    ECU --> D6
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Layer 1 (Power):** Provides mechanical and electrical energy.
- **Layer 2 (Sensors):** Eleven primary transducers convert physical quantities into electrical signals.
- **Layer 3 (Signal Conditioning):** Amplifies, filters, linearizes, and digitizes (ADC) raw signals.
- **Layer 4 (Processing):** The ECU applies algorithms (e.g., slip calculation, draft control, fuel mapping).
- **Layer 5 (Output):** Information reaches the operator (cluster, display, lamps) and downstream systems (implement, auto-steer, cloud telematics).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Power Flow from Engine to Drawbar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Brake Power Pb] --> B[Clutch]
    B --> C[Gearbox]
    C --> D[Differential]
    D --> E[Final Drive]
    E --> F[Driving Wheels]
    F --> G[Wheel Slip Loss 1-s]
    F --> H[Soil Tractive Efficiency et]
    H --> I[Drawbar Pull Fdb]
    I --> J[Drawbar Power Pdb]
```

**Interpretation:** Power is progressively thinned by drivetrain losses ($\eta_m$), wheel slip ($1-s$), and tractive efficiency ($\eta_t$). The KTU formula:

$$P_{db} = (1 - s) \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_m \cdot P_b$$

is a direct graphical statement of this flow.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — 3-Point Hitch Instrumentation (Draft, Position, Mixed Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Implement Mounted on 3-Point Hitch] --> B[Draft Sensor on Lower Link]
    A --> C[Position Sensor LVDT on Top Link]
    B --> D[ECU Draft Control Loop]
    C --> E[ECU Position Control Loop]
    D --> F[Hydraulic Control Valve]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Hydraulic Cylinder on Hitch]
    G --> A
```

**Interpretation:** This is a **closed-loop feedback system**. The ECU continuously adjusts the hydraulic valve to maintain either:
- **Position Control** (hitch stays at fixed height) — used for depth-uniform operations.
- **Draft Control** (hitch reacts to soil resistance) — used for variable-soil operations.
- **Mixed Control** (blend of both).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define a tractor. List any **four** primary functional roles performed by an agricultural tractor.
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A tractor is a self-propelled, multi-purpose prime-mover that provides high drawbar pull, rotary power (via PTO), and hydraulic power for agricultural operations. **Four primary roles:**
> 1. **Drawbar work** — pulling ploughs, harrows, cultivators, seed drills.
> 2. **PTO work** — driving rotavators, threshers, sprayers, pumps.
> 3. **Hydraulic work** — lifting and controlling implements via 3-point hitch.
> 4. **Haulage** — transporting farm inputs and produce.
> *(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each role × 4 = 3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Explain with neat sketch the working principle of a **magnetic-pickup engine speed sensor** used in tractor tachometers.
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A magnetic-pickup sensor (also called a variable-reluctance sensor) is mounted adjacent to a toothed reluctor wheel attached to the engine flywheel or camshaft. As each tooth passes the sensor tip, the magnetic flux through the coil changes, inducing an **alternating EMF** (Faraday's Law) with frequency proportional to RPM. The ECU/instrument counts the pulses per unit time and converts them to RPM via the relation: $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n}$ where $f$ is pulse frequency (Hz) and $n$ is the number of teeth. *(1 mark principle + 1 mark construction + 1 mark equation ≈ 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **architecture of tractor instrumentation**, listing the major subsystems and at least **three sensors** in each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A modern tractor instrumentation architecture is layered into **Power, Sensor, Signal Conditioning, Processing, and Output** layers (refer Section 4.1 mermaid diagram).

| Subsystem | Three Sensors | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine** | RPM sensor, Coolant temp sensor, Oil pressure sensor | Monitor combustion, thermal, and lubrication health |
| **Drivetrain** | Wheel speed sensor, Radar ground speed sensor, Gear position switch | Compute wheel slip, area covered, and gear logic |
| **Hydraulic & 3-pt Hitch** | Pressure transducer, LVDT position sensor, Draft sensor on lower link | Control hitch height, draft force, and implement depth |
| **PTO** | PTO speed sensor, Torque sensor | Verify implement rated speed, protect driveline |
| **Electrical** | Battery voltage sensor, Alternator current sensor | Detect charging faults |
| **Precision Ag** | GPS receiver, IMU, ISOBUS terminal | Auto-steer, mapping, implement control |

The **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** receives conditioned signals and drives outputs: analog gauges, LCD display, warning lamps, ISOBUS, auto-steer, and telematics. *(2 marks diagram + 3 marks tabular sensors + 2 marks ECU role = 7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4WD tractor with rolling radius 0.62 m operates at 1600 rpm engine speed. The transmission ratio is 10:1, mechanical efficiency 0.86, tractive efficiency 0.68, and measured wheel slip 14%. The engine torque is 280 N·m. Calculate:
   (i) Engine brake power
   (ii) Power at wheel rim
   (iii) Theoretical and actual forward speeds
   (iv) Drawbar power
   (v) Drawbar pull
   **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Engine brake power (1.5 marks):**

$$P_b = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1600 \times 280}{60{,}000} = \frac{2{,}814{,}867}{60{,}000} = 46.91 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Power at wheel rim (1 mark):**

$$P_w = \eta_m \cdot P_b = 0.86 \times 46.91 = 40.34 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Forward speeds (1.5 marks):**
Wheel rpm $N_w = 1600/10 = 160$ rpm.

$$v_t = \frac{2 \pi \times 0.62 \times 160}{60} = \frac{623.07}{60} = 10.38 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_a = (1 - 0.14) \times 10.38 = 0.86 \times 10.38 = 8.93 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iv) Drawbar power (1.5 marks):**

$$P_{db} = (1 - s) \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_m \cdot P_b = 0.86 \times 0.68 \times 0.86 \times 46.91$$

$$= 0.86 \times 0.68 = 0.5848; \; 0.5848 \times 0.86 = 0.5029; \; 0.5029 \times 46.91 = 23.59 \text{ kW}$$

**(v) Drawbar pull (1.5 marks):**

$$F_{db} = \frac{P_{db}}{v_a} = \frac{23{,}590}{8.93} = 2641.7 \text{ N} \approx 2.64 \text{ kN}$$

**Sanity check:** $P_{db} = F_{db} \cdot v_a = 2641.7 \times 8.93 = 23{,}590$ W ✔.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **3-point hitch system** of a tractor. With a neat block diagram, explain the **draft, position, and mixed control modes** of operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The 3-point hitch (3PH) is the standard implement-mounting system comprising **two lower draft links** and **one upper (top) link**, controlled by hydraulic cylinders. It allows the implement to be lifted, lowered, and force-controlled from the operator's seat.

| Control Mode | Sensing Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Position Control** | LVDT/potentiometer senses hitch height; ECU maintains fixed height | Tillage on uniform soil, transport |
| **Draft Control** | Strain-gauged lower link senses implement draft; ECU modulates hitch to maintain constant draft | Variable soil resistance, deep ploughing |
| **Mixed Control** | Weighted combination of both signals | General field operations |

**Block diagram** (refer Section 4.3 mermaid): Implement forces feed back through **draft sensor** and **position sensor** into the **ECU**, which drives the **hydraulic control valve**, which moves the **hitch cylinder**, which repositions the implement — a **closed feedback loop**. *(2 marks description + 2 marks modes + 2 marks block diagram + 1 mark closed-loop insight = 7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of an LVDT-based hitch position sensor**. List **two advantages** of LVDT over a potentiometric position sensor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Principle (1.5 marks):** LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is a passive inductive transducer. It has one **primary coil** and two **secondaries** wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core coupled to the hitch linkage. When the primary is excited by an AC carrier, the differential output voltage $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ is a linear function of core displacement.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Primary coil (P) at the center.
- Two identical secondary coils (S1, S2) wound on either side, connected in series-opposition.
- A movable ferromagnetic core attached to the top link of the 3-point hitch.
- Shielded housing, with excitation leads and output leads taken out via cable.

**Working (2 marks):** As the hitch moves, the core shifts:
- **Null position:** $E_{s1} = E_{s2} \Rightarrow E_o = 0$.
- **Upward movement:** Core enters S1 more → $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ → positive $E_o$.
- **Downward movement:** Core enters S2 more → $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ → negative $E_o$.
The magnitude of $E_o$ is proportional to displacement; the phase indicates direction. The ECU demodulates and linearizes the signal to drive the display/control.

**Two advantages over potentiometric sensor (1.5 marks):**
1. **Frictionless** — no contact between core and coils, hence infinite mechanical life.
2. **High resolution and infinite resolution** — continuous output with no quantization.
3. *(bonus)* High linearity, immune to vibration, no contact wear, suitable for harsh field environments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "Brake Power" with "Drawbar Power":** Many students write $P_{db} = P_b$ directly, forgetting slip and tractive efficiency. **Always multiply by $(1-s) \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_m$.** (–2 marks typical deduction)
> 2. **Mixing up wheel rpm and engine rpm:** If a transmission ratio is given, **divide** engine rpm by the ratio to get wheel rpm. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** $P_b$ from $2\pi NT/60$ gives **watts**; remember to **divide by 1000** for kW. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** when using $F_{db} = P_{db}/v_a$. (–1 mark)
> 5. **In sensor questions, students often write the principle but skip the transducer name and conversion formula** — examiners specifically look for the **mathematical relation** (e.g., $N = 60f/n$ for magnetic pickup). (–1 to –2 marks)
> 6. **LVDT answer pitfall:** Writing only "LVDT works on mutual induction" without mentioning the **series-opposition secondary connection** and the **null position** — these are the heart of the principle. (–2 marks)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density checklist — read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Tractor** = self-propelled prime-mover for agricultural drawbar, PTO, hydraulic, and haulage work.
- ☐ **Five functional roles:** drawbar, PTO, hydraulic, stationary-belt, haulage.
- ☐ **Six instrumentation subsystems:** Engine, Drivetrain, Hydraulic/3-pt, PTO, Operator-console, Precision-Ag.
- ☐ **Master formula:** $P_{db} = (1 - s) \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_m \cdot P_b$
- ☐ **Engine Power:** $P_b = 2\pi NT / 60{,}000$ (kW)
- ☐ **Drawbar Power from force & velocity:** $P_{db} = F_{db} \cdot v_a / 1000$ (kW)
- ☐ **Wheel slip:** $s = (v_t - v_a)/v_t$ — optimum range 8–15% for max tractive efficiency.
- ☐ **Tractive efficiency** typically 60–75% in fields, up to 85% on hard surfaces.
- ☐ **Magnetic-pickup sensor** uses Faraday's law; output frequency proportional to RPM.
- ☐ **LVDT** = Linear Variable Differential Transformer — three-coil, AC-excited, frictionless, infinite resolution.
- ☐ **Strain-gauge draft sensor** mounted on lower link senses implement draft force.
- ☐ **GPS + auto-steer** = RTK-GNSS, sub-2 cm accuracy, reduces overlap to <1%.
- ☐ **ISOBUS (ISO 11783)** = standardized communication between tractor and implement ECUs.
- ☐ **PTO speeds** = 540 rpm (standard), 540E (economy), 1000 rpm (large).
- ☐ **Field capacity** $C = 0.36 \cdot w \cdot v_a$ ha/h — use this for any "area covered per hour" question.
- ☐ **Remember unit conversions:** 1 hp = 0.746 kW; 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h.
- ☐ **Common sensors and their faults** (refer Section 3.4 analytical matrix) — quick recall before the exam.

> **END OF MODULE 4 — TRACTOR NOTES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Pumps: Pump Characteristics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTUTRE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Pumps: Pump Characteristics

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pumps in Agricultural Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **pump** is a mechanical device that converts mechanical energy (from an electric motor, IC engine, or renewable source) into hydraulic energy, thereby moving fluids (water, fertilizer solutions, or pesticide mixtures) from a lower elevation/pressure to a higher elevation/pressure against gravitational and frictional resistances. In agriculture, pumps form the **prime mover of every irrigation, fertigation, and chemigation system.**

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket on a Rope" Intuition

Imagine lifting water from a well using a bucket on a rope:

- **You pulling the rope** = mechanical energy input (analogous to the motor/engine shaft).
- **The bucket rising with water** = hydraulic energy output (pressure + kinetic + potential energy).
- **The height the water reaches** = **HEAD (H)**, measured in metres of water column.
- **The volume of water delivered per second** = **DISCHARGE (Q)**, measured in litres per second (L/s) or m³/hr.

A pump simply automates this "bucket on a rope" action thousands of times per minute — but in three different physical styles (centrifugal, reciprocating, rotary), each suited to a particular farm scenario.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used in Pump Engineering:**
> - Density of water, **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Atmospheric pressure head, **H_atm ≈ 10.33 m of water**
> - 1 H.P. = **745.7 W ≈ 0.746 kW**

---

## 1.2 Classification of Pumps — The Master Taxonomy

Pumps are classified using **three independent criteria**, which together uniquely identify any pump used in agricultural engineering.

### Criterion 1: Principle of Energy Transfer (The Most Important KTU Classification)

```
                    PUMPS
                       |
        +--------------+--------------+
        |                             |
   DYNAMIC PUMPS             POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT PUMPS
 (Continuous kinetic          (Mechanical trapping +
  energy transfer)             fixed-volume displacement)
        |                             |
   +----+----+              +---------+---------+
   |    |    |              |                   |
Centri- Axial Mixed     RECIPROCATING        ROTARY
fugal  flow  flow       (Piston/Plunger)   (Vane/Gear/Lobe)
```

#### A. Dynamic (Kinetic) Pumps
Energy is added to the fluid continuously by an **impeller** spinning at high speed, converting shaft work into velocity head, which then diffuses into pressure head.

| Sub-type | Flow Direction vs Shaft | Typical Agricultural Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Radial / Centrifugal** | Perpendicular to shaft (radial outward) | Surface irrigation, drip systems, sprinkler booster pumps |
| **Axial Flow** | Parallel to shaft (along axis) | High-discharge, low-head canal pumping (paddy fields, drainage) |
| **Mixed Flow** | Diagonal (between radial and axial) | Medium-head borewell / medium-discharge river lift schemes |

#### B. Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps
A **fixed volume of fluid is mechanically trapped**, then mechanically forced (displaced) into the discharge pipe. Discharge is theoretically independent of pressure.

| Sub-type | Mechanism | Typical Agricultural Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating** (Piston / Plunger) | Back-and-forth piston in cylinder | High-pressure pesticide sprayers, hydrostatic testing |
| **Rotary** (Vane, Gear, Lobe, Screw) | Rotating element traps and pushes fluid | Oil/diesel transfer, viscous liquid fertilizer dosing, thick slurry pumping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centrifugal vs Reciprocating — Selection Rule of Thumb for KTU Viva:**
> - Need **high flow at low–medium pressure** → **Centrifugal pump** (most common in irrigation).
> - Need **low flow at very high pressure** → **Reciprocating pump** (e.g., orchard/airblast sprayers that need 20–40 bar).

### Criterion 2: Type of Drive / Power Source

| Drive Type | Symbol Used in Catalogues | Agricultural Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| **Electric Motor (3-phase / 1-phase)** | E | Grid-connected farm, dairy effluent pumping |
| **Diesel Engine** | D | Remote farms without electricity |
| **Solar PV (DC or AC via VFD)** | S | Off-grid irrigation (PM-KUSUM scheme in India) |
| **Tractor PTO** | T | Mobile irrigation, custom-hiring centres |
| **Manual / Human** | M | Tribal / hilly regions, livestock watering |

### Criterion 3: Installation Geometry (Specific to Agricultural Use)

- **Surface (monoblock) pump** — installed at ground level, draws water by suction. Max suction lift ≈ **7 m** (atmospheric limit).
- **Submersible pump** — fully immersed in water inside the borewell. Pushes water upward.
- **Open-well submersible / Jet pump** — combined suction + ejector for deeper draft from open wells.
- **Floating / Trash pump** — barge-mounted, used in flooded paddy or aquaculture.

---

## 1.3 Introduction to Pump Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Pump Characteristic:**
> A *pump characteristic* is a **graphical or mathematical relationship** between a pump's operating variable (Head, Power, Efficiency) and its operating point (Discharge Q), **at a constant impeller speed (N)** and a constant impeller diameter (D).
> These curves are obtained experimentally at the manufacturer's test bed and are published as the *pump performance chart* (ISO 9906 standard).

The four fundamental characteristic curves every KTU student must know are:
1. **Head vs Discharge** → *H–Q curve*
2. **Power Input vs Discharge** → *P–Q curve*
3. **Efficiency vs Discharge** → *η–Q curve*
4. **Net Positive Suction Head vs Discharge** → *NPSH–Q curve*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Conversion Inside a Pump — Stage by Stage

The mechanical power supplied at the shaft (P_sh) goes through the following energy transformations:

$$P_{sh} \;\xrightarrow{\eta_{mech}}\; P_{water,\,internal} \;\xrightarrow{\eta_{vol}}\; P_{useful\;hydraulic} \;=\; \rho\,g\,Q\,H_{man}$$

Where each step loses a fraction of energy:

1. **Mechanical losses** (bearing friction, disc friction on impeller shroud) → **Mechanical efficiency η_mech** (typical 95–98%).
2. **Volumetric losses** (fluid slip from high-pressure to low-pressure side via clearance gaps) → **Volumetric efficiency η_vol** (typical 95–99%).
3. **Hydraulic losses** (shock, friction, turbulence inside the volute) → **Manometric / Hydraulic efficiency η_man** (typical 85–95%).

---

## 2.2 The Three Efficiencies and Overall Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overall Efficiency of a Pump (KTU Favourite Formula):**

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{overall} \;=\; \eta_{man} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} \;=\; \frac{\rho\,g\,Q\,H_{man}}{P_{sh}}\;}$$

### 2.2.1 Manometric Efficiency (η_man)
Ratio of **manometric head (H_man)** actually imparted to the fluid (measured at pump flanges) to the **head imparted by the impeller (V_w₁·u₁/g)** theoretically.

$$\eta_{man} \;=\; \frac{g\,H_{man}}{V_{w_1}\,u_1}$$

Where:
- $V_{w_1}$ = velocity of whirl at impeller exit (m/s) — the tangential component of absolute velocity
- $u_1$ = blade tip speed at exit (m/s)

### 2.2.2 Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)

$$\eta_{mech} \;=\; \frac{P_{water\,input\;to\,impeller}}{P_{shaft}}$$

### 2.2.3 Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol)

$$\eta_{vol} \;=\; \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{actual} + Q_{slip}}$$

---

## 2.3 The Four Characteristic Curves — Logic & "Why"

### A. H–Q Curve (Head vs Discharge)
- As discharge Q increases, the head **decreases monotonically** (for a centrifugal pump with backward-curved vanes — the most common).
- **Reason:** Higher flow means higher friction inside the volute, leaving less pressure head.
- For an axial-flow pump, the drop is **much steeper** — they are sensitive to head changes.

### B. P–Q Curve (Power vs Discharge)
- For centrifugal pumps with backward-curved vanes: power **rises slightly** with Q (a "non-overloading" characteristic — desirable for safe motor sizing).
- For forward-curved vanes: power rises **sharply** → can overload the motor.
- For PD pumps: power rises **linearly** with discharge pressure.

### C. η–Q Curve (Efficiency vs Discharge)
- Bell-shaped curve with a single **Best Efficiency Point (B.E.P.)**.
- Operation far from BEP → cavitation risk, vibration, reduced bearing life.
- **Design rule:** Always operate the pump within ±15% of Q at BEP.

### D. NPSH–Q Curve (Net Positive Suction Head vs Discharge)
- As Q increases, the required NPSH **rises** (because higher velocity → lower pressure at the impeller eye → higher cavitation risk).
- **NPSH_available > NPSH_required** must always hold, else cavitation occurs.

---

## 2.4 Affinity Laws (Pump Scaling Laws — Frequently Asked in KTU)

When speed (N) or impeller diameter (D) is changed, all performance parameters scale predictably:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} \;=\; \frac{N_1}{N_2}\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{3}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{H_1}{H_2} \;=\; \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{2}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{P_1}{P_2} \;=\; \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^{3}\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{5}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Useful Memory Hook:** Q ∝ N·D³, H ∝ N²·D², P ∝ N³·D⁵. If you remember only one number, remember **"cubic law"** for power — that's why even a small speed increase dramatically raises energy consumption.

---

## 2.5 Specific Speed (Ns) — Pump Family Identifier

Specific speed is a **dimensionless number** that classifies the geometric family of a pump (radial, mixed, axial) irrespective of its size.

$$\boxed{\;N_s \;=\; \frac{N\,\sqrt{Q}}{(g\,H)^{3/4}}\;}$$

(N in rad/s, Q in m³/s, H in m, g = 9.81 m/s² — some texts omit g; both forms accepted in KTU)

| Ns range (in SI) | Pump Family |
|---|---|
| 10 – 50 | Radial (Centrifugal) — high head, low discharge |
| 50 – 150 | Mixed flow — medium head, medium discharge |
| 150 – 400 | Axial flow — low head, high discharge |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Sheet — Print this and pin it to your wall.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | SI Unit | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | Water power delivered | W | Compute power output |
| 2 | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Shaft power input | W | Motor sizing |
| 3 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man}\eta_{vol}\eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency | dimensionless | KTU 14-mark problems |
| 4 | $H_{man} = \dfrac{V_{w_1}u_1 \pm V_{w_2}u_2}{g}$ | Euler head equation | m | Derivation questions |
| 5 | $u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$ | Blade tip speed | m/s | Speed scaling |
| 6 | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{(gH)^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed | rad/s | Pump family ID |
| 7 | $NPSH_{avail} = H_{atm} - H_{vap} - H_{suction} - h_{f,suction}$ | Available NPSH | m | Cavitation check |
| 8 | $Q \propto ND^3$ | Affinity law (flow) | — | Variable-speed operation |
| 9 | $H \propto N^2 D^2$ | Affinity law (head) | — | Variable-speed operation |
| 10 | $P \propto N^3 D^5$ | Affinity law (power) | — | Energy audit in irrigation |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Agricultural Utility

| Application | Pump Type Selected | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Drip irrigation for a 2-hectare tomato farm | Centrifugal monoblock (1–3 HP) | Moderate head (15–25 m), continuous duty |
| Sprinkler system for a 1-acre lawn | Centrifugal high-head (3–5 HP) | Higher pressure (30–40 m) for sprinkler nozzles |
| Pesticide power sprayer (orchard) | Reciprocating / Diaphragm pump | High pressure (20–40 bar) required |
| Paddy field dewatering | Axial flow pump | Very high discharge, low head |
| Borewell at 200 m depth | Submersible centrifugal (5–10 HP) | Pushes water from below, no suction lift |
| Solar-powered irrigation (PM-KUSUM) | DC surface centrifugal or submersible | Direct coupling to PV array, variable speed |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Turbomachine Equation for Pump Head

This is a **core KTU derivation** — it converts the velocity triangles at the impeller inlet and outlet into a usable head formula.

### Setting Up the Velocity Triangles at Inlet (suffix 1) and Outlet (suffix 2)

At any point on the impeller blade, the fluid has two velocity components:
- **Absolute velocity V** (resultant of fluid motion + blade motion)
- **Relative velocity W** (velocity of fluid relative to the rotating blade)
- **Blade velocity u** (tangential velocity of the blade itself)

Each velocity can be resolved into:
- **Tangential component** (subscript w, for "whirl")
- **Radial / Meridional component** (subscript f, for "flow")

### Step 1 — Blade (tip) velocities at inlet and outlet

$$\boxed{\;u_1 = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60}\quad\quad u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}\;}$$

**Explanation:** The blade tip moves in a circle of circumference π·D once per revolution. There are N/60 revolutions per second, hence the formula.

### Step 2 — Work done per second per unit weight of fluid (Euler's equation)

Applying the angular momentum theorem between inlet (1) and outlet (2):

$$W_{Euler} \;=\; \frac{1}{g}\,(V_{w_1}\,u_1 \pm V_{w_2}\,u_2)$$

**Explanation of ± sign:**
- The **positive sign** is used when the **whirl at exit has the same direction as the impeller rotation** (called "free vortex" design — most common in centrifugal pumps).
- The **negative sign** is used in turbines (where the fluid loses energy).

For pumps, we keep the **positive sign** for radial-flow designs:

$$\boxed{\;H_{Euler} = \frac{V_{w_1}\,u_1 + V_{w_2}\,u_2}{g}\;}$$

### Step 3 — Introducing slip factor (σ) to account for finite blade count

Real impellers have a **finite number of blades** (5–9 typically), which causes the relative flow to deviate from the design angle. This is accounted for by the **slip coefficient σ** (Stodola/Pfaff), typically between 0.85 and 0.95:

$$H_{actual} \;=\; \sigma\,H_{Euler}$$

### Step 4 — Manometric head (measured at pump flanges)

$$H_{man} \;=\; \frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^{2} - V_1^{2}}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1)$$

For a pump tested with identical inlet/outlet pipe sizes (V₁ ≈ V₂) and on the same horizontal level (z₁ ≈ z₂):

$$H_{man} \;\approx\; \frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g}$$

### Step 5 — Manometric efficiency (η_man) — definition

$$\eta_{man} \;=\; \frac{g\,H_{man}}{V_{w_1}\,u_1 \,+\, V_{w_2}\,u_2} \;=\; \frac{actual\;manometric\;head}{Euler\;head}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — A Typical KTU 14-Mark Numerical

> **Problem Statement:**
> A centrifugal pump delivers **0.05 m³/s** of water against a manometric head of **20 m**. The shaft power input is **15 kW**. Calculate:
> (a) The overall efficiency.
> (b) The water power output.
> (c) If the manometric, volumetric, and mechanical efficiencies are **0.90, 0.95, and 0.96** respectively, verify the overall efficiency.

### Part (a) — Overall Efficiency

**Given:**
- Q = 0.05 m³/s
- H_man = 20 m
- P_sh = 15 kW = 15,000 W
- ρ = 1000 kg/m³
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1:** Compute water power (useful hydraulic power):

$$P_{water} \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, H_{man}$$

$$P_{water} \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 20$$

$$P_{water} \;=\; 9810 \;\text{W} \;=\; 9.81 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 2:** Apply efficiency definition:

$$\eta_{overall} \;=\; \frac{P_{water}}{P_{sh}} \;=\; \frac{9810}{15000}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{overall} \;=\; 0.654 \;\approx\; 65.4\%\;}$$

### Part (b) — Water Power Output

$$\boxed{\;P_{water} \;=\; 9.81\;\text{kW}\;}$$

(Already computed in Step 1 above.)

### Part (c) — Verify η_overall from individual efficiencies

**Step 1:** Multiply the three given efficiencies:

$$\eta_{overall} \;=\; \eta_{man} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$$

$$\eta_{overall} \;=\; 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.96$$

$$\eta_{overall} \;=\; 0.8208 \;\approx\; 82.1\%$$

**Step 2 — Inconsistency observation:**
The computed overall efficiency from (a) was **65.4%**, but the product of given efficiencies yields **82.1%**. This indicates that the **actual measured discharge is lower than 0.05 m³/s**, or the manometric head was measured at a different point.

To find the **actual discharge** at which 82.1% efficiency holds:

$$Q_{actual} \;=\; \frac{\eta_{overall} \cdot P_{sh}}{\rho g H_{man}} \;=\; \frac{0.821 \times 15000}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 20}$$

$$Q_{actual} \;=\; \frac{12315}{196200} \;=\; 0.0628\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The discrepancy may also be due to the difference between *manometric head* (measured inside the pump) and *static head* (measured at the tank). This is a common KTU examination trap — read carefully whether the head given is **manometric, static, or total dynamic head (TDH)**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Pump Family from Specific Speed

Suppose the following data is given: **N = 1450 rpm, Q = 0.025 m³/s, H = 25 m.**

**Step 1:** Convert N to rad/s:

$$N \;=\; 1450 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} \;=\; 151.84\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute specific speed:

$$N_s \;=\; \frac{N\,\sqrt{Q}}{(g\,H)^{3/4}}$$

$$N_s \;=\; \frac{151.84 \times \sqrt{0.025}}{(9.81 \times 25)^{0.75}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerator:

$$\text{Num} \;=\; 151.84 \times 0.1581 \;=\; 24.01$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate denominator:

$$(9.81 \times 25)^{0.75} \;=\; (245.25)^{0.75}$$

Using $(245.25)^{0.75} = 245.25^{3/4}$:

$$245.25^{1/2} \times 245.25^{1/4} \;=\; 15.66 \times 12.53 \;=\; 196.2$$

**Step 5:** Final answer:

$$\boxed{\;N_s \;\approx\; \frac{24.01}{196.2} \;\approx\; 0.122\;\text{rad/s}\;}$$

This value lies in the **low range**, identifying it as a **radial (centrifugal) pump**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Centrifugal vs Reciprocating Pump Characteristics

| Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Q variation with H | Q ∝ 1/√H approximately (steep drop) | Q nearly independent of H |
| P variation with Q | Slow rise (non-overloading for backward-curved) | Linear rise with pressure |
| η–Q curve | Bell-shaped, BEP narrow | Plateaus over wider range |
| Suitability for viscous fluids | Poor (efficiency falls sharply) | Better |
| Suitability for solids / slurry | Poor (clogs impeller) | Moderate (with ball valves) |
| Discharge pulsation | Smooth (continuous flow) | Pulsating (needs air vessel) |
| Self-priming | No (unless primed externally) | Yes (creates vacuum) |
| Typical agricultural use | Irrigation, drip, sprinkler | High-pressure spraying |
| Max suction head | ~7 m (atmospheric limit) | ~7 m (atmospheric limit) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Pump Selection Workflow for Agricultural Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Farm Water Requirement Litres per Day] --> B[Source Type Open Well / Borewell / River / Canal]
    B --> C[Total Dynamic Head TDH in metres]
    C --> D[Required Discharge Q in L per s]
    A --> D
    D --> E{Specific Speed Ns}
    E -->|10 to 50| F[Radial Centrifugal Pump]
    E -->|50 to 150| G[Mixed Flow Pump]
    E -->|150 to 400|H[Axial Flow Pump]
    F --> I[Power Source Selection]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|Grid| J[3 Phase Electric Motor]
    I -->|Off grid| K[Diesel Engine]
    I -->|Renewable| L[Solar PV with VFD]
    I -->|Mobile| M[Tractor PTO]
    J --> N[Final Pump Set]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Performance Validation H Q P Efficiency Curves]
```

## 4.2 Characteristic Curves — Schematic Plot Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph X[Q axis Discharge increasing]
        direction LR
        Q0[Q equals 0 Shutoff] --> Q1[Q equals 0.5 Qbep] --> Q2[Q equals Qbep BEP] --> Q3[Q equals 1.2 Qbep Max]
    end
    subgraph Y1[H Q Curve]
        H1[Head Max at Q equals 0] --> H2[Head decreases] --> H3[Head at BEP] --> H4[Head Min at Max Q]
    end
    subgraph Y2[P Q Curve]
        P1[Power Min at Q equals 0] --> P2[Power rises slowly] --> P3[Power at BEP] --> P4[Power Max at Max Q]
    end
    subgraph Y3[Eta Q Curve]
        E1[Eta low at zero flow] --> E2[Eta rising] --> E3[Peak at BEP] --> E4[Eta falling]
    end
```

## 4.3 Velocity Triangle at Pump Impeller Outlet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blade Velocity u2 tangential] --> B[Relative Velocity W2 along blade]
    A --> C[Absolute Velocity V2 resultant]
    B --> C
    C --> D[Whirl Component Vw2 tangential projection]
    C --> E[Flow Component Vf2 meridional projection]
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Centrifugal Pump (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Suction Pipe from Reservoir] --> B[Eye of Impeller Inlet]
    B --> C[Impeller Rotating Blades Adds Kinetic Energy]
    C --> D[Volute Casing Diffuser Converts Kinetic to Pressure]
    D --> E[Delivery Pipe to Irrigation System]
    F[Electric Motor Shaft] -->|coupled to| C
```

## 4.5 Cavitation Boundary — NPSH Margin Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Available NPSH minus Required NPSH] --> B{Result}
    B -->|Greater than zero by 0.5 to 1 m| C[Safe Operation No Cavitation]
    B -->|Zero| D[Cavitation Onset Damage Begins]
    B -->|Less than zero| E[Continuous Cavitation Impeller Pitting Failure]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: Differentiate between a centrifugal pump and a positive displacement pump. Give one agricultural application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Centrifugal Pump | Positive Displacement Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Continuous kinetic energy transfer | Mechanical trapping + displacement |
| Discharge vs Head | Q falls as H rises (sensitive to head) | Q nearly independent of H |
| Discharge flow | Smooth, non-pulsating | Pulsating (needs air vessel for reciprocating) |
| Efficiency | Falls sharply for viscous fluids | Maintains efficiency for viscous fluids |
| Self-priming | Requires external priming | Self-priming |

**Agricultural applications:**
- Centrifugal pump → Drip / sprinkler irrigation for 1–5 hectare farms.
- Positive displacement pump → Power sprayer for orchards / pesticide application at high pressure (20–40 bar).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define manometric efficiency of a centrifugal pump and state its typical range.**

**Model Answer:**

Manometric efficiency is the ratio of the **manometric head (H_man)** actually delivered to the fluid (as measured between pump suction and delivery flanges) to the **head theoretically imparted by the impeller** as predicted by Euler's equation.

$$\eta_{man} \;=\; \frac{g\,H_{man}}{V_{w_1}\,u_1 + V_{w_2}\,u_2}$$

**Typical range:** **85% – 95%** for well-designed centrifugal pumps used in agricultural service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't mix units** — Always convert N from rpm to rad/s when using N_s formula; convert Q from L/s to m³/s; convert H from cm of water to m.
> 2. **Don't forget the 3 efficiencies** — Many students write only η_overall = P_water / P_sh and miss the manometric / volumetric / mechanical breakdown — KTU examiners award separate marks for this.
> 3. **Read "head" carefully** — "Head" in pump problems may be **static head, manometric head, or total dynamic head (TDH)**. They are different. Verify which one is given.
> 4. **Always state the affinity law assumptions** — N and D scaling assumes geometric similarity and constant efficiency.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Classify pumps used in agriculture with a neat block diagram. Explain the working principle of a **centrifugal pump** with a labelled sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump running at **1450 rpm** delivers **0.04 m³/s** of water against a head of **25 m**. The impeller diameter is **0.30 m** and its width at outlet is **0.025 m**. If the manometric efficiency is **0.90**, determine:
   - (i) The velocity of whirl at outlet (V_w2)
   - (ii) The angle of the vane at outlet
   - (iii) The shaft power if the overall efficiency is **0.70**. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Classification (Block Diagram)** — See SECTION 1.2, Criterion 1 diagram.

**Working principle of centrifugal pump (8 steps):**
1. The pump casing is first **primed** (filled) with water — air is removed.
2. The **electric motor / engine** rotates the **impeller** at high speed (typically 1450–2900 rpm).
3. Water enters the **eye (centre)** of the impeller axially.
4. The rotating impeller **sweeps water outward** via centrifugal force, gaining kinetic energy.
5. Water leaves the impeller tip at high velocity into the **volute casing** (a gradually expanding spiral passage).
6. In the volute, the **kinetic energy is diffused (decelerated)** and converted into **pressure energy** (Bernoulli's principle).
7. Pressurized water is delivered through the **delivery pipe** to the irrigation system.
8. A partial **vacuum** is created at the eye, which draws more water up the suction pipe (suction lift ≤ 7 m).

#### Solution to (b):

**Given:** N = 1450 rpm, Q = 0.04 m³/s, H_man = 25 m, D₂ = 0.30 m, B₂ = 0.025 m, η_man = 0.90, η_overall = 0.70.

**Step 1:** Blade (tip) velocity at outlet:

$$u_2 \;=\; \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60} \;=\; 22.78\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 (i):** Velocity of whirl at outlet (V_w2):

From Euler's equation with free-vortex design (V_w1·u₁ ≈ 0 for radial inlet):

$$H_{Euler} \;=\; \frac{V_{w_2}\,u_2}{g}$$

And $\eta_{man} = H_{man}/H_{Euler}$, therefore:

$$H_{Euler} \;=\; \frac{H_{man}}{\eta_{man}} \;=\; \frac{25}{0.90} \;=\; 27.78\;\text{m}$$

$$V_{w_2} \;=\; \frac{g \cdot H_{Euler}}{u_2} \;=\; \frac{9.81 \times 27.78}{22.78}$$

$$\boxed{\;V_{w_2} \;=\; 11.96\;\text{m/s}\;}$$

**Step 2 (ii):** Velocity of flow at outlet (V_f2):

$$V_{f_2} \;=\; \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2} \;=\; \frac{0.04}{\pi \times 0.30 \times 0.025}$$

$$V_{f_2} \;=\; \frac{0.04}{0.02356} \;=\; 1.698\;\text{m/s}$$

Angle of vane at outlet (β₂):

$$\tan\beta_2 \;=\; \frac{V_{f_2}}{u_2 - V_{w_2}} \;=\; \frac{1.698}{22.78 - 11.96} \;=\; \frac{1.698}{10.82}$$

$$\beta_2 \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.157) \;=\; \boxed{\;8.93^{\circ}\;}$$

**Step 2 (iii):** Shaft power:

$$P_{sh} \;=\; \frac{\rho g Q H_{man}}{\eta_{overall}} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 25}{0.70}$$

$$P_{sh} \;=\; \frac{9810}{0.70} \;=\; 14{,}014\;\text{W} \;\approx\; \boxed{\;14.0\;\text{kW}\;}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of characteristic curves, explain the performance of a **centrifugal pump**. Define Best Efficiency Point (BEP) and explain its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A farmer wants to operate an existing centrifugal pump (rated **5 HP, 2900 rpm, Q = 5 L/s, H = 20 m**) at a reduced speed of **2200 rpm** using a VFD. Calculate the new discharge, head, and power required. State one benefit and one precaution of this approach. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

The four characteristic curves (see SECTION 4.2 schematic) and their key behaviour:

1. **H–Q Curve:** Head decreases monotonically with Q. At zero discharge (Q = 0), head is maximum (shut-off head).
2. **P–Q Curve:** For backward-curved vanes (most agricultural pumps), power rises gently with Q — non-overloading characteristic, so motor can be sized at BEP.
3. **η–Q Curve:** Bell-shaped curve; efficiency is low at zero flow and at very high flow; peaks at **Best Efficiency Point (B.E.P.)**.
4. **NPSH–Q Curve:** NPSH_required rises with Q; cavitation risk increases at high flows.

**Definition of B.E.P.:** The Best Efficiency Point is the single operating point (Q_bep, H_bep) on the η–Q curve where the overall efficiency attains its **maximum value**.

**Significance:**
- Operating near BEP ensures **minimum energy consumption** per unit water delivered.
- Reduces **cavitation risk**, vibration, bearing wear.
- Maximizes **motor life**.
- Operating far from BEP (>15%) is one of the main reasons for premature pump failure in Indian farms.

#### Solution to (b):

**Given:** N₁ = 2900 rpm, Q₁ = 5 L/s = 0.005 m³/s, H₁ = 20 m, N₂ = 2200 rpm.

**Apply Affinity Laws:**

**New Discharge Q₂:**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \;=\; \frac{N_2}{N_1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad Q_2 \;=\; 0.005 \times \frac{2200}{2900}$$

$$Q_2 \;=\; 0.005 \times 0.7586 \;=\; \boxed{\;0.00379\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}\;=\;3.79\;\text{L/s}\;}$$

**New Head H₂:**

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} \;=\; \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad H_2 \;=\; 20 \times (0.7586)^2$$

$$H_2 \;=\; 20 \times 0.5755 \;=\; \boxed{\;11.51\;\text{m}\;}$$

**New Power P₂:**

Assuming same efficiency at the new operating point:

$$P_1 \;=\; \frac{5 \times 0.746}{0.7} \;\approx\; 5.33\;\text{kW (at design efficiency 70%)}$$

Wait — let me recompute properly. The water power at original point:

$$P_{water,1} \;=\; \rho g Q_1 H_1 \;=\; 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005 \times 20 \;=\; 981\;\text{W}$$

Assuming η_overall ≈ 0.70 at original point:

$$P_{sh,1} \;=\; \frac{981}{0.70} \;=\; 1401\;\text{W} \;=\; 1.88\;\text{HP}$$

(This is less than the rated 5 HP — implies that 5 HP is the *maximum allowable* power at a different operating point. We'll take η_overall ≈ const.)

**New shaft power** using affinity law:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} \;=\; \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \quad\Rightarrow\quad P_2 \;=\; 1401 \times (0.7586)^3$$

$$P_2 \;=\; 1401 \times 0.4365 \;=\; \boxed{\;611.5\;\text{W}\;\approx\; 0.82\;\text{HP}\;}$$

**One Benefit:** Energy saving — power reduces to 43.6% of original while delivering 75.8% of original flow → huge electricity savings for the farmer.

**One Precaution:** Operating speed below ~50% of rated speed may push the pump operating point away from BEP, causing reduced efficiency and potential overheating of the motor (since motor cooling fans slow down too). For VFD operation, use a **constant-torque (CT-rated) motor** or ensure VFD has a safe minimum frequency (typically 25–30 Hz).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [x] Pumps are classified into **Dynamic** (centrifugal, axial, mixed flow) and **Positive Displacement** (reciprocating, rotary).
- [x] **Centrifugal pumps** are the workhorse of agricultural irrigation; **PD pumps** dominate high-pressure spraying.
- [x] The **four characteristic curves** are H–Q (decreasing), P–Q (slowly increasing for non-overloading designs), η–Q (bell-shaped with BEP), NPSH–Q (increasing).
- [x] **Overall efficiency** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_{man}}{P_{sh}}$.
- [x] **Euler head** $H_{Euler} = (V_{w1}\,u_1 + V_{w2}\,u_2)/g$; with **slip factor σ** (0.85–0.95) for finite blades.
- [x] **Tip speed** $u_1 = \pi D_1 N / 60$ and $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$.
- [x] **Affinity Laws:** Q ∝ N·D³, H ∝ N²·D², P ∝ N³·D⁵.
- [x] **Specific Speed** $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/(gH)^{3/4}$ identifies pump family: < 50 → centrifugal, 50–150 → mixed, > 150 → axial.
- [x] **NPSH_available > NPSH_required + 0.5 m** for safe, cavitation-free operation.
- [x] **Maximum practical suction lift** for any pump = ~7 m at sea level (limited by atmospheric pressure 10.33 m minus vapour pressure and friction).
- [x] **B.E.P.** is the design operating point; running 15% away from it causes wear, vibration, and energy loss.
- [x] Agricultural pump selection flow: **Q + H + Power source → Specific speed → Pump family → Drive → Performance validation**.
- [x] **Slip factor** is the reason real pumps deliver slightly less head than Euler's equation predicts.
- [x] **Variable frequency drives (VFDs)** in solar pumps exploit affinity laws for enormous energy savings — a frequently tested KTU concept.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Memory Hook for KTU Viva / Interview:**
> *"Centrifugal pumps love steady flow and stable head; PD pumps love high pressure and viscous fluids. The four curves — H, P, η, NPSH — drawn against Q at constant N are the pump's fingerprint. Always size the pump at BEP."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pump Selection and Installation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT753 - INSTRUMENTATION FOR AGRICULTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pump Selection and Installation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pump Selection** is the systematic engineering process of determining the most suitable pumping equipment for a given agricultural water delivery system by matching the **operating point** (intersection of the pump characteristic curve and the system resistance curve) with the field's hydraulic requirements, efficiency goals, and economic constraints.

**Pump Installation** refers to the engineered deployment of the selected pump—including its foundation, suction and delivery piping, priming arrangement, valves, control instrumentation, and electrical drive—ensuring that the pump operates continuously near its **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** without cavitation, vibration, or mechanical failure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terms, the selection-installation pair is treated as a single **integrated design decision** under the Course Outcome *CO4: Design and select appropriate pumping systems for agricultural applications.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Water Tanker Problem**
> Imagine you must supply water to a farmhouse located 50 m uphill from a river. A bucket carried on foot (low flow, low head) is analogous to a *hand pump*; a motorcycle water-tanker (moderate flow, low head) is analogous to a *centrifugal pump*; a fire-truck pumper (high flow, high pressure) is analogous to a *multi-stage submersible pump*. Choosing the wrong "vehicle" wastes fuel (energy), causes breakdowns (cavitation), or simply fails to deliver water (insufficient head). **Pump selection is exactly this matching exercise—matching the system's hydraulic demand to the pump's delivery capability.**

**Why it matters in Agriculture:**
- A **centrifugal pump** on a drip-irrigation line that requires 40 m head but is selected for 20 m head will fail to push water to the farthest emitters.
- A **submersible pump** installed without proper NPSH margin will cavitate, destroy impellers within weeks, and waste electricity.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used (Bolded)

Every pump selection problem in KTU examinations references these **five mandatory metrics**:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Q** | Volumetric flow rate (discharge) | **m³/s** (or L/s) |
| **H** | Total Dynamic Head (TDH) | **m of water** |
| **P** | Shaft power input | **W (watts)** or **kW** |
| **η** | Pump efficiency (overall) | **dimensionless, %** |
| **NPSH** | Net Positive Suction Head available/required | **m** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **operating point** of a pump is the unique point on the H–Q curve where the pump's delivered head exactly equals the system's required head at the corresponding flow rate. This is the single most important concept in pump selection.

---

### 1.4 Quick Visualization of the Operating Point Concept

The operating point is where **two curves meet**:

1. **Pump Characteristic Curve** — supplied by the manufacturer (H decreases as Q increases for a centrifugal pump).
2. **System Head Curve** — calculated from the site (H increases roughly as Q² due to friction).

The intersection gives the **actual flow rate and head** the system will deliver — not the maximum values printed on the pump nameplate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Agricultural Pumps

Agricultural pumping systems (per KTU 2024 syllabus) are classified along three axes:

**A. By Principle of Operation**
- **Displacement (Positive) Pumps** — deliver a fixed volume per stroke.
  - *Reciprocating pumps* (piston, plunger) — high head, low flow, used in pesticide spraying.
  - *Rotary pumps* (gear, vane, screw) — used in fertilizer injection.
- **Rotodynamic (Centrifugal) Pumps** — impart kinetic energy to fluid.
  - *Radial flow* (e.g., plain centrifugal) — high head, moderate flow.
  - *Mixed flow* — moderate head, high flow.
  - *Axial flow* (propeller) — very high flow, low head, used in large canal lifts.
- **Jet Pumps & Air-Lift Pumps** — used where suction lifts are excessive.

**B. By Drive/Location**
- *Surface pumps* (above water level, suction lift < 7 m).
- *Submersible pumps* (immersed in well/borehole, push water upward).
- *Booster pumps* (inline, increase pressure in piped systems).

**C. By Application in Agriculture**
- *Irrigation pumps* (drip, sprinkler, flood).
- *Drainage pumps* (remove excess field water).
- *Fertigation/chemigation pumps* (low flow, high pressure, corrosion-resistant).

---

### 2.2 Selection Criteria — The 7-Step Decision Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to **list and explain the selection parameters in this exact order:**

1. **Required flow rate (Q)** — derived from crop water requirement, field area, and irrigation cycle.
2. **Total Dynamic Head (TDH)** — sum of static head + friction head + velocity head + minor losses.
3. **Suction conditions** — determine available NPSH (NPSHₐ) and whether a self-priming or submersible pump is needed.
4. **Power source** — electric (3-phase preferred for >5 HP), diesel, or solar (for off-grid farms).
5. **Fluid properties** — clean water, muddy water, fertilizer solution, slurry (each demands different impeller material).
6. **Duty cycle & reliability** — continuous (drip) vs intermittent (flood).
7. **Economics** — capital cost, energy cost (kWh × tariff × hours), and life-cycle maintenance.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 8 equations — they cover ~90% of KTU numerical problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Hydraulic (water) power delivered | W |
| 2 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Shaft power input to pump | W |
| 3 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}}$ | Motor input power (electrical) | W |
| 4 | $H_{sys} = H_{static} + h_{f} + h_{minor} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | Total system head | m |
| 5 | $h_{f} = \dfrac{4 f L V^{2}}{2 g D} = K \cdot Q^{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss (K = constant for given pipe) | m |
| 6 | $NPSH_{a} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} \pm H_{s} - h_{f,suction}$ | Available NPSH at pump suction | m |
| 7 | $N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (for impeller classification) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Affinity Laws: $\dfrac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} = \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}$, $\dfrac{H_{2}}{H_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{2}$ | Flow/Head scaling with speed | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA CLARIFICATION** — In KTU exam papers, the friction loss is always written using the discharge form:
> $$h_{f} = K \cdot Q^{2}$$
> where the constant $K$ incorporates pipe length, diameter, friction factor, and the unit conversion $V = Q/A$. This quadratic form is what produces the **parabolic system curve**.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Drip Irrigation:** Q is small (2–10 L/s), H is moderate (10–30 m), and **specific speed** is low — a *vane-type positive displacement* or *small radial centrifugal* pump is optimal.
- **Sprinkler Irrigation:** H = 30–60 m, Q = 5–30 L/s — a *radial flow centrifugal* pump with a BEP matching the system curve is standard.
- **Borehole Supply:** Suction lift > 7 m is impossible for a centrifugal pump — a *submersible* or *jet* pump is mandatory.
- **Canal Lift Irrigation:** Very high Q, low H — an *axial flow propeller pump* is the most energy-efficient choice.

The proper match of pump-type to duty saves **20–40% of electricity**, which is the single largest operating cost in irrigated agriculture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the System Head Equation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU **frequently asks for the derivation of total system head** (worth 4–5 marks). Below is the complete, step-by-step working.

**Starting Principle:** The total head a pump must supply equals the elevation rise plus all energy losses between the source reservoir and the delivery point.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation** between the source water surface (point 1) and the discharge point (point 2):

$$
\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} + H_{pump} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2} + h_{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify boundary conditions for an open reservoir delivering to a free surface:**
- $P_{1} = P_{2} = P_{atm}$ (both surfaces are at atmospheric pressure).
- $V_{1} \approx 0$ (large reservoir, negligible velocity at the surface).
- $V_{2} \approx 0$ if discharging into open air; otherwise retain $V_{2}^{2}/2g$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the boundary conditions:**

$$
H_{pump} = (z_{2} - z_{1}) + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g} + h_{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the static head:** $H_{static} = z_{2} - z_{1}$ — this is the fixed elevation difference between the source and the delivery point, independent of flow.

**Step 5 — Define the velocity head term:** $H_{velocity} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ — kinetic energy of the discharged jet.

**Step 6 — Express the head loss as two components:** major (friction in straight pipe) and minor (fittings, valves, entry/exit):

$$
h_{L} = h_{f,major} + h_{f,minor}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the Darcy–Weisbach formula for major loss:**

$$
h_{f,major} = \frac{4 f L V^{2}}{2 g D}
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute the equivalent-length method for minor losses** (each fitting has a resistance coefficient $K_i$):

$$
h_{f,minor} = \sum_{i} K_{i} \frac{V^{2}}{2g}
$$

**Step 9 — Group all velocity-squared terms** (using $V = Q/A = 4Q/\pi D^{2}$):

$$
h_{L} = \left[ \frac{4 f L}{2 g D} + \sum K_{i} \right] \cdot \frac{V^{2}}{2g} = K_{total} \cdot Q^{2}
$$

**Step 10 — Write the final compact system head equation:**

$$
\boxed{H_{sys} = H_{static} + K_{total} \cdot Q^{2} + \frac{V_{d}^{2}}{2g}}
$$

This quadratic-in-Q form is what allows the **system curve** to be plotted as a parabola on the H–Q chart.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Pump Power Equation

> [!NOTE]
> A second KTU favorite. Worth memorizing with units.

**Step 1 — Define hydraulic (water) power as the rate of useful work delivery:**

$$
P_{water} = \text{(weight delivered per second)} \times \text{(head)}
$$

**Step 2 — Weight delivered per second** equals $\rho g Q$ (mass flow $\rho Q$ times $g$):

$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q H
$$

**Step 3 — Account for mechanical, volumetric, and hydraulic losses** via the overall efficiency $\eta$:

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for motor losses** to get the electrical input:

$$
P_{electrical} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}
$$

**Step 5 — Standard numerical form** (with $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$
\boxed{P_{kW} = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H}{1000 \cdot \eta_{overall}}}
$$

where $Q$ is in m³/s, $H$ in m, and $P$ in kW.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump is required to deliver 15 L/s of water from a sump 4 m below the pump centerline to an overhead tank 18 m above the pump. The suction pipe is 6 m long, 80 mm diameter, with a foot valve and strainer (total minor loss coefficient = 6.0, friction factor = 0.025). The delivery pipe is 50 m long, 60 mm diameter, with two gate valves and four elbows (total minor $K = 3.5$, $f = 0.028$). Calculate: (a) total system head, (b) water power, (c) shaft power if $\eta_{pump} = 65\%$.

**Given:** $Q = 15$ L/s $= 0.015$ m³/s, $H_{static} = +18 - (-4) = 22$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step (a1) — Suction pipe velocity:**

$$
V_{s} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D_{s}^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 0.015}{\pi \times (0.080)^{2}} = \frac{0.060}{0.0201} = 2.985 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step (a2) — Suction friction loss (Darcy–Weisbach with minor):**

$$
h_{f,s} = \left( \frac{4 f L}{2 g D} + \sum K \right) \frac{V^{2}}{2g}
= \left( \frac{4 \times 0.025 \times 6}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.080} + 6.0 \right) \times \frac{(2.985)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
= (3.823 + 6.0) \times 0.4540 = 9.823 \times 0.4540 = 4.460 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (a3) — Delivery pipe velocity:**

$$
V_{d} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D_{d}^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 0.015}{\pi \times (0.060)^{2}} = 5.305 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step (a4) — Delivery friction loss:**

$$
h_{f,d} = \left( \frac{4 \times 0.028 \times 50}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.060} + 3.5 \right) \times \frac{(5.305)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
= (4.756 + 3.5) \times 1.434 = 8.256 \times 1.434 = 11.84 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (a5) — Total system head:**

$$
H_{sys} = H_{static} + h_{f,s} + h_{f,d} = 22 + 4.460 + 11.84 = 38.30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (b) — Water power:**

$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 38.30 = 5636 \text{ W} = 5.636 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (c) — Shaft power:**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{5.636}{0.65} = 8.67 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Implication:** A standard 10 HP (7.46 kW) motor would be undersized. A **12.5 HP (9.3 kW)** motor with a service factor of 1.15 must be specified. This is the level of reasoning KTU expects in the 14-mark question.

---

### 3.4 NPSH Analysis (Cavitation Margin)

To prevent cavitation, the condition is:

$$
NPSH_{available} \geq NPSH_{required} + \text{safety margin (typically 0.5–1.0 m)}
$$

For a pump installed **above** the sump (suction lift $H_s$ positive, in m):

$$
NPSH_{a} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - H_{s} - h_{f,suction}
$$

If $NPSH_{a} < NPSH_{req}$, the installation must be modified: (i) lower the pump, (ii) enlarge the suction pipe, (iii) cool the liquid, or (iv) switch to a submersible pump.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Net Positive Suction Head" (NPSH). Why is it the most critical parameter in pump installation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

NPSH is the total head available at the pump suction port, above the vapor pressure of the liquid, expressed in meters. It comprises atmospheric pressure head minus vapor pressure head minus suction lift minus suction-line friction loss.

$$
NPSH_{a} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - H_{s} - h_{f,suction}
$$

NPSH is the most critical installation parameter because **if the available NPSH falls below the pump's required NPSH, the liquid vaporizes at the impeller eye, forming vapor bubbles that collapse violently — this is cavitation**. Cavitation causes: (i) loss of head and capacity, (ii) pitting and erosion of the impeller, (iii) noise and vibration, and (iv) eventual mechanical failure of the pump. Hence, the KTU 2024 design rule is $NPSH_{a} \geq NPSH_{req} + 0.5$ m safety margin.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any four selection criteria for agricultural pumps and explain the role of "specific speed" in pump classification.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Four selection criteria: (i) **Required discharge Q** (from crop water requirement), (ii) **Total dynamic head H** (from site topography and pipe layout), (iii) **Fluid properties** (clean water, muddy water, fertilizer), and (iv) **Power source availability** (electric/diesel/solar).

**Specific speed** $N_s$ is a dimensionless number that classifies a pump's impeller geometry based on its flow-head-speed relationship:

$$
N_{s} = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}
$$

A **low** $N_s$ (below ~50) indicates a radial-flow impeller (high head, low flow). A **medium** $N_s$ (50–150) indicates mixed flow. A **high** $N_s$ (above 150) indicates axial flow (low head, high flow). Specific speed is the key index that tells the engineer **which geometric family of pump** to look at in a manufacturer's catalogue, ensuring the impeller shape matches the duty requirement.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. The standard sub-part split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both alternatives are given below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Centrifugal Pump for Drip Irrigation**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** A 10 HP centrifugal pump is proposed for a drip-irrigation system delivering water from a sump 3 m below the pump to a fertilizer tank 9 m above the pump. The suction line is 8 m long, 100 mm diameter, with a foot valve (K = 4.5) and a 90° elbow (K = 0.9). The delivery line is 40 m long, 75 mm diameter, with a gate valve (K = 0.2), a check valve (K = 2.0), and two elbows (K = 0.6 each). Friction factor $f = 0.025$ for both lines. Compute: (i) maximum discharge at 25 m total head, (ii) total system head at this discharge, (iii) NPSH available if water is at 30 °C and atmospheric pressure is 10 m of water. (7 marks)

**(b)** With reference to part (a), explain the step-by-step procedure to verify whether the selected pump is correctly matched to the system. Include the role of the BEP, impeller trimming, and VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) in the context of agricultural pump energy saving. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Write the system-head equation.** For maximum discharge estimation, assume $V_{d}^{2}/2g$ is included in minor losses; use:

$$
H_{sys} = H_{static} + \left[ \frac{4 f L}{2 g D} + \sum K \right]_{suction} \frac{V_s^{2}}{2g} + \left[ \frac{4 f L}{2 g D} + \sum K \right]_{delivery} \frac{V_d^{2}}{2g}
$$

**Step 2 — Static head.** $H_{static} = 3 + 9 = 12$ m.

**Step 3 — Compute K terms.**
- Suction: $K_{s} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.025 \times 8}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.100} + 4.5 + 0.9 = 0.4077 + 5.4 = 5.808$
- Delivery: $K_{d} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.025 \times 40}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075} + 0.2 + 2.0 + 2 \times 0.6 = 2.718 + 3.4 = 6.118$

**Step 4 — Express velocities in terms of Q (m³/s):**
- $V_{s} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi (0.100)^{2}} = 127.32 \, Q$
- $V_{d} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi (0.075)^{2}} = 226.35 \, Q$

**Step 5 — Substitute and form a quadratic in Q:**

$$
H_{sys} = 12 + 5.808 \times \frac{(127.32 Q)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} + 6.118 \times \frac{(226.35 Q)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
H_{sys} = 12 + 5.808 \times 826.0 \, Q^{2} + 6.118 \times 2610.8 \, Q^{2}
$$

$$
H_{sys} = 12 + 4797.7 \, Q^{2} + 15975 \, Q^{2} = 12 + 20773 \, Q^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Q at $H_{sys} = 25$ m:**

$$
25 = 12 + 20773 \, Q^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^{2} = \frac{13}{20773} = 6.26 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 0.0250 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s} = 25.0 \text{ L/s}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute NPSH available.** For water at 30 °C, vapor pressure head $P_{v}/\rho g \approx 0.43$ m. Suction lift $H_s = 3$ m. Suction friction at this Q:

$$
h_{f,s} = 4797.7 \times (0.0250)^{2} = 3.00 \text{ m}
$$

$$
NPSH_{a} = 10 - 0.43 - 3 - 3.00 = 3.57 \text{ m}
$$

Assuming the manufacturer specifies $NPSH_{req} = 2.5$ m for this pump, the margin is $3.57 - 2.5 = 1.07$ m, which is **acceptable** (above 0.5 m minimum).

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Operating Point Concept.** Plot the pump H–Q curve (manufacturer's data) and the system H–Q curve on the same graph. Their intersection gives the operating point. If this point lies within ±10% of the BEP on either side, the pump is correctly matched.

**Step 2 — Three Mismatch Scenarios and Remedies:**
- **Over-sized pump** (operating point far left of BEP): the pump is inefficient and the motor is overloaded. Remedies: (i) **trim the impeller diameter** to reduce the H–Q curve, or (ii) throttle the gate valve on the delivery line (wastes energy but simple).
- **Under-sized pump** (operating point far right of BEP, low head): add a **second stage** (convert to multi-stage) or replace with a larger pump.
- **Variable demand** (e.g., drip irrigation running fewer zones): install a **VFD** to vary motor speed N. From the affinity law, $Q \propto N$ and $H \propto N^{2}$, so a 20% speed reduction cuts power consumption to ~51% of full speed — a major energy saver for seasonal agricultural duty.

**Step 3 — Energy Economics.** A 10 HP pump running 8 h/day for 150 days consumes 10 × 0.746 × 8 × 150 = 8952 kWh/season. At ₹8/kWh, this is ₹71,616 per season. A 20% VFD-driven efficiency gain saves ₹14,323/season, paying back the VFD cost in one year.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Submersible Pump Installation**

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe, with a neat block diagram, the standard installation procedure for a **borehole submersible pump** used in a 60 m deep agricultural well. Include the items: pump mounting, drop pipe, foot piece, cable joint, control panel, and water-level sensor. (7 marks)

**(b)** A submersible pump must deliver 30 L/s against a total head of 75 m. Compute the required motor power assuming overall efficiency of 60%. If the farmer wants to add a second identical pump in parallel for redundancy, explain how the combined H–Q curve is obtained and state the rule for parallel operation. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

A borehole submersible pump installation is critical in KTU 2024 syllabus under "Installation of Pumps." The standard components and their arrangement are:

1. **Submersible pump end (impeller stack)** — directly coupled to a waterproof submersible motor. Both are lowered into the borehole.
2. **Drop pipe (rising main)** — GI or HDPE pipe screwed section-by-section, carrying water from the pump discharge to the surface. Must be centrally aligned and supported by cable ties to the **safety rope/cable**.
3. **Cable joint** — a heat-shrink, resin-filled joint at every 25–50 m of the submersible power cable to maintain IP68 water ingress protection.
4. **Check valve (non-return valve)** — installed every 50 m of drop pipe to prevent backflow during shutdown (prevents water hammer and motor reversal).
5. **Top termination** — at the wellhead: a gate valve, pressure gauge, and air-release valve.
6. **Control panel** — houses the motor starter (DOL or star-delta), single-phase preventer, dry-run protection relay, and ammeter/voltmeter.
7. **Water-level sensors** — (i) upper float switch to stop the pump when the overhead tank is full, (ii) lower probe/electrode sensors in the well to detect low water level and trip dry-run protection.
8. **Earthing** — proper earth electrode with resistance less than 5 Ω to prevent electric shock from a submerged, energized motor.

A **block diagram** of this sequence is given in SECTION 4.2 above (B1 → B2 → B3 → B4 → B5/B6 → B7 → B9 → B10). The examiner's expectation is that the student draws a labelled sequence of at least 6 blocks in correct vertical order.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the hydraulic power.**

$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 \times 75 = 22072 \text{ W} = 22.07 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required motor (shaft) power input.**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{22.07}{0.60} = 36.78 \text{ kW}
$$

A standard **40 kW (~54 HP)** 3-phase motor with a 1.15 service factor is the correct commercial selection.

**Step 3 — Parallel Operation Concept.** When two identical pumps are operated in parallel, the **heads are added at common flow**; the combined curve is obtained by **adding the discharges of the two pumps at the same head value**:

$$
Q_{combined}(H) = Q_{1}(H) + Q_{2}(H)
$$

For identical pumps:

$$
Q_{combined}(H) = 2 \times Q_{single}(H)
$$

**Step 4 — Rule for Parallel Operation.**
- Parallel operation is used when the system requires **higher flow at the same head** (e.g., two adjacent fields being irrigated simultaneously).
- The combined curve is **not simply double the flow at every point**; it must be plotted head-wise by reading the single-pump curve at multiple head values and doubling the discharge.

**Step 5 — Practical Note for Agriculture.** In practice, parallel pumps should have **separate non-return valves** to prevent one pump from "back-driving" the other during shutdown. The control panel must synchronize the starters to avoid simultaneous inrush currents that trip the main breaker.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $V^{2}/2g$ velocity head** at the discharge — examiners award 1 mark specifically for this term when present.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — Q in L/s vs m³/s, H in m vs kPa. Always show the unit conversion step. $1$ m of water $\approx 9.81$ kPa.
> 3. **Conflating NPSH available with NPSH required.** $NPSH_a$ depends on the *installation*; $NPSH_r$ depends on the *pump design*. The rule is always $NPSH_a > NPSH_r$.
> 4. **Omitting the safety margin on motor power.** A motor selected at exactly $P_{shaft}$ will overheat. Always add 10–15% margin (i.e., use a Service Factor of 1.15).
> 5. **Ignoring minor losses in short pipe runs.** In drip systems, fittings dominate friction. Computing only major loss and omitting $\sum K_i$ loses 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this on the morning of the exam):**

- **Pump selection is curve matching**, not catalogue shopping. Plot the system curve on the same axes as the manufacturer's H–Q curve.
- The **Operating Point** is the single most important concept — it is *not* the maximum values on the nameplate.
- **Five key equations** to memorize with units:
  1. $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$
  2. $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}$
  3. $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$
  4. $NPSH_{a} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - H_{s} - h_{f,suction}$
  5. $N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$
- **Centrifugal pump rule:** suction lift must be **less than 7 m** (atmospheric head in practical terms). Beyond that, use a submersible or jet pump.
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** always try to operate within ±10% of the BEP flow. Off-BEP operation causes vibration, noise, and reduced life.
- **Impeller trimming** reduces both head and flow (parallels the curve downward). Each 10% diameter reduction ≈ 19% head reduction (using the affinity law $H \propto D^{2}$).
- **VFD energy saving** comes from $P \propto N^{3}$ (cube law). Halving speed cuts power to 12.5%.
- **Parallel operation** adds discharges at constant head; **series operation** adds heads at constant discharge. Use parallel for *more flow*, series for *more pressure*.
- **Cavitation prevention** is non-negotiable in the KTU 2024 answer script — always mention the 0.5 m safety margin on NPSH.
- **Installation golden rules:** pump foundation must be level, suction pipe must slope continuously upward (no air pockets), pipe diameter should be ≥ pump port size, foot valve must be submerged 0.5 m below the lowest water level, and the pump-motor shaft alignment tolerance is **0.05 mm**.
- **Code/Standard reference (no specific page):** BIS IS 6595 (Horizontal centrifugal pumps) and IS 8034 (Submersible pumps) — do not cite specific page numbers; cite only the IS number and title.

---
